    - **Proto-Sinaitic script** (~1800–1500 BCE): discovered at Serabit el-Khadim (Sinai, Egyptian turquoise mines) by Flinders Petrie 1905; ~40 brief inscriptions by Semitic-speaking mine workers; acrophonic principle: pictogram of the first letter of the Semitic word for that object = the consonant; ʾalpu (ox head) → *aleph* (first letter shape); bêt (house floor plan) → *beth*; etc.; decipherment by Alan Gardiner (1916), further by W.F. Albright
    - **Proto-Canaanite and Ugaritic linear alphabet** (~1400–1200 BCE): developed from Proto-Sinaitic; earliest examples at Lachish, Gezer, Tell el-Hesi; the Ugaritic cuneiform alphabet is a parallel development
    - **Phoenician alphabet** (~1050 BCE onward): standardized 22-consonant (abjad — consonants only, no vowels) linear alphabet; adapted from Proto-Canaanite; Phoenicians spread it across the Mediterranean via trade
    - **Greek alphabet** (~800 BCE): Greeks adopted Phoenician alphabet and made the critical innovation of adding vowel letters (repurposing Semitic consonant letters for sounds absent in Semitic: *aleph* → alpha (A), *he* → epsilon (E), *yod* → iota (I), *ayin* → omicron (O), *waw* → upsilon (Y)); world's first true alphabet (vowels + consonants); branched into Latin alphabet (→ Western languages), Cyrillic (→ Slavic), Coptic, Gothic, etc.
    - **Aramaean script** (~900 BCE onward): Phoenician adapted for Aramaic; became the East's dominant script; basis for Hebrew square script, Arabic, Syriac, Nabataean, Brahmi (possibly) → Devanagari, and all South Asian alphabets; 2022 genetic-linguistic study of script diffusion traced detailed family tree

  - **Phoenicia (~1200–63 BCE)**
    - Not a unified state but a network of independent city-states sharing language, religion, and maritime culture: **Tyre, Sidon, Byblos (Gebal), Beirut, Arwad, Berytus**
    - Byblos: oldest continuously inhabited Levantine city; major Egyptian contact from ~3000 BCE; source of cedar for Egyptian ships and temples and coffins; so much papyrus imported there that Greeks named the city's product *biblos* (papyrus) → **bible** (book)
    - **Phoenician maritime supremacy**: cedar forests of Lebanon; expert shipbuilders; two ship types: round-hulled merchant *gaulos*; slim war galley; circumnavigated Africa for Pharaoh Necho II ~600 BCE per Herodotus (Greek sailors lost their courage when the sun was on their right [north] — this detail actually confirms the account, proving they rounded the Cape); navigated by stars (Ursa Minor = "Phoenician Star"); Carthage's sailors reached Britain (tin from Cornwall) and West Africa (*Hanno the Navigator's Periplus*, ~500 BCE)
    - **Phoenician colonies**: planted across the Mediterranean from ~900–600 BCE; Kition (Cyprus), Utica (Tunisia, pre-Carthage), **Carthage** (~814 BCE tradition; Dido legend), Sardinia (Sulki, Tharros, Cagliari), Sicily (Motya, Panormus), Malta (Mdina), Ibiza, Cádiz (Gadir, ~1100 BCE? — oldest city in western Europe if genuine), Mogador (Morocco), Lixus
    - **Carthage**: founded ~814 BCE (traditional; archaeology suggests ~800–760 BCE); grew to greatest western Mediterranean power; **Punic Wars** with Rome 264–146 BCE; Hannibal Barca's Alps crossing 218 BCE; Rome's eventual destruction of Carthage 146 BCE (*Carthago delenda est* — attributed to Cato); Punic language (Phoenician dialect) survived until ~400 CE
    - **Phoenician glass and purple dye**: Murex sea-snail used to produce **Tyrian purple** dye (extraordinarily expensive: ~10,000 snails for 1 gram of dye); reserved for royalty; Tyrian purple = imperial purple = reference to Tyre's most famous export; massive Murex shell middens at Sidon and Tyre excavated
    - **Hiram of Tyre** (~969–936 BCE): contemporaries with Solomon; *cedars of Lebanon* sent for Temple construction; biblical texts (1 Kings 5–9; 2 Chronicles 2–4); Phoenician craftsmen (Hiram-Abi, the bronze-caster) worked on Solomon's Temple; Tyrian-Israelite alliance evidenced archaeologically (pottery, architecture)
    - Decline: Assyrian depredations (Sennacherib, Ashurbanipal attacked Tyre and Sidon); Babylonian 13-year siege of Tyre by Nebuchadnezzar II (~585–573 BCE — inconclusive); Alexander the Great destroyed Tyre 332 BCE after 7-month siege (built 0.8 km causeway to reach the island city; turned island into peninsula that remains today); Sidon surrendered
    - **Sarcophagus of Ahiram** (~1000 BCE): found at Byblos by Pierre Montet 1923; earliest known Phoenician alphabetic inscription; curse inscription on the rim; now Beirut National Museum

  - **Israel and Judah (~1200–587 BCE)**
    - **Origins — the historicity debate**:
      - *Maximalist* position (William Foxwell Albright tradition): biblical narrative broadly historical; the Exodus, the Conquest of Canaan, David and Solomon's united monarchy largely as described
      - *Minimalist* position (Thomas L. Thompson, Niels Peter Lemche, Philip Davies — the "Copenhagen School"): the early biblical narratives are largely literary/theological compositions of Persian or Hellenistic period; David and Solomon are mythological or massively legendary
      - *Archaeological consensus (middle ground)*: Israel Finkelstein and Neil Asher Silberman (*The Bible Unearthed*, 2001): highland village culture appears ~1200 BCE from Canaanite collapse; no archaeological evidence for large Exodus or Joshua-style conquest; David/Solomon probably existed but as minor chieftains, not imperial kings as described; Jerusalem in ~1000 BCE was a small unwalled town; Omride Dynasty (9th c. BCE) leaves first substantial archaeological evidence for a real Israelite state
      - **Merneptah Stele** (~1208 BCE): only Egyptian text mentioning "Israel" by name; "Israel is laid waste and his seed is no more" (victory boast after Canaanite campaign); proves existence of a group called Israel in Canaan ~1200 BCE, but nothing about their origin
      - **Tell Dan Stele** (~835 BCE): Aramean king Hazael's victory inscription; mentions "House of David" (*bytdwd*) — first extrabiblical reference to the Davidic dynasty; confirmed David as a historical person or dynasty name
      - **Khirbet Qeiyafa** (Israel, excavations by Yossi Garfinkel 2007–2013): 10th century BCE fortified city facing Philistine territory; supports historicity of early Judahite polity during "Davidic" period; disputed interpretation
    - **The United Monarchy (~1010–930 BCE)**:
      - Saul: first king; tribal coalition; defeated by Philistines at Mount Gilboa; suicide
      - **David** (~1010–970 BCE): capital moved to Jerusalem (neutral city between northern and southern tribes); brought the Ark of the Covenant to Jerusalem; expansion; reorganization; literary patron of Psalms tradition
      - **Solomon** (~970–930 BCE): First Temple built in Jerusalem (~957 BCE per biblical chronology; archaeology: Eilat Mazar controversial City of David excavations; Temple Mount itself inaccessible to archaeology due to Al-Aqsa Mosque/Dome of the Rock above it); trade with Phoenicia, Sheba; *wisdom literature* tradition; **Megiddo, Gezer, Hazor** gate complexes attributed to Solomon in 1 Kings 9:15 — excavated by Yadin at Hazor, Macalister at Gezer, Lamon/Shipton at Megiddo; large 6-chambered gates of remarkably similar plan; the "Solomonic gates" debate (Finkelstein dates some to Omride period)
    - **The Divided Monarchy (~930–722/587 BCE)**:
      - Division after Solomon's death: northern **Kingdom of Israel** (10 tribes; capital Shechem then **Samaria**) and southern **Kingdom of Judah** (2 tribes; capital **Jerusalem**)
      - **Omride Dynasty** (Israel, ~885–842 BCE): Omri founded Samaria as new capital; **Ahab** his son (with Jezebel of Tyre) most powerful northern king; Assyrian sources call Israel "House of Omri" (*bīt Ḫumria*) for generations after the dynasty's end; geopolitical significance confirmed
      - **Elijah and Elisha prophetic tradition**: archetypal Hebrew prophets; 1 Kings 18 *contest on Mount Carmel* between Elijah and 450 prophets of Baal; fire from heaven on waterlogged altar
      - **Jehu's Coup** (~842 BCE): killed Jehoram; Jezebel thrown from window; Jehu's black obelisk tribute scene shows him (or his envoy) bowing to **Shalmaneser III of Assyria** — Israel's humiliation; Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III in British Museum; one of most important objects for synchronizing Israelite and Assyrian chronologies
      - **Amos, Hosea, Isaiah (Isaiah 1–39), Micah** — 8th-century BCE prophets; social justice, covenant violation, approaching doom; formed the great prophetic literary tradition
      - **Fall of Samaria 722/720 BCE**: Sargon II of Assyria (who also claimed the conquest — vs. his predecessor Shalmaneser V who began the siege); Annals of Sargon record deportation of 27,280 Israelites; replaced with other peoples (**Samaritans**: complex ethnic-religious community descending from this mixing; rejected by returning Judahites → centuries of animosity seen in NT "Good Samaritan" parable context); the **Ten Lost Tribes** myth (fascinated theologians, explorers, colonial-era speculators; Mormon origin narrative; British Israelism; modern DNA evidence: no substantial Israelite genetic signature in claimed descendant communities — British, etc.)
    - **Kingdom of Judah (~930–587 BCE)**:
      - Smaller but longer-lasting; continuous Davidic dynasty; Jerusalem temple as national center gave greater religious cohesion
      - **Hezekiah** (~727–698 BCE): religious reforms; removed "high places"; **Siloam Tunnel** (~701 BCE): 533-meter rock-cut water tunnel bringing water from Gihon Spring into the city of Jerusalem in anticipation of Sennacherib's siege; workers tunneling from both ends met in middle; **Siloam Inscription** found 1880 in tunnel records the meeting point ("the tunnelers...while there were still 3 cubits to pierce...heard the voice of one calling to the other") — most important pre-exilic Hebrew inscription; **Sennacherib's Prism** (Chicago Oriental Institute): Assyrian annals record siege of Jerusalem; Hezekiah "shut up like a bird in a cage"; does NOT claim capture of Jerusalem (contrast biblical: "angel of the Lord smote 185,000 Assyrians" and Sennacherib withdrew); scholarly debate over what happened; 2019 plague theory (Chloe Bhatt et al.) suggests disease caused Assyrian retreat
      - **Josiah** (~640–609 BCE): greatest reforming king; **Deuteronomic Reform**: centralized all worship in Jerusalem; abolished country shrines; **Discovery of the Law Book** in the Temple (~621 BCE, 2 Kgs 22): almost certainly an early form of Deuteronomy; scholarly consensus that Deuteronomy composed/compiled in this Josianic context (Deuteronomistic History: Joshua, Judges, Samuel, Kings edited with Deuteronomy as interpretive lens — scholars W.M.L. de Wette 1805 identified Josiah's book with Deuteronomy; Noth 1943 named the "Deuteronomistic History")
      - **Jeremiah**: prophet active 627–~580 BCE; witnessed fall of Jerusalem; deeply personal poetry; "new covenant" written on hearts (Jer 31:31–34) — most influential intertextual promise in Hebrew Bible, referenced in NT Last Supper narratives, Epistle to the Hebrews
      - **LMLK Storage Jars**: "belonging to the king" jar handles stamped with royal seal; found at dozens of sites; evidence of centralized Late 8th century Judahite administrative economy (Hezekiah's military-logistical preparation for Sennacherib's invasion)
      - **Lachish**: second most important city; **Lachish Letters** (Ostraca) ~589 BCE: broken pottery with ink inscriptions; military communications during Babylonian advance; "we can no longer see the fire-signals of Azekah" — signal fires extinguished, city fallen; extraordinary first-person witness to Jerusalem's last days (British Museum); **Sennacherib's siege reliefs at Nineveh**: large relief panels (now British Museum) showing Lachish's siege in exquisite detail — battering rams, Assyrian soldiers; deportation; an Assyrian visual record of conquest confirming archaeological evidence of massive destruction layer at Tell el-Duweir (Lachish)
      - **Fall of Jerusalem 587 BCE** (or 586 BCE — both dates in scholarly literature):
        - Nebuchadnezzar II (also Nebuchadrezzar): Babylonian king; first siege 597 BCE, deported Jehoiachin and 10,000 Judahites to Babylon; second siege 587 BCE; walls breached; **First Temple destroyed** (9th of Av, according to later tradition); Jerusalem burned; **Zedekiah** (last king) fled; captured at Jericho; sons killed before his eyes; his eyes put out; taken in chains to Babylon
        - Archaeological evidence of 587 destruction: burned destruction layers at Jerusalem City of David, Lachish, Megiddo; arrowheads (probably Babylonian), skeletal remains, collapsed walls; Eilat Mazar's excavations at the "Bullae House" found clay seal impressions (*bullae*) of officials named in Jeremiah — Gemariah son of Shaphan, Seraiah son of Neriah — preserved in the burning of the building (fired clay survives fire that destroys papyrus documents)

  - **The Babylonian Exile and Persian Period (~587–332 BCE)**
    - **The Babylonian Exile**: Judahites in Babylon; enormous religious and literary creativity responding to catastrophe; Documentary Hypothesis (*JEDP* sources): many scholars date the final edition of the Pentateuch to the exilic or post-exilic periods; **Priestly source (P)** very likely exilic; response to lost Temple, land, and kingship
    - **Psalm 137**: "By the rivers of Babylon, there we sat down and wept, as we remembered Zion... How can we sing the LORD's song in a foreign land?" — exilic lament; most vivid literary response to deportation
    - **Deutero-Isaiah (Isaiah 40–55)**: Second Isaiah; composed in Babylon; "Comfort, comfort my people" (Isa 40:1); Servant Songs (Isa 42, 49, 50, 52–53) — most theologically contested texts; Christian interpretation: prophetic of Jesus; Jewish interpretation: collective Israel; **Cyrus as YHWH's "Messiah/Anointed"** (Isa 45:1) — remarkable religious innovation acknowledging a pagan king as God's instrument
    - **Ezekiel**: visionary prophet in Babylon; **Vision of the Chariot/Merkabah** (Ezek 1): extraordinary cosmic vision; later Jewish mystical tradition (*Merkabah mysticism*) built on this; four living creatures = four gospel symbols in Christian interpretation; dry bones vision (Ezek 37): national resurrection metaphor
    - **Cyrus Cylinder** (~539 BCE, found Babylon 1879, British Museum): Achaemenid propaganda cylinder; Cyrus conquers Babylon for Marduk; allows deported peoples to return home and rebuild their temples; describes return of divine statues; **parallel to Isaiah's Cyrus oracle and Ezra's decree** allowing Jewish return; the cylinder has been called a "charter of human rights" — modern scholars more cautious (standard Mesopotamian beneficent-king genre), but text genuinely records Cyrus's policy; UN Secretary General's statement called it "first declaration of human rights" — contested by scholars
    - **Return from Exile and restoration**: Zerubbabel (governor), Jeshua (high priest); rebuilding of the Second Temple ~520–516 BCE; **Haggai and Zechariah** prophets of this period; Ezra (scribe; brought the Torah) and Nehemiah (governor ~445 BCE): post-exilic reconstitution of Jewish community; **canonization of the Torah** as legal and religious foundation; circumcision, Sabbath observance, and endogamy (no intermarriage) as identity markers especially emphasized
    - **Second Temple Period**: 516 BCE–70 CE; spans Persian, Hellenistic, Hasmonean, and Roman periods; crucial for formation of both Rabbinic Judaism and Christianity

  - **Aram and the Aramaean Kingdoms (~1200–700 BCE)**
    - Aramaeans: Northwest Semitic pastoralists who settled Syria-Mesopotamia ~1200 BCE; never a unified empire but powerful city-states
    - **Damascus (Aram-Damascus)**: most powerful Aramaean kingdom; under Hazael (~842–800 BCE) raided Israel and Judah; under Bar-Hadad I and II
    - **Tell Halaf (Guzana)**: Aramaean center in NE Syria; spectacular Neo-Hittite/Aramaean mixed art (Max von Oppenheim excavations 1899, 1911–1913; Berlin Vorderasiatisches Museum; many sculptures bombed in WWII)
    - **Aramaean language and script as universal medium**: Aramaic became the *lingua franca* of the entire Near East by 7th–6th c. BCE onward; Official Aramaic of the Persian empire; then dominant in Judea and the entire Levant (Jesus spoke Aramaic; the *Abba* prayer in Aramaic in NT); survived in liturgy (Jewish Kaddish, Syriac Christian liturgy); Neo-Aramaic dialects still spoken today (Turoyo, Assyrian Neo-Aramaic, Ma'lula dialect in Syria)

  - **The Philistines (~1175–604 BCE)**
    - Sea Peoples who settled in five cities (*Pentapolis*) on southern Canaanite coast: **Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Ekron (Tel Miqne), Gath (Tell es-Safi)**
    - Indo-European-influenced material culture: Aegean pottery (Philistine bichrome ware), pig consumption (unlike Israelites), beer-straining vessels; iron-working tradition (contested — not exclusive)
    - **Tel Miqne-Ekron Inscription** (1996, Seymour Gitin and team): "Ekron" city name confirmed for first time; names five Philistine rulers agreeing with Assyrian records; confirms biblical Pentapolis
    - **Goliath's name**: 2005 find at Tell es-Safi (Gath): pottery sherd ~950 BCE inscribed with two names (*'lwt / wlt*) cognate with "Goliath" in Philistine/IE linguistic context — doesn't prove the biblical story, but shows such names existed at Gath in right period
    - Philistines destroyed by Nebuchadnezzar II (~604 BCE at Ashkelon and Ekron); absorbed into Babylonian empire; identity eventually lost
    - **Ancient DNA of Philistines (2019, Harney et al., *Science Advances*)**: ancient DNA from 10 Ashkelon Philistine individuals; individuals from ~1200 BCE arrival layer had significant European ancestry (Aegean/Eastern European), confirming Sea People migration from west; by Iron Age II (~900 BCE), this ancestry had been largely diluted by local Canaanite gene flow — genetic assimilation within ~200 years

- **The Achaemenid Persian Empire (~550–330 BCE)**
  - **Overview**
    - First true world empire: extended from Aegean coast and Egypt in the west to northwestern India and Central Asia in the east; at peak ~5.5 million km²; ~50 million people (~44% of world population estimate for ~480 BCE — Bryan et al. estimate, widely cited; exact figure contested)
    - Founded by **Cyrus the Great**; run by a sophisticated multi-ethnic administrative system (satrapies); collapsed under Alexander the Great 334–330 BCE
    - Primary sources: Persepolis Fortification Archive (Elamite administrative tablets, ~30,000); royal inscriptions in Old Persian + Elamite + Babylonian (Behistun, Persepolis, Naqsh-e Rostam, Susa); Babylonian chronicles; Hebrew Bible (Ezra, Nehemiah, Esther, Daniel partly); Greek sources (Herodotus, Thucydides, Xenophon, Ctesias, Diodorus); Egyptian demotic papyri
    - Key scholarly works: Pierre Briant *From Cyrus to Alexander* (2002; 1,196 pages — the definitive treatment); Amélie Kuhrt *The Persian Empire: A Corpus of Sources* (2007); Maria Brosius *The Persian Empire from Cyrus II to Artaxerxes I*; David Stronach Pasargadae excavations; papers from the Achaemenid History Workshop (Leiden)
  - **Cyrus the Great (~559–530 BCE)**
    - Born: Anshan (Fars region, SW Iran); Achaemenid clan; subject to Median empire; **revolted against Median king Astyages ~550 BCE**: Astyages' own army defected to Cyrus at Pasargadae; Ecbatana (Median capital, modern Hamadan) fell; Median empire absorbed; Cyrus adopted dual title King of the Medes and Persians
    - **Conquest of Lydia (~547 BCE)**: Croesus of Lydia (proverbially wealthy; "rich as Croesus"; consulted Delphi oracle before attacking Persia; oracle said "a great empire will fall" — famously ambiguous); Croesus invaded; Battle of Pteria (inconclusive); Croesus retreated to Sardis for winter; Cyrus pursued swiftly; Battle of Thymbra; Sardis captured; Lydia annexed; Greek Ionian cities submitted or were taken; control of all Asia Minor and its silver mines
    - **Conquest of Babylon (~539 BCE)**: Cyrus diverted the Euphrates (damming it upstream); troops marched into Babylon along the riverbed during a festival; Nabonidus's forces collapsed; Cyrus entered peacefully; proclaimed liberator; **restoration policy**: returned exiled gods' statues to their cities; reversed Nabonidus's centralizing of all worship to Babylon; **Cyrus Cylinder** as propaganda of benevolent overlordship; Jewish community in Babylon released
    - Death ~530 BCE campaigning against the Massagetae nomads (NE Iran/Central Asia); legendary account: queen Tomyris defeated and killed him; dunked his severed head in blood ("you thirsted for blood, now drink it"); Herodotus account; archaeological evidence uncertain
    - **Pasargadae**: Cyrus's capital; constructed from ~547 BCE; gardens (*pairidaeza* = Persian "paradise" → Greek *paradeisos* → English "paradise"); columned palaces; **Tomb of Cyrus** — simple gabled stone chamber on 6 stepped platforms still standing; when Alexander visited, reportedly found it untouched; inscription reportedly said "O man, whoever you are and wherever you come from... I am Cyrus who won the Persians their empire. Do not begrudge me this little earth that covers my body"; Alexander ordered it restored
  - **Cambyses II (~530–522 BCE)** — *see Egyptian section above*
  - **Darius I the Great (~522–486 BCE)**
    - Seized throne after killing pretender Gaumata; his legitimacy always somewhat compromised; consolidated through **Behistun Inscription**
    - **Behistun Inscription** (515 BCE approx., carved on sheer cliff 100m above ground on Royal Road between Babylon and Ecbatana): trilingual (Old Persian, Elamite, Babylonian); Darius narrates suppression of 19 rebellions in first year of reign; illustrated with Darius's foot on prostrate Gaumata, foreign kings in chains; **decipherment**: Henry Rawlinson (British) copied the text by rope in 1838–1847; cracked Old Persian; Babylonian section key to cuneiform decipherment; one of the great feats of epigraphic fieldwork
    - **Administrative genius**: divided empire into **20 satrapies**; each ruled by a satrap (governor, often a Persian nobleman or royal family member); separate military commanders and royal secretaries (spies); **King's Eyes and Ears** = royal inspectors reporting directly to king; effective checks and balances against satrapal revolt
    - **Royal Road**: 2,575 km from Sardis (Lydia) to Susa; way stations (angareia system) every ~25 km; royal courier could traverse entire road in ~7 days; Herodotus: "Nothing mortal travels so fast as these Persian messengers"; inspired by Assyrian system; **direct precursor to modern postal systems**
    - **Persepolis**: Darius began construction ~518 BCE; major building by Xerxes I and Artaxerxes I continued for 150 years; ceremonial capital (not administrative); site of the New Year (*Nowruz*) reception of tribute delegations; **Apadana reliefs**: processions of 23 delegations bringing tribute gifts from across the empire (Lydians, Greeks, Babylonians, Ethiopians, Indians); most complete image of Persian empire's multiethnic character; **Throne Hall (Hall of 100 Columns)**: 70×70m; royal audience hall; **Gate of All Nations**: inscriptions of Xerxes; colossal lamassu (bull-man gate guardians); Persepolis burned by Alexander 330 BCE (deliberately, possibly in retaliation for Xerxes' burning of the Acropolis 480 BCE, or in a drunken incident — Diodorus and Plutarch give different versions)
    - **Persepolis Fortification Archive (PFA)**: ~30,000 Elamite administrative tablets found 1933–1934; documenting rations paid to workers, administrators, and royal women at Persepolis; extraordinary social history: rations for pregnant women, for wine vs. beer production, for foreign workers; **fortification workers included women who received maternity pay** — one of the earliest documented examples; Richard Hallock's publication (1969); Matthew Stolper's ongoing Persepolis Fortification Archive Project at Oriental Institute Chicago
    - **Greek Wars**: Darius invaded Greece to punish Athens for supporting Ionian Revolt; **Battle of Marathon 490 BCE**: 9,000 Athenians + 1,000 Plataeans defeated ~25,000 Persians; General Miltiades' envelopment tactic; legend of Pheidippides running to Athens with news; Persians retreated; enormous psychological impact on Greeks (concept of "barbarian" Persian threat crystallized)
  - **Xerxes I (~486–465 BCE)**
    - Suppressed revolts in Egypt and Babylon; then invaded Greece
    - **Second Persian Invasion of Greece 480–479 BCE**:
      - **Battle of Thermopylae** (480 BCE): Leonidas I and 300 Spartans (+ ~7,000 other Greeks) held mountain pass for two days; Ephialtes betrayed the mountain path; Greeks outflanked; Spartans chose to remain; all died; *"Go, tell the Spartans, stranger passing by: that here, obedient to their laws, we lie"* (Simonides' epitaph); enormous cultural legacy in Western tradition; Victor Davis Hanson; Frank Miller *300*
      - **Battle of Salamis** (480 BCE): Themistocles of Athens tricked Xerxes into fighting in narrow straits where Persian numbers were negated; Greek triremes (faster, more maneuverable in confined water) devastated Persian fleet; Xerxes watched from throne on hill; one of the most decisive naval battles in history; Aeschylus's *The Persians* (472 BCE): only surviving Greek tragedy about contemporary history; written from Persian perspective; rare reversed viewpoint
      - **Battle of Plataea** (479 BCE): Greek coalition under Pausanias defeated Persian land force under Mardonius; complete expulsion of Persians from Greece
    - **Sacking of Athens (480 BCE)**: Persians burned the Acropolis; destroyed the original Parthenon foundations and earlier temples; Greeks stored this rubble ("Persian debris") beneath the new Acropolis — this *Persian debris fill* is an archaeological deposit dated by pottery to just before 480 BCE; essential chronological anchor for Greek archaeology
    - Xerxes' assassination 465 BCE by courtier Artabanus; succession crisis
  - **Artaxerxes I (~465–424 BCE)**
    - Relatively stable reign; lost Egypt temporarily (Inaros Revolt 460–454 BCE — see Egyptian section); Athenian-Persian **Peace of Callias** (~449 BCE — contested: some scholars doubt the treaty's historicity; Michael Brosius and others; the debate remains open); patronized imperial administration; Nehemiah sent to Jerusalem as governor ~445 BCE per the Bible
    - The famous Athenian politician **Themistocles** died at Magnesia-on-the-Maeander as a Persian satrap (~459 BCE) — remarkable career arc from Salamis hero to Persian official
  - **Later Achaemenids (~424–330 BCE)**
    - Multi-decade period of relative stability punctuated by dynastic violence; satrapal autonomy increasing; Greek mercenaries used in Persian armies; Persian gold (*darics*) corrupting Greek politics
    - **Artaxerxes II** (~404–358 BCE): longest-reigning Achaemenid; brother **Cyrus the Younger** rebelled; hired 10,000 Greek mercenaries; **Battle of Cunaxa 401 BCE**: Cyrus killed; Greek mercenaries stranded deep in Mesopotamia; **Xenophon** (*Anabasis* — "March Up Country"): describes epic 6-month march of the "Ten Thousand" Greeks from Babylon through mountain passes to Black Sea coast; classic adventure narrative; Xenophon's encounter with Persians revealed empire's military vulnerability to Greek observers; information later used by Alexander
    - **Artaxerxes III** (~358–338 BCE): reconquered Egypt 343 BCE; brutal; assassinated by eunuch Bagoas
    - **Darius III** (~336–330 BCE): placed on throne by Bagoas; had Bagoas poisoned; faced Alexander; three major battles (Granicus 334, Issus 333, Gaugamela 331 BCE); fled each time; murdered by his own Bactrian satrap Bessus 330 BCE; Alexander reportedly wept at his corpse; gave him royal burial at Persepolis
  - **Zoroastrianism: The Religion of the Achaemenids**
    - **Zarathustra (Zoroaster)**: founder; dates contested: traditional Zoroastrian reckoning ~1200–1000 BCE; some scholars ~1750–1500 BCE (based on Avestan language archaism); others place him ~600 BCE; his historicity not doubted but details minimal
    - **Gathas**: 17 hymns in Gathic Avestan; oldest portion of the Avesta; composed by Zarathustra himself; among the oldest surviving Indo-European religious texts; difficult to translate; describe a cosmic moral dualism
    - **Core theology**:
      - **Ahura Mazda** ("Wise Lord"): supreme deity, creator of all good things; associated with fire, light, truth (*asha*)
      - **Angra Mainyu / Ahriman**: destructive spirit; principle of evil, darkness, chaos (*druj* = lie/deceit)
      - The cosmos as battleground between Asha (truth, order) and Druj (lie, chaos); humans have free will to choose
      - **Amesha Spentas**: six divine emanations of Ahura Mazda (Good Mind, Truth, Dominion, Devotion, Wholeness, Immortality)
      - **Yazatas**: angels/divine beings; sacred fire, water, cattle, plants honored
      - **Eschatology**: final purification of the world; resurrection of the dead; last judgment; molten metal flood purifying the wicked; cosmic renovation (*frashokereti*)
      - **Apocalypticism and influence**: Zoroastrian eschatology (final judgment, resurrection, heaven/hell, cosmic savior/Saoshyant) profoundly influenced Jewish apocalypticism during Persian period → early Christianity → Islam; the "Devil" concept (adversary figure) likely entered Judaism from Zoroastrianism during Achaemenid period
    - **Fire temples**: sacred fires maintained continuously; three grades (Atash-Behram, Atash-Adaran, Atash-Dadgah); Persian royal fire (royal ideology legitimated by fire connection); Naqsh-e Rustam: royal tombs with fire altar reliefs
    - **Later Avesta**: Yasna, Yashts, Vendidad (priestly code handling ritual purity); composed after Gathas over many centuries; standardized in Sasanid period
    - Zoroastrianism today: **Parsis (India, ~60,000)** — descendants of Persian Zoroastrians who fled Islamic conquest ~900 CE; settled Gujarat; Mumbai Parsi community world-famous for business/intellectual achievement (Tata family, Dadabhai Naoroji, Homi Bhabha); **Iranian Zoroastrians (~15,000–20,000)** remaining in Iran (Yazd, Kerman); global diaspora; spring new year festival *Nowruz* (New Year at spring equinox) persists across Iran, Central Asia, Afghanistan, even non-Zoroastrians observe; declared UNESCO intangible cultural heritage 2009
    - **Magi**: priestly caste of Median Zoroastrian priests; the "Magi from the East" in Matthew 2 (the Three Wise Men) = Zoroastrian priests; asterism observation, astrological expertise; gave English word "magic"
  - **Persian Cultural Achievements**
    - **Imperial Aramaic**: standardized for empire-wide administration; earliest example of a language deliberately selected as administrative *lingua franca*; used from Egypt to India; all official correspondence, legal documents, imperial orders in Aramaic
    - **Gardens and landscape design**: *pairidaeza* (paradise garden); walled enclosures with water channels, trees, flowers, game animals; model for Islamic garden design; Cyrus's Pasargadae garden excavated; Persian palace gardens at Susa and Ecbatana described by Xenophon; the word "paradise" (→ Persian, → Greek *paradeisos*, → Latin *paradisus* → English) is our lasting linguistic inheritance
    - **Achaemenid art style**: synthesis of Egyptian, Babylonian, Assyrian, Lydian, Greek, and Iranian elements; distinctive court style seen at Persepolis; formal, hierarchical, non-narrative in contrast to Assyrian narrative reliefs; human figures stylized; animals (bull, lion, griffins) powerful; gold and silver vessels, jewelry (Oxus Treasure: largest surviving collection of Achaemenid metalwork, found 1877 near Amu Darya river, mostly in British Museum)
    - **The Persian Postal System** (*angarion/angareia*): network of mounted couriers with horses kept at each relay station; Herodotus describes them as faster than anything mortal; directly inspired Roman *cursus publicus* and later postal networks
    - **Religious tolerance**: Achaemenid policy of allowing conquered peoples to maintain their own religions and customs (within limits); contrast with Assyrian deportation-and-suppression policy; Cyrus to Babylonians/Jews; Darius to Egyptians; the policy was politically motivated but functionally tolerant by ancient standards; some scholars argue this shaped development of Persian imperial ideology that later influenced Roman *pax deorum* approach

- **The Fall of the Achaemenid Empire: Alexander the Great (334–323 BCE)**
  - **Background**: Philip II of Macedon (assassinated 336 BCE) had already organized the League of Corinth and planned Persian invasion; Alexander III (*megas* = "the Great") executed the plan on an unimaginable scale
  - **Granicus 334 BCE**: Alexander crossed Hellespont with ~43,000 men; defeated Persian satrapal army at Granicus River; visited Troy (identified with Achilles); **Gordian Knot** at Gordion: tradition that whoever untied it would rule Asia; Alexander cut it with his sword (or pulled out the pin, per another version); the oracle at Gordion confirmed his destiny
  - **Issus 333 BCE**: Darius III personally commanded; massive Persian army; Alexander's oblique cavalry charge to right; Persian center held but Darius fled; Darius's mother, wife, and children captured; Alexander treated them as royalty; Darius's wife Stateira died in captivity ~332 BCE; Alexander reportedly wept
  - **Conquest of Levant 333–332 BCE**: Tyre besieged 7 months (causeway construction as above); Gaza; Egypt (welcomed, oracle of Siwa, founded Alexandria)
  - **Gaugamela 331 BCE**: Darius chose flat ground for chariots; Alexander's audacious diagonal attack; Darius fled; Alexander pursued to Persepolis; burned the palace
  - **Eastern conquests 330–323 BCE**: Bactria (Balkh, Afghanistan); Sogdiana; Hindu Kush crossings (rival of Hannibal's Alps crossing for audacity); marriage to **Roxana** (Bactrian princess); India (*see Ancient India section*); Hydaspes 326 BCE — Indian king Porus's elephants; mutiny at Hyphasis; Alexander wept that there were no more worlds to conquer (apocryphal); returned via Makran desert (catastrophic losses); death Babylon 10/11 June 323 BCE, age 32; cause disputed: typhoid with Guillain-Barré syndrome (Katherine Hall, 2019); poisoning; alcoholism-related; pancreatitis; encephalitis
  - **Legacy**: the Hellenistic world; fusion of Greek and oriental cultures (*synkretismos*); spread of Koine Greek as *lingua franca*; Greek cities founded across the East (Alexandrias, Antiochias, Seleucias); Greek philosophy, science, art reaching India and Central Asia; Indian thought reaching the West; the world Alexander created was the world in which Christianity was born

- **Frontier & Contemporary Research (2020–2026)**
  - **Hittite studies**: Boğazköy continued excavation (Andreas Schachner, DAI); new tablet fragments found almost every season; 2021 unpublished tablet fragments from lower city area; online Hittite cuneiform tablet corpus (*eTCL* project, CDLI); computational analysis of tablet fractures for fragment matching; machine learning Hittite sign recognition (University of Würzburg project)
  - **Luwian Decipherment advances**: Craig Melchert, Fred Wörterbuch (Luwian dictionary); 2015–2023 new hieroglyphic Luwian inscriptions from Türkiye recontextualizing late Bronze Age politics; James Mellaart's disputed "maps" (2017 posthumous publication controversy — possible forgery controversy; Eberhard Zangger *Luwian Civilization* 2017)
  - **Ugarit**: ongoing publication of unpublished Ugaritic tablets by CNRS team; digital Ugaritic corpus online; Ugaritic text database; Gregorio del Olmo Lete's *Canaanite Religion* (expanded 3rd edition); comparative Ugaritic-Hebrew Bible studies (John Day, David Pardee)
  - **Hebrew Bible Archaeology**: Khirbet Qeiyafa ongoing analysis; **Tel Abel Beth Maacah excavations** (Nava Panitz-Cohen, 2013–2022): finds Iron Age II material relevant to northern Kingdom of Israel-Aram border; **Ophel Excavations** (Eilat Mazar legacy); **Gath/Tell es-Safi ongoing** (Aren Maeir, Bar-Ilan University) — massive 9th c. BCE destruction layer confirming Hazael's attack on Gath; extensive Philistine material; 2022–2023: 3,800-year-old canaanite altar found
  - **Tel Megiddo**: renewed excavations by Israeli Antiquities Authority and Technion; 2022–2023: exposed new palace-area finds
  - **Dead Sea Scrolls New Fragments**: 2021 discovery of new tiny fragments in Cave 12, Judean Desert — first new scrolls found since 1956; fragments contain text from **Zechariah and Nahum**; Joint Israel-Jordan expedition using multispectral imaging found scroll fragments; complete survey of Judean Desert caves underway 2021–2025
  - **Philistine aDNA expanded study**: follow-up to 2019 Ashkelon study; 2022–2023 additional aDNA from Gath and Ashdod to trace Philistine genetic trajectory more completely
  - **Persepolis Fortification Archive**: complete digital publication ongoing (Oriental Institute, Chicago); OCHRE database; all 30,000 tablets being published in phases; Matthew Stolper (emeritus), Mark Garrison (seals), Annalisa Azzoni; last publication batches expected 2025–2030
  - **Achaemenid period epigraphy**: new Old Persian inscriptions found at Persepolis during Iranian excavation seasons (pre-2023); many not yet published due to political/access restrictions; remote-sensing survey of Persepolis surroundings (Irano-Italian missions before 2019)
  - **Zoroastrian studies**: Yuhan Vevaina (*Avestan*); Michael Stausberg (*Zoroastrianism: An Introduction*, 2011); growing digitization of Avestan manuscripts; comparative genetic study of global Parsi community tracing migration from Iran to Gujarat ~900 CE (2020 study confirming founder population from Greater Khorasan)
  - **Coastal Levant underwater archaeology**: Caesarea Maritima underwater survey (Robert Hohlfelder); continued search for Phoenician harbor installations submerged by tectonic activity; Sidon harbor studies
  - **Persian period Jerusalem**: archaeological evidence for Achaemenid-period occupation of Jerusalem (Oded Lipschits; Lily Singer-Avitz) — thinner than expected; much smaller settlement than under the monarchy; gradual rebuilding under Nehemiah archaeologically corroborated

#### Ancient History — Ancient Greece

- **Basics & Scope**
  - Ancient Greece: ~3000 BCE (Minoan Crete) to 31 BCE (Battle of Actium, Roman absorption of last Hellenistic kingdom)
  - Not a unified nation-state but a shared cultural world (*koinē*) of city-states (*poleis*), colonies, and kingdoms united by language, religion, athletic competitions, and literary tradition
  - Fundamental to Western civilization: democracy, philosophy, drama, Olympic games, systematic history, rational medicine, formal logic, Euclidean geometry, narrative epic poetry — all emerge from or are shaped by ancient Greece
  - Primary sources: Homer (*Iliad*, *Odyssey*); lyric poets (Sappho, Pindar, Archilochus); tragedians (Aeschylus, Sophocles, Euripides); comedians (Aristophanes, Menander); historians (Herodotus, Thucydides, Xenophon, Polybius, Diodorus); philosophers (Plato, Aristotle and their schools); orators (Demosthenes, Isocrates); inscriptions (IG = *Inscriptiones Graecae*, SEG = *Supplementum Epigraphicum Graecum*); papyri; visual art, architecture, numismatics
  - Key excavation sites: Knossos (Crete), Mycenae, Tiryns, Pylos (Mycenaean palaces); Athens (Acropolis, Agora), Sparta, Corinth, Delphi, Olympia, Delos, Ephesus, Miletus, Pergamon
  - Major reference works: Cambridge Ancient History; Simon Hornblower & Antony Spawforth (eds.) *Oxford Classical Dictionary* (4th ed. 2012); Robin Osborne *Greece in the Making 1200–479 BC*; Josiah Ober *Mass and Elite in Democratic Athens*; Victor Davis Hanson *The Western Way of War*; M.I. Finley *The Ancient Economy*
  - Key journals: *Hesperia* (American School of Classical Studies), *Annual of the British School at Athens* (BSA), *Zeitschrift für Papyrologie und Epigraphik* (ZPE), *American Journal of Archaeology* (AJA), *Classical Quarterly*, *Classical Philology*

- **Bronze Age Greece (~3000–1100 BCE)**
  - **Minoan Civilization (~3000–1450 BCE)**
    - Centered on Crete; named by Arthur Evans for the mythological King Minos
    - **Discovery**: Arthur Evans purchased Knossos site 1899; excavated from 1900; controversial reconstructions using reinforced concrete (criticized as over-imaginative but enabling tourist access); Evans's *The Palace of Minos* (4 vols., 1921–1936)
    - **Palace system**: Knossos (largest; ~1,300 rooms), Phaistos, Malia, Zakros; multi-story palace complexes with storerooms (giant *pithoi* storage jars), light wells, drainage systems, frescoes; no massive fortification walls — interpreted as peaceful or as island security providing natural defense
    - **Knossos fresco program**: brightly colored; bull-leaping (*taurokathapsia*) — acrobats vaulting over charging bulls; lilies, dolphins, griffins, processions of gift-bearers; "Minoan Ladies" (fashionable women with bared breasts, elaborate hairstyles); extraordinary aesthetic sophistication
    - **Cretan Hieroglyphic and Linear A**: undeciphered scripts; Cretan Hieroglyphic (~2100–1700 BCE) used on seals and tablets; **Linear A** (~1800–1450 BCE) on administrative clay tablets; the underlying language (Minoan) is not Indo-European and remains unknown; ~1,500 Linear A tablets; decipherment attempts ongoing but no consensus; only Linear A's accounting conventions (not the language) can be inferred from Linear B parallels
    - **Religion**: "Snake Goddess" figurines (bare-breasted female figure with snakes); bull as sacred animal; peak sanctuaries (mountaintop shrines); cave sanctuaries (Psychro Cave, Diktaean Cave — mythological birthplace of Zeus); no temples in the later Greek sense
    - **Trade network**: Crete at hub of eastern Mediterranean Bronze Age trade; Egyptian faience, Aegean obsidian, Anatolian metals, Levantine goods; Minoan pottery found from Spain to the Levant
    - **Thera (Santorini) eruption (~1628–1600 BCE or ~1500 BCE — dates debated)**:
      - Enormous volcanic eruption; VEI ~7; destroyed the Minoan settlement at Akrotiri on Thera; the eruption and attendant tsunamis struck northern Crete
      - **Akrotiri** (excavated Spyridon Marinatos 1967–1974, then Christos Doumas): perfectly preserved Bronze Age town under volcanic ash; 3–4 story buildings; extraordinary fresco program (Boxing Children, Spring Fresco, Ship Fresco, Blue Monkeys); no bodies or valuables found — inhabitants apparently evacuated; now under protective roof; UNESCO tentative list
      - Radiocarbon dates: ~1628–1600 BCE (ice core, tree ring, radiocarbon evidence); conventional archaeological date: ~1500 BCE; debate ongoing with profound chronological implications for entire eastern Mediterranean Bronze Age (Sturt Manning vs. traditional Egyptological chronology)
      - Possible Atlantis origin? Plato's Atlantis (~360 BCE): island civilization swallowed by sea; some scholars (Galanopoulos, Marinatos) linked to Thera; mainstream scholars skeptical — Plato's account is philosophical allegory or fiction
    - Minoan decline: palaces destroyed ~1450 BCE; Mycenaean takeover of Crete; Linear B (Greek) now appears in Crete
  - **Mycenaean Civilization (~1700–1100 BCE)**
    - Indo-European (Greek-speaking) palace culture in mainland Greece; shaft graves, tholos tombs, fortified citadels
    - **Discovery**: Heinrich Schliemann excavated Mycenae 1876; Lion Gate; Grave Circle A; "Mask of Agamemnon" (gold funeral mask); telegraphed King of Greece: "I have gazed upon the face of Agamemnon" — romanticism at its finest; mask now known to be ~3 centuries older than any historical Agamemnon
    - **Key sites**:
      - **Mycenae**: citadel with Lion Gate (~1250 BCE — monumental limestone relief sculpture; oldest known monumental sculpture in Europe); Treasury of Atreus (tholos tomb; ~1250 BCE; corbelled dome 14.5m diameter; stood for 3,500 years); Grave Circle A (shaft graves with gold masks, inlaid daggers, amber)
      - **Tiryns**: cyclopean walls (so massive later Greeks thought Cyclopes built them); corbelled galleries within walls; palace megaron
      - **Pylos** ("Palace of Nestor"): best-preserved Mycenaean palace; excavated Carl Blegen 1939; extraordinary fresco program; extensive archive of Linear B tablets — found in the destruction level of ~1180 BCE (baked by fire that destroyed the palace); tablets record palace economic management; oil, land, chariots, rowers, textiles
      - **Midea, Gla (Boeotia), Dimini, Orchomenos** — further Mycenaean centers
    - **Linear B decipherment** (1952): **Michael Ventris** (British architect, self-trained); working from John Chadwick's philological collaboration; cracked Linear B by assuming it was an archaic form of Greek; confirmed by Pylos tablet Tn 316 listing sacrifice-offerings to Poseidon; *Documents in Mycenaean Greek* (Ventris & Chadwick, 1956); Ventris died in car accident 1956 at 34
    - Linear B tablets document: palace economy (strict bureaucratic accounting); Mycenaean Greek vocabulary; names of gods (Zeus, Hera, Poseidon, Dionysus, Athena — confirming continuity of Greek religion); trade goods; place names; workers' rations; land-holding
    - **Mycenaean religion**: essentially same major gods as later Greece; animal sacrifice; feasting; no surviving mythological texts from Mycenaean period (mythology transmitted orally)
    - **The Trojan War and Troy**: Schliemann excavated Hisarlık (NW Turkey) 1871–1890; identified it with Homer's Troy; **Frank Calvert** had identified the site first; Schliemann controversially removed "Priam's Treasure" (actually 1,000 years earlier than any Trojan War); Manfred Korfmann (University of Tübingen) renewed excavations 1988–2005: revealed Troy VI/VIIa (~1300–1180 BCE) was a major city; large lower town; probably had population of ~10,000; destruction layer consistent with Bronze Age Collapse period (~1180 BCE); whether Homer's war has any historical kernel remains debated but a real late Bronze Age conflict at the site is plausible; *Troiforschung* controversy: Korfmann vs. Frank Kolb debate in 2001–2002 over size claims
    - **Mycenaean collapse (~1180–1100 BCE)**: palaces burned; Linear B disappears; population collapse; long-distance trade ends — part of the broader Bronze Age Collapse (see Near East section); Greek mainland enters the *Dark Ages*

- **Greek Dark Ages (~1100–800 BCE)**
  - Population collapse: estimates of 75–90% population reduction in some regions over the collapse period
  - Loss of writing (Linear B disappears; Greeks re-adopt writing only ~800 BCE with Phoenician alphabet)
  - Loss of monumental architecture, long-distance trade, palace economy
  - **Continuities**: Greek language survives; polytheistic religion continuous; heroic oral tradition (Homer transmits this); iron metallurgy introduced (displacing Bronze Age bronze)
  - **Athens** shows least demographic disruption; **Euboea** recovers early; contacts with Levant maintained via Phoenician merchants; **Lefkandi (Euboea)**: extraordinary 10th c. BCE *Heroön* — large apsidal building (~45×10 m); elite burials including cremated warrior and his horse interred beneath it; extensive Near Eastern imports (Egyptian faience, Phoenician bronze bowl); challenge to the "Dark Ages" narrative — elite activity continuing
  - **Protogenometric and Geometric pottery** (~1050–700 BCE): elegant abstract geometric designs; gradual increase in complexity; Attic Geometric (750–700 BCE) shows narrative scenes (funerary, battles) re-emerging; Dipylon kraters (large funeral vessels); foundational Athenian artistic identity
  - **Homer** (~750–700 BCE): *Iliad* and *Odyssey* — composed in this transition period, drawing on centuries of oral tradition about the Bronze Age world; **Oral-Formulaic Theory** (Milman Parry and Albert Lord, 1928–1960): Homer composed in live performance using formulaic phrases (*"wine-dark sea"*, *"rosy-fingered Dawn"*); the "Homeric question" (was there a single Homer? are both epics by the same poet?); modern consensus: probably a single gifted poet drawing on a rich oral tradition; enormous influence on all later Western literature

- **Archaic Period (~800–479 BCE)**
  - **Overview**
    - Population recovery; explosion of polis formation; colonization across Mediterranean and Black Sea; development of coinage (~600 BCE); emergence of philosophy and science; invention of democracy; Persian Wars
    - Technological/social innovations: hoplite warfare, symposium culture, lyric poetry, coinage, tyranny as political form, written law codes
  - **The Polis (City-State)**
    - Core institution of Greek civilization; typical polis: urban center with acropolis (fortified hilltop sanctuary) + lower town (*asty*) + surrounding agricultural territory (*chora*)
    - Size range: Athens (~25,000 km² territory; ~250,000–300,000 total population at peak) to tiny islands with populations of a few hundred
    - ~1,000 poleis identified in the ancient Greek world at various times; **Copenhagen Polis Centre** (Mogens Herman Hansen) catalogued ~1,035 credibly attested poleis (*An Inventory of Archaic and Classical Poleis*, 2004)
    - Political forms: monarchy (king/basileus), aristocracy (rule by noble families), oligarchy (rule by wealthy few), tyranny (sole ruler, seizing power outside normal channels — not inherently negative in Greek usage), democracy (rule by the *demos*)
    - **Panhellenic identities**: Panhellenic sanctuaries (Olympia, Delphi, Corinth, Nemea); Greek identity articulated against *barbaros* (those who say "bar-bar" — cannot speak Greek); shared calendar, myths, games
  - **Colonization (~750–550 BCE)**
    - Greeks established hundreds of colonies around the Mediterranean and Black Sea (*apoikiai* = "homes away from home")
    - Causes: land shortage, famine, stasis (civil strife), trade interests, adventure; Oracle at Delphi frequently consulted for guidance
    - Regions colonized: Sicily and South Italy ("Magna Graecia" — greater Greece): Syracuse (Corinthian, ~733 BCE; became largest Greek city), Acragas (Agrigento), Selinous, Metapontion, Sybaris, Croton; North Africa: Cyrene; Black Sea coast: Byzantion (Megarian colony, ~660 BCE; later Constantinople), Olbia, Sinope, Trapezus; Northwest: Massalia (Marseille, ~600 BCE); Spain: Emporion
    - **Impact on colonized peoples**: Greek cultural transmission; adoption of coinage and literacy by neighboring peoples; displacement of indigenous peoples; complex interactions
  - **Written Law Codes**
    - **Draco** (~621 BCE, Athens): first written Athenian law code; legendarily harsh (almost everything punishable by death; origin of "draconian"); at least established that law was public and written, not at aristocrats' discretion
    - **Zaleucus of Locri** (~650 BCE): possibly earliest Greek law-giver; Western Locri (S. Italy); laws protecting the poor
    - **Charondas of Catana**: Sicilian lawgiver; law code applied to Catana and other Chalcidian colonies
    - Lawgiving connects to the **Seven Sages** tradition (Thales, Solon, Bias, Pittacus, Cleobulus, Chilon, Myson/Periander): wise men associated with practical political wisdom; Solon and Pittacus both actual legislators
  - **Tyranny in the Archaic Period**
    - Wave of tyrants across Archaic Greece; typically wealthy individuals who seized power with popular support against entrenched aristocracies; often economically beneficial
    - **Pisistratus** of Athens (~561–527 BCE, with interruptions): patronized arts and festivals; Panathenaic festival expanded; Homer performed at Panathenaia (possibly first fixed written text of Homer); Acropolis building program; economic prosperity
    - **Periander** of Corinth (~625–585 BCE): Corinth at height of prosperity; engineered *diolkos* (ship-portage across Corinthian isthmus); major pottery exports (Corinthian ware dominant before Attic)
    - **Polycrates of Samos** (~538–522 BCE): island tyrant; naval power; famous friendship with Egyptian pharaoh Amasis; executed by Persians
    - **Gelon and Hiero** of Syracuse: powerful Sicilian tyrants; Hiero's court patronized Pindar, Simonides, Bacchylides, Aeschylus
  - **Sparta (Lacedaemon)**
    - Located in Laconia (southern Peloponnese); controlled fertile Eurotas valley
    - **Helots**: enslaved population (mostly conquered Messenians); farmed Spartan land; extremely vulnerable; Spartans lived in constant military readiness partly due to helot threat; periodic ritual declaration of war on helots (*krypteia* institution — young Spartan men killed helots as training)
    - **The Lycurgan System** (attributed to legendary lawgiver Lycurgus): communal messes (*syssitia*); *agoge* (state education/military training from age 7); communal barracks until age 30; emphasis on collective over individual; *homoioi* ("equals") — idealized equality among Spartan citizen-warriors; girls trained athletically (shocking to other Greeks)
    - Spartan women: more freedom than Athenian women; managed estates while men were at war; Plutarch records Spartan mother handing son his shield: "With this, or on this" (return victorious or dead)
    - **Spartan army**: phalanx warfare at its finest; the "Spartan mirage" (*lakonismos*) — other Greeks projected utopian idealization onto Sparta; modern classical scholarship (Paul Cartledge) emphasizing complexity of Spartan society
    - **Battle of Leuctra** (371 BCE, against Thebes): Sparta's military dominance broken by Theban general Epaminondas; oblique attack with deep flank (*Sacred Band* of Thebes — 150 elite male couples); Sparta never recovered hegemony
  - **Athens: The Road to Democracy**
    - **Solon** (~594 BCE archonship): reformed Athenian constitution; abolished debt slavery (*seisachtheia* = "shaking off of burdens" — canceled debts, freed enslaved debtors); divided citizens into four property classes (replacing hereditary principle with wealth); created *Boule* of 400 as deliberative council; established popular courts (*heliaia*); foundation of Athenian democracy; left Athens to avoid being asked to revoke reforms; his poetry reflects his political philosophy
    - **Cleisthenes** (~508–507 BCE): "Father of Athenian Democracy"; after fall of tyranny (Hippias expelled by Spartan intervention + internal faction); reorganized Athenian political tribes from 4 genealogical to 10 new tribes mixing regional units (*trittyes*); **ostracism** introduced — annual vote to exile anyone seen as too powerful (by writing name on pottery shard = *ostrakon*); **Boule of 500** (50 representatives from each tribe); **Ekklesia** (full citizen assembly); created isonomia (equality under law) and isegoria (equal speech)
    - Athenian democracy in full operation: all adult male citizens could attend assembly; could speak, vote; hold office by lottery; **strategoi** (generals) elected annually; **Council of 500** prepared agenda; **popular courts** (*dikasteria*) with large juries (201, 501, 1001 citizens) replacing aristocratic judges; pay for jury service (enabling poorer citizens to participate)
    - Limits: excluded women, metics (resident foreigners), slaves (who constituted ~30% of population)
  - **Pre-Socratic Philosophy**
    - Miletus (Ionian coast, Asia Minor): birthplace of *philosophia*; first attempts to explain nature by natural rather than mythological causes
    - **Thales of Miletus** (~624–546 BCE): *arche* (primary substance) = water; predicted solar eclipse 585 BCE (per Herodotus; probably by Babylonian eclipse cycle knowledge); first person in Western tradition explicitly identified as a philosopher
    - **Anaximander** (~610–546 BCE): *arche* = *apeiron* (the "Boundless/Indefinite"); worlds form and dissolve in eternal process; earliest surviving prose fragment in Western philosophy ("they give justice and reparation to one another for their injustice according to the ordering of time"); proposed that humans evolved from fish-like creatures
    - **Anaximenes** (~585–525 BCE): *arche* = air; condensation (→ water, earth, stone) and rarefaction (→ fire)
    - **Heraclitus of Ephesus** (~535–475 BCE): *panta rhei* (everything flows); *logos* (rational principle underlying reality); conflict of opposites generates all things; "You cannot step in the same river twice"; "The way up and the way down are one and the same"; extraordinarily difficult, aphoristic style; influenced Stoicism, Plato, Hegel, Nietzsche
    - **Parmenides of Elea** (~515–450 BCE): radical monism; true Being is one, unchanging, eternal; motion and change are illusions; what is cannot not-be; **profoundly challenged Heraclitean flux**; raised fundamental problems for all later metaphysics; Plato's dialogue *Parmenides* the most difficult in the corpus
    - **Zeno of Elea** (~490–430 BCE): pupil of Parmenides; **Zeno's paradoxes**: Achilles and the Tortoise (motion involves infinite subdivisions, therefore impossible); Arrow Paradox (at each instant an arrow is motionless, therefore motion is impossible); triggered mathematical analysis of infinity; resolved only in 19th c. with Cauchy/Weierstrass epsilon-delta analysis of limits
    - **Democritus** (~460–370 BCE): **Atomism**; reality consists of indivisible atoms (*atomos* = uncuttable) moving in void; infinite variety of atoms explain all phenomena; no divine purpose; "Nothing exists except atoms and empty space; everything else is opinion"; anticipated modern atomic theory by 2,300 years; also wrote on ethics, music theory, mathematics; Epicurus and Lucretius built on atomism
    - **Pythagoras of Samos** (~570–495 BCE): mathematical-philosophical religious community at Croton (S. Italy); Pythagorean theorem (known earlier in Babylon but Pythagoras/school may have given first proof); transmigration of souls (*metempsychosis*); mathematical harmony of the cosmos; music theory (octave, fifth, fourth as ratios 2:1, 3:2, 4:3); "All is number"; Pythagorean communities (probably more than one person's contribution attributed to the founder)
    - **Empedocles** (~494–434 BCE): four "roots" (earth, water, fire, air) combined by Love/Strife; early evolutionary theory (incomplete creature-parts combined randomly; only viable combinations survived)
    - **Anaxagoras of Clazomenae** (~500–428 BCE): *nous* (Mind) initiated cosmic rotation; moved to Athens; associate of Pericles; prosecuted for impiety (sun is a hot stone, not a god); fled; proposed that all things contain portions of all things
  - **Lyric Poetry and Early Drama**
    - **Sappho of Lesbos** (~630–570 BCE): greatest lyric poet of antiquity; poetry of personal emotion (love, longing, jealousy), addressed mainly to women; "I think about god and about how I will feel" (fr. 16); her dialect (Lesbian Aeolic) was the language of female poetry; her reputation survived antiquity (Plato called her "the tenth Muse"); only ~650 lines survive until recently; **2014 "Brothers Poem"** discovered in Cologne Museum: Obbink-identified new Sappho poem on Cologne papyrus adding 20 lines; 2016 **"Kypris Poem"** fragment published; ongoing controversy over Obbink's role and provenance of some fragments (possible illegal antiquities trade)
    - **Pindar** (~518–438 BCE): greatest choral lyric poet; *Epinician Odes* (victory odes for Panhellenic games — Olympian, Pythian, Nemean, Isthmian); extravagant mythological imagery; theology of divine favor; patron: aristocratic victors across Greek world; 45 complete odes survive; "Become what you are" (Pythian 2.72)
    - **Archilochus, Alcaeus, Ibycus, Anacreon, Simonides, Bacchylides**: major lyric poets; diverse meters and subjects (personal, political, religious, erotic)
    - **Drama**: Athenian invention; developed from dithyramb chorus at festival of Dionysus; **Thespis** (~534 BCE): tradition of first actor (speaking individual separate from the chorus) at Great Dionysia festival; "thespian" from his name
  - **The Persian Wars (~499–479 BCE)**
    - **Ionian Revolt (~499–494 BCE)**: Greek cities of Ionian coast (Persian subjects since ~547 BCE) revolted; Athens and Eretria sent ships; Sardis burned; Miletus destroyed by Persians 494 BCE; Herodotus later opened his *Histories* with these events as background
    - **Marathon 490 BCE** *(see Persian section above)*; Miltiades' generalship; Athenian spear charge at a run; Pheidippides' legendary run to Sparta before battle (not the post-battle run to Athens — that is a later tradition)
    - **Themistocles and the Athenian Navy**: after silver strikes at Laurion mines (~483 BCE), Themistocles persuaded Athenians to build 200 triremes (*tis theos* — "the wooden walls" per Delphic oracle interpreted by Themistocles); the decision that saved Greece
    - **Thermopylae and Salamis 480 BCE** *(see Persian section above for detail)*
    - **Plataea 479 BCE**: Greek coalition under Spartan regent Pausanias; Theban Greek medizers (those who sided with Persia) punished; complete expulsion of Persians from Greece; **Pausanias's hubris** (he began behaving like a Persian satrap in victory) → recalled, tried, accused, died walled into temple
    - **Persian Wars legacy**: enormous Greek confidence; *Athenian exceptionalism* (Athens saved Greece; foundation of Athenian imperialism); tragedy and comedy flourish as civic celebrations of survival; philosophy accelerates; Herodotus writes the first extended historical narrative; concept of West vs. East encoded

- **Classical Period (~479–323 BCE)**
  - **Overview**
    - Greece's cultural golden age; Athenian democracy at full flowering; drama (Aeschylus, Sophocles, Euripides, Aristophanes); philosophy (Socrates, Plato, Aristotle); Parthenon built; Herodotus and Thucydides write history; Peloponnesian War divides Greece; Macedonian rise ends polis independence
  - **The Athenian Empire and the Delian League (~477–404 BCE)**
    - Immediately after Persian Wars: Athens leads Greek poleis in **Delian League** (~477 BCE) — anti-Persian defensive alliance; treasury on Delos (sacred island); member states pay tribute or ships
    - **Pericles** (~495–429 BCE): dominant Athenian statesman ~461–429 BCE; strategically a democratic politician who used popular courts and assembly against traditional aristocratic rivals; moved Delian Treasury to Athens 454 BCE; used tribute to fund Athenian building program — "converting alliance into empire"
    - **The Parthenon** (~447–432 BCE): Pheidias supervised sculpture; Ictinus and Callicrates architects; dedicated to Athena Parthenos (Virgin); gold-and-ivory *chryselephantine* cult statue by Pheidias (~12 m tall; lost); Doric exterior; extraordinary optical refinements (no straight lines — columns taper, lean inward, platform curves upward) to correct visual distortions; entire marble temple; **Elgin Marbles** (Parthenon Sculptures): most removed by Thomas Bruce, 7th Earl of Elgin 1801–1812 with contested Ottoman permission; now British Museum; Greece campaigns for return since 1980s (Melina Mercouri); "we were right to take them" / "they're safer here" debate ongoing
    - **Erechtheion** (~421–406 BCE): Caryatid Porch (6 female figure columns); housed the ancient olive wood statue of Athena and Poseidon's salt-water spring; Ionic order
    - **Temple of Hephaestus** ("Theseion"): best-preserved ancient Greek temple; Doric; still standing at the Athenian Agora
    - **Acropolis Museum** (opened 2009): new museum at foot of Acropolis; displays all Acropolis finds; explicitly designed so the Parthenon Sculptures could rejoin the pieces still in Athens; serves as argument for return of Elgin Marbles
    - **Athens as cultural capital**: Herodotus gave readings at Athens; tragedians competed at Dionysia (state-funded festival; entire polis attended; seats ~17,000); sophists taught for fees; Socrates questioned everyone; Plato's Academy; Aristotle's Lyceum; schools of rhetoric; Pericles' **Funeral Oration** (Thucydides 2.34–46): most celebrated political speech from antiquity; democracy as a "school for all of Greece"; freedom, civic participation, beauty, and wisdom praised; delivered over war dead ~431 BCE
  - **The Peloponnesian War (~431–404 BCE)**
    - **Thucydides** (~460–400 BCE): *History of the Peloponnesian War*; unfinished (ends mid-sentence in 411 BCE); the greatest work of ancient historical analysis; rationalistic; no divine intervention; searches for underlying causes (*aitia*); "Thucydides Trap" (Spartan fear of rising Athenian power → war); direct speeches are Thucydides' own compositions of what was probably said; "what actually happened" approach (*to akribēs*); profoundly influential on modern international relations theory (realism; Graham Allison *Destined for War*, 2017)
    - **Causes**: Peloponnesian League (Sparta) feared Athenian imperial expansion; immediate triggers: Corinthian complaints about Corcyra and Potidaea; the "Megarian Decree" (Athenian commercial embargo on Megara)
    - **Archidamian War (~431–421 BCE)**: Spartan annual invasions of Attica (Pericles' strategy: stay behind walls, do not fight on land); **Great Plague of Athens** 430–426 BCE: Thucydides' clinical description (2.47–54); symptoms match smallpox, typhus, or Ebola (*Ebola of Athens* hypothesis: 2006 dental pulp study found toxin evidence for typhoid; disputed); killed ~25% of Athens, including Pericles 429 BCE; **Pylos and Sphacteria 425 BCE**: Athenian general Cleon + Demosthenes trapped Spartan hoplites on island; 120 Spartan prisoners taken — stunning event (Spartans "do not surrender"); **Peace of Nicias 421 BCE**: truce
    - **Sicilian Expedition (~415–413 BCE)**: greatest strategic blunder in ancient history; Athens voted to attack Syracuse (enormous, wealthy city in Sicily); scale escalated to ~200 ships, ~40,000 men; commanded by **Nicias** (reluctant) and **Alcibiades** (recalled for sacrilege on eve of departure; defected to Sparta); Alcibiades advised Sparta to send Gylippus to Syracuse; Syracusan resistance hardened; Athenian fleet destroyed in harbor; retreating army massacred; ~40,000 Athenians killed or enslaved; Thucydides 6–7; Athens never recovered full strength
    - **Alcibiades** (~450–404 BCE): most fascinating and destructive figure of the war; brilliant, beautiful, aristocratic, amoral; defected to Sparta, then to Persia, then back to Athens; finally assassinated; his life as cautionary tale about democratic celebrity and personal ambition
    - **Final Phase (~413–404 BCE)**: Persian gold funds Spartan navy (Alcibiades' contribution in exile); Aegean control contested; Athens lost Battle of Aegospotami 405 BCE (entire fleet captured); Athens starved into surrender 404 BCE; democracy suspended; **Thirty Tyrants** (pro-Spartan oligarchs under Critias) set up; thousands killed; democracy restored 403 BCE after resistance led by Thrasybulus
    - **Corinthian War (~395–387 BCE)**: anti-Spartan coalition (Athens, Argos, Corinth, Thebes) backed by Persian gold; inconclusive; ended by **King's Peace (Peace of Antalcidas) 387 BCE**: Persian king dictates settlement — Ionian cities revert to Persia; other Greeks to be autonomous; Greece fragmented
    - **Theban Hegemony (~371–362 BCE)**: Epaminondas of Thebes; **Sacred Band** (150 pairs of male lovers — unit believed to fight to the death rather than flee before lovers); Leuctra 371 BCE (see above); temporary Theban dominance; Epaminondas died at Battle of Mantinea 362 BCE; Theban hegemony died with him
  - **Athenian Drama**
    - **Great Dionysia**: annual festival in honor of Dionysus; state-organized; choregia (wealthy citizens funded and trained choruses as public service); three days of tragedy, one day of comedy; prizes; entire democracy attended; religious and civic event simultaneously
    - **Aeschylus (~525–456 BCE)**: "Father of Tragedy"; introduced second actor (enabling dialogue); *Oresteia* trilogy (458 BCE — only complete tragic trilogy): Agamemnon → Libation Bearers → Eumenides; justice evolves from blood revenge to civic court (Athens' Areopagus); *Persians* (472 BCE; see above); *Prometheus Bound*, *Seven Against Thebes*, *Suppliants*; total: 90 plays, 7 survive complete
    - **Sophocles (~496–406 BCE)**: introduced third actor; 120+ plays; 7 survive complete; **tragedies of character and fate**: *Oedipus Rex* (best constructed tragedy by Aristotle's *Poetics*: discovery and reversal; Freud's *Oedipus complex*); *Antigone* (individual conscience vs. state law; *unwritten laws* of the gods vs. Creon's decree); *Electra*, *Ajax*, *Women of Trachis*, *Philoctetes* (409 BCE), *Oedipus at Colonus* (produced posthumously 401 BCE)
    - **Euripides (~484–406 BCE)**: most "modern" tragedian; psychological realism; sympathy for women, slaves, barbarians; gods portrayed as morally dubious; *Medea* (431 BCE; woman murders children out of revenge — radical subject); *Bacchae* (405 BCE; Dionysus destroys Pentheus who denies his divinity — most disturbing Greek tragedy); *Hippolytus*, *Trojan Women*, *Hecuba*, *Electra*, *Iphigenia in Aulis*, *Ion*, *Helen*; 18 plays survive (most of any tragedian)
    - **Aristophanes (~446–386 BCE)**: greatest comic playwright; Old Comedy (highly topical, obscene, politically scathing); *Clouds* (423 BCE — satirizes Socrates as a sophist teaching young men to make weaker argument stronger; used against Socrates at his trial); *Wasps* (jurors' obsession with litigation); *Lysistrata* (411 BCE; women's sex strike to end Peloponnesian War — anti-war comedy); *Frogs* (405 BCE; Dionysus descends to Hades to bring back Euripides, who just died; contest between Aeschylus and Euripides for throne of tragedy); 11 plays survive of 40+
    - **Middle Comedy and New Comedy**: **Menander** (~342–290 BCE): "New Comedy" — plots of domestic life, mistaken identity, long-lost relatives, love across social barriers; *Dyskolos* ("The Grouch") found complete on papyrus 1952 (only complete ancient comedy); influenced Plautus and Terence; influenced Shakespeare's romantic comedies → all Western romantic comedy genre
  - **Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle**
    - **Socrates (~470–399 BCE)**:
      - Did not write; known through Plato's dialogues, Xenophon's *Memorabilia*, Aristophanes' *Clouds*
      - *Socratic method* (*elenchus*): cross-examination to reveal inconsistencies in interlocutor's beliefs; "I know that I know nothing"; the *daimonion* (divine inner voice); mission from the god
      - **Trial and death 399 BCE**: charged with *asebeia* (impiety) and corrupting the youth; jury of 501 voted 280–221 to convict; Socrates refused to flee or grovel; sentenced to death by hemlock; described in *Apology*, *Crito*, *Phaedo* (Plato); death scene in *Phaedo* among the most moving passages in world literature; **"The unexamined life is not worth living"**
      - Post-Peloponnesian War context: Critias (one of the Thirty Tyrants) and Alcibiades associated with Socrates → democratic suspicious of his influence (though Socrates himself was a democrat)
    - **Plato (~428–348 BCE)**:
      - Founded the **Academy** ~387 BCE (west of Athens, grove of Academus); first institution of higher learning in the Western world; operated for ~900 years until Justinian closed it 529 CE
      - Wrote ~30 dialogues; all survive (unique among ancient philosophers); early dialogues (Socratic, aporetic: *Euthyphro*, *Apology*, *Crito*, *Meno*, *Symposium*); middle dialogues (Theory of Forms: *Phaedo*, *Republic*, *Phaedrus*, *Symposium*); late dialogues (more analytic, less dramatic: *Parmenides*, *Sophist*, *Timaeus*, *Laws*)
      - **Theory of Forms**: sensible world of change is inferior copy of eternal, immaterial world of Forms (Ideas); *Form of the Good* at the apex; the *Sun analogy*, *Divided Line*, and **Allegory of the Cave** (*Republic* Books 6–7): prisoners in cave see only shadows of reality; the philosopher escapes to see the sun (the Good) directly, then must return to educate others
      - **The Republic** (*Politeia*): ideal city; philosopher-kings; three-class state (philosopher-guardians, auxiliaries, producers); *noble lie*; critique of democracy (mob rule; Plato traumatized by Socrates' death under democracy); censorship of art; communism of property and wives among guardians; **profoundly influential and profoundly controversial**; Karl Popper *The Open Society and Its Enemies* (1945): Plato as proto-totalitarian
      - *Symposium*: Eros as philosophical drive toward the Beautiful itself; Diotima's ladder; Alcibiades' speech about Socrates
      - *Timaeus*: cosmology; Demiurge creates world from preexistent matter according to eternal Forms; *chora* (receptacle/space); four elements; World Soul; most Platonic text known in medieval West (Latin translation by Calcidius)
      - *Laws*: Plato's last and longest work; practical constitution for a real city; abandoned philosopher-kings; more realistic
    - **Aristotle (~384–322 BCE)**:
      - Born Stagira, Macedonia; father physician to Macedonian court; studied at Plato's Academy 20 years; tutor to young Alexander (343–340 BCE); founded **Lyceum** (Athens, 335 BCE — peripatetic school, named for walking covered walkways); *exoteric* works (published dialogues, mostly lost) vs. *esoteric* works (lecture notes, mostly preserved)
      - Breadth: logic, biology, physics, metaphysics, ethics, political science, rhetoric, poetics, psychology, meteorology, cosmology — essentially invented or defined most academic disciplines
      - **Logic (Organon)**: *Categories*, *De Interpretatione*, *Prior Analytics*, *Posterior Analytics*, *Topics*, *Sophistical Refutations*; syllogistic logic; remained the foundation of Western logic for 2,000 years until Frege (1879)
      - **Metaphysics**: substance (*ousia*), essence, form vs. matter (*hylomorphism*); potentiality vs. actuality; the four causes (material, formal, efficient, final); the Prime Mover (unmoved mover, pure actuality); categories of being
      - **Physics and Biology**: *Physics*, *On the Heavens*, *Meteorologica*; geocentric cosmos; four elements + aether; *Historia Animalium*, *Parts of Animals*, *Generation of Animals*; pioneered systematic zoology; dissected ~50 animal species; described cephalopod ink sac, placental shark, hyena "hermaphroditism" — confirmed only in modern times
      - **Ethics and Politics**: *Nicomachean Ethics* (10 books); eudaimonia (flourishing/happiness) as the human good; virtues as means between extremes (*mesotēs*); intellectual virtues; *phronesis* (practical wisdom); *Politics* (8 books): humans as *politikon zōion* (political animals); best constitution; analysis of 158 Greek constitutions; constitutions classified by number of rulers and whether they serve the common good
      - **Rhetoric** (3 books): *ethos*, *pathos*, *logos*; the handbook for persuasion; **Poetics** (survives partially): definition of tragedy; *catharsis* (purgation of pity and fear); *hamartia* (tragic flaw/error); *peripeteia* (reversal of fortune); *anagnorisis* (recognition); Oedipus Rex as the perfect tragedy; the lost Book II on Comedy (the plot of Umberto Eco's *The Name of the Rose*)
      - **Arab transmission**: Aristotle's works largely lost in West after Rome's decline; preserved in Arabic translations (Hunayn ibn Ishaq, Al-Kindi, Al-Farabi, Avicenna, Averroes); Latin translations from Arabic in 12th c. sparked the **Scholastic movement** (Aquinas's synthesis of "the Philosopher" with Catholic theology)
  - **The Sophists**
    - Professional teachers of rhetoric and excellence (*aretē*) for fees; primarily interested in practical persuasion not abstract truth
    - **Protagoras** (~490–420 BCE): "Man is the measure of all things" — relativism; *logos* can defend both sides of any argument; skilled in *antilogic* (arguing opposite positions)
    - **Gorgias** (~483–375 BCE): master rhetorician; *Encomium of Helen* and *On Non-Being* (nothing exists; if it did we couldn't know it; if we could we couldn't communicate it) — virtuosic exercises in argument; Plato's dialogue *Gorgias* critiques his position
    - **Thrasymachus** (in Plato's *Republic*): "justice is the advantage of the stronger"; power ethics
    - **Hippias**: claimed expertise in all subjects; polymath; memory champion; natural law over convention
    - **Prodicus**: precise distinction of synonyms; allegory of Heracles at the Crossroads (virtue vs. pleasure)
    - Plato deeply hostile to sophists (separated them from Socrates); modern scholars (G.B. Kerferd *The Sophistic Movement*) have rehabilitated them as legitimate intellectual tradition
  - **Greek Science and Medicine**
    - **Hippocrates of Cos** (~460–370 BCE): "Father of Medicine"; Hippocratic Corpus (~60 texts, not all by Hippocrates); systematic clinical observation; *prognosis* before diagnosis; the four humors (blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile) corresponding to four elements; *Airs, Waters, Places* — environment affects health; *On the Sacred Disease* (*epilepsy is not caused by gods but by brain pathology*); **Hippocratic Oath** (original version differs significantly from modern version)
    - **Galen** (~129–216 CE): synthesized Hippocratic and Aristotelian traditions; dominated Western medicine for 1,500 years
    - **Democritus, Empedocles** (see above); **Anaxagoras on astronomy**: moon receives light from sun; sun is a glowing rock
    - **Eudoxus of Cnidus** (~408–355 BCE): mathematical model of planetary motion with concentric spheres; first mathematical astronomy; also contributions to proportion theory and integral methods used by Archimedes
    - **Military technology**: Greek trireme; hoplite phalanx; torsion catapults (Philip II's innovations); siege engines (Dionysius I of Syracuse); Archimedes' machines at Syracuse
  - **Panhellenic Sanctuaries and Religious Life**
    - **Olympia**: sanctuary of Zeus; **Olympic Games** (traditionally founded 776 BCE — used as chronological anchor); four-year cycle (*Olympiad*); sports: foot races (stade, diaulos, dolichos), wrestling, boxing, *pankration* (brutal full-contact combat), pentathlon, chariot racing; women excluded from watching (exception: Demeter's priestess); **women's games (Heraia)** held separately; Olympia treasuries of Greek city-states; Pheidias's statue of Zeus (gold and ivory, ~13 m) — one of Seven Wonders; ancient games ended by Theodosius I ~393 CE; modern games founded by Pierre de Coubertin 1896
    - **Delphi**: sanctuary of Apollo; oracle (*Pythia* — priestess seated over chasm inhaling vapors; geological study found ethylene gas from fault line — de Boer and Piccardi 2001); consultations by poleis and individuals; inscriptions "Know thyself" and "Nothing in excess"; treasury buildings; Delphi as symbolic center of the world (*omphalos* = navel stone); Charioteer of Delphi (bronze, ~478 BCE — surviving masterpiece of early Classical sculpture); Python slain by Apollo (foundation myth)
    - **Eleusis**: Eleusinian Mysteries; initiates swore never to reveal what was seen; concerned with Demeter and Persephone, death and rebirth; initiates from all Greece including slaves and women; experiential religious revelation (possibly kykeon — barley drink with ergot?) — enthusiastically discussed now by scholars (Ruck, Wasson, Hoffman *The Road to Eleusis*); modern interpretation careful; Cicero said the Mysteries were the greatest gift of Athens to the world
