  - **Classical**: Aristotle's Poetics (see above); Horace, Ars Poetica (~19 BCE — "dulce et utile"; teach and delight); Longinus, On the Sublime (~1st century CE — elevation, transport, ecstasy in literature; "sublimity is the echo of a great mind")
  - **Renaissance–18th century**: Sidney, Defence of Poesie (1595); Boileau, L'Art Poétique (1674); Samuel Johnson (Preface to Shakespeare; Lives of the Poets); neoclassical rules (unities of time, place, action — derived from Aristotle via Italian and French codification)
  - **Romantic criticism**: Wordsworth, Preface to Lyrical Ballads; Coleridge, Biographia Literaria (imagination, organic form); Shelley, "Defence of Poetry"; Keats's letters (negative capability); Friedrich Schlegel (irony, fragment, progressive universal poetry)
  - **Victorian / 19th century**: Matthew Arnold, "The Function of Criticism at the Present Time" (1864; "the best that has been thought and said"); Hippolyte Taine (race, milieu, moment); Sainte-Beuve (biographical criticism)
  - **Russian Formalism (~1915–1930)**: Viktor Shklovsky, "Art as Technique" (1917 — ostranenie/defamiliarization: "art exists to make one feel things, to make the stone stony"); Boris Eichenbaum; Roman Jakobson (linguistics + poetics; "the poetic function"); Vladimir Propp, Morphology of the Folktale (1928 — 31 narrative functions; narrative grammar); Mikhail Bakhtin (dialogism, polyphony, chronotope, carnival, heteroglossia — The Dialogic Imagination, 1981; Problems of Dostoevsky's Poetics)
  - **New Criticism (~1930s–1960s, Anglo-American)**: close reading of text; autonomy of the literary work; I.A. Richards, Practical Criticism (1929 — reader responses to poems stripped of author/title); Cleanth Brooks, The Well Wrought Urn (1947 — "the heresy of paraphrase"; paradox, irony, ambiguity); William Empson, Seven Types of Ambiguity (1930); W.K. Wimsatt and Monroe Beardsley: "The Intentional Fallacy" (1946 — author's intention irrelevant) and "The Affective Fallacy" (1949 — reader's emotional response irrelevant); John Crowe Ransom coined "New Criticism" (1941)
  - **Structuralism and semiotics**: Ferdinand de Saussure (langue/parole — applied to literature); Claude Lévi-Strauss (mytheme analysis); Roland Barthes: early structuralist (S/Z, 1970 — five codes; reading Balzac); "The Death of the Author" (1967 — "the birth of the reader must be at the cost of the Death of the Author"); Gérard Genette, Narrative Discourse (1972/1980 — narratological categories: order, duration, frequency, mood, voice; foundational); Tzvetan Todorov (coined "narratology"; fantastic literature theory); A.J. Greimas (actantial model); Jonathan Culler, Structuralist Poetics (1975)
  - **Post-structuralism and deconstruction**: **Jacques Derrida (1930–2004)**: Of Grammatology (1967) — deconstruction; différance; "there is nothing outside the text" (il n'y a pas de hors-texte — often misunderstood); logocentrism; binary opposition undermined; Paul de Man (Blindness and Insight; Allegories of Reading; Yale School; posthumous collaboration controversy); J. Hillis Miller; Geoffrey Hartman; Barbara Johnson, The Critical Difference (1980)
  - **Reader-response theory**: Wolfgang Iser, The Act of Reading (1976) — implied reader; gaps and indeterminacies; Hans Robert Jauss, "horizon of expectations" (reception aesthetics, Constance School); Stanley Fish, Is There a Text in This Class? (1980) — interpretive communities determine meaning; "the reader makes the text"
  - **Psychoanalytic criticism**: Freud himself on literature (Oedipus; "The Uncanny," 1919; Leonardo; Hamlet); Jacques Lacan (mirror stage applied to literature; Seminar on "The Purloined Letter" — Poe; signifier/signified; the Real, Symbolic, Imaginary); Julia Kristeva (abjection; intertextuality coined — from Bakhtin; Revolution in Poetic Language, 1974); Slavoj Žižek (Lacanian culture critique); Harold Bloom, The Anxiety of Influence (1973 — poets wrestle with strong precursors; six "revisionary ratios": clinamen, tessera, kenosis, daemonization, askesis, apophrades)
  - **Marxist / materialist criticism**: Georg Lukács (The Historical Novel, 1937; realism as progressive; Balzac, Tolstoy); Frankfurt School: Theodor Adorno (Aesthetic Theory; "to write poetry after Auschwitz is barbaric"); Walter Benjamin ("The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction," 1935 — aura, reproducibility; "Theses on the Philosophy of History"); Raymond Williams, Culture and Society (1958); The Long Revolution (1961); Keywords (1976); Terry Eagleton, Literary Theory: An Introduction (1983 — most widely read introduction to theory); Fredric Jameson, Postmodernism, or, The Cultural Logic of Late Capitalism (1991); Pierre Macherey, A Theory of Literary Production (1966)
  - **Feminist literary criticism**: Virginia Woolf, A Room of One's Own (precursor); Simone de Beauvoir (precursor); Kate Millett, Sexual Politics (1970); Elaine Showalter, A Literature of Their Own (1977 — three phases: feminine, feminist, female; "gynocriticism"); Sandra Gilbert and Susan Gubar, The Madwoman in the Attic (1979 — 19th-century women writers; Bertha Mason); Hélène Cixous, "The Laugh of the Medusa" (1975 — écriture féminine); Luce Irigaray; Gayatri Spivak; Judith Butler, Gender Trouble (1990 — performativity; "there is no gender identity behind the expressions of gender"); bell hooks, Feminist Theory: From Margin to Center (1984)
  - **Postcolonial criticism**: Edward Said, Orientalism (1978) — foundational; Gayatri Spivak, "Can the Subaltern Speak?" (1988); Homi Bhabha, The Location of Culture (1994 — hybridity, mimicry, third space); Frantz Fanon (Black Skin, White Masks; The Wretched of the Earth); Chinua Achebe, "An Image of Africa: Racism in Conrad's Heart of Darkness" (1977); postcolonial rewriting/revision (Jean Rhys, Coetzee, Rushdie)
  - **New Historicism and Cultural Materialism**: Stephen Greenblatt, Renaissance Self-Fashioning (1980); "the circulation of social energy"; literature in/as history; anecdote as method; Louis Montrose ("the textuality of history, the historicity of texts"); Cultural Materialism (British counterpart): Jonathan Dollimore and Alan Sinfield, Political Shakespeare (1985); Raymond Williams's influence
  - **Ecocriticism**: Cheryll Glotfelty and Harold Fromm, The Ecocriticism Reader (1996); Lawrence Buell, The Environmental Imagination (1995); Timothy Morton (ecology without nature; "hyperobjects"); Rob Nixon, Slow Violence and the Environmentalism of the Poor (2011); posthumanism and literature
  - **Digital humanities and computational literary studies**: Franco Moretti, "Conjectures on World Literature" (2000); Distant Reading (2013 — graphs, maps, trees; computational analysis of 1,000+ novels); Matthew Jockers, Macroanalysis (2013); Ted Underwood, Distant Horizons (2019); Stanford Literary Lab pamphlets; topic modelling of literary corpora; stylometry; network analysis of character interactions

- **Rhetoric and poetics**
  - Classical rhetoric: Aristotle, Rhetoric (three modes of persuasion: ethos, pathos, logos); Cicero, De Oratore (ideal orator); Quintilian, Institutio Oratoria (education of the orator; "the good man speaking well"); the five canons: inventio, dispositio, elocutio, memoria, actio
  - Figures and tropes: metaphor, simile, metonymy, synecdoche, irony, hyperbole, litotes, anaphora, chiasmus, antithesis, paradox, oxymoron, personification, apostrophe; Aristotle: "the greatest thing by far is to be a master of metaphor" (Poetics); George Lakoff and Mark Turner, More Than Cool Reason (1989)
  - Modern rhetoric: Kenneth Burke, A Rhetoric of Motives (1950 — identification as key; dramatism; pentad); Chaïm Perelman and Lucie Olbrechts-Tyteca, The New Rhetoric (1958/1969); Wayne Booth, The Rhetoric of Fiction (1961 — reliable/unreliable narrator; implied author); rhetorical genre studies (Carolyn Miller, "Genre as Social Action," 1984)
  - Poetics: prosody (meter — iambic pentameter, hexameter, alexandrine; foot: iamb, trochee, dactyl, anapest, spondee; scansion; free verse — from Whitman onward; vers libre); rhyme (end, internal, slant, eye); stanza forms (couplet, tercet, quatrain, sestet, octave); fixed forms (sonnet — Petrarchan/Shakespearean/Spenserian; villanelle, sestina, ghazal, haiku, pantoum, rondeau); narratology (Genette, Prince, Bal); Derek Attridge, Poetic Rhythm (1995); Timothy Steele, All the Fun's in How You Say a Thing (1999)

- **Comparative and world literature**
  - Comparative literature: Goethe coined "Weltliteratur" (world literature, 1827 — "national literature is now a rather unmeaning term; the epoch of world literature is at hand"); discipline institutionalized in 19th century (Hugo Meltzl de Lomnitz, first journal 1877); René Wellek and Austin Warren, Theory of Literature (1949 — foundational textbook); comparative method: cross-cultural, cross-linguistic, inter-art, inter-disciplinary; structuralism and narratology deeply comparative
  - Franco Moretti, "Conjectures on World Literature" (New Left Review, 2000) — distant reading; world-systems approach to literary history; "trees" of genre diffusion; Pascale Casanova, The World Republic of Letters (1999 — literary world-system; Paris as center; unequal exchange of literary capital); David Damrosch, What Is World Literature? (2003 — "a mode of circulation and reading"); Emily Apter, Against World Literature (2013 — untranslatability; critique of flattening)
  - Translation studies: Eugene Nida (formal vs. dynamic equivalence); Lawrence Venuti, The Translator's Invisibility (1995 — foreignizing vs. domesticating); "world literature is writing that gains in translation" (Damrosch); "Three Percent" problem (only ~3% of books published in US are translations); International Booker Prize effect
  - Frontier: digital world literature (computational comparison of literary traditions); literature and AI (creative writing by LLMs; authorship debate; genre detection across languages); climate literature as global genre; planetary literature beyond national paradigms

### Visual Arts

#### Visual Arts — Full Chronological & Theoretical Expansion

- Foundations & Scope of the Visual Arts
  - Definition: arts primarily visual in nature — painting, drawing, sculpture, printmaking, photography, ceramics, glass, fibre/textile art, architecture (covered separately), installation, performance-adjacent visual art, digital/new media art, design fields
  - Distinctions: fine art vs. applied/decorative art vs. craft — boundaries historically contingent; "Art" as autonomous concept emerges c. 18th century Europe (Kristeller, "The Modern System of the Arts," 1951)
  - Visual culture: broader frame encompassing advertising, fashion, film stills, memes, scientific imagery — Nicholas Mirzoeff, An Introduction to Visual Culture (1999)
  - Key questions the field addresses: What is art? Who decides? What is beauty? How does visual representation shape thought? How do materials and processes shape meaning?

- Prehistoric & Ancient Art
  - Paleolithic Art (~40 000–10 000 BCE)
    - Cave painting: Chauvet-Pont-d'Arc (France, ~36 000 BP) — oldest known figurative paintings; discovered 1994 by Jean-Marie Chauvet, Éliette Brunel Deschamps, Christian Hillaire
    - Lascaux (France, ~17 000 BP) — Hall of the Bulls; discovered 1940; closed 1963 due to CO₂ damage; Lascaux II–IV replicas
    - Altamira (Spain, ~14 000 BP) — polychrome bison; discovered 1879 by Marcelino Sanz de Sautuola; initially dismissed as forgery
    - Sulawesi cave art (Indonesia, ~45 500 BP) — hand stencils and pig painting; 2021 re-dating pushes non-European art earlier than European
    - Maros-Pangkep therianthrope hunting scene (~43 900 BP) — earliest known narrative scene (Aubert et al., Nature 2019)
    - Portable art: Venus figurines — Venus of Willendorf (~28 000 BP), Venus of Hohle Fels (~35 000 BP, oldest known figurative sculpture); bone/antler carvings, perforated shells
    - Theories of cave art function: hunting magic (Abbé Breuil); structuralism (Leroi-Gourhan, André — binary symbolic systems); shamanic trance hypothesis (Lewis-Williams and Dowson); social/territorial marking; acoustic theory (resonant chambers selected)
    - Rock art traditions: Saharan rock art (Tassili n'Ajjer, "Round Head" period ~10 000 BP); San rock art (South Africa, ~20 000+ years); Kimberley rock art/Gwion Gwion (Australia, ~12 000 BP); Bhimbetka (India, ~10 000 BP)
    - Frontier: AI-assisted dating of rock art via uranium-thorium; LIDAR discovery of new sites; aDNA from pigment residues
  - Neolithic & Bronze Age Art (~10 000–1200 BCE)
    - Neolithic Near East: Çatalhöyük murals (Anatolia, ~7500 BCE) — earliest known landscape painting (volcano scene); plastered skulls (Jericho, PPNB); Ain Ghazal statues (Jordan, ~7000 BCE)
    - Megalithic art: Newgrange passage tomb (Ireland, ~3200 BCE) — tri-spiral motifs; Stonehenge (ceremonial landscape); Carnac standing stones
    - Mesopotamian art: cylinder seals, Warka Vase (Uruk, ~3200 BCE), Standard of Ur (~2600 BCE), ziggurat decoration, Assyrian palace reliefs (Nineveh, Nimrud — lion hunts of Ashurbanipal ~645 BCE)
    - Egyptian art: conventions of flatness, hierarchical scale, composite view (head profile, eye frontal, shoulders frontal, legs profile); Old Kingdom: Palette of Narmer (~3100 BCE), Seated Scribe, Great Sphinx; Middle Kingdom: introspective royal portraiture; New Kingdom: Amarna art under Akhenaten — revolutionary naturalism; tomb painting (Valley of the Kings); Tutankhamun's golden mask
    - Aegean Bronze Age: Cycladic figurines (~2800–2300 BCE — abstract marble forms, influence on Brancusi/Modigliani); Minoan fresco: Toreador Fresco, Saffron Gatherers (Thera/Akrotiri, ~1600 BCE); Mycenaean: gold death masks, Lion Gate relief, pottery
    - Indus Valley: Priest-King of Mohenjo-daro, Dancing Girl bronze, terracotta figurines, seal art (Pashupati seal)
    - Chinese Bronze Age: Shang ritual bronzes — taotie motif, piece-mold casting technique; jade cong and bi discs; Zhou bronzes with long inscriptions
    - Pre-Columbian: Olmec colossal heads (La Venta, San Lorenzo); Valdivian figurines (Ecuador, ~3500 BCE)
  - Classical Antiquity
    - Greek Art
      - Geometric Period (~900–700 BCE): Dipylon Master vases; meander patterns; schematic human figures in funerary scenes
      - Orientalizing Period (~700–600 BCE): influence from Near East/Egypt; floral, animal motifs; Corinthian pottery
      - Archaic Period (~600–480 BCE): kouros and kore sculptures — Egyptian-influenced frontal stance, "Archaic smile"; black-figure pottery: Exekias (Ajax and Achilles Playing Dice, ~540 BCE); red-figure pottery: Euphronios Krater (~515 BCE)
      - Early Classical / Severe Style (~480–450 BCE): Kritios Boy — first contrapposto; Riace Bronzes (~460 BCE); Charioteer of Delphi
      - High Classical (~450–400 BCE): Phidias — Athena Parthenos, Zeus at Olympia (one of Seven Wonders); Parthenon sculpture program: metopes, frieze, pediments; Polykleitos — Doryphoros (canon of proportions, 1:7 head-to-body); Myron — Discobolos
      - Late Classical (~400–323 BCE): Praxiteles — Hermes with the Infant Dionysus, Aphrodite of Knidos (first monumental female nude); Skopas — emotional intensity; Lysippos — elongated proportions (1:8 canon), court sculptor to Alexander
      - Hellenistic Period (~323–31 BCE): dramatic emotion, theatricality, diversity of subject; Laocoön and His Sons (Vatican, rediscovered 1506 — Pliny attributed to Agesander, Athenodoros, Polydorus of Rhodes); Venus de Milo (~130 BCE); Winged Victory of Samothrace (~190 BCE); Dying Gaul; Pergamon Altar (~180 BCE); genre sculpture: Old Market Woman, Drunken Old Woman; Fayum mummy portraits (Egypt, 1st c. BCE–3rd c. CE — encaustic on wood; precursors of portraiture)
      - Greek painting: almost entirely lost; evidence from literary sources (Pliny, Pausanias), vase painting, Macedonian tomb paintings (Vergina, ~340 BCE); Zeuxis, Apelles, Parrhasius (legendary masters)
    - Etruscan Art (~700–100 BCE): tomb painting (Tomb of the Leopards, Tarquinia); terracotta sarcophagi (Sarcophagus of the Spouses); bronze she-wolf (Capitoline Wolf — medieval dating debate); Chimera of Arezzo
    - Roman Art
      - Republican Period: veristic portrait busts — unflinching realism; history painting on triumphal monuments
      - Imperial Period: Augustus of Prima Porta (~20 BCE) — idealized Classical Greek body + Roman veristic face; Roman wall painting: Four Pompeian Styles (Mau classification: 1st — Incrustation; 2nd — Architectural illusionism; 3rd — Ornamental; 4th — Fantastic/Intricate); mosaic art: Alexander Mosaic (Pompeii, copy of ~300 BCE painting); Trajan's Column (~113 CE) — narrative spiral relief, 155 scenes; Pantheon (architectural sculpture); portrait bust tradition; sarcophagus relief sculpture
      - Late Antique/Transition (~3rd–5th c. CE): loss of naturalistic ideal; frontal, hieratic style; Arch of Constantine (~315 CE) — spolia from earlier monuments + new crude reliefs; emergence of Christian art: catacombs painting, Good Shepherd motif, Chi-Rho symbol; Dura-Europos synagogue frescoes (~244 CE)
    - Indian Classical Art: Gandhara sculpture (Greco-Buddhist, 1st–5th c. CE) — first anthropomorphic Buddha images; Mathura school — indigenous Indian style; Amaravati stupa reliefs; Ajanta cave paintings (2nd c. BCE–6th c. CE — finest surviving ancient painting cycle in India); Ellora caves (rock-cut temples, Kailasa Temple ~8th c.)
    - Chinese Classical: Terracotta Army (~210 BCE); lacquerware; Han dynasty tomb murals; Six Dynasties calligraphy (Wang Xizhi, "Sage of Calligraphy"); landscape painting origins

- Medieval Art (~500–1400 CE)
  - Early Christian & Byzantine Art
    - Early Christian: basilica decoration; apse mosaics (Santa Pudenziana, Rome, ~400 CE); ivory diptychs; illuminated manuscripts (Codex Purpureus Rossanensis)
    - Byzantine Art: icon tradition — theology of the image; Iconoclasm (726–843 CE): destruction and defense of icons (John of Damascus; Second Council of Nicaea 787); Hagia Sophia mosaics (6th c., Deësis mosaic 13th c.); Ravenna mosaics: San Vitale (Emperor Justinian and Theodora panels, ~547 CE); icon painting technique: encaustic → tempera on wood panel; gold ground as symbol of divine light; Christ Pantocrator (Sinai, 6th c. — oldest icon); manuscript illumination (Paris Psalter, 10th c.)
    - Post-Byzantine/Russian: Andrei Rublev, Trinity icon (~1411); Theophanes the Greek; iconostasis tradition; Moscow School vs. Novgorod School
  - Early Medieval Western Art (~500–1000 CE)
    - Insular art: Book of Kells (~800 CE); Lindisfarne Gospels (~715 CE) — interlace, zoomorphic ornament, carpet pages; metalwork: Tara Brooch, Ardagh Chalice
    - Carolingian Renaissance: revival of classical forms under Charlemagne; Aachen Chapel architecture and mosaics; Godescalc Evangelistary; ivory bookcovers; Utrecht Psalter (~830 CE) — dynamic pen drawings
    - Ottonian art: bronze doors of Hildesheim Cathedral (Bishop Bernward, ~1015); Gero Crucifix (~970) — first monumental crucifix in Western art; illuminated manuscripts (Codex Aureus of Echternach)
    - Viking/Norse art: Oseberg ship carvings; animal styles (Borre, Jellinge, Mammen, Ringerike, Urnes); stave church carvings
    - Anglo-Saxon: Sutton Hoo treasures (~625 CE); Alfred Jewel; cross-cultural synthesis
  - Islamic Art (~7th c.–present, medieval peak ~800–1500)
    - Principles: aniconism in religious contexts (hadith-based, not Quranic absolute prohibition); geometric pattern (girih tiles, muqarnas); arabesque; calligraphy as highest art form (kufic, naskh, thuluth, nastaliq scripts)
    - Architecture-integrated art: Dome of the Rock mosaics (Jerusalem, ~691 CE); Great Mosque of Córdoba (horseshoe arches, double arches); Alhambra (Granada, Nasrid, ~14th c. — stucco, tile, muqarnas); Samarkand and Timurid tile-work; Isfahan Shah Mosque; Ottoman İznik tile tradition
    - Manuscript illumination: Persian miniature painting — Shahnameh illustrations; Behzad (Herāt school, ~1460–1535); Mughal painting: synthesis of Persian and Indian traditions (Akbarnama, Jahangir's naturalist paintings); Ottoman court painting
    - Metalwork: Blacas Ewer (Mosul, ~1232 — inlaid brass); ceramics: lustreware (9th c. Iraq); İznik pottery; carpets and textiles
    - Calligraphy: Ibn Muqla (10th c. — proportional system); Yaqut al-Musta'simi; contemporary Arabic calligraphy renaissance
  - Romanesque Art (~1000–1200 CE)
    - Architecture-sculpture integration: tympanum programs (Autun Cathedral — Gislebertus, Last Judgment ~1130; Vézelay, Moissac); historiated capitals; cloister sculpture
    - Manuscript illumination: Winchester Bible; Bury Bible; Benedictional of St Æthelwold
    - Metalwork: Limoges enamel; bronze casting; Gloucester Candlestick
    - Wall painting: Sant Climent de Taüll (Catalonia, ~1123); Berze-la-Ville
    - Characteristics: round arches, thick walls, barrel vaults; stylization and expressive distortion of human form
  - Gothic Art (~1150–1400 CE)
    - Stained glass: Chartres Cathedral (12th–13th c. — "Chartres blue"); Sainte-Chapelle (Paris, ~1248); typological windows
    - Sculpture: jamb figures of Chartres (Royal Portal, ~1150; North Porch); Reims Cathedral "Smiling Angel" (~1240); naturalistic trend; International Gothic elegance
    - Panel painting emergence: Cimabue (~1240–1302) — Santa Trinita Maestà; Duccio di Buoninsegna — Maestà (Siena, 1308–11); Giotto di Bondone (~1267–1337) — Arena Chapel frescoes (Padua, ~1305): spatial illusionism, emotional naturalism, break from Byzantine flatness; "father of Western painting"
    - International Gothic (~1370–1420): Limbourg Brothers — Très Riches Heures du Duc de Berry (~1412–16); Gentile da Fabriano — Adoration of the Magi (1423); courtly elegance, rich detail, sinuous line
    - Manuscript illumination peak: Jean Pucelle (Paris, 14th c.); Bedford Hours; devotional Books of Hours
  - African Medieval Art: Ile-Ife terracotta and bronze heads (12th–14th c.) — Naturalistic bronze casting rivaling Italian Renaissance; Benin Kingdom bronzes (from 13th c.); Great Zimbabwe soapstone birds; Ethiopian church manuscripts and murals
  - Southeast Asian / East Asian Medieval Art
    - Khmer: Angkor Wat bas-reliefs (~12th c.); Bayon temple face towers
    - Japanese: Heian period painting: yamato-e (Tale of Genji scrolls, 12th c.); Kamakura realism: Unkei and Kaikei sculptors (Todai-ji guardians); Zen ink painting origins
    - Chinese: Song dynasty landscape painting: Fan Kuan (Travelers Among Mountains and Streams, ~1000); Guo Xi (Early Spring, 1072); Ma Yuan; Xia Gui; Southern vs. Northern Song schools; literati painting (wenren hua) origins; celadon and blue-and-white ceramics

- Renaissance Art (~1400–1600)
  - Early Renaissance (Quattrocento, ~1400–1490)
    - Florence as crucible: Medici patronage (Cosimo, Lorenzo "il Magnifico"); guild commissions; humanist intellectual context
    - Brunelleschi, Filippo (1377–1446): linear perspective demonstration (~1415, Baptistery experiment); dome of Florence Cathedral; Ospedale degli Innocenti
    - Donatello (~1386–1466): David (first free-standing male nude since antiquity, ~1440s); Gattamelata equestrian statue; Mary Magdalene (wood, ~1455)
    - Masaccio (1401–1428): Holy Trinity (Santa Maria Novella, ~1427 — first mathematically correct perspective painting); Brancacci Chapel frescoes (with Masolino)
    - Fra Angelico (~1395–1455): San Marco frescoes; Annunciation; devotional luminosity
    - Paolo Uccello (~1397–1475): Battle of San Romano (~1438–40) — perspective obsession
    - Piero della Francesca (~1415–1492): Flagellation; Legend of the True Cross (Arezzo); treatises on perspective (De Prospectiva Pingendi)
    - Sandro Botticelli (~1445–1510): Birth of Venus (~1485); Primavera (~1480); neo-Platonic allegory; Savonarola crisis
    - Andrea Mantegna (~1431–1506): Camera degli Sposi (Mantua, ~1474 — trompe-l'œil ceiling); Lamentation of Christ (~1480 — extreme foreshortening); antiquarian precision
    - Giovanni Bellini (~1430–1516): Venetian color tradition; Sacra Conversazione; landscape backgrounds
    - Ghiberti, Lorenzo (~1378–1455): Gates of Paradise (Florence Baptistery East Doors, 1425–52)
    - Leon Battista Alberti (1404–1472): De Pictura (1435) — first treatise on perspective painting; architect-theorist
    - Perugino (~1446–1523): Christ Giving the Keys to St Peter (Sistine Chapel, ~1482); teacher of Raphael; spatial clarity
  - Northern Renaissance (~1400–1560)
    - Early Netherlandish painting: revolutionary oil technique (linseed/walnut oil glazing — luminosity, detail)
    - Jan van Eyck (~1390–1441): Ghent Altarpiece (1432, with Hubert van Eyck); Arnolfini Portrait (1434) — convex mirror, symbolic realism; Madonna of Chancellor Rolin; minute detail, light effects
    - Robert Campin / Master of Flémalle (~1375–1444): Mérode Altarpiece (~1425–28); domestic religious setting
    - Rogier van der Weyden (~1400–1464): Descent from the Cross (~1435) — emotional intensity; Portrait of a Lady; influenced all subsequent Flemish painting
    - Hugo van der Goes (~1440–1482): Portinari Altarpiece (~1475) — sent to Florence, shocked Italian artists with oil technique
    - Hieronymus Bosch (~1450–1516): Garden of Earthly Delights (~1490–1510); Temptation of St Anthony; surreal, fantastical imagery; moralizing allegory; proto-Surrealist
    - Albrecht Dürer (1471–1528): greatest Northern Renaissance artist; Self-Portrait (1500) — Christlike self-image; Apocalypse woodcuts (1498); Melencolia I (1514); Knight, Death and the Devil; Adam and Eve engraving (1504); bridged Italian and Northern traditions; treatises on proportion and geometry
    - Lucas Cranach the Elder (1472–1553): court painter to Saxon electors; Luther portraits; mythological scenes; distinctive style
    - Hans Holbein the Younger (1497–1543): The Ambassadors (1533 — anamorphic skull); Henry VIII portraits; supreme portrait painter
    - Matthias Grünewald (~1470–1528): Isenheim Altarpiece (~1512–16) — extreme expressionist suffering; Crucifixion panel among most powerful in art history
    - Pieter Bruegel the Elder (~1525–1569): Hunters in the Snow (1565); Peasant Wedding; Tower of Babel; panoramic landscapes; encyclopedic detail; social observation
  - High Renaissance (~1490–1530)
    - Leonardo da Vinci (1452–1519): Mona Lisa (~1503–19); Last Supper (~1495–98 — experimental secco technique, deterioration); sfumato technique; Vitruvian Man; anatomical drawings; notebooks (~7000 pages); Virgin of the Rocks; paradigm of "universal genius"
    - Michelangelo Buonarroti (1475–1564): David (1501–04); Sistine Chapel ceiling (1508–12 — Creation of Adam, 300+ figures); Last Judgment (Sistine, 1536–41); Pietà (1498–99); Moses; Medici Chapel sculptures (Night, Day, Dawn, Dusk); terribilità — awesome creative power; architect of St Peter's dome; sonnets
    - Raphael Sanzio (1483–1520): School of Athens (1509–11 — Vatican Stanze); Sistine Madonna; Transfiguration (1516–20); La Fornarina; synthesis of Leonardo's sfumato + Michelangelo's dynamism; ideal of classical harmony; workshop production system
    - Venetian Renaissance: color (colore) vs. Florentine line (disegno) — great Renaissance debate (paragone)
    - Giorgione (~1477–1510): The Tempest (~1505 — first "mood landscape" in Western art); Sleeping Venus
    - Titian (Tiziano Vecellio, ~1488–1576): Assumption of the Virgin (1518); Venus of Urbino (1538); late style: loose, almost proto-Impressionist brushwork; court painter to Charles V and Philip II; Bacchus and Ariadne; poesie series
    - Tintoretto (1518–1594): Last Supper (San Giorgio Maggiore, 1592–94); Crucifixion (Scuola Grande di San Rocco); dramatic lighting, dynamic compositions
    - Veronese (1528–1588): Wedding at Cana (1563); Inquisition trial over Last Supper (renamed Feast in the House of Levi, 1573); opulent color
    - Correggio (~1489–1534): Assumption of the Virgin (Parma Cathedral dome, ~1526–30) — proto-Baroque illusionism; sensual Madonnas
  - Mannerism (~1520–1600)
    - Reaction to/extension of High Renaissance perfection; elongated proportions, ambiguous space, complex poses, artifice, sophistication
    - Pontormo (1494–1557): Deposition (~1528) — floating figures, acid colors; Visdomini Altarpiece
    - Parmigianino (1503–1540): Madonna with the Long Neck (~1535) — quintessential Mannerist work
    - Bronzino (1503–1572): Allegory with Venus and Cupid (~1545) — cold eroticism, allegoric complexity; Medici court portraits
    - Benvenuto Cellini (1500–1571): Perseus with the Head of Medusa (1545–54); goldsmithing; Autobiography
    - El Greco (Doménikos Theotokópoulos, 1541–1614): Burial of the Count of Orgaz (1586); View of Toledo; elongated figures, luminous color, spiritual intensity; Byzantine → Venetian → Mannerist → unique synthesis
    - Arcimboldo (1526–1593): composite heads made of fruit, vegetables, books — precursor of Surrealism
    - Giambologna (1529–1608): Rape of the Sabine Women (1583) — serpentinata (spiral composition viewable from all angles)

- Baroque, Rococo, & Neoclassical Art (~1600–1830)
  - Baroque Art (~1600–1750)
    - Origins in Counter-Reformation: Council of Trent demands emotional, accessible religious art; Jesuit patronage
    - Italian Baroque
      - Caravaggio (Michelangelo Merisi, 1571–1610): revolutionary tenebrism/chiaroscuro; Calling of St Matthew (1599–1600); Judith Beheading Holofernes; Supper at Emmaus; criminal life and early death; Caravaggisti followers (Orazio Gentileschi, Artemisia Gentileschi, Bartolomeo Manfredi, Gerrit van Honthorst)
      - Artemisia Gentileschi (1593–~1656): Judith Slaying Holofernes (~1620); feminist icon; first woman admitted to Accademia di Arte del Disegno
      - Gian Lorenzo Bernini (1598–1680): Ecstasy of Saint Teresa (1647–52); Apollo and Daphne (1622–25); St Peter's Square colonnade; Baldacchino; fountains (Four Rivers); baroque synthesis of architecture, sculpture, painting
      - Annibale Carracci (1560–1609): Farnese Gallery ceiling (1597–1604) — classical Baroque alternative to Caravaggio; Bolognese School revival
      - Guido Reni (1575–1642): Aurora ceiling fresco; idealized beauty
      - Pietro da Cortona (1596–1669): Allegory of Divine Providence (Palazzo Barberini ceiling, 1632–39) — quadratura illusionism
      - Andrea Pozzo (1642–1709): Apotheosis of St Ignatius (Sant'Ignazio, Rome, 1691–94) — extreme di sotto in sù ceiling perspective
    - Spanish Baroque
      - Diego Velázquez (1599–1660): Las Meninas (1656 — "the theology of painting," Foucault; complex perspective and viewer implication); Surrender of Breda; portraits of Philip IV and court; late impressionistic brushwork; The Spinners
      - Francisco de Zurbarán (1598–1664): monastic subjects; Still Life with Lemons, Oranges and a Rose (1633); extreme tenebrism
      - Bartolomé Esteban Murillo (1617–1682): Immaculate Conception paintings; street children; popular piety
    - Flemish Baroque
      - Peter Paul Rubens (1577–1640): Descent from the Cross (1612–14); Rape of the Daughters of Leucippus; Marie de' Medici cycle (24 paintings, Louvre); colorist virtuosity; enormous workshop; diplomat-artist; muscular dynamism, fleshy sensuality
      - Anthony van Dyck (1599–1641): court portraiture (Charles I); elegant, aristocratic style; influenced English portraiture for two centuries
      - Jacob Jordaens (1593–1678): boisterous genre scenes; The King Drinks
    - Dutch Golden Age (~1600–1680)
      - Rembrandt van Rijn (1606–1669): Night Watch (1642); Self-portraits (~80+ across career — most sustained self-examination in art history); Anatomy Lesson of Dr Nicolaes Tulp (1632); Return of the Prodigal Son (~1668); etching mastery; late impasto; light as spiritual metaphor; bankruptcy and decline
      - Johannes Vermeer (1632–1675): Girl with a Pearl Earring (~1665); Art of Painting (~1666); milkmaid; only ~35 known paintings; camera obscura use debated (Philip Steadman, Vermeer's Camera, 2001; Tim Jenison experiment); pointillé light effects
      - Frans Hals (~1582–1666): Laughing Cavalier (1624); group portraits; loose brushwork anticipating Impressionism
      - Genre painting: Jan Steen (dissolute households); Pieter de Hooch (domestic interiors); Gerard ter Borch (satin textures)
      - Landscape: Jacob van Ruisdael; Meindert Hobbema (Avenue at Middelharnis, 1689); Aelbert Cuyp (golden light)
      - Still life: vanitas (skull, candle, timepiece — memento mori); flower painting (Rachel Ruysch, ~1700; Jan Davidsz. de Heem); pronkstilleven (ostentatious still life — Willem Kalf)
      - Marine painting: Willem van de Velde the Younger
      - Art market revolution: paintings as commodities; guild system (Guild of Saint Luke); dealer networks; first art auctions
    - French Baroque/Classicism
      - Nicolas Poussin (1594–1665): Et in Arcadia Ego (~1637–38); Landscape with the Burial of Phocion; intellectual, classicizing art; "Poussiniste" vs. "Rubéniste" debate at French Academy
      - Claude Lorrain (1600–1682): ideal landscapes; golden harbor scenes; Liber Veritatis catalogue
      - Académie Royale de Peinture et de Sculpture (founded 1648): hierarchy of genres — history painting > portraiture > genre > landscape > still life; art theory codified (André Félibien; Charles Le Brun)
    - English Baroque: James Thornhill (Painted Hall, Greenwich)
  - Rococo (~1710–1770)
    - Origins in French aristocratic taste; rejection of Baroque grandeur for intimacy, lightness, pastel color, asymmetry, sensuality
    - Antoine Watteau (1684–1721): Pilgrimage to Cythera (1717) — fête galante genre; Gilles; melancholy beneath elegance
    - François Boucher (1703–1770): Madame de Pompadour portraits; mythological scenes; sensual nudes; Sèvres porcelain designs
    - Jean-Honoré Fragonard (1732–1806): The Swing (1767) — quintessential Rococo; The Progress of Love series
    - Giovanni Battista Tiepolo (1696–1770): ceiling frescoes (Würzburg Residenz, 1750–53 — largest ceiling painting in world); last great Baroque-Rococo decorative painter
    - Rococo sculpture: Clodion (terracotta bacchanales); Étienne Maurice Falconier (Bronze Horseman, St Petersburg)
    - Decorative arts: porcelain (Meissen, Sèvres); furniture (Louis XV); gilded boiserie
    - Critique: Diderot, Hogarth, Enlightenment thinkers denounced Rococo as frivolous, decadent
    - English Rococo/Satire: William Hogarth (1697–1764): A Rake's Progress; Marriage A-la-Mode; moral narratives; The Analysis of Beauty (1753)
    - Canaletto (1697–1768): Venetian vedute; Grand Tour paintings; camera obscura use
  - Neoclassicism (~1750–1830)
    - Context: Enlightenment rationalism; Winckelmann's History of the Art of Antiquity (1764) — "noble simplicity and quiet grandeur"; excavations of Pompeii/Herculaneum (1738/1748); Grand Tour
    - Jacques-Louis David (1748–1825): Oath of the Horatii (1784 — civic virtue, Republican ideals); Death of Marat (1793); Napoleon Crossing the Alps; Coronation of Napoleon; Revolutionary-era painter, link between art and politics
    - Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres (1780–1867): Grande Odalisque (1814); Apotheosis of Homer; supreme draughtsman; idealized female form; "Ingres line" vs. Delacroix color = replay of Poussin/Rubens debate
    - Antonio Canova (1757–1822): Psyche Revived by Cupid's Kiss (1787–93); Three Graces; Napoleon as Mars the Peacemaker; neoclassical sculpture apex
    - Bertel Thorvaldsen (1770–1844): Danish sculptor; Lion of Lucerne; Christ (Copenhagen Cathedral)
    - Angelica Kauffman (1741–1807): founding member of Royal Academy; history painter in neoclassical mode
    - American neoclassicism: John Singleton Copley; Benjamin West (Death of General Wolfe, 1770 — modern dress in history painting)

- 19th Century Art: Revolution and Fragmentation
  - Romanticism (~1790–1850)
    - Philosophical roots: Sublime (Burke, Kant); emotion over reason; individualism; nature-worship; medievalism; nationalism
    - Caspar David Friedrich (1774–1840): Wanderer above the Sea of Fog (~1818); Abbey in the Oakwood; Rückenfigur motif; spiritual landscape
    - J.M.W. Turner (1775–1851): The Fighting Temeraire (1838); Rain, Steam and Speed (1844); Slave Ship (1840); dissolution of form in light and color; proto-abstraction; "modern painter" (Ruskin)
    - John Constable (1776–1837): The Hay Wain (1821); Salisbury Cathedral; plein air oil sketches; English pastoral landscape
    - Eugène Delacroix (1798–1863): Liberty Leading the People (1830); Death of Sardanapalus (1827); colorist; Orientalist subjects; Romantic manifesto
    - Théodore Géricault (1791–1824): Raft of the Medusa (1818–19) — political scandal painting; horse paintings; portraits of insane patients
    - Francisco Goya (1746–1828): Third of May 1808 (1814); Saturn Devouring His Son (~1820–23); Los Caprichos etchings; Disasters of War; Black Paintings; transition from court painter to unflinching social critic; bridge from Old Master to modern art
    - William Blake (1757–1827): illuminated books (Songs of Innocence and Experience); visionary art; Great Red Dragon; Ancient of Days
    - Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood (1848–): Dante Gabriel Rossetti, John Everett Millais (Ophelia, 1851–52), William Holman Hunt (The Light of the World); rebellion against Royal Academy conventions; medievalism; truth to nature
    - Later Pre-Raphaelites & Arts and Crafts: Edward Burne-Jones; William Morris — wallpapers, tapestries, Kelmscott Press; integration of art and craft; anti-industrial aesthetic
  - Realism (~1840–1880)
    - Rejection of Romantic idealization; depiction of contemporary life, ordinary people, labor
    - Gustave Courbet (1819–1877): A Burial at Ornans (1849–50); The Stone Breakers (1849); The Painter's Studio (1855 — "real allegory"); self-proclaimed Realist; Salon des Refusés
    - Jean-François Millet (1814–1875): The Gleaners (1857); The Angelus (1857–59); peasant subjects; Barbizon School
    - Honoré Daumier (1808–1879): political caricature/lithography; Third-Class Carriage; social criticism
    - Rosa Bonheur (1822–1899): The Horse Fair (1852–55); animalier; cross-dressing to study anatomy at horse fairs
    - Russian Realism: Wanderers (Peredvizhniki) movement — Ilya Repin (Barge Haulers on the Volga, 1870–73; Ivan the Terrible and His Son, 1885); Vasily Surikov
    - American Realism: Winslow Homer (seascapes); Thomas Eakins (The Gross Clinic, 1875 — surgical realism); Ashcan School precursors
  - Impressionism (~1860–1886)
    - Context: invention of portable paint tubes; plein air painting; photography challenge to representational painting; Japanese ukiyo-e influence (Japonisme); Haussmann's Paris
    - Claude Monet (1840–1926): Impression, Sunrise (1872 — name origin); Water Lilies series (Nymphéas, 250+ paintings, Orangerie installation); Rouen Cathedral series; Haystacks series; seriality and light
    - Pierre-Auguste Renoir (1841–1919): Dance at Le Moulin de la Galette (1876); Luncheon of the Boating Party; sensuous brushwork; later "Ingresque" period
    - Edgar Degas (1834–1917): ballet dancers; racecourses; pastels; sculpture (Little Fourteen-Year-Old Dancer, 1881); unusual compositions influenced by photography and Japanese prints; technically Realist who exhibited with Impressionists
    - Camille Pissarro (1830–1903): "dean of Impressionists"; mentor to Cézanne, Gauguin; Neo-Impressionism experiments
    - Berthe Morisot (1841–1895): first woman Impressionist; domestic subjects; loose brushwork
    - Mary Cassatt (1844–1926): American in Paris; mother and child; Japanese print influence; printmaking
    - Alfred Sisley (1839–1899); Gustave Caillebotte (1848–1894 — Paris Street; Rainy Day, 1877; patron-painter)
    - Exhibitions: 8 Impressionist exhibitions (1874–1886); dealer Paul Durand-Ruel; critical hostility (Louis Leroy's mocking review)
    - Édouard Manet (1832–1883): Olympia (1863); Le Déjeuner sur l'herbe (1863); A Bar at the Folies-Bergère (1882); "father of modern art" — flatness, visible brushwork, contemporary subjects; influence on but not formally part of Impressionism
  - Post-Impressionism (~1886–1905)
    - Term coined by Roger Fry (1910 exhibition); diverse artists building on and reacting against Impressionism
    - Paul Cézanne (1839–1906): Mont Sainte-Victoire series; The Card Players; Still Life with Apples; "treat nature by the cylinder, the sphere, the cone"; constructive brushstroke; passage (merged edges); "father of modern art" (Matisse, Picasso both claimed him); bridge to Cubism
    - Vincent van Gogh (1853–1890): The Starry Night (1889); Sunflowers; self-portraits; bedroom at Arles; expressive color and brushwork; 2100+ artworks in decade; mental illness; ear incident; suicide; only sold one painting in lifetime (myth partially); letters to Theo — crucial art documents
    - Paul Gauguin (1848–1903): Vision After the Sermon (1888); Where Do We Come From? What Are We? Where Are We Going? (1897); Tahiti works; Cloisonnism; Synthetism; Primitivism; Post-Impressionism → Symbolism → early Modernism
    - Georges Seurat (1859–1891): A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte (1884–86); Pointillism/Divisionism; Neo-Impressionism; optical color mixing; scientific approach to color (Chevreul, Rood, Ogden)
    - Paul Signac (1863–1935): D'Eugène Delacroix au Néo-Impressionnisme (1899) — theory of Neo-Impressionism
    - Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec (1864–1901): Moulin Rouge posters; Parisian nightlife; lithographic poster as art form; Japanese print influence
  - Symbolism (~1880–1910)
    - Literary roots: Baudelaire, Mallarmé, Verlaine; rejection of positivism and materialism; dreams, mythology, the occult
    - Gustave Moreau (1826–1898): Salomé; Jupiter and Semele; visionary, jewel-like surfaces
    - Odilon Redon (1840–1916): Noirs (charcoal/lithograph); later pastel visions; one eye floating; dreamscapes
    - Pierre Puvis de Chavannes (1824–1898): mural painting; Sacred Grove; flattened, archaic style
    - Fernand Khnopff (1858–1921) — Belgian Symbolism; The Caress
    - Gustav Klimt (1862–1918): The Kiss (1907–08); Portrait of Adele Bloch-Bauer I; gold leaf; Vienna Secession (founded 1897); Beethoven Frieze; ornamental sensuality
    - Edvard Munch (1863–1944): The Scream (1893); Madonna; The Frieze of Life; existential anxiety; proto-Expressionism
    - James Ensor (1860–1949): Entry of Christ into Brussels (1889); masks; macabre carnival
    - Art Nouveau (~1890–1910): total design; organic/whiplash curves; Alphonse Mucha (posters); Émile Gallé (glass); Hector Guimard (Paris Métro entrances); Antoni Gaudí (Sagrada Família, Park Güell); Tiffany (stained glass lamps); Vienna Secession; Jugendstil; Modernisme; Glasgow School (Charles Rennie Mackintosh)

- Early 20th Century Modernism (~1900–1945)
  - Fauvism (~1905–1908)
    - Henri Matisse (1869–1954): Woman with a Hat (1905); The Dance (1910); Joy of Life; Red Studio (1911); late cut-outs (Jazz, 1947; Chapel of the Rosary, Vence, 1951); "color used for color's sake"; lifelong pursuit of decorative harmony; rival/friend of Picasso
    - André Derain: London Bridge; Fauvist landscapes
    - Maurice de Vlaminck; Raoul Dufy; Kees van Dongen
    - Salon d'Automne 1905: critic Louis Vauxcelles — "Donatello parmi les fauves!" ("among the wild beasts")
  - Expressionism (~1905–1930)
    - Die Brücke (1905–1913, Dresden→Berlin): Ernst Ludwig Kirchner (Street, Berlin, 1913); Erich Heckel; Karl Schmidt-Rottluff; Emil Nolde; raw emotion; angular forms; woodcut revival; influence of African/Oceanic art
    - Der Blaue Reiter (1911–1914, Munich): Wassily Kandinsky (Composition VII, 1913 — pathway to abstraction); Franz Marc (Blue Horse); August Macke; Gabriele Münter; Paul Klee (associated); almanac Der Blaue Reiter (1912)
    - Egon Schiele (1890–1918): tortured self-portraits; raw eroticism; angular line
    - Oskar Kokoschka (1886–1980): Bride of the Wind; psychological portraiture
    - German Expressionist film connection: The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari (1920); Nosferatu (1922) — visual arts ↔ cinema
    - Neue Sachlichkeit (New Objectivity, ~1920s): Otto Dix (Triptych Der Krieg, 1929–32); George Grosz (political satire); Max Beckmann (triptychs); Weimar Republic disillusion
  - Cubism (~1907–1925)
    - Proto-Cubism: Cézanne's geometric landscapes; African/Iberian sculpture
    - Pablo Picasso (1881–1973): Les Demoiselles d'Avignon (1907 — revolutionary break); Guernica (1937 — anti-war masterpiece); Blue Period, Rose Period, Cubism, Neoclassicism, Surrealism; 50,000+ works; Weeping Woman; most influential artist of 20th century
    - Georges Braque (1882–1963): Houses at l'Estaque (1908, Cubist landscapes); papier collé invention; collaboration with Picasso 1908–14 ("roped together like mountaineers")
    - Analytic Cubism (~1909–12): fragmented forms; monochrome palette; multiple viewpoints; near-abstraction
    - Synthetic Cubism (~1912–25): collage; papier collé; brighter color; flatter forms; Picasso's Still Life with Chair Caning (1912 — first collage)
    - Juan Gris (1887–1927): systematic, colorful Cubism; Still Life with Checked Tablecloth
    - Fernand Léger (1881–1955): Tubism; machine aesthetic; The City (1919); mural painting
    - Cubist sculpture: Alexander Archipenko; Jacques Lipchitz; Henri Laurens; Julio González
    - Orphism: Robert Delaunay (Simultaneous Windows, 1912); Sonia Delaunay — color abstraction
  - Futurism (~1909–1944)
    - Filippo Tommaso Marinetti: Futurist Manifesto (1909) — speed, technology, violence, destruction of museums
    - Umberto Boccioni (1882–1916): Unique Forms of Continuity in Space (1913); States of Mind triptych; Futurist painting and sculpture manifestos
    - Giacomo Balla: Dynamism of a Dog on a Leash (1912); Abstract Speed + Sound
    - Carlo Carrà; Gino Severini; Luigi Russolo (noise music — intonarumori)
    - Russian Futurism: connections to Cubo-Futurism, Constructivism
  - Suprematism & Constructivism (~1913–1930s)
    - Kazimir Malevich (1879–1935): Black Square (1915) — "zero degree of painting"; White on White (1918); Suprematist Composition
    - El Lissitzky (1890–1941): Proun compositions; Beat the Whites with the Red Wedge (1919 — political graphic art); exhibition design
    - Vladimir Tatlin (1885–1953): Monument to the Third International (1919–20, model — never built; Constructivist icon); corner counter-reliefs
    - Alexander Rodchenko (1891–1956): photography; graphic design; productivist art
    - Liubov Popova; Varvara Stepanova; Naum Gabo (kinetic sculpture); Antoine Pevsner
    - Constructivism: art in service of revolution; collapse into Socialist Realism under Stalin (~1934)
  - De Stijl / Neoplasticism (~1917–1931)
    - Piet Mondrian (1872–1944): Composition with Red, Blue, and Yellow (1930); Broadway Boogie-Woogie (1942–43); evolution from naturalism through Cubism to pure abstraction; primary colors + black lines + white ground; Theosophical underpinning
    - Theo van Doesburg (1883–1931): De Stijl journal founder; introduced diagonal (Elementarism) — broke with Mondrian
    - Gerrit Rietveld: Rietveld Schröder House (1924); Red and Blue Chair — architecture as De Stijl art
  - Dadaism (~1916–1924)
    - Origins: Cabaret Voltaire, Zurich 1916 (Hugo Ball, Emmy Hennings, Tristan Tzara, Jean Arp, Richard Huelsenbeck); reaction to WWI absurdity
    - Marcel Duchamp (1887–1968): Nude Descending a Staircase, No. 2 (1912); readymades: Fountain (1917 — urinal signed "R.Mutt"; most influential artwork of 20th century per surveys); Bicycle Wheel (1913); The Bride Stripped Bare by Her Bachelors, Even (The Large Glass, 1915–23); Étant donnés (1946–66, posthumously revealed); challenged definition of art itself
    - Man Ray (1890–1976): Rayographs (photograms); Gift (iron with tacks); Ingres's Violin
    - Hannah Höch (1889–1978): Cut with the Kitchen Knife (1919–20) — photomontage; feminist Dada
    - Kurt Schwitters (1887–1948): Merzbau (environmental installation); Merz collages
    - Francis Picabia (1879–1953): machine drawings; proto-conceptual
    - Berlin Dada: George Grosz; John Heartfield (photomontage as political weapon); Raoul Hausmann
    - New York Dada: Duchamp, Man Ray, Baroness Elsa von Freytag-Loringhoven (recently re-attributed as possible author of Fountain)
  - Bauhaus (1919–1933)
    - Founded by Walter Gropius in Weimar (1919); moved to Dessau (1925), Berlin (1932); closed under Nazi pressure 1933
    - Faculty: Kandinsky, Klee, Moholy-Nagy, Josef Albers, Johannes Itten (Vorkurs), Oskar Schlemmer, Marcel Breuer, Anni Albers (textiles), Marianne Brandt (metalwork)
    - Principles: unity of art, craft, technology; Gesamtkunstwerk; functional design; typography; stage design
    - Legacy: International Style architecture; industrial design; graphic design; art education (foundation course); emigration to US (Black Mountain College, IIT Chicago — Mies van der Rohe, Moholy-Nagy's New Bauhaus/IIT Institute of Design)
  - Surrealism (~1924–1960s)
    - André Breton: Surrealist Manifesto (1924) — automatic writing; Freudian unconscious; "pure psychic automatism"
    - Salvador Dalí (1904–1989): The Persistence of Memory (1931); paranoiac-critical method; Lobster Telephone; Un Chien Andalou (1929, with Buñuel); showmanship
    - René Magritte (1898–1967): The Treachery of Images ("Ceci n'est pas une pipe," 1929); The Son of Man (1964); philosophical wit; word-image disjunction
    - Max Ernst (1891–1976): frottage; grattage; The Elephant Celebes (1921); Europe After the Rain II; collage novels (Une Semaine de Bonté, 1934)
    - Joan Miró (1893–1983): The Tilled Field (1923–24); Constellations series; biomorphic abstraction; playfulness
    - Yves Tanguy (1900–1955): Indefinite Divisibility (1942); abstract dreamscapes
    - Alberto Giacometti (1901–1966): early Surrealist objects (Palace at 4 a.m.); later existentialist figures (Walking Man, 1960)
    - Meret Oppenheim (1913–1985): Object (Fur Teacup, 1936)
    - Leonora Carrington (1917–2011); Remedios Varo (1908–1963); Dorothea Tanning — women Surrealists, recently re-evaluated
    - Frida Kahlo (1907–1954): The Two Fridas (1939); self-portraits; often associated with Surrealism but resisted label ("I paint my own reality")
    - Surrealist techniques: automatic drawing; exquisite corpse; decalcomania; fumage; found objects
  - American Scene & Social Realism (~1920s–1940s)
    - Regionalism: Grant Wood (American Gothic, 1930); Thomas Hart Benton (murals); John Steuart Curry
    - Social Realism: Ben Shahn; Jacob Lawrence (Migration Series, 1940–41); Diego Rivera, José Clemente Orozco, David Alfaro Siqueiros (Mexican Muralists — art as revolutionary politics)
    - Harlem Renaissance visual arts: Aaron Douglas (Aspects of Negro Life, 1934); Augusta Savage; Romare Bearden (later associated with collage)
    - WPA Federal Art Project (1935–43): murals in post offices, public buildings; employment of artists during Great Depression
    - Edward Hopper (1882–1967): Nighthawks (1942); psychological isolation; American urban/rural solitude
    - Georgia O'Keeffe (1887–1986): flower paintings (Black Iris III, 1926); New Mexico landscapes; skull paintings; first woman to have retrospective at MoMA (1946)

- Post-WWII — Late 20th Century Art (~1945–2000)
  - Abstract Expressionism (~1943–1965)
    - Context: New York replaces Paris as art-world center; émigré artists (Mondrian, Ernst, Breton in NYC); Peggy Guggenheim's Art of This Century gallery; Clement Greenberg criticism; CIA covert promotion during Cold War (revisionist thesis — Frances Stonor Saunders, The Cultural Cold War, 1999)
    - Action Painting / Gestural Abstraction
      - Jackson Pollock (1912–1956): drip/pour technique from 1947 (Number 1A, 1948; Autumn Rhythm, 1950); "Jack the Dripper"; Hans Namuth films; alcoholism; car crash death; Greenberg's champion
      - Willem de Kooning (1904–1997): Woman I (1950–52); gestural figuration within abstraction; Excavation (1950)
      - Franz Kline (1910–1962): monumental black-and-white gestural paintings (Mahoning, 1956)
      - Lee Krasner (1908–1984): large-scale abstractions; Little Image series; long undervalued as "Mrs. Pollock"
    - Color Field Painting
      - Mark Rothko (1903–1970): luminous color rectangles (No. 61 (Rust and Blue), 1953); Rothko Chapel (Houston, 1971 — posthumous); "tragedy, ecstasy, and doom"; suicide 1970
      - Barnett Newman (1905–1970): Vir Heroicus Sublimis (1950–51) — 18-foot red field with "zips"; Stations of the Cross series; Onement I (1948); concept of the Sublime in abstraction
      - Clyfford Still (1904–1980): jagged, flame-like abstractions; refused commercial gallery system; Clyfford Still Museum (Denver, 2011)
      - Helen Frankenthaler (1928–2011): Mountains and Sea (1952) — soak-stain technique; bridge to Color Field; Morris Louis, Kenneth Noland influenced
    - Other AbEx figures: Robert Motherwell (Elegies to the Spanish Republic); Adolph Gottlieb (Bursts); Philip Guston (late figurative turn — hooded figures, cartoon forms; massively influential on 21st c. painting)
    - Harold Rosenberg: "The American Action Painters" (1952) — act of painting as existential event
    - Clement Greenberg: "Modernist Painting" (1960); medium specificity; flatness; formal purity; most influential modernist critic
  - Post-Painterly Abstraction & Hard-Edge
    - Morris Louis (1912–1962): Unfurleds; Veils — soak-stain on raw canvas
    - Kenneth Noland (1924–2010): concentric circles; chevrons
    - Frank Stella (b. 1936): Black Paintings (1958–60) — "What you see is what you see"; Irregular Polygons; Protractor series; later exuberant 3D reliefs
    - Ellsworth Kelly (1923–2015): shaped canvases; single-color panels; plant drawings
  - Pop Art (~1955–1970)
    - British origins: Richard Hamilton (Just what is it that makes today's homes so different, so appealing?, 1956); Eduardo Paolozzi (I was a Rich Man's Plaything, 1947); Independent Group at ICA London
    - Andy Warhol (1928–1987): Campbell's Soup Cans (1962); Marilyn Diptych (1962); Silver Clouds; The Factory; Screen Tests; Interview magazine; Brillo Boxes; "fifteen minutes of fame"; silkscreen as mechanical reproduction; death and disaster series; most commercially influential artist of late 20th c.
    - Roy Lichtenstein (1923–1997): Whaam! (1963); Drowning Girl; Ben-Day dots; comic strip appropriation; art about art
    - Claes Oldenburg (b. 1929): soft sculptures (Floor Burger); Coosje van Bruggen collaborations; monumental public sculptures (Clothespin, Spoonbridge and Cherry)
    - James Rosenquist (1933–2017): F-111 (1964–65) — 86-foot billboard-scale painting
    - Tom Wesselmann: Great American Nude series; still lifes with commercial imagery
    - Japanese Pop: Yayoi Kusama (infinity rooms begun 1965; polka dots as obsessive motif; current international phenomenon — most visited artist worldwide 2023); Takashi Murakami (Superflat, 2000)
  - Minimalism (~1960–1975)
    - Reduction to essential geometric forms; industrial materials; rejection of expression and illusion
    - Donald Judd (1928–1994): "Specific Objects" (1965 essay); stacked box sculptures; Marfa, Texas permanent installations; Chinati Foundation
    - Dan Flavin (1933–1996): fluorescent light tube installations; Diagonal of Personal Ecstasy (1963); Untitled (to you, Heiner, with admiration and affection)
    - Carl Andre (b. 1935): Equivalent VIII (Tate "bricks" controversy, 1976); floor sculptures; modular repetition
    - Sol LeWitt (1928–2007): wall drawings (instructions-based — conceptual leap); open modular structures; "Paragraphs on Conceptual Art" (1967)
    - Robert Morris (1931–2018): "Notes on Sculpture" (1966); felt pieces; anti-form transition
    - Agnes Martin (1912–2004): grid paintings; delicate pencil lines on pale wash; Minimalism ↔ transcendence
    - Anne Truitt (1921–2004): painted wooden columns; color and memory
    - Richard Serra (b. 1938): Tilted Arc (1981, removed 1989 — public art controversy); torqued ellipses; monumental steel
  - Conceptual Art (~1965–present)
    - Idea over object; art as proposition; language-based; dematerialization of art object (Lucy Lippard, Six Years, 1973)
    - Joseph Kosuth (b. 1945): One and Three Chairs (1965) — actual chair, photograph, dictionary definition
    - Lawrence Weiner (1942–2021): text-based wall declarations; "language + the materials referred to"
    - On Kawara (1932–2014): Today series (date paintings); I Got Up postcards; conceptual time/existence projects
    - John Baldessari (1931–2020): text-and-image works; Cremation Project (1970, burnt all paintings); influence on CalArts graduates (Pictures Generation)
    - Art & Language group (UK, 1968–): collaborative conceptual project; journal Art-Language
    - Institutional Critique: Marcel Broodthaers (Musée d'Art Moderne, Département des Aigles, 1968–72); Hans Haacke (MoMA Poll, 1970; exposing corporate/museum ties); Daniel Buren (striped paintings as site-specific critique); Andrea Fraser (Museum Highlights, 1989); Fred Wilson (Mining the Museum, 1992)
  - Fluxus (~1960–1978)
    - George Maciunas; Yoko Ono (Cut Piece, 1964; Instruction Paintings); Nam June Paik (video art pioneer — TV Buddha, 1974); Joseph Beuys (How to Explain Pictures to a Dead Hare, 1965; social sculpture; I Like America and America Likes Me, 1974 — coyote performance); Wolf Vostell; Dick Higgins; intermedia; happenings; anti-art events
  - Land Art / Earthworks (~1968–present)
    - Robert Smithson (1938–1973): Spiral Jetty (Great Salt Lake, Utah, 1970); "entropy"; non-sites (gallery ↔ remote site dialectic)
    - Michael Heizer (b. 1944): Double Negative (1969–70); City (Nevada, begun 1972, opened 2022 — largest artwork ever)
    - Walter De Maria (1935–2013): The Lightning Field (New Mexico, 1977)
    - James Turrell (b. 1943): Roden Crater (Arizona, ongoing since 1979 — naked-eye observatory); Skyspaces; perception and light
    - Nancy Holt (1938–2014): Sun Tunnels (1973–76)
    - Christo and Jeanne-Claude: Wrapped Reichstag (1995); The Gates (Central Park, 2005); Running Fence; L'Arc de Triomphe, Wrapped (2021, posthumous)
    - Andy Goldsworthy (b. 1956): ephemeral natural sculptures; photography as documentation; Rivers and Tides documentary
  - Feminist Art (~1960s–present)
    - Judy Chicago (b. 1939): The Dinner Party (1974–79) — 39 place settings honoring women in history; founding Feminist Art Program at CalArts (1971, with Miriam Schapiro); Womanhouse (1972)
    - Carolee Schneemann (1939–2019): Meat Joy (1964); Interior Scroll (1975); body-as-material
    - Ana Mendieta (1948–1985): Silueta series — earth-body works; Cuban-American identity; mysterious death
    - Cindy Sherman (b. 1954): Untitled Film Stills (1977–80) — self as every "woman in film"; History Portraits; constructed identity
    - Barbara Kruger (b. 1945): Untitled (Your Body Is a Battleground) (1989); text-over-image; consumer critique
    - Guerrilla Girls (formed 1985): "Do women have to be naked to get into the Met. Museum?"; anonymous activism; statistics-based protest art
    - Tracey Emin (b. 1963): My Bed (1998); confessional art; Young British Artists context
    - Kara Walker (b. 1969): black-paper silhouette panoramas (Gone, 2004); race, gender, power, violence in American history; A Subtlety (2014, Domino Sugar Factory)
  - Performance Art
    - Origins in Futurist/Dada events; Gutai group (Japan, 1954 — Shiraga painting with feet; Murakami bursting through paper); Allan Kaprow Happenings (1959)
    - Marina Abramović (b. 1946): Rhythm 0 (1974 — audience given 72 objects); The Artist Is Present (MoMA, 2010 — 736 hours); endurance and body limits
    - Vito Acconci (1940–2017): Following Piece (1969); Seedbed (1972)
    - Chris Burden (1946–2015): Shoot (1971 — shot in arm); Trans-fixed (1974 — nailed to VW Beetle)
    - Tehching Hsieh (b. 1950): One Year Performances (1978–86) — punching time clock every hour for year; outdoor piece; rope tied to Linda Montano
    - Tino Sehgal (b. 1976): constructed situations; no documentation allowed; Turner Prize nominee
  - Neo-Expressionism (~1975–1990)
    - Return to figuration and emotional content after Minimalism/Conceptualism
    - Jean-Michel Basquiat (1960–1988): SAMO graffiti → gallery star; Untitled (1982, skull — sold $110.5 million in 2017); Irony of the Negro Policeman; neo-expressionism meets hip-hop, race, art history; collaboration with Warhol
    - Julian Schnabel (b. 1951): plate paintings; bombastic scale
    - Georg Baselitz (b. 1938): inverted figures; raw paint handling
    - Anselm Kiefer (b. 1945): massive mixed-media paintings (Margarete, 1981); German history/mythology; lead books; scorched earth; straw and ash; ongoing monumental installations (La Ribaute)
    - Sigmar Polke (1941–2010): "Capitalist Realism"; chemical painting experiments; Raster dots
    - Francesco Clemente; Sandro Chia; Enzo Cucchi — Italian Transavanguardia (Achille Bonito Oliva, 1979)
    - David Salle (b. 1952): layered appropriated imagery
  - Photography as Fine Art (selected milestones)
    - Pioneers: Nicéphore Niépce (first photograph, ~1826); Louis Daguerre (daguerreotype, 1839); William Henry Fox Talbot (calotype/negative-positive process); wet plate/dry plate evolution
    - Pictorialism: Alfred Stieglitz (Camera Work journal; The Steerage, 1907; Photo-Secession; 291 Gallery); Edward Steichen
    - Straight Photography: Paul Strand; Edward Weston; Ansel Adams (Zone System; Moonrise, Hernandez); f/64 Group
    - Documentary: Lewis Hine (child labor); Dorothea Lange (Migrant Mother, 1936); Farm Security Administration (FSA) project; Walker Evans (American Photographs, 1938)
    - Street Photography: Henri Cartier-Bresson (The Decisive Moment, 1952); Robert Frank (The Americans, 1958); Garry Winogrand; Lee Friedlander; Diane Arbus (marginalized subjects); Vivian Maier (discovered posthumously 2007)
    - Color photography as art: William Eggleston (MoMA show 1976; Eggleston's Guide); Stephen Shore (Uncommon Places); Joel Meyerowitz; Saul Leiter
    - Conceptual/Postmodern photography: Bernd and Hilla Becher (typologies — industrial structures); Düsseldorf School: Andreas Gursky (Rhein II, 1999 — sold $4.3M 2011; 99 Cent, 1999); Thomas Struth; Thomas Ruff; Candida Höfer
    - Contemporary fine art photography: Jeff Wall (lightbox transparencies; staged tableaux); Gregory Crewdson (cinematic suburban uncanny); Wolfgang Tillmans (Turner Prize 2000 — first photographer); Nan Goldin (The Ballad of Sexual Dependency, 1985; Sackler activism); Zanele Muholi (South African LGBTQ+ portraiture)
  - Video Art & New Media
    - Nam June Paik: TV sculptures; first video synthesizer; Electronic Superhighway (1995)
    - Bill Viola (b. 1951): The Crossing (1996); Tristan's Ascension; Renaissance-scale video installations; emotion and spirituality
    - Bruce Nauman (b. 1941): neon text (The True Artist Helps the World by Revealing Mystic Truths, 1967); video surveillance corridors; Clown Torture; Golden Lion Venice 2009
    - Gary Hill; Stan Douglas; Tony Oursler; Pipilotti Rist (Pour Your Body Out, 2008); Hito Steyerl (digital politics: How Not to Be Seen, 2013)
    - Net art: Jodi.org; Olia Lialina; Rhizome.org archive
    - Interactive/digital installations: TeamLab (immersive digital art spaces, Tokyo/global); Random International (Rain Room, 2012)
  - Installation Art
    - Ilya Kabakov (1933–2023): The Man Who Flew into Space from His Apartment (1985); total installations; Soviet everyday
    - Olafur Eliasson (b. 1967): The Weather Project (Tate Turbine Hall, 2003 — artificial sun); Ice Watch (melting icebergs)
    - Ai Weiwei (b. 1957): Sunflower Seeds (Tate, 2010 — 100 million hand-painted porcelain seeds); Dropping a Han Dynasty Urn; political dissident art; surveillance/activism
    - Do Ho Suh (b. 1962): fabric architecture; Staircase-III; translucent rooms
    - Yayoi Kusama: Infinity Mirror Rooms (1965–present); immersive dot environments; pumpkins; global museum phenomenon
  - Young British Artists (YBAs, ~1988–2000s)
    - Freeze exhibition (1988, organized by Damien Hirst while student); Saatchi Gallery patronage
    - Damien Hirst (b. 1965): The Physical Impossibility of Death in the Mind of Someone Living (1991 — shark in formaldehyde); For the Love of God (2007 — diamond skull); spot paintings; pharmacy installations
    - Tracey Emin: My Bed; Everyone I Have Ever Slept With (1995)
    - Rachel Whiteread (b. 1963): House (1993 — concrete cast of interior of Victorian terraced house, demolished); Ghost; Holocaust Memorial, Vienna; Turner Prize 1993 (first woman)
    - Chris Ofili (b. 1968): The Holy Virgin Mary (1996 — elephant dung; Giuliani controversy); Turner Prize 1998
    - Sarah Lucas; Gary Hume; Marc Quinn (Self, 1991 — frozen blood head); Jake and Dinos Chapman
  - Global Contemporary Movements (~1980s–2000)
    - Chinese Contemporary: '85 New Wave; Political Pop (Wang Guangyi — Great Criticism series); Cynical Realism (Yue Minjun — laughing figures); Ai Weiwei; Cai Guo-Qiang (gunpowder art); Zhang Xiaogang (Bloodline); Xu Bing (Book from the Sky)
    - Middle Eastern/North African contemporary: Shirin Neshat (Women of Allah series; Turbulent, 1998); Mona Hatoum (Hot Spot; Present Tense)
    - Latin American: Hélio Oiticica (Tropicália, 1967); Lygia Clark (relational objects); Gabriel Orozco (La DS, 1993); Doris Salcedo (Shibboleth, Tate, 2007; Palimpsest, 2017 — water names of drowned migrants)
    - African contemporary: El Anatsui (Ghanaian, b. 1944 — recycled bottle-cap tapestries; In the World But Don't Know the World); William Kentridge (South African, b. 1955 — charcoal animation; apartheid themes; Refusal of Time); Yinka Shonibare (headless mannequins in African-print Victorian dress)
    - Indigenous Australian: Emily Kame Kngwarreye (1910–1996); Desert painting movement; dot painting traditions entering global art market from 1970s
    - South Asian contemporary: Subodh Gupta (stainless steel); Nalini Malani (video/shadow play installations); Bharti Kher (bindi works)

- 21st Century Art (~2000–Present)
  - Post-Internet Art: art made after the internet's cultural saturation; Cory Arcangel (Super Mario Clouds, 2002); Petra Cortright; Artie Vierkant ("The Image Object Post-Internet," 2010); Amalia Ulman (Instagram performance, Excellences & Perfections, 2014); DIS collective
  - Social Practice / Participatory Art: Theaster Gates (Dorchester Projects, Chicago — art as urban regeneration); Tania Bruguera (Immigrant Movement International); Rick Lowe (Project Row Houses, Houston); relational aesthetics continued (Nicolas Bourriaud); community engagement as medium
  - Figurative Painting Revival (21st c.): renewed interest after decades of conceptual/installation dominance; Kerry James Marshall (b. 1955 — Mastry, 2016–17; monumental Black figuration); Amy Sherald (Michelle Obama portrait, 2018); Kehinde Wiley (Barack Obama portrait, 2018; staged Old Master poses with Black subjects); Jordan Casteel; Njideka Akunyili Crosby; Lynette Yiadom-Boakye; Toyin Ojih Odutola; Nicole Eisenman; Michaela Yearwood-Dan; Cecily Brown; Dana Schutz — "zombie formalism" critique vs. genuine renewal debate
  - Digital Art, NFTs & Generative Art
    - Generative/algorithmic art: roots in 1960s computer art (Vera Molnár; Harold Cohen's AARON program, 1973–); Casey Reas and Ben Fry (Processing, 2001); Tyler Hobbs (Fidenza, 2021); Art Blocks platform
    - NFT boom (2021): Beeple (Mike Winkelmann) — Everydays: The First 5000 Days sold at Christie's for $69.3 million (March 2021); CryptoPunks; Bored Ape Yacht Club; market crash 2022; questions of environmental impact (proof-of-work), speculation, and artistic legitimacy
    - AI-generated art: DALL-E (OpenAI, 2021–); Midjourney; Stable Diffusion; Refik Anadol (machine-hallucination immersive environments, 2019–; Unsupervised, MoMA, 2022); controversy: Jason Allen's AI image wins Colorado State Fair competition (2022); copyright debates (Thaler v. Perlmutter, 2023 — AI cannot be author under US law); Holly Herndon and Mat Dryhurst (Spawning.ai); ethics of training data
    - VR/AR art: Acute Art platform; Olafur Eliasson VR works; virtual exhibitions (Serpentine Gallery virtual pavilions)
  - Immersive & Experiential Art: teamLab Borderless (Tokyo, 2018; relocated 2024); Meow Wolf (Santa Fe, 2016); Van Gogh immersive experiences (controversial — spectacle vs. art); James Turrell retrospectives
  - Climate & Ecological Art: Olafur Eliasson (Ice Watch); Maya Lin (What Is Missing?, 2009–); John Akomfrah (Purple, 2017 — six-screen video on climate); Tomás Saraceno (Aerocene — solar-powered flight); collectives (Forensic Architecture — spatial analysis of environmental violence; Cooking Sections — Climavore); Extinction Rebellion art activism; projection mapping on glaciers and forests
  - Art and Social Justice (2020s): Black Lives Matter and art response; museum decolonization debates; repatriation of looted objects (Benin Bronzes returns from Germany, UK, US, 2022–); Kara Walker, Theaster Gates, Arthur Jafa (Love Is the Message, the Message Is Death, 2016); protest art and murals; Sackler name removals from museums (2021–)
  - Frontier & Contemporary Research Directions
    - Artificial intelligence as creative collaborator vs. tool vs. threat: ongoing debate in art schools and journals (October, Artforum, e-flux); AAAI Creativity and AI workshops; ethics of scraping artists' work for training data; "prompt engineering" as creative practice?
    - Bio-art: Eduardo Kac (GFP Bunny, 2000 — fluorescent green rabbit); Suzanne Anker; living tissue sculptures; CRISPR as medium
    - Blockchain beyond NFTs: provenance tracking; decentralized autonomous organizations (DAOs) for collective art ownership; smart contracts for artist royalties
    - Neuroscience and art: neuroaesthetics (Semir Zeki, Inner Vision, 1999); measuring viewer response; Kandel, The Age of Insight (2012)
    - Conservation science: hyperspectral imaging of paintings; AI attribution/dating; XRF scanning; digital restoration debates (Ecce Homo "restoration" as cautionary tale, 2012)
    - Decolonizing art history: Global Art History (Hans Belting, Andrea Buddensieg — The Global Contemporary, 2013); World Art Studies; questioning Western canon; Indigenous curatorial sovereignty; Documenta 15 (2022, ruangrupa collective — non-Western collective curating; antisemitism controversy)
    - Art market: auction records (most expensive painting: Salvator Mundi attributed to Leonardo, $450.3M, 2017 — attribution still debated); mega-galleries (Gagosian, Hauser & Wirth, Pace, David Zwirner); art fairs (Art Basel, Frieze, Venice Biennale); NFT market collapse and recovery; financialization of art; freeport storage; inequality and access
    - Museum futures: digital twin museums; virtual access post-COVID; community-centered models; universal museum debate; climate actions (gallery temperature debates — National Gallery 2023 relaxation)

- Art Theory, Criticism & Aesthetics
  - Classical: Plato (art as imitation of imitation — mimesis — dangerous); Aristotle (katharsis; mimesis as natural/beneficial; Poetics); Horace (ut pictura poesis — "as is painting so is poetry")
  - Renaissance theory: Alberti (De Pictura, 1435 — istoria, composition, light); Leonardo (Trattato della Pittura — paragone: painting vs. sculpture); Vasari (Lives of the Artists, 1550/1568 — first art history; rinascita ("rebirth"); biography as art historical method; teleological model culminating in Michelangelo)
  - Enlightenment aesthetics: Kant (Critique of Judgment, 1790 — aesthetic judgment, disinterested pleasure, sublime, beautiful); Burke (sublime and beautiful, 1757); Baumgarten (coined "aesthetics," 1750); Hegel (art as manifestation of Absolute Spirit; "end of art" thesis); Lessing (Laocoön, 1766 — spatial vs. temporal arts)
  - 19th century criticism: John Ruskin (Modern Painters, 1843–60; championed Turner and Pre-Raphaelites; "truth to nature"); Walter Pater (Studies in the History of the Renaissance, 1873 — "art for art's sake"); Charles Baudelaire ("The Painter of Modern Life," 1863 — flâneur; modernity as subject)
  - Formalism: Heinrich Wölfflin (Principles of Art History, 1915 — pairs of formal categories: linear/painterly, plane/recession, closed/open, multiplicity/unity, clearness/unclearness); Alois Riegl (Kunstwollen); Roger Fry (formalist criticism in English; coined "Post-Impressionism"); Clive Bell (Art, 1914 — "significant form"); Clement Greenberg (medium specificity; flatness as painting's essence; "Avant-Garde and Kitsch," 1939; "Modernist Painting," 1960)
  - Iconography/Iconology: Aby Warburg (1866–1929) — Mnemosyne Atlas (image panels tracing motifs across centuries); pathosformel (pathos formula); Warburg Library → Warburg Institute (London); Erwin Panofsky (1892–1968): Studies in Iconology (1939) — three levels of meaning (pre-iconographic, iconographic, iconological)
  - Social history of art: Arnold Hauser (The Social History of Art, 1951); T.J. Clark (The Painting of Modern Life, 1985 — Manet in Haussmann's Paris); Marxist art history; Michael Baxandall (Painting and Experience in 15th-Century Italy, 1972 — "period eye")
  - Structuralism & Semiotics in art: Ferdinand de Saussure → Roland Barthes ("The Death of the Author," 1967; Mythologies); Umberto Eco; semiotics of visual culture
  - Postmodern art theory: Jean-François Lyotard (The Postmodern Condition, 1979); Jean Baudrillard (simulacra); Fredric Jameson (Postmodernism, or, The Cultural Logic of Late Capitalism, 1991); Douglas Crimp (Pictures generation; "The End of Painting," 1981); Hal Foster (The Return of the Real, 1996); Rosalind Krauss ("Sculpture in the Expanded Field," 1979; The Originality of the Avant-Garde, 1985); October journal
  - Feminist art theory: Linda Nochlin ("Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?" 1971 — foundational essay); Griselda Pollock (Vision and Difference, 1988); Laura Mulvey ("Visual Pleasure and Narrative Cinema," 1975); bell hooks; Amelia Jones
  - Postcolonial art theory: Edward Said (Orientalism's impact on visual culture); Homi Bhabha (hybridity); Gayatri Spivak; decolonizing the museum debate; Global South art writing
  - Relational aesthetics: Nicolas Bourriaud (Relational Aesthetics, 1998) — art as social encounter; critique by Claire Bishop ("Antagonism and Relational Aesthetics," 2004 — need for conflict, not just conviviality)
  - Object-Oriented Ontology and art: Graham Harman; speculative realism; things beyond human perception as subject
  - Key journals: October; Artforum; Art Bulletin; Burlington Magazine; e-flux journal; Third Text; Texte zur Kunst

- Materials, Techniques & Media (Cross-Period Reference)
  - Drawing: graphite, charcoal, chalk, conte crayon, silverpoint, pen and ink (quill, reed, steel nib), brush drawing; techniques: hatching, cross-hatching, stippling, blending, chiaroscuro drawing
  - Painting media: tempera (egg yolk binder, Byzantine/Early Renaissance); fresco (buon fresco — pigment on wet plaster; fresco secco — on dry); oil painting (van Eyck popularized; linseed, walnut, poppy oils; glazing, scumbling, impasto, alla prima/wet-on-wet); watercolor; gouache; acrylic (developed 1940s–50s; Liquitex 1955; fast-drying, versatile); encaustic (hot wax); spray paint; house paint (Pollock, Basquiat)
  - Printmaking: relief (woodcut, linocut); intaglio (engraving, etching, drypoint, mezzotint, aquatint); planographic (lithography — Senefelder, 1796); stencil (screenprint/serigraphy — Warhol); monotype; collagraph; digital printing (giclée)
  - Sculpture materials and processes: stone carving (marble, granite, limestone — subtractive); wood carving; bronze casting (lost-wax/cire perdue process); welded metal; assemblage; ready-made; installation; kinetic sculpture (Calder mobiles); site-specific; earthwork; 3D printing; resin/fiberglass
  - Ceramics & pottery: earthenware, stoneware, porcelain; wheel-throwing, hand-building (coil, slab, pinch); glazing chemistry; raku; kiln types (wood, gas, electric); studio pottery movement (Bernard Leach, Hamada Shōji; Lucie Rie; Peter Voulkos — Abstract Expressionist ceramics)
  - Glass: blown glass; stained glass (medieval); studio glass movement (Harvey Littleton, Dominick Labino, 1962); Dale Chihuly; kiln-formed/fused glass; Murano tradition
  - Textile arts: weaving (Anni Albers; Bauhaus textiles; Gunta Stölzl); tapestry (Bayeux Tapestry, ~1070; contemporary: El Anatsui); embroidery; quilting (Gee's Bend quilts — entered fine art canon 2002 Whitney exhibition); fiber art (Sheila Hicks; Magdalena Abakanowicz)
  - Photography technology: daguerreotype → calotype → wet collodion → dry plate gelatin → roll film (Kodak 1888) → 35mm (Leica 1925) → color film (Kodachrome 1935; Ektachrome) → digital sensors (Bayer filter CCD → CMOS); darkroom processes; Photoshop (1990); computational photography; smartphone photography revolution
  - Digital/new media: pixel art; vector art; 3D modeling (ZBrush, Blender, Maya); motion graphics; projection mapping; VR/AR creation tools; generative code (Processing, openFrameworks, TouchDesigner); machine learning image generation (GANs — StyleGAN; diffusion models — Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, Midjourney)

- Non-Western & Global Art Traditions (Supplementary Survey)
  - African art: Nok terracottas (~900 BCE); Ife bronze/terracotta heads (12th–14th c.); Benin bronzes (15th–19th c.); Kuba textiles (Congo); Shona stone sculpture (Zimbabwe); Makonde sculpture; Tingatinga painting (Tanzania); contemporary: El Anatsui, William Kentridge, Julie Mehretu, Ibrahim El-Salahi, Njideka Akunyili Crosby
  - East Asian art traditions
    - Chinese: landscape painting as highest genre; ink wash (shui-mo); Four Treasures of the Study; calligraphy; scholar-painter tradition; major painters: Gu Kaizhi (~345–406), Fan Kuan, Guo Xi, Ma Yuan, Zhao Mengfu, Ni Zan, Shen Zhou, Dong Qichang; Qing eccentric individualists (Bada Shanren, Shitao); 20th c.: Xu Beihong (Western-influenced); Qi Baishi; Zhang Daqian; contemporary revolution
    - Japanese: yamato-e; emakimono (scroll painting); Zen ink painting (sumi-e: Sesshū Tōyō); Rinpa school (Tawaraya Sōtatsu, Ogata Kōrin — Irises screens); ukiyo-e: Hishikawa Moronobu; Kitagawa Utamaro; Katsushika Hokusai (Great Wave, ~1831; Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji); Utagawa Hiroshige (Fifty-Three Stations of the Tōkaidō); influence on Impressionism/Post-Impressionism (Japonisme); Nihonga (modern Japanese painting); Gutai group; Mono-ha; Superflat (Murakami)
    - Korean: celadon ceramics (Goryeo, 10th–14th c.); Joseon literati painting; minhwa (folk painting); chaekgeori (trompe-l'œil book paintings); contemporary: Lee Ufan (Mono-ha → international career); Park Seo-Bo (Dansaekhwa — monochrome painting)
  - South & Southeast Asian art: Hindu temple sculpture (Khajuraho; Chola bronzes — Shiva Nataraja); Mughal miniature painting (Akbar → Jahangir → Shah Jahan courts); Rajput painting schools (Pahari, Rajasthani); Tibetan thangka painting; Thai, Cambodian, Javanese temple art (Borobudur reliefs, ~9th c.); Balinese painting; contemporary: Raqib Shaw, Subodh Gupta
  - Islamic art: (see Medieval section above for detail); calligraphy traditions; geometric tiling (Penrose-tile connection — Lu and Steinhardt, Science 2007); Iznik ceramics; Persian miniature; carpet weaving; contemporary: Shirin Neshat, Monir Shahroudy Farmanfarmaian (mirror mosaics)
