  - Pre-Columbian & Latin American: Olmec; Maya (murals of Bonampak, ~800 CE; painted ceramics); Aztec (Sun Stone); Inca (textiles/quipu — artistic objects); Moche portrait vessels; colonial art (Cuzco School); Muralism (Rivera, Orozco, Siqueiros); Frida Kahlo; Concrete art (Lygia Clark, Hélio Oiticica); Tropicália; contemporary: Gabriel Orozco, Doris Salcedo, Beatriz Milhazes, Os Gêmeos
  - Oceanic art: Polynesian tapa cloth; Maori carving (wharenui meeting houses); Aboriginal Australian art (dreamtime painting; Papunya Tula movement 1971–); Papua New Guinean haus tambaran spirit houses; Hawaiian featherwork; contemporary Indigenous art: Emily Kame Kngwarreye, Clifford Possum Tjapaltjarri, Vernon Ah Kee
  - Native North American: Northwest Coast (totem poles; Haida, Tlingit, Kwakwaka'wakw: formline design); Pueblo pottery (Maria Martinez, black-on-black); Navajo sand painting and weaving; birch bark biting; quillwork; beadwork; ledger art (Plains); contemporary: Jaune Quick-to-See Smith; Kent Monkman; Nicholas Galanin

- Museum Studies, Curation & the Art World
  - History of museums: Wunderkammer (cabinet of curiosities, 16th–17th c.); Ashmolean Museum (1683 — first public museum); Louvre opened to public (1793, Revolutionary France); British Museum (1753); Metropolitan Museum of Art (1870); MoMA (1929, Alfred H. Barr); Guggenheim (Frank Lloyd Wright, 1959); Centre Pompidou (1977, Piano + Rogers — inside-out architecture); Tate Modern (2000, Herzog & de Meuron conversion of Bankside Power Station)
  - Curatorial theory: "white cube" gallery (Brian O'Doherty, Inside the White Cube, 1976); site-specific installation; blockbuster exhibition model; biennial culture (Venice 1895, São Paulo, Documenta, Sharjah, Gwangju, Istanbul, Manifesta); curator as auteur (Harald Szeemann, Catherine David, Okwui Enwezor, Carolyn Christov-Bakargiev, ruangrupa)
  - Art market: auction houses (Christie's 1766, Sotheby's 1744); gallery system (Leo Castelli; Arne Glimcher/Pace; Larry Gagosian); art fairs (Art Basel 1970; FIAC; Frieze 2003); art investment funds; freeport storage; forgery and authentication (Beltracchi scandal; Knoedler Gallery scandal — Mark Landis, Glafira Rosales); provenance research
  - Conservation and restoration: preventive conservation; cleaning controversies (Sistine Chapel restoration 1980–99 — debate over removed "patina"); digital restoration; ethical frameworks (reversibility, minimal intervention); material science in conservation; climate control standards debate (2023 ICOM/National Gallery reassessment)
  - Repatriation and ethics: Elgin/Parthenon Marbles (ongoing Greek-British dispute); Benin Bronzes (German returns 2022; Smithsonian, Horniman Museum returns); Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act (NAGPRA, 1990; 2024 update strengthening tribal rights); Nazi-looted art restitution (Washington Principles 1998; Gurlitt trove 2012); universal museum vs. national patrimony debate
  - Art education: academic training (Royal Academy system; École des Beaux-Arts; Prix de Rome); Bauhaus model; Black Mountain College (Josef Albers, John Cage, Robert Rauschenberg, Buckminster Fuller); CalArts (Walt Disney endowed; feminist art program; conceptualism); contemporary MFA economy debate; online art education explosion post-2020

### Performing Arts

#### Music — Full Depth

- **Foundations of Music**
  - Definition and nature: organized sound in time; pitch, duration, timbre, dynamics, texture as fundamental parameters
  - Sound physics: frequency (Hz), amplitude, harmonics, overtone series; standing waves on strings and in air columns
  - Psychoacoustics: perception of pitch, loudness, consonance/dissonance; critical bands (Helmholtz, Plomp & Levelt 1965); auditory scene analysis (Bregman 1990)
  - Music cognition: expectation (Meyer 1956, Huron 2006 *Sweet Anticipation*); statistical learning; neural substrates of musical processing (Zatorre, Peretz)
  - Absolute pitch vs. relative pitch; amusia (congenital and acquired); beat-deafness
  - Debate: is music universal? Cross-cultural commonalities (Mehr et al. 2019 *Science*: song universals study); Savage et al. 2015 statistical universals
  - Evolutionary theories: sexual selection (Darwin, Miller 2000); social bonding (Dunbar 2012); mother-infant communication (Dissanayake); musilanguage (Brown 2000); auditory cheesecake (Pinker 1997)
  - Music and emotion: BRECVEMA model (Juslin 2013); chills/frisson; musical affect as intentional vs. merely felt

- **Music Theory — Fundamentals**
  - **Pitch and scales**
    - Note names, octave equivalence, enharmonic spellings
    - Intervals: minor second through perfect octave; compound intervals; interval quality (perfect, major, minor, augmented, diminished)
    - Scales: major (Ionian), natural minor (Aeolian), harmonic minor, melodic minor (ascending and descending forms)
    - Church modes: Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian, Ionian — medieval theory (Boethius, Guido of Arezzo)
    - Pentatonic scales (major and minor); whole-tone scale; chromatic scale
    - Octatonic (diminished) scale; hexatonic scale; bebop scales
    - Non-Western scales: maqam system (Arab/Turkish/Persian); raga system (Hindustani and Carnatic); gamelan pelog and slendro; Chinese pentatonic modes
    - Tuning and temperament: Pythagorean tuning (pure fifths); just intonation (Zarlino); meantone temperament; well temperament (Werckmeister, Kirnberger); equal temperament (12-TET); historical tuning debates (Barbour 1951); Bohlen–Pierce scale; 19-TET, 31-TET, 53-TET and other microtonal equal temperaments
    - Cent system (Alexander Ellis 1885); frequency ratios; the syntonic comma and Pythagorean comma

  - **Rhythm and meter**
    - Beat, tempo (BPM), pulse; rubato; agogic accent
    - Simple meters (2/4, 3/4, 4/4); compound meters (6/8, 9/8, 12/8); asymmetric/complex meters (5/8, 7/8, 11/8); additive meters (e.g., 2+2+3/8)
    - Polyrhythm: simultaneous conflicting metric patterns; 3 against 2, 4 against 3; West African polyrhythmic roots
    - Polymetric structures; metric modulation (Elliott Carter); tempo relationships
    - Syncopation; hemiola; cross-rhythm; displaced accents
    - Time-line patterns: West African bell patterns (standard pattern / Agbekor); clave (son and rumba); tresillo
    - Isorhythm (talea and color) in Ars Nova (Vitry, Machaut)
    - Tala in Indian music: additive time cycles (e.g., tintal = 4+4+4+4; rupak = 3+2+2); khali and sam
    - Free rhythm: unmeasured preludes; recitative; aleatoric rhythm; Messiaen's added values and non-retrogradable rhythms
    - Metric perception and entrainment: London 2012 *Hearing in Time*; dynamic attending theory (Large & Jones 1999)

  - **Harmony**
    - Triad construction: major, minor, augmented, diminished
    - Seventh chords: major 7, dominant 7, minor 7, half-diminished 7 (ø), fully diminished 7
    - Extended tertian chords: 9ths, 11ths, 13ths; chord voicings and spacing
    - Roman numeral analysis: diatonic function (I through vii°); primary triads (I, IV, V) and secondary triads
    - Functional harmony: tonic (T), predominant (PD), dominant (D); standard progressions (I–IV–V–I; I–vi–IV–V; ii–V–I)
    - Cadences: authentic (perfect and imperfect), half, plagal, deceptive, Phrygian half-cadence
    - Secondary dominants (V/V, V/vi, etc.); secondary leading-tone chords
    - Modulation: pivot chord, direct/phrase, chromatic, enharmonic; common-tone modulation
    - Chromatic harmony: Neapolitan sixth (♭II6); augmented sixth chords (Italian, French, German); chromatic mediants; omnibus progression
    - Borrowed chords (modal mixture/interchange); pedal tones; passing and neighbor tones; appoggiaturas; suspensions; escape tones; anticipations
    - Chorale harmonization: Bach chorales as pedagogical model (Riemenschneider 371 chorales)
    - Harmonic rhythm: rate of chord change; phrase rhythm; hypermetric analysis
    - Non-functional harmony: planing/parallelism (Debussy); quartal and quintal harmony; polychords; pandiatonicism

  - **Melody**
    - Conjunct vs. disjunct motion; melodic contour; arch shapes
    - Phrase structure: antecedent-consequent; sentence structure (presentation–continuation–cadence; Caplin 1998)
    - Motif, motive development: augmentation, diminution, inversion, retrograde, fragmentation, sequence
    - Melodic embellishment: neighbor tones, passing tones, turns, trills, grace notes, mordents, appoggiaturas
    - Thematic transformation (Liszt, Wagner); Leitmotif technique

  - **Texture**
    - Monophony: single melodic line (Gregorian chant prototype)
    - Heterophony: simultaneous variation of a single melody (common in Javanese gamelan, Middle Eastern music)
    - Homophony: melody + accompaniment; chorale style (homorhythmic homophony)
    - Polyphony: independent melodic lines (see Counterpoint below)
    - Micropolyphony: dense chromatic clusters of independent lines (Ligeti: *Atmosphères*, *Lontano*)

- **Music Theory — Advanced**
  - **Counterpoint**
    - Species counterpoint (Fux, *Gradus ad Parnassum* 1725): first through fifth species
    - Strict vs. free counterpoint; dissonance treatment rules
    - Renaissance counterpoint: Palestrina style (*stile antico*); rules of voice leading; modal counterpoint (Jeppesen 1939)
    - Baroque counterpoint: Bach as summit; invertible counterpoint at octave, tenth, twelfth; canon (strict imitation); fugue (subject, answer, countersubject, episode, stretto, augmentation, diminution)
    - *The Art of Fugue* (BWV 1080): encyclopedic exploration of fugal technique; unfinished final quadruple fugue
    - *Musical Offering* (BWV 1079): canons and ricercars on Frederick the Great's theme
    - Dissonant counterpoint (Charles Seeger 1930): inverted species rules (dissonance as norm)
    - Neo-Bachian counterpoint: Hindemith (*Ludus Tonalis*); Shostakovich (24 Preludes and Fugues Op. 87)

  - **Form and structure**
    - Binary form (AB; rounded binary); ternary form (ABA; da capo aria)
    - Sonata form: exposition (first theme, transition, second theme, closing), development, recapitulation, optional coda; Hepokoski & Darcy's Sonata Theory (2006): five types of sonata form; essential sonata closure (ESC); rotation
    - Rondo form: ABACA, ABACABA (sonata-rondo); refrain and episode
    - Theme and variations: continuous, sectional; passacaglia and chaconne (ground bass variants)
    - Strophic form; through-composed; verse-chorus (popular song); 12-bar blues; 32-bar AABA (Tin Pan Alley standard)
    - Ritornello form (Baroque concerto: Vivaldi); da capo aria (ABA with ornamented return)
    - Fugue as process (not rigid form): exposition, episodes, middle entries, stretto, final entry
    - Multi-movement forms: suite (allemande, courante, sarabande, gigue, optional galanteries); sonata cycle (fast–slow–[minuet/scherzo]–fast); symphony; concerto (fast–slow–fast; cadenza); string quartet
    - Cyclic form: thematic recurrence across movements (Franck, Berlioz *idée fixe*, Wagner Leitmotif integrated into symphonic structure)
    - Open and mobile forms: moment form (Stockhausen); Earle Brown open scores; Lutosławski's controlled aleatoricism
    - Process music: gradual phase shifting (Steve Reich *Piano Phase*); additive process (*Music for 18 Musicians*)
    - Formenlehre tradition: A. B. Marx, Czerny, Riemann, Rosen (*The Classical Style*, *Sonata Forms*), Caplin (*Classical Form* 1998), Hepokoski & Darcy (2006)

  - **Orchestration and instrumentation**
    - Berlioz *Grand traité d'instrumentation et d'orchestration modernes* (1844): first major treatise
    - Rimsky-Korsakov *Principles of Orchestration* (1913, posthumous): systematic approach to orchestral color
    - Samuel Adler *The Study of Orchestration* (standard modern textbook, 4th ed. 2016)
    - String family: violin, viola, cello, double bass; bowing techniques (arco, pizzicato, spiccato, sul ponticello, sul tasto, col legno, harmonics, tremolo, con sordino)
    - Woodwinds: flute/piccolo, oboe/English horn, clarinet/bass clarinet, bassoon/contrabassoon; extended techniques (multiphonics, flutter-tonguing, key clicks, whistle tones)
    - Brass: trumpet, French horn, trombone, tuba; mutes (straight, cup, harmon/wah-wah, plunger); stopped horn; extended techniques (multiphonics, split tones, quarter tones)
    - Percussion: pitched (timpani, marimba, vibraphone, xylophone, glockenspiel, celesta, tubular bells) and unpitched (snare drum, bass drum, cymbals, tam-tam, triangle, tambourine, wood block, claves, bongos, congas); orchestral percussion expanded since Berlioz
    - Keyboard: piano (inside-piano/prepared piano: Cage); organ; harpsichord; synthesizer integration (Penderecki, Boulez)
    - Harp: pedal mechanism, glissando, harmonics, bisbigliando
    - Voice in orchestral context: vocal writing conventions by voice type (soprano, mezzo, alto, tenor, baritone, bass); choral orchestration
    - Orchestral balance: Strauss, Mahler, Ravel as master orchestrators; Ravel's orchestration of Mussorgsky's *Pictures at an Exhibition* as case study
    - Timbre as structural parameter: Klangfarbenmelodie (Schoenberg Op. 16/3 "Farben"); spectralism (Grisey, Murail) treats timbre analysis as compositional basis

  - **Schenkerian analysis**
    - Heinrich Schenker (1868–1935): Viennese theorist; key works: *Harmonielehre* (1906), *Kontrapunkt* (1910/1922), *Der freie Satz* (1935)
    - Core concepts: Ursatz (fundamental structure) = Urlinie (fundamental line: 3̂–2̂–1̂, 5̂–4̂–3̂–2̂–1̂, or 8̂–…–1̂) + Bassbrechung (bass arpeggiation: I–V–I)
    - Structural levels: background (Hintergrund), middleground (Mittelgrund), foreground (Vordergrund)
    - Prolongation: neighbor note, passing tone, arpeggiation; Auskomponierung (composing-out of a chord)
    - Graphic notation: beams, slurs, stems, open noteheads = structural; filled noteheads = prolongational
    - Extensions and critiques: Forte & Gilbert *Introduction to Schenkerian Analysis* (1982); Cadwallader & Gagné (2011); Lerdahl & Jackendoff *A Generative Theory of Tonal Music* (1983) as cognitive parallel
    - Critiques: Eurocentrism (only applies to common-practice tonal music); ideological baggage (Schenker's racism/nationalism — Cook 2007, Ewell 2020); difficulty of application to post-tonal or non-Western music
    - Post-Schenkerian developments: application to popular music (Everett 2004); jazz Schenkerian analysis (Larson 2009); interaction with neo-Riemannian theory

  - **Post-tonal theory**
    - Atonality: dissolution of functional tonality (Schoenberg c. 1908: *String Quartet No. 2* Op. 10, *Drei Klavierstücke* Op. 11, *Pierrot lunaire* Op. 21)
    - Twelve-tone technique (dodecaphony): Schoenberg c. 1921; tone row, prime (P), retrograde (R), inversion (I), retrograde-inversion (RI); matrix (magic square)
    - Extensions: combinatoriality (Schoenberg, Babbitt); all-combinatorial hexachords; derived rows; trichordal arrays (Babbitt)
    - Total/integral serialism: serialization of pitch, duration, dynamics, articulation (Messiaen *Mode de valeurs et d'intensités* 1949; Boulez *Structures Ia* 1952; Stockhausen *Kreuzspiel* 1951; Babbitt *Three Compositions for Piano* 1947)
    - Pitch-class set theory: Allen Forte *The Structure of Atonal Music* (1973); pitch-class sets; normal order; prime form; interval-class vector; set-class catalog (Forte numbers); Z-relation; complement; subset/superset relations
    - Transformational theory: David Lewin *Generalized Musical Intervals and Transformations* (GMIT, 1987); GIS (Generalized Interval System); transformation graphs and networks
    - Neo-Riemannian theory: Cohn, Hyer; parsimonious voice leading among triads; P, L, R transformations; Tonnetz; hexatonic and octatonic systems; application to 19th-century chromaticism (Brahms, Wagner, Liszt, Schubert)
    - Klumpenhouwer networks (K-nets): isography among pitch-class sets
    - Contour theory: Marvin & Laprade, Morris; contour segments (cseg); contour equivalence classes

  - **Rhythm and meter — advanced theory**
    - Lerdahl & Jackendoff *A Generative Theory of Tonal Music* (GTTM, 1983): grouping, meter, time-span reduction, prolongational reduction; well-formedness and preference rules
    - Hasty *Meter as Rhythm* (1997): process-based view; projection
    - London *Hearing in Time* (2004/2012): many meters hypothesis; perceptual constraints; non-isochronous meters; empirical grounding
    - Cohn *Metric Manipulations in Haydn and Mozart* (2001); ski-hill graphs; metric conflict
    - Krebs *Fantasy Pieces* (1999): metric consonance vs. dissonance; displacement vs. grouping dissonance (D vs. G dissonance)

  - **Microtonality and alternative tuning**
    - Quarter-tone music: Charles Ives (*Three Quarter-Tone Pieces* 1924); Alois Hába (quarter-tone and sixth-tone opera *The Mother* 1931); Ivan Wyschnegradsky
    - Just intonation as compositional practice: Harry Partch (43-tone scale, custom instruments: *Genesis of a Music* 1949); La Monte Young (*The Well-Tuned Piano*); Ben Johnston (string quartets in extended JI)
    - Spectral microtonality: derived from overtone series analysis (Grisey, Murail, Haas)
    - Xenharmonic movement: Erv Wilson's combination product sets (CPS), Moment of Symmetry (MOS) scales; William Sethares *Tuning, Timbre, Spectrum, Scale* (1998/2005): matched timbres for inharmonic tunings
    - Microtonal electronic music: Sevish, Aphex Twin quartertone experiments, Amon Tobin; Lumatone isomorphic keyboard
    - Contemporary microtonalists: Georg Friedrich Haas (*in vain*, *Limited Approximations*); Hans Zender; Chaya Czernowin

  - **Spectralism**
    - Origins: IRCAM, Paris, 1970s; influence of acoustics research and real-time spectral analysis
    - Gérard Grisey (1946–1998): *Les espaces acoustiques* cycle (1974–85) esp. *Partiels* (1975): orchestral synthesis of trombone spectrum; temporal perception: *Tempus ex machina* (1982 article)
    - Tristan Murail (b. 1947): *Gondwana* (1980), *Désintégrations* (1982/83 with IRCAM electronics); frequency modulation and ring modulation as harmonic models
    - Hugues Dufourt: coined term "musique spectrale" (1979 article)
    - Kaija Saariaho (1952–2023): Finnish-French; axis of noise–tone as formal parameter; *L'amour de loin* (opera, 2000); *Graal théâtre* (violin concerto)
    - Georg Friedrich Haas: microtonality + spectralism; *in vain* (2000): 24 instruments, extended just intonation
    - Post-spectral: Rebecca Saunders, Raphaël Cendo (saturation), Yann Robin, Ashley Fure, Unsuk Chin
    - Techniques: spectral analysis (FFT) → transcription to acoustic instruments; slow-motion timbral transformations; inharmonic spectra; difference tones; spectral fusion/fission

- **Music History — Western Art Music**
  - **Ancient and early music (antiquity–500 CE)**
    - Music in ancient Mesopotamia: lyres of Ur (~2600 BCE); temple hymns; Hurrian Hymn No. 6 (~1400 BCE, Ugarit): oldest known melody with notation
    - Ancient Greek music theory: Pythagoras (ratios of consonance); Aristoxenus (*Elementa Harmonica*, empiricist rival to Pythagorean ratios); Ptolemy (*Harmonics*); Greek modes (Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian — not identical to medieval modes); Plato on music's ethical power (*Republic* Book III); Aristotle (*Politics* Book VIII)
    - Surviving Greek music: Delphic Hymns (128 BCE); Epitaph of Seikilos (~1st c. CE): oldest complete notated composition
    - Roman music: adoption of Greek theory; use in theater, military, gladiatorial games; hydraulis (water organ); tibia (double-pipe); Boethius (*De institutione musica* ~500 CE): transmitted Greek theory to medieval West; music as quadrivium (arithmetic of the ear)

  - **Medieval music (~500–1400)**
    - Gregorian chant (plainchant): monophonic, Latin liturgical; neumatic notation; modal system (8 church modes); Liber Usualis; origins myth (Pope Gregory I); actual development over centuries across Frankish/Roman traditions
    - Guido of Arezzo (~991–1033): solmization (ut-re-mi-fa-sol-la); staff notation; Guidonian hand; revolutionized sight-singing pedagogy
    - Hildegard of Bingen (1098–1179): visionary abbess; *Symphonia armonie celestium revelationum*; *Ordo Virtutum* (earliest morality play with music); wide melodic range; ecstatic, melismatic style
    - Early polyphony: organum (parallel, oblique, free); Musica enchiriadis (~900 CE); Winchester Troper (~1000)
    - Notre-Dame school (~1160–1250): Léonin (Magnus Liber Organi: organum duplum) and Pérotin (organum triplum, quadruplum: *Viderunt omnes*, *Sederunt principes*); rhythmic modes; discant style; clausulae; conductus
    - Ars Antiqua (~1250–1320): modal notation → mensural notation (Franco of Cologne, *Ars cantus mensurabilis* ~1280); motet (polytextual); Adam de la Halle (*Jeu de Robin et Marion* ~1284: secular dramatic work)
    - Ars Nova (~1320–1380): Philippe de Vitry (*Ars Nova* treatise ~1322): new rhythmic notation, isorhythm; Guillaume de Machaut (1300–1377): greatest 14th-c. composer; *Messe de Nostre Dame* (earliest complete polyphonic ordinary by single composer); secular chansons: ballade, rondeau, virelai; *Le Remède de Fortune*
    - Trecento (Italian Ars Nova): Francesco Landini (1325–1397): blind organist-composer; ballata, madrigal, caccia; Landini cadence
    - Ars Subtilior (~1380–1420): extreme rhythmic complexity; Baude Cordier (*Belle, bonne, sage*: heart-shaped notation); Solage; Philippus de Caserta
    - English music: John Dunstaple (c. 1390–1453): *contenance angloise*; sweet thirds and sixths; continental influence

  - **Renaissance music (~1400–1600)**
    - Franco-Flemish (Netherlandish) school: dominant tradition for ~150 years
      - Guillaume Du Fay (c. 1397–1474): *Nuper rosarum flores* (1436, Florence cathedral dedication; proportional structure debated); fauxbourdon; cyclic mass; blended medieval and Renaissance
      - Johannes Ockeghem (c. 1410–1497): Masses with extraordinary contrapuntal ingenuity; *Missa prolationum* (each voice in different mensuration simultaneously); *Requiem* (earliest surviving polyphonic setting); widely mourned at death (Josquin's *Nymphes des bois*)
      - Josquin des Prez (c. 1450–1521): "the Beethoven of the Renaissance"; mastery of text expression; *Ave Maria* ... *virgo serena*; *Missa Pange lingua*; imitative polyphony as standard texture; Luther: "Josquin is master of the notes"
      - Later Franco-Flemish: Adrian Willaert (founder of Venetian school); Orlando di Lasso / Lassus (1532–1594): over 2,000 works across all genres; *Prophetiae sibyllarum* (extreme chromaticism)
    - Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina (1525–1594): ideal of Counter-Reformation sacred polyphony; legend of *Missa Papae Marcelli* saving polyphony at Council of Trent; smooth voice leading, controlled dissonance; *stile antico* model for centuries
    - Venetian school: polychoral style (cori spezzati) at St. Mark's Basilica; Andrea Gabrieli, Giovanni Gabrieli (*Sacrae Symphoniae* 1597: first specified dynamic markings); spatial music
    - William Byrd (1543–1623): English master; Catholic in Protestant England; masses, motets, consort songs, keyboard works; *My Ladye Nevells Booke*
    - Thomas Tallis (c. 1505–1585): *Spem in alium* (40-part motet)
    - Carlo Gesualdo (1566–1613): extreme chromaticism in madrigals; harmonically ahead of his time by 300 years; infamous for murdering his wife and her lover
    - Madrigal: secular vocal, setting vernacular poetry; Italian (Marenzio, Monteverdi, Gesualdo) and English (Morley, Weelkes, Wilbye); madrigal comedy
    - Instrumental music emerging: ricercar, canzona, fantasia; lute music (John Dowland *Lachrimae*); keyboard (Frescobaldi)
    - Music printing: Ottaviano Petrucci (*Harmonice Musices Odhecaton* 1501): first major printed music collection; Pierre Attaingnant (single-impression printing)
    - Theory: Zarlino (*Le istitutioni harmoniche* 1558: just intonation, dual nature of major/minor); Glarean (*Dodecachordon* 1547: 12-mode system); transition from modal to tonal thinking

  - **Baroque music (~1600–1750)**
    - **Early Baroque (~1600–1650)**
      - Monody and basso continuo: Florentine Camerata (Bardi, Corsi, Vincenzo Galilei); stile rappresentativo; figured bass; thoroughbass realization
      - Birth of opera: Jacopo Peri (*Dafne* 1598: lost; *Euridice* 1600); Giulio Caccini (*Le nuove musiche* 1602: ornamentation treatise + songs)
      - Claudio Monteverdi (1567–1643): bridge from Renaissance to Baroque; *Orfeo* (1607: first great opera); *L'incoronazione di Poppea* (1643); seconda pratica (text over rules); nine books of madrigals; Vespers of 1610
      - Heinrich Schütz (1585–1672): greatest German composer before Bach; studied with Gabrieli and Monteverdi; *Symphoniae sacrae*; *Musikalische Exequien*; brought Italian style to Germany
      - Basso ostinato forms: chaconne, passacaglia, lament bass (descending tetrachord); Monteverdi *Lamento della ninfa*
    - **Middle Baroque (~1650–1700)**
      - Jean-Baptiste Lully (1632–1687): Italian-born; dominated French music; *tragédie en musique* (French opera); French overture; collaboration with Molière; control of Parisian musical life; died from conducting injury (staff hit foot)
      - Arcangelo Corelli (1653–1713): established trio sonata and concerto grosso as forms; *12 Concerti grossi* Op. 6; pure, balanced style; enormous influence on string playing and composition
      - Henry Purcell (1659–1695): England's greatest Baroque composer; *Dido and Aeneas* (opera, with "Dido's Lament" = lament bass); semi-operas (*The Fairy Queen*, *King Arthur*); anthems, fantasias, odes
      - Dieterich Buxtehude (c. 1637–1707): Lübeck organ master; *Abendmusik* concerts; Bach walked 250 miles to hear him
      - Oratorio development: Giacomo Carissimi (*Jephte*); Alessandro Stradella; sacred dramatic music outside stage
    - **Late Baroque (~1700–1750)**
      - Antonio Vivaldi (1678–1741): "the Red Priest"; *Le quattro stagioni* (*The Four Seasons*, from *Il cimento dell'armonia e dell'inventione* Op. 8); ~500 concertos; ritornello form codified; influenced Bach; opera composer (rediscovered 20th c.)
      - Johann Sebastian Bach (1685–1750): towering figure; synthesis of national styles (Italian, French, German); *Well-Tempered Clavier* (Books I and II: 48 preludes and fugues); *Brandenburg Concertos* (6); *Mass in B minor*; *St. Matthew Passion*, *St. John Passion*; *Goldberg Variations*; *The Art of Fugue*; *Musical Offering*; ~1,100+ surviving works; master of counterpoint, harmony, form; relatively obscure after death until Mendelssohn's 1829 revival of *St. Matthew Passion*; Bach Gesellschaft edition (1850–99); Neue Bach-Ausgabe (1954–2007); BWV catalog (Schmieder)
      - George Frideric Handel (1685–1759): German-born, Italian-trained, London career; *Messiah* (1742: most performed oratorio); *Water Music*, *Music for the Royal Fireworks*; Italian operas (*Giulio Cesare*, *Rinaldo*); turned to English oratorios when opera declined; HWV catalog
      - Georg Philipp Telemann (1681–1767): most prolific Baroque composer (~3,600+ works); overshadowed by Bach and Handel in later reception; *Tafelmusik*; innovative orchestral suites
      - Jean-Philippe Rameau (1683–1764): *Traité de l'harmonie* (1722): founding text of tonal harmonic theory (fundamental bass); operas (*Hippolyte et Aricie*, *Les Indes galantes*); querelle des Bouffons
      - Domenico Scarlatti (1685–1757): 555 keyboard sonatas; harpsichord virtuosity; Spanish influence (acciaccaturas mimicking guitar)
      - François Couperin (1668–1733): *Pièces de clavecin* (4 books); *L'Art de toucher le clavecin*; French Baroque keyboard art

  - **Classical period (~1750–1820)**
    - Style galant and Empfindsamer Stil (sensitive style): reaction against Baroque complexity; melody-dominant, lighter texture
    - C. P. E. Bach (1714–1788): *Essay on the True Art of Playing Keyboard Instruments* (1753/62); bridge figure; empfindsam style; improvisatory fantasias
    - Christoph Willibald Gluck (1714–1787): opera reform; *Orfeo ed Euridice* (1762); preface to *Alceste* (1769): simplicity, dramatic truth; swept away castrato-dominated opera seria conventions
    - Joseph Haydn (1732–1809): "father of the symphony" and "father of the string quartet" (though both overstatements); 104 symphonies (*Farewell*, *Surprise*, *Clock*, *London* symphonies); 68 string quartets (Op. 33 "Russian": "entirely new and special way"; Op. 76); *The Creation* (oratorio); wit, experimentation, formal innovation; Esterházy court; triumph in London 1791–95
    - Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart (1756–1791): child prodigy; unmatched melodic gift and dramatic instinct; operas (*Le nozze di Figaro*, *Don Giovanni*, *Così fan tutte*, *Die Zauberflöte*, *Idomeneo*); 41 symphonies (*Jupiter* K.551); 27 piano concertos (K.466, K.467, K.491, K.595); *Requiem* K.626 (unfinished, completed by Süssmayr); chamber music (string quintets, *Dissonance* Quartet K.465); Köchel catalog
    - Ludwig van Beethoven (1770–1827): pivotal figure linking Classical and Romantic; THREE PERIODS: Early (to ~1802): Op. 18 quartets, *Pathétique* Sonata; Middle/Heroic (~1803–14): Symphonies 3 (*Eroica*), 5, 6 (*Pastoral*), 7; *Waldstein* and *Appassionata* sonatas; *Razumovsky* quartets; *Emperor* concerto; Late (~1815–27): Symphonies 8, 9 (*Choral*); *Hammerklavier* Sonata Op. 106; *Diabelli Variations* Op. 120; Late Quartets (Op. 127, 130, 131, 132, 135: *Große Fuge* Op. 133); *Missa solemnis*; deafness (Heiligenstadt Testament 1802); expanded symphony, sonata, quartet to unprecedented scale and emotional depth
    - Other Classical figures: Boccherini (quintets); Clementi (piano pedagogy, sonatas); Hummel; Dussek; Salieri (Beethoven's teacher, not Mozart's poisoner)
    - Rise of public concert life: Concert des Amateurs, Gewandhaus Leipzig, Salomon concerts London; decline of court patronage; freelance musician model (Mozart's struggles)

  - **Romantic period (~1820–1900)**
    - **Early Romantic (~1820–1850)**
      - Franz Schubert (1797–1828): Lieder master (~600 songs: *Erlkönig*, *Die schöne Müllerin*, *Winterreise*, *Schwanengesang*); Symphony No. 8 (*Unfinished*); Symphony No. 9 (*Great C major*); string quintet D.956; late piano sonatas; trout quintet D.667
      - Carl Maria von Weber (1786–1826): *Der Freischütz* (1821): founding German Romantic opera; Wolf's Glen scene; nationalist folk elements
      - Hector Berlioz (1803–1869): radical orchestrator; *Symphonie fantastique* (1830: idée fixe, programmatic symphony); *Harold en Italie*; *Roméo et Juliette* (dramatic symphony); *Les Troyens* (epic opera); *Grande Messe des morts* (Requiem: massive forces); *Treatise on Instrumentation* (1844)
      - Felix Mendelssohn (1809–1847): precocious genius; *Octet* (age 16); *Overture to A Midsummer Night's Dream* (age 17); *Violin Concerto in E minor*; *Italian* and *Scottish* symphonies; *Elijah* (oratorio); revived Bach's *St. Matthew Passion* (1829); founded Leipzig Conservatory
      - Robert Schumann (1810–1856): piano cycles (*Carnaval*, *Kinderszenen*, *Kreisleriana*); *Dichterliebe* (song cycle); symphonies; championed Chopin and Brahms in *Neue Zeitschrift für Musik*; mental illness; Eusebius–Florestan dual identity
      - Frédéric Chopin (1810–1849): poet of the piano; nocturnes, études, preludes, ballades, scherzos, polonaises, mazurkas, waltzes; expanded piano technique and color; bel canto influence on melody; Parisian exile from partitioned Poland
      - Franz Liszt (1811–1886): greatest pianist of his era; invented solo piano recital; transcendental études; thematic transformation; symphonic poems (*Les Préludes*, *Hamlet*, *Tasso*); *Faust Symphony*; *Sonata in B minor*; late experimental works (Bagatelle sans tonalité; *Nuages gris*: anticipate atonality); generous promoter of others' music; Weimar years; Abbé Liszt (minor orders)
      - Niccolò Paganini (1782–1840): violin virtuoso; *24 Caprices*; inspired Liszt's pianism; legend of diabolical ability

    - **Middle/High Romantic (~1850–1880)**
      - Richard Wagner (1813–1883): opera reform → music drama (*Gesamtkunstwerk*: total art work); *Der Ring des Nibelungen* (14h tetralogy: *Rheingold*, *Walküre*, *Siegfried*, *Götterdämmerung*); *Tristan und Isolde* (1865: "Tristan chord" → dissolution of common-practice harmony); *Die Meistersinger von Nürnberg*; *Parsifal*; Leitmotif technique; endless melody; Bayreuth Festspielhaus (opened 1876); prose writings (*Das Kunstwerk der Zukunft*, *Oper und Drama*); antisemitic essay *Das Judenthum in der Musik*; enormous influence on all subsequent music
      - Giuseppe Verdi (1813–1901): Italian opera master; *Nabucco* (chorus "Va, pensiero": Italian nationalist symbol); *Rigoletto*, *Il trovatore*, *La traviata* (popular trilogy); *Aida*, *Otello*, *Falstaff* (late masterpieces); development from early galley years (pot-boiler operas) through Shakespearean depth; rivalry with Wagner (exaggerated); *Requiem*
      - Johannes Brahms (1833–1897): Classical-Romantic synthesis; 4 symphonies (No. 1 "Beethoven's Tenth"); *Ein deutsches Requiem*; piano concertos (No. 1 D minor, No. 2 B♭ major); violin concerto; chamber music (piano quartets, string sextets, clarinet quintet/sonatas); *Variations on a Theme by Haydn*; developing variation; War of the Romantics: Brahms (absolute music) vs. Wagner/Liszt (program music/New German School)
      - Anton Bruckner (1824–1896): massive symphonies (9 completed; No. 4 *Romantic*, No. 7, No. 8, No. 9 unfinished); influenced by Wagner; cathedral-like structures; "Bruckner Problem" (multiple versions edited by disciples: Haas vs. Nowak editions)
      - Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky (1840–1893): Russian melodist; Symphonies 4, 5, 6 (*Pathétique*); piano concerto No. 1; violin concerto; ballets (*Swan Lake*, *Sleeping Beauty*, *Nutcracker*); operas (*Eugene Onegin*, *Queen of Spades*); *1812 Overture*; death mystery (cholera vs. forced suicide debate)
      - The Mighty Five (*Moguchaya Kuchka*): Balakirev, Borodin, Cui, Mussorgsky, Rimsky-Korsakov — Russian nationalist school; Mussorgsky *Boris Godunov*, *Pictures at an Exhibition*; Rimsky-Korsakov *Scheherazade*, orchestration treatise; Borodin *Prince Igor* (*Polovtsian Dances*)
      - Bedřich Smetana (1824–1884): Czech father; *Má vlast* (*Vltava*/*Moldau*); *The Bartered Bride* (opera)
      - Antonín Dvořák (1841–1904): Czech Romantic; Symphony No. 9 *From the New World*; *Slavonic Dances*; Cello Concerto; *American* Quartet; New York tenure 1892–95; influence on American composers

    - **Late Romantic (~1880–1910)**
      - Gustav Mahler (1860–1911): symphony as world; 9 completed symphonies + unfinished 10th; *Das Lied von der Erde*; orchestral song cycles (*Kindertotenlieder*, *Rückert-Lieder*); Symphony No. 2 *Resurrection*, No. 3, No. 6 *Tragic*, No. 8 *Symphony of a Thousand*, No. 9 (farewell); conductor (Vienna Court Opera); prophetic: "My time will come"; Alma Mahler's role and diaries
      - Richard Strauss (1864–1949): tone poems (*Don Juan*, *Till Eulenspiegel*, *Also sprach Zarathustra*, *Ein Heldenleben*, *Tod und Verklärung*); operas (*Salome* 1905, *Elektra* 1909: extreme dissonance, then *Der Rosenkavalier* 1911: Mozartean retreat; *Ariadne auf Naxos*, *Capriccio*); *Vier letzte Lieder* (1948: autumnal valediction); master orchestrator; complex relationship with Nazism (Reichsmusikkammer president 1933–35)
      - Sergei Rachmaninoff (1873–1943): late Russian Romantic; Piano Concertos No. 2 and No. 3; *Rhapsody on a Theme of Paganini*; symphonies; *The Bells*; *Vespers* (All-Night Vigil); supreme pianist; emigrated 1917
      - Alexander Scriabin (1872–1915): Russian visionary; early Chopin influence; harmonic innovation: mystic chord (C-F♯-B♭-E-A-D); late works (*Poem of Ecstasy*, *Prometheus: Poem of Fire*); synesthesia; planned multi-sensory *Mysterium* (unfinished)
      - Hugo Wolf (1860–1903): Lied master; *Mörike-Lieder*, *Goethe-Lieder*, *Spanisches Liederbuch*, *Italienisches Liederbuch*; declamatory precision; mental collapse (syphilis)
      - Late Romantic opera: Puccini (*La bohème*, *Tosca*, *Madama Butterfly*, *Turandot*); verismo (Mascagni *Cavalleria rusticana*, Leoncavallo *Pagliacci*); Strauss (above)

  - **20th-century art music**
    - **Impressionism / early modernism (~1890–1920)**
      - Claude Debussy (1862–1918): rejected "Impressionist" label but parallels hold; *Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune* (1894: foundational modern work); *La Mer*; *Pelléas et Mélisande* (opera); *Préludes* for piano; whole-tone scales, planing, non-functional harmony; Javanese gamelan influence (1889 Exposition universelle)
      - Maurice Ravel (1875–1937): *Daphnis et Chloé*, *Boléro*, *Rapsodie espagnole*, Piano Concerto in G; orchestration genius; *Gaspard de la nuit*; *String Quartet*; precision, clarity; vs. Debussy comparison (unfair but persistent)
      - Erik Satie (1866–1925): provocateur; *Gymnopédies*, *Gnossiennes*; furniture music (*musique d'ameublement*: precursor to ambient); anti-Romantic; influenced Cage, Minimalists
      - Early Stravinsky: *The Firebird* (1910), *Petrushka* (1911), *The Rite of Spring* (1913: premiere riot; polyrhythm, polytonality, primitivism; most revolutionary orchestral work of 20th c.); Russian period ballets for Diaghilev's Ballets Russes
      - Béla Bartók (1881–1945): Hungarian; systematic ethnomusicological fieldwork with Kodály; synthesis of folk music and modernism; *Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta*; *Concerto for Orchestra*; string quartets (6: regarded as 20th c.'s greatest, alongside Shostakovich's 15); *Mikrokosmos* (153 piano pieces: graded); Fibonacci and golden section in formal proportions (Lendvai theory, debated); axis system; Bulgarian rhythms
      - Alexander Scriabin, Charles Ives (*The Unanswered Question*, *Concord Sonata*, *Three Places in New England*: polytonality, quotation, spatial music; predated European innovations but poorly known until mid-century)

    - **Expressionism and the Second Viennese School**
      - Arnold Schoenberg (1874–1951): free atonality (*Erwartung* 1909, *Pierrot lunaire* 1912: Sprechstimme); twelve-tone method (~1921: *Suite for Piano* Op. 25); *Moses und Aron* (opera, unfinished); *A Survivor from Warsaw*; also tonal works (*Verklärte Nacht*, *Gurrelieder*); emigrated to USA 1933; UCLA teaching
      - Alban Berg (1885–1935): student of Schoenberg; *Wozzeck* (opera 1925: atonal but dramatically gripping; Sprechgesang, Leitmotifs, traditional forms embedded); *Lulu* (unfinished: completed by Cerha 1979); *Violin Concerto* ("To the memory of an angel"; quotes Bach chorale); lyricism within serialism
      - Anton Webern (1883–1945): extreme conciseness; pointillism; Klangfarbenmelodie; *Symphony* Op. 21; *Concerto* Op. 24 (derived row); *Variations for Piano* Op. 27; entire output ~3 hours; enormous influence on post-WWII serialists (Boulez, Stockhausen); accidentally shot by American soldier 1945

    - **Neoclassicism (~1920–1950)**
      - Stravinsky's Neoclassical period: *Pulcinella* (1920: Pergolesi-based), *Symphony of Psalms*, *Oedipus Rex*, *Symphony in C*, *The Rake's Progress* (1951: Mozartean opera); "wrong-note" style; objectivity vs. Romantic expression
      - Paul Hindemith (1895–1963): *Mathis der Maler* (symphony and opera); *Ludus Tonalis*; tonal system based on overtone series (*Craft of Musical Composition*); Gebrauchsmusik (functional music)
      - Sergei Prokofiev (1891–1953): *Classical* Symphony; Piano Concerto No. 3; *Romeo and Juliet* (ballet); *Peter and the Wolf*; *War and Peace* (opera); *Alexander Nevsky*; Soviet context; Zhdanov decree 1948
      - Dmitri Shostakovich (1906–1975): 15 symphonies (No. 5 "reply to just criticism"; No. 7 *Leningrad*; No. 10, No. 13 *Babi Yar*, No. 14, No. 15); 15 string quartets; Lady Macbeth of Mtsensk (Pravda denunciation 1936: "Muddle Instead of Music"); *Testimony* (Volkov memoirs: authenticity controversy); yurodivy/holy fool interpretation; Soviet artist's dilemma; DSCH motif
      - Aaron Copland (1900–1990): American sound; *Appalachian Spring*, *Billy the Kid*, *Rodeo* (ballets); *Fanfare for the Common Man*; Symphony No. 3; film scores; also serial works (Piano Quartet, Connotations); teacher and advocate

    - **Post-WWII avant-garde (~1945–1975)**
      - Darmstadt School: Internationale Ferienkurse für Neue Musik (1946–): center of post-war European modernism
      - Pierre Boulez (1925–2016): total serialism (*Structures*); controlled complexity (*Le Marteau sans maître*, *Pli selon pli*); conductor (BBCSO, NYPO, Cleveland); IRCAM founder (1977); polemicist ("Schoenberg is dead" 1952); later works lusher (*Répons*, *...explosante-fixe...*)
      - Karlheinz Stockhausen (1928–2007): electronic music pioneer (*Gesang der Jünglinge* 1955/56: first masterpiece integrating electronic and voice; *Kontakte*); spatial music (*Gruppen* for 3 orchestras); moment form (*Momente*); intuitive music (*Aus den sieben Tagen*); *Stimmung* (1968: overtone singing); *Licht* (7-opera cycle: one per day of week; ~29 hours; 1977–2003)
      - John Cage (1912–1992): prepared piano (*Sonatas and Interludes*); chance operations (I Ching): *Music of Changes* (1951); *4'33"* (1952: any instrument(s), three movements of silence — most radical conceptual gesture in 20th-c. music); indeterminacy; Cage-Feldman conversation; Zen influence; number pieces (late)
      - Morton Feldman (1926–1987): graphic notation (early); very quiet, very slow, very long (late works: *For Philip Guston* ~4.5 hours; *String Quartet II* ~6 hours); floating, non-rhetorical sound; painter friends (Rothko, de Kooning, Guston)
      - Iannis Xenakis (1922–2001): architect-engineer-composer; stochastic music (*Metastasis* 1954: glissandi clouds → Philips Pavilion with Le Corbusier); *Pithoprakta*; *Persepolis* (electronic); game theory, set theory, Markov chains in composition; *Formalized Music* (1971/1992 treatise)
      - Witold Lutosławski (1913–1994): controlled aleatoricism (*Jeux vénitiens*, Symphony No. 3, *Concerto for Orchestra*); chain technique; harmonic language from interval classes
      - Krzysztof Penderecki (1933–2020): sonorism (*Threnody to the Victims of Hiroshima* 1960: tone clusters, extended techniques for 52 strings; *St. Luke Passion*); later neo-Romantic turn
      - György Ligeti (1923–2006): micropolyphony (*Atmosphères*, *Lontano*); *Requiem*; *Lux Aeterna* (all used in Kubrick's *2001: A Space Odyssey*); *Le Grand Macabre* (opera); Piano Études (3 books: African rhythm + Nancarrow + Chopin); Horn Trio; polyrhythmic complexity; microtonality; arguably most influential late-20th-c. composer
      - Luciano Berio (1925–2003): *Sequenzas* (solo virtuoso series); *Sinfonia* (1969: 3rd mvt. collage over Mahler 2 scherzo); folk music integration; electroacoustic (*Thema: Omaggio a Joyce*); *Coro*
      - Electronic and tape music: musique concrète (Pierre Schaeffer 1948: *Études de bruits*; Pierre Henry; GRM Paris); Elektronische Musik (Cologne: Stockhausen, Eimert); Columbia-Princeton Electronic Music Center (Babbitt, Ussachevsky, Luening); Pauline Oliveros; IRCAM; Max Mathews (1957: first computer-generated music, Bell Labs)

    - **Minimalism (~1960–present)**
      - La Monte Young (b. 1935): drone-based; *Composition 1960 #7* (sustained fifth: "to be held for a long time"); *The Well-Tuned Piano* (6+ hour just intonation piano); Theatre of Eternal Music (Tony Conrad, John Cale, Marian Zazeela)
      - Terry Riley (b. 1935): *In C* (1964: 53 modular phrases; foundational minimalist work); *A Rainbow in Curved Air* (tape loops, keyboards)
      - Steve Reich (b. 1936): phasing (*It's Gonna Rain*, *Come Out*, *Piano Phase*); process music; *Drumming*; *Music for 18 Musicians* (1976: most influential minimalist work); *Different Trains* (1988: speech melody + Holocaust narrative); *City Life*; Tehillim; influence of Ghanaian drumming, Balinese gamelan, cantillation
      - Philip Glass (b. 1937): additive/subtractive process; operas (*Einstein on the Beach* with Robert Wilson, 1976; *Satyagraha*; *Akhnaten*: portrait trilogy); symphonies; film scores (*Koyaanisqatsi*, *The Hours*, *Notes on a Scandal*); string quartets; prolific output
      - John Adams (b. 1947): post-minimalism; orchestral richness; opera (*Nixon in China*, *Doctor Atomic*, *The Death of Klinghoffer*); *Harmonielehre*, *Shaker Loops*, *Short Ride in a Fast Machine*; *On the Transmigration of Souls* (Pulitzer 2003)
      - Post-minimalism and totalism: Mikel Rouse, Michael Gordon, David Lang, Julia Wolfe (Bang on a Can collective); William Duckworth; Gavin Bryars (*Jesus' Blood Never Failed Me Yet*)

    - **Late 20th and 21st century trends (~1975–present)**
      - New Complexity: Brian Ferneyhough (*Carceri d'Invenzione*, *Time and Motion Studies*); James Dillon; Michael Finnissy (*English Country Tunes*); Richard Barrett; extreme notational density; performer as athlete
      - New Simplicity/New Tonality: Wolfgang Rihm; Arvo Pärt (tintinnabuli method: *Tabula Rasa*, *Fratres*, *Spiegel im Spiegel*, *Passio*); Henryk Górecki (Symphony No. 3: commercial hit 1992); John Tavener (*The Protecting Veil*); holy minimalism
      - Polystylism: Alfred Schnittke (*Concerto Grosso No. 1*; clash of tonal and atonal); eclecticism as method; Bernd Alois Zimmermann (*Die Soldaten*: pluralism)
      - Spectralism (detailed above): Grisey, Murail, Saariaho, Haas
      - Salvatore Sciarrino (b. 1947): Italian; "ecology of listening"; threshold sounds; breath-like textures; *Vanitas*
      - Helmut Lachenmann (b. 1935): *musique concrète instrumentale* (acoustic instruments producing noise/texture); *Das Mädchen mit den Schwefelhölzern* (opera); *Air*; *Gran Torso*
      - Beat Furrer (b. 1954): Austrian; voice + ensemble; *FAMA* (music theater); micropolyphony
      - Thomas Adès (b. 1971): British; *Asyla*, *The Tempest* (opera), *The Exterminating Angel* (opera 2016); *Concentric Paths*; wide harmonic vocabulary
      - George Benjamin (b. 1960): British; *Written on Skin* (2012: widely hailed as outstanding 21st-c. opera); *Lessons in Love and Violence* (2018); orchestral precision
      - Unsuk Chin (b. 1961): Korean-German; *Alice in Wonderland* (opera); Piano Concerto; spectral influence + virtuosity
      - Olga Neuwirth (b. 1968): Austrian; *Orlando* (opera after Virginia Woolf, 2019: Vienna Staatsoper); electronics + orchestra
      - Tyshawn Sorey (b. 1980): American; multi-instrumentalist; *Autoschediasms* (conducted improvisation); *Monochromatic Light (Afterlife)* (6-hour opera, 2024); crosses jazz/new music boundary
      - Du Yun (b. 1977): Chinese-American; *Angel's Bone* (Pulitzer 2017); performance art + opera
      - Frontier directions (~2020–2026): AI-assisted composition (AIVA, Google Magenta, David Cope's EMI/Emily Howell legacy); machine learning tools for orchestration (OrchIdea, Orchestal); live-coding performance; spatial audio (ambisonics, object-based) in concert halls (Elbphilharmonie, Philharmonie de Paris); 3D audio opera; ecological composition (John Luther Adams *Become Ocean*; acoustic ecology lineage from R. Murray Schafer); decolonization of new music institutions; pandemic-driven remote collaboration; distributed network music; generative/algorithmic music frameworks (SuperCollider, Max/MSP, TidalCycles)

- **Music History — Jazz**
  - **Origins and early jazz (~1890–1930)**
    - Antecedents: West African musical principles (polyrhythm, call-and-response, blue notes); spirituals and work songs; blues (12-bar structure, AAB lyric form); ragtime (Scott Joplin: *Maple Leaf Rag* 1899, *Treemonisha* opera)
    - New Orleans as birthplace: brass band tradition; Storyville district (myth partially); second-line parades; multi-ethnic mixing (Creole of Color musicians as bridge: classically trained + bluesy); Congo Square
    - Buddy Bolden (1877–1931): legendary cornetist; no recordings survive; possibly first jazz musician
    - Original Dixieland Jazz Band (ODJB): first jazz recording 1917 (*Livery Stable Blues*); white band; controversy over primacy
    - King Oliver (1881–1938): mentor to Armstrong; Creole Jazz Band recordings 1923 (Gennett sessions)
    - Louis Armstrong (1901–1971): foundational soloist; *Hot Five* and *Hot Seven* recordings (1925–28): *West End Blues* (1928: trumpet cadenza = jazz Rite of Spring moment); swing feel, improvisatory genius, vocal innovation (scat singing); transformed jazz from collective improvisation to soloist's art
    - Jelly Roll Morton (1890–1941): self-proclaimed inventor of jazz (exaggeration but influential); *Black Bottom Stomp*; Library of Congress recordings (Alan Lomax 1938); arranged New Orleans style for recording
    - Sidney Bechet (1897–1959): soprano saxophone pioneer; expatriate in France

  - **Swing era (~1930–1945)**
    - Big band format: 4–5 saxophones, 3–4 trumpets, 3–4 trombones, rhythm section (piano, bass, drums, guitar)
    - Duke Ellington (1899–1974): greatest jazz composer; jungle style at Cotton Club; *Black, Brown and Beige* (1943: extended suite); *Far East Suite*; ~1,000+ compositions; Billy Strayhorn (1915–1967: *Take the "A" Train*, *Lush Life*: collaborative genius, underrecognized due to racial and sexual orientation dynamics)
    - Count Basie (1904–1984): Kansas City swing; All American Rhythm Section (Basie, Freddie Green, Walter Page, Jo Jones); riff-based arrangements; Lester Young as star soloist
    - Benny Goodman (1909–1986): "King of Swing"; Carnegie Hall concert 1938; racially integrated groups (Teddy Wilson, Lionel Hampton); Fletcher Henderson arrangements key to Goodman's success
    - Fletcher Henderson (1897–1952): pioneered big band arrangement; Don Redman as arranger; employed Coleman Hawkins, Louis Armstrong
    - Other key bandleaders: Cab Calloway, Jimmie Lunceford, Artie Shaw, Glenn Miller, Chick Webb (Ella Fitzgerald discovery), Woody Herman
    - Female musicians: Mary Lou Williams (arranger, pianist, mentor across eras); International Sweethearts of Rhythm (integrated all-woman big band); Hazel Scott; Lil Hardin Armstrong
    - Swing rhythm: swung eighth notes; walking bass; ride cymbal pattern; "four on the floor" bass drum (pre-bebop)
    - Lindy Hop and Savoy Ballroom culture; jazz as popular dance music; V-Disc recordings in WWII

  - **Bebop (~1940–1955)**
    - After-hours sessions at Minton's Playhouse and Monroe's Uptown House, Harlem; fast tempos, complex harmony, virtuosity as gatekeeper
    - Charlie Parker (1920–1955): "Bird"; alto saxophone genius; *Ko-Ko*, *Ornithology*, *Confirmation*, *Now's the Time*; harmonic vocabulary (substitutions, upper extensions, chromatic approach tones); Savoy and Dial sessions; heroin addiction; died at 34
    - Dizzy Gillespie (1917–1993): trumpet virtuoso; Afro-Cuban jazz pioneer (with Chano Pozo: *Manteca* 1947); *A Night in Tunisia*; *Salt Peanuts*; big band bebop; international ambassador
    - Thelonious Monk (1917–1982): pianist-composer; angular melodies, dissonant voicings, percussive attack; *'Round Midnight*, *Blue Monk*, *Straight, No Chaser*, *Epistrophy*; idiosyncratic style; Blue Note and Riverside recordings; Five Spot residency with John Coltrane (1957)
    - Bud Powell (1924–1966): translated Parker's vocabulary to piano; set template for bebop piano; *Un Poco Loco*, *Tempus Fugit*; mental health struggles
    - Kenny Clarke and Max Roach: shifted timekeeping from bass drum to ride cymbal; "dropping bombs" on bass drum and snare; liberated drumming as interactive commentary
    - Bebop harmony: ii-V-I as fundamental progression; tritone substitution; chord extensions (♯9, ♯11, ♭13); contrafact (new melody over existing changes: *Anthropology* over *I Got Rhythm*)

  - **Cool jazz and West Coast (~1949–1960)**
    - Miles Davis *Birth of the Cool* nonet sessions (1949–50; Capitol: arranged by Gil Evans, Gerry Mulligan, John Lewis): lighter texture, arranged passages, counterpoint
    - Lennie Tristano (1919–1978): free improvisation experiments before Ornette Coleman; linear, Bach-influenced; *Intuition*, *Digression* (1949)
    - Dave Brubeck Quartet: *Time Out* (1959: *Take Five* in 5/4, Paul Desmond alto); odd meters popularized; enormous commercial success
    - Chet Baker (1929–1988): trumpet/vocal; Pacific Jazz; lyrical, intimate style
    - Gerry Mulligan (1927–1996): baritone sax; pianoless quartet with Chet Baker
    - Modern Jazz Quartet (John Lewis, Milt Jackson): third stream ambitions; classical form + jazz

  - **Hard bop and soul jazz (~1955–1970)**
    - Return to blues, gospel, R&B roots; hot rhythm; emotional intensity
    - Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers: *Moanin'*; launching pad for talent (Lee Morgan, Wayne Shorter, Freddie Hubbard, Horace Silver, Wynton Marsalis)
    - Horace Silver: funky jazz; *Song for My Father*, *Señor Blues*
    - Sonny Rollins (1930–2024): tenor saxophone colossus; *Saxophone Colossus* (1956: *St. Thomas*, *Blue 7*: Gunther Schuller's thematic analysis); Williamsburg Bridge sabbatical; unaccompanied *Way Out West*
    - Clifford Brown (1930–1956): trumpet brilliance; *Study in Brown*; killed in car accident at 25
    - Miles Davis (1926–1991): constant reinvention; cool → hard bop (*'Round About Midnight*) → modal jazz (*Kind of Blue* 1959: most influential jazz album; *So What*'s D Dorian vamp; George Russell's *Lydian Chromatic Concept* influence) → post-bop (second great quintet with Shorter, Hancock, Carter, Williams) → electric/fusion (*In a Silent Way* 1969, *Bitches Brew* 1970, *On the Corner* 1972) → 1980s comeback; Gil Evans collaborations (*Miles Ahead*, *Porgy and Bess*, *Sketches of Spain*)
    - John Coltrane (1926–1967): tenor/soprano saxophone; *Giant Steps* (1960: rapid key changes = "Coltrane changes"); *My Favorite Things* (1961: soprano sax, modal vamp); *A Love Supreme* (1965: suite, spiritual quest); *Ascension* (1966: free jazz large ensemble); sheets of sound technique; spiritual trajectory; enormous influence on all subsequent saxophone playing
    - Charles Mingus (1922–1979): bassist, composer, bandleader; *Mingus Ah Um* (*Better Git It in Your Soul*, *Fables of Faubus*: civil rights protest); *The Black Saint and the Sinner Lady*; *Pithecanthropus Erectus*; extended forms; written-out yet spontaneous-sounding; volcanic temperament
    - Soul jazz / organ trio: Jimmy Smith (Hammond B-3 organ); Wes Montgomery (octave guitar technique); Grant Green; Cannonball Adderley (*Mercy, Mercy, Mercy*)

  - **Free jazz and avant-garde (~1958–present)**
    - Ornette Coleman (1930–2015): *The Shape of Jazz to Come* (1959); *Free Jazz* (1961: collective improvisation, double quartet); harmolodics; plastic alto saxophone; controversy (is it jazz?); Prime Time (electric free funk); *Sound Grammar* (Pulitzer 2007)
    - Cecil Taylor (1929–2018): percussive piano; total energy music; *Unit Structures*; dance and poetry integration; uncompromising; Blue Note recordings
    - Albert Ayler (1936–1970): raw, ecstatic saxophone; folk melodies + extreme overblowing; *Spiritual Unity*; *Ghosts*; mysterious death
    - Sun Ra (1914–1993): Afrofuturism; Saturn mythology; Arkestra (communal big band spanning decades); early synthesizer adoption; *Space Is the Place*; cosmic philosophy
    - AACM (Association for the Advancement of Creative Musicians, Chicago, 1965): Muhal Richard Abrams (founder); Art Ensemble of Chicago (Roscoe Mitchell, Lester Bowie, Malachi Favors, Joseph Jarman, Don Moye: "Great Black Music — Ancient to the Future"); Anthony Braxton (systems, diagrams); Wadada Leo Smith; Henry Threadgill (Pulitzer 2016: *In for a Penny, In for a Pound*)
    - European free improvisation: Derek Bailey (*Guitar, Volume 1*); Evan Parker (circular breathing soprano sax); AMM (Cardew, Rowe, Prévost); Spontaneous Music Ensemble (John Stevens); Company (Bailey); Peter Brötzmann (*Machine Gun* 1968)
    - Loft jazz scene (1970s NYC): Sam Rivers' Studio Rivbea; David Murray; Oliver Lake; Julius Hemphill (World Saxophone Quartet)

  - **Fusion / jazz-rock (~1969–1985)**
    - Miles Davis (see above); Tony Williams Lifetime (*Emergency!*); Herbie Hancock (*Head Hunters* 1973: funk-jazz); Chick Corea (Return to Forever: Latin-jazz-fusion); Weather Report (Joe Zawinul, Wayne Shorter: *Heavy Weather*, *Birdland*); John McLaughlin (Mahavishnu Orchestra: virtuosic heavy fusion); Pat Metheny (Pat Metheny Group; ECM recordings; *Bright Size Life*)
    - Jaco Pastorius (1951–1987): fretless electric bass revolution; *Portrait of Tracy* (harmonics); Weather Report; self-titled solo album; tragic decline and death

  - **Post-bop, neo-traditional, and contemporary (~1980–present)**
    - Wynton Marsalis (b. 1961): neo-traditionalist; Classical and Jazz Grammys same year (1984); Jazz at Lincoln Center (artistic director); *Blood on the Fields* (Pulitzer 1997); controversial: attacked fusion and free jazz; championed "jazz canon"
    - Jazz education institutionalization: Berklee College of Music; New England Conservatory; Thelonious Monk Institute (now Herbie Hancock Institute); proliferation of university jazz programs
    - M-BASE Collective: Steve Coleman (alto saxophone); complex polyrhythm; asymmetric structures; Greg Osby; Cassandra Wilson; influence on 21st-c. rhythm
    - Brad Mehldau (b. 1970): piano; reimagined pop/rock covers through jazz lens (*Art of the Trio*); Brahmsian depth
    - Robert Glasper (b. 1978): *Black Radio* (2012): jazz-hip-hop-R&B fusion; beat scene crossover
    - Kamasi Washington (b. 1981): *The Epic* (2015: 3-hour jazz vision); brought jazz to new audiences via hip-hop connections (Kendrick Lamar *To Pimp a Butterfly*)
    - Esperanza Spalding (b. 1984): bassist-vocalist; *Emily's D+Evolution*; *Songwrights Apothecary Lab* (2021: musical healing); Grammy for Best New Artist (2011: upset over Justin Bieber)
    - Contemporary London jazz scene: Shabaka Hutchings (Sons of Kemet, The Comet Is Coming), Nubya Garcia, Moses Boyd, Ezra Collective (Mercury Prize 2023); Afro-Caribbean roots + electronics + punk energy
    - Frontier directions: algorithmic jazz (George Lewis *Voyager* interactive computer improvisation); live-looping performance; global jazz fusions; jazz + electronic music (Floating Points *Promises* with Pharoah Sanders 2021); new spiritual jazz wave; jazz as gallery/installation art

- **Music History — Popular Music**
  - **Pre-rock popular music**
    - Tin Pan Alley (1885–1950s): NYC song publishing industry; AABA 32-bar form; Irving Berlin (*White Christmas*); Cole Porter (*Night and Day*); George & Ira Gershwin (*Summertime*, *I Got Rhythm*); Jerome Kern (*Ol' Man River*); Richard Rodgers & Lorenz Hart / Oscar Hammerstein II
    - Broadway musical: *Show Boat* (1927: Kern/Hammerstein, racially integrated cast); Golden Age: Rodgers & Hammerstein (*Oklahoma!*, *South Pacific*, *The Sound of Music*); Leonard Bernstein (*West Side Story*); Stephen Sondheim (*Sweeney Todd*, *Into the Woods*, *Company*); contemporary: Jonathan Larson (*Rent*); Lin-Manuel Miranda (*Hamilton* 2015)
    - Blues: Delta blues (Robert Johnson, Charley Patton, Son House); Piedmont (Blind Blake, Reverend Gary Davis); urban/Chicago blues (Muddy Waters, Howlin' Wolf, Little Walter); electric blues (B.B. King, Albert King, Freddie King)
    - Country/folk: Appalachian folk; Carter Family, Jimmie Rodgers; Grand Ole Opry; honky-tonk (Hank Williams); Nashville Sound; Woody Guthrie; Pete Seeger; folk revival (Kingston Trio, Peter Paul & Mary)
    - Gospel and R&B: Thomas A. Dorsey (father of gospel); Mahalia Jackson; Ray Charles (genius synthesis of gospel + blues + jazz); Sam Cooke; doo-wop; jump blues (Louis Jordan)

  - **Rock and pop era (~1950–present)**
    - Rock and roll origins (~1950s): Chuck Berry (*Johnny B. Goode*: rock guitar prototype); Little Richard; Fats Domino; Elvis Presley (cultural explosion; racial dynamics of white artist performing Black-derived music); Buddy Holly; Jerry Lee Lewis; rockabilly; Alan Freed (DJ, coined "rock and roll")
    - British Invasion (~1964): The Beatles (Lennon-McCartney songwriting; albums: *Rubber Soul*, *Revolver*, *Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band*, *Abbey Road*, *The White Album*; studio innovation with George Martin; most influential pop act); The Rolling Stones (blues-based); The Who; The Kinks; Animals
    - Motown: Berry Gordy; Holland-Dozier-Holland; Smokey Robinson; Supremes; Temptations; Marvin Gaye (*What's Going On* 1971); Stevie Wonder (*Innervisions*, *Songs in the Key of Life*); Funk Brothers (house band)
    - Soul: Stax/Atlantic (Otis Redding, Aretha Franklin — "Queen of Soul", Sam & Dave, Wilson Pickett); James Brown (Godfather of Soul/Funk; *Papa's Got a Brand New Bag*; the One); Philadelphia soul (Gamble & Huff, MFSB)
    - Psychedelic rock (~1966–1971): The Beatles (*Tomorrow Never Knows*); Jimi Hendrix (*Are You Experienced*, *Electric Ladyland*: guitar revolution); Pink Floyd (*The Dark Side of the Moon*); Grateful Dead (jam band); Jefferson Airplane; The Doors
    - Progressive rock (~1969–1980): Yes, Genesis, King Crimson (*In the Court of the Crimson King*), Emerson Lake & Palmer; extended compositions, concept albums; classical and jazz influence; virtuosity
    - Hard rock and heavy metal: Led Zeppelin; Black Sabbath (invented heavy metal); Deep Purple; later: Judas Priest, Iron Maiden (NWOBHM); thrash metal (Metallica, Slayer, Megadeth); subgenres: death metal, black metal, doom metal, progressive metal, djent
    - Punk and post-punk (~1976–1985): Ramones, Sex Pistols (*Never Mind the Bollocks*), The Clash; DIY ethos; post-punk: Joy Division (*Unknown Pleasures*), Siouxsie and the Banshees, Bauhaus (goth), Talking Heads, Wire
    - New wave and synth-pop: Depeche Mode, New Order, Gary Numan, Kraftwerk influence; MTV era
    - Funk and disco: Parliament-Funkadelic (George Clinton); Sly & the Family Stone; disco (~1975–1980: Bee Gees, Donna Summer, Saturday Night Fever); disco backlash
    - Hip-hop/rap: origins in South Bronx (1973: DJ Kool Herc breakbeat innovation); four elements (DJing, MCing, breakdancing, graffiti); Grandmaster Flash (*The Message* 1982); Run-DMC; Public Enemy (*It Takes a Nation of Millions to Hold Us Back*); golden age (~1986–1996): Rakim, Nas (*Illmatic*), The Notorious B.I.G., Tupac Shakur, A Tribe Called Quest, Wu-Tang Clan; West Coast: N.W.A., Dr. Dre (*The Chronic*), Snoop Dogg; Southern: OutKast, UGK; 2000s: Jay-Z, Kanye West (*My Beautiful Dark Twisted Fantasy*), Eminem; trap (T.I., Gucci Mane → Future, Young Thug, Migos); drill (Chief Keef → UK drill); conscious: Kendrick Lamar (*To Pimp a Butterfly*, *DAMN.*: Pulitzer 2018 — first non-classical/non-jazz Pulitzer for Music)
    - Electronic dance music (EDM): Detroit techno (Juan Atkins, Derrick May, Kevin Saunderson); Chicago house (Frankie Knuckles: "Godfather of House"); UK rave culture and acid house; drum and bass/jungle; trance; dubstep (UK: Burial, Skrillex popularization); Berlin techno (Tresor, Berghain); IDM (Aphex Twin, Autechre, Boards of Canada); ambient (Brian Eno *Music for Airports* 1978)
    - Alternative/indie: R.E.M., Pixies (quiet-loud dynamic → Nirvana); Nirvana (*Nevermind* 1991: grunge explosion); Radiohead (*OK Computer*, *Kid A*: electronic + rock); Björk; Arcade Fire; contemporary: streaming-era indie
    - R&B/neo-soul: D'Angelo (*Voodoo*), Erykah Badu, Lauryn Hill (*The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill*); contemporary R&B: Frank Ocean (*Blonde*), SZA, Daniel Caesar
    - K-pop: industrial training system; BTS global phenomenon; BLACKPINK; cultural export strategy; fan communities (ARMY etc.)
    - Latin music: reggaeton (Daddy Yankee *Despacito*; Bad Bunny: most-streamed artist globally); Latin trap; bachata; cumbia; regional Mexican (corridos tumbados: Peso Pluma 2023–24)
    - Afrobeats global rise: Burna Boy, Wizkid, Tems, Davido; Nigerian + Ghanaian + Diasporic; Grammy category established 2024

- **Music of the World — Non-Western Traditions**
  - **Indian classical music**
    - Two systems: Hindustani (North) and Carnatic (South); shared raga-tala framework but divergent repertoire, instruments, ornamentation
    - Raga: melodic framework; ascending (aroha) and descending (avaroha) patterns; characteristic phrases (pakad); mood (rasa); time of day/season associations; thousands cataloged but ~100–200 in active performance
    - Tala: rhythmic cycle; Hindustani: tintal (16 beats), rupak (7), jhaptal (10), ektal (12); Carnatic: adi (8), rupaka (3), misra chapu (7), khanda chapu (5); sam (first beat) as point of resolution
    - Hindustani: dhrupad (austere, ancient: Dagar family); khayal (dominant vocal: Bade Ghulam Ali Khan, Bhimsen Joshi, Kishori Amonkar); thumri (light-classical, emotional); instruments: sitar (Ravi Shankar, Vilayat Khan, Nikhil Banerjee), sarod (Ali Akbar Khan, Amjad Ali Khan), bansuri (Hariprasad Chaurasia), sarangi (Ram Narayan), tabla (Alla Rakha, Zakir Hussain), santoor (Shivkumar Sharma); alap-jor-jhala-gat structure
    - Carnatic: compositions (kriti) central; Trinity: Tyagaraja, Muthuswami Dikshitar, Shyama Shastri (late 18th–early 19th c.); instruments: vina (Veena Dhanammal), mridangam, violin (adapted: L. Subramaniam, L. Shankar), flute (T. R. Mahalingam), ghatam; concert format: varnam → kritis → ragam-tanam-pallavi → lighter pieces; gamaka (ornament) essential
    - Fusion: Ravi Shankar + Yehudi Menuhin; shakti (John McLaughlin + Zakir Hussain + L. Shankar); Anoushka Shankar

  - **East Asian music**
    - Chinese: guqin (seven-string zither: scholar-literati instrument; UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage 2003); pipa (lute); erhu (bowed, 2-string); Chinese opera (Peking/Beijing opera: jingju; Kunqu: UNESCO 2001); pentatonic basis (gong, shang, jue, zhi, yu); silk and bamboo (sizhu) ensembles; revolutionary opera (model operas: Cultural Revolution)
    - Japanese: gagaku (ancient court music, from Tang China/Korea, ~7th c.; togaku, komagaku); noh (hayashi ensemble: flute and drums); kabuki (shamisen, vocals, sound effects); koto (13-string zither: Michio Miyagi modernization); shakuhachi (bamboo flute: honkyoku/zen meditation; international revival); taiko drumming (Ondekoza, Kodo); min'yō (folk); enka (sentimental popular); J-pop; vocaloid (Hatsune Miku)
    - Korean: gugak (traditional); pansori (epic sung narrative: UNESCO 2003; *Chunhyangga*); sanjo (solo instrumental); gayageum (12-string zither); samulnori (percussion quartet); court music (aak); K-pop (see popular music above)
    - Gamelan (Indonesia): Java: meditative, restrained, layered; slendro (5-tone) and pelog (7-tone) scales; colotomic structure (gong cycles: kenong, kempul, gong ageng); Bali: dynamic, energetic, kecak (monkey chant); gender wayang (shadow puppet accompaniment); stratification (core melody = balungan; elaboration layers); influenced Debussy, Cage, Britten, Reich, Lou Harrison

  - **Middle Eastern and Central Asian music**
    - Maqam: modal system (Arabic/Turkish/Persian variants); microtonal intervals (quarter-tones); taqasim (improvisation within maqam); Arabic: oud, qanun, ney, riq, darbuka; Umm Kulthum (1898–1975: greatest Arabic singer; 4-hour concerts); Fairuz (Lebanese); Turkish: makam; tanbur, kemençe; whirling dervish music (Mevlevi sema); Persian: dastgah system; tar, setar, santur, kamancheh; radif (repertoire); classical Persian singing (avaz); Mugham (Azerbaijani)
    - Central Asian: Uzbek/Tajik shashmaqam; Kazakh dombra; Tuvan/Mongolian throat singing (khoomei/overtone singing: UNESCO; Huun-Huur-Tu)

  - **African music**
    - West African griot traditions: hereditary musician-historians; kora (21-string harp-lute: Toumani Diabaté, Ballaké Sissoko); djembe (Mamady Keïta); balafon; talking drums (dundun); Mande musical tradition
    - Central African: pygmy polyphony (Aka, Mbuti: interlocking hocket, yodel); likembe/mbira (thumb piano); unique vocal polyphony recognized by ethnomusicologists (Arom 1991)
    - East African: taarab (Zanzibar: Arabic + Indian + African blend); Ethiopian: pentatonic scales, masinko (bowed lute), krar (lyre), Ethio-jazz (Mulatu Astatke: *Éthiopiques* series rediscovery)
    - Southern African: Shona mbira traditions (Zimbabwe: *mbira dzavadzimu*; spirit medium ceremonies; Paul Berliner *The Soul of Mbira* 1978); Zulu isicathamiya (Ladysmith Black Mambazo); South African jazz (Abdullah Ibrahim, Hugh Masekela); kwaito; amapiano (2020s global spread)
    - Highlife (Ghana/Nigeria); Afrobeat (Fela Kuti: political activism, polyrhythm, extended form — *Zombie*; Tony Allen: drummer; Seun and Femi Kuti continuation); jùjú (King Sunny Adé); mbalax (Youssou N'Dour, Senegal); soukous (Congo: Franco, OK Jazz)

  - **Latin American and Caribbean music**
    - Afro-Cuban: son cubano (foundation of salsa: *son clave*); rumba (guaguancó, yambú, columbia); Arsenio Rodríguez; Buena Vista Social Club (Ry Cooder, 1997 rediscovery); mambo (Pérez Prado, Machito, Tito Puente); cha-cha-chá; timba; Cuban nueva trova (Silvio Rodríguez)
    - Salsa: NYC 1970s (Fania Records; Willie Colón, Héctor Lavoe, Rubén Blades, Celia Cruz); Larry Harlow
    - Brazilian: samba (carnival; batucada percussion); bossa nova (João Gilberto, Antonio Carlos Jobim, *The Girl from Ipanema*; synthesis of samba + cool jazz); tropicália (Caetano Veloso, Gilberto Gil, Os Mutantes; psychedelic + Brazilian traditions; politically radical); MPB; axé; maracatu; forró; baile funk (MC Carol, Anitta global crossover)
    - Jamaican: mento → ska → rocksteady → reggae (Bob Marley: *Exodus*, global icon; Peter Tosh; Jimmy Cliff); dub (Lee "Scratch" Perry, King Tubby: studio as instrument; reggae roots taken apart and reassembled with echo/reverb effects; enormous influence on electronic music, hip-hop, post-punk); dancehall (Yellowman, Sean Paul, Vybz Kartel)
    - Trinidadian: calypso (Lord Kitchener, Mighty Sparrow); steelpan (invented ~1930s–40s from oil drums); soca
    - Andean: charango, quena, zampoña; nueva canción (Chile: Víctor Jara, Violeta Parra, Inti-Illimani — music as political resistance under Pinochet)
    - Tango: Argentina/Uruguay; Carlos Gardel (voice); Astor Piazzolla (bandoneón: nuevo tango — jazz + classical + tango fusion; *Libertango*); milonga; vals criollo
    - Colombian: cumbia (origins: African + Indigenous + Spanish; global spread); vallenato (Carlos Vives modernization); champeta; reggaeton's roots partly here

  - **Other traditions**
    - Australian Aboriginal: didgeridoo (yidaki); songlines/dreaming tracks (music as navigation and cosmology); clapsticks; world's oldest continuous musical tradition
    - Native American: powwow drum and vocal traditions; Plains-style singing; Pueblo ceremonial; cedar flute (R. Carlos Nakai); peyote songs; Northern vs. Southern drumming styles
    - Celtic: Irish (uilleann pipes, fiddle, bodhrán, tin whistle; sean-nós: unaccompanied vocal tradition; The Chieftains, Planxty); Scottish (bagpipes: pibroch = ceol mòr; Cape Breton fiddling); Breton (fest-noz; Alan Stivell)
    - Romani (Roma) music: flamenco influence; Balkan brass (Boban Marković); Hungarian Roma violin tradition; Django Reinhardt (Sinti jazz: Gypsy jazz / jazz manouche); Taraf de Haïdouks

- **Ethnomusicology**
  - Definition: study of music in its cultural context; "the study of people making music" (Jeff Todd Titon)
  - Founders and key figures: Comparative musicology (Berlin school: Hornbostel, Sachs — Hornbostel-Sachs instrument classification 1914; Carl Stumpf); Alan Lomax (cantometrics: cross-cultural song style analysis; field recordings: Library of Congress); Frances Densmore (Native American music documentation); Béla Bartók and Zoltán Kodály (Hungarian/Romanian/Slovak folk music systematic collection); Jaap Kunst (coined "ethnomusicology" 1950; Javanese music); Mantle Hood (bi-musicality concept; UCLA program); John Blacking (*How Musical Is Man?* 1973: Venda music); Bruno Nettl (fieldwork methodology; *The Study of Ethnomusicology* (1983/2005/2015)); Anthony Seeger (Suyá/Kĩsêdjê music); Timothy Rice (phenomenological approach; *May It Fill Your Soul*: Bulgarian music); Kay Kaufman Shelemay; Martin Stokes (music and place)
  - Fieldwork methodology: participant-observation; applied ethnomusicology; ethics of recording and representation; repatriation of recordings
  - Key journals: *Ethnomusicology* (SEM), *Yearbook for Traditional Music* (ICTM), *Popular Music*, *World of Music*
  - Debates: universalism vs. cultural relativism; insider/outsider (emic/etic); music and identity; music and diaspora; decolonizing ethnomusicology; digital ethnomusicology (virtual fieldwork during COVID-19)
  - Applied ethnomusicology: music in conflict resolution; music therapy across cultures; music and refugee communities; sound archives and digital preservation (EVIA, Smithsonian Folkways); UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage lists and their politics
  - Frontier: computational ethnomusicology (automatic transcription of non-Western music; cross-cultural corpus studies; MIR for world music); Pamela Z and sound art; climate change and musical traditions at risk; viral globalization of local genres (amapiano, reggaeton)

- **Music Technology**
  - **Acoustics and recording**
    - Sound recording history: phonograph (Edison 1877); gramophone (Berliner disc); electrical recording (1925: Western Electric); magnetic tape (1930s Germany: Magnetophon; Mullin brought to US for Bing Crosby); stereo (Blumlein 1931 patent; commercial stereo LPs 1958); multitrack recording (Les Paul; Ampex 8-track to ~24-track); digital recording (PCM: Stockham; Sony PCM-1600 1978); CD (Philips/Sony 1982); Pro Tools (1991: DAW revolution)
    - Key recording technologies: microphone types (dynamic, condenser, ribbon); mixing console; reverb (plate, spring, chamber, digital); compression, EQ, delay; Abbey Road Studios (Beatles); Electric Ladyland (Hendrix); Muscle Shoals; FAME Studios
    - Mastering: loudness war (dynamic range compression escalation); LUFS standards; vinyl vs. digital debate
  - **Electronic instruments**
    - Theremin (Léon Theremin 1920); Ondes Martenot (Maurice Martenot 1928: used by Messiaen, Varèse); Hammond organ (1935: tonewheel; jazz/gospel/rock); Mellotron (tape-replay: Beatles *Strawberry Fields Forever*; King Crimson)
    - Synthesizers: RCA Mark II (1957: Columbia-Princeton); Moog synthesizer (Robert Moog 1964; Wendy Carlos *Switched-On Bach* 1968); Buchla (West Coast: voltage-controlled modules); Minimoog (1970: portable, monophonic); ARP Odyssey, ARP 2600; Prophet-5 (1978: Dave Smith, first polyphonic programmable); DX7 (Yamaha 1983: FM synthesis; ubiquitous); Roland TR-808 (1980: drum machine defining hip-hop/electro sound) and TR-909 (1984: techno/house); Roland TB-303 (acid house); Fairlight CMI (1979: sampling); Emulator; Akai MPC (1988: sampling + sequencing for hip-hop); modular synth revival (Eurorack: Doepfer A-100 1996)
    - Synthesis methods: subtractive, additive, FM (Chowning 1967/1973), wavetable, granular (Xenakis concept; Roads implementation), physical modeling (Karplus-Strong; Stanford CCRMA), sample-based, spectral resynthesis, concatenative
  - **Digital audio and software**
    - DAWs: Pro Tools, Logic Pro, Ableton Live (2001: live performance + production; electronic music standard), FL Studio, Reaper, Cubase/Nuendo (Steinberg), Bitwig
    - MIDI: Musical Instrument Digital Interface (1983: Dave Smith & Ikutaro Kakehashi; standardized communication); MIDI 2.0 (2020: higher resolution, bidirectional); OSC (Open Sound Control: alternative protocol)
    - Software synthesis: Max/MSP (Miller Puckette/IRCAM 1988; Cycling '74); Pure Data (Pd: open source); SuperCollider (James McCartney 1996); Csound; software instruments (Native Instruments, Spectrasonics, Arturia)
    - Audio plugins: VST (Steinberg 1996), AU (Apple), AAX (Avid); convolution reverb; amp modeling; mastering plugins
    - Music Information Retrieval (MIR): automatic chord estimation; melody extraction; beat tracking; genre classification; mood/emotion recognition; source separation (Demucs, Open-Unmix); key conferences: ISMIR (since 2000)
  - **AI and music (~2015–present)**
    - Neural network composition: Google Magenta (2016: RNN/Transformer-based music generation; Music Transformer); OpenAI Jukebox (2020: raw audio generation); MuseNet
    - Text-to-music models: MusicLM (Google 2023); MusicGen (Meta 2023); Stable Audio; Suno, Udio (2024: consumer-facing, copyright controversy)
    - AI-assisted tools: LANDR (automated mastering); iZotope (intelligent audio repair); Amper/Shutterstock (production music); AIVA (classical composition)
    - Ethical debates: copyright (training on copyrighted music; lawsuit wave 2023–25: RIAA vs. Suno/Udio); authenticity; displacement of session musicians; deepfake vocals; unauthorized voice cloning
    - Live performance: Ableton Push; touchscreen instruments (Roli Seaboard MPE); laptop orchestras (PLOrk, Princeton: Ge Wang, Perry Cook); algorave (live-coding: TidalCycles, Sonic Pi, FoxDot); networked music performance (JackTrip, SoundJack)
    - Spatial audio: Dolby Atmos Music (Apple Music adoption 2021); binaural; ambisonics; Wave Field Synthesis (WFS); IRCAM spatialization; immersive concert venues
    - Frontier (~2024–2026): real-time AI accompaniment; diffusion models for audio; multimodal models linking score, audio, text; brain-computer music interfaces (BCI: P300 speller for disabled musicians); haptic audio (SubPac, Woojer); open-source AI music tools proliferating

- **Music Education and Pedagogy**
  - Historical: Guido of Arezzo (solmization); conservatory system (Naples ~1537; Paris Conservatoire 1795); Leipzig Conservatory (Mendelssohn 1843)
  - Pedagogical methods: Suzuki method (Shinichi Suzuki: mother-tongue approach; violin from age 3); Kodály method (Zoltán Kodály: solfège, folk song-based; Hungary); Orff Schulwerk (Carl Orff: body percussion, speech, movement, pitched percussion; *Musik für Kinder*); Dalcroze Eurhythmics (Émile Jaques-Dalcroze: music through movement/kinesthetic); Gordon Music Learning Theory (Edwin Gordon: audiation); Colourstrings (Géza Szilvay: Finnish/Hungarian); El Sistema (Venezuela, José Antonio Abreu 1975: youth orchestras as social transformation; Gustavo Dudamel as most famous product)
  - Music theory pedagogy: Fux species counterpoint tradition; Rameau-descended harmony texts; Aldwell & Schachter; Kostka & Payne; Clendinning & Marvin; Open Music Theory (open-source textbook movement)
  - Performance pedagogy: master class tradition; competition circuit (Tchaikovsky, Chopin, Queen Elisabeth, Van Cliburn); practice science (Ericsson deliberate practice; motor learning; Alexander Technique; Body Mapping)
  - Research: *Journal of Research in Music Education*; *Music Perception*; *Psychology of Music*; symposium: ISME (International Society for Music Education)

- Theatre - Full Depth
  - Basics
    - Scope of theatre
      - Theatre as live, embodied, time-bound performance organized before spectators in shared or mediated space
      - Core elements
        - Performer, spectator, action, space, time, convention, repeatability-with-variation
        - Text is optional rather than universal: improvisation, dance-theatre, ritual, puppetry, devised work, and immersive environments may decenter scripted dialogue
      - Border disciplines
        - Performance studies overlaps with ritual, games, protest, ceremony, fashion, drag, digital streaming, and social performance
        - Theatre studies intersects literary study, anthropology, architecture, musicology, film studies, media archaeology, and cultural history
    - Foundational questions
      - What distinguishes theatre from ritual, game, spectacle, and everyday performance?
