      - **DV8 Physical Theatre** (Lloyd Newson, 1986–): film adaptations of stage works: *Dead Dreams of Monochrome Men* (1990, film), *The Cost of Living* (2004, film); physical theatre on screen
    - **Digital choreography and interactive technologies**
      - LifeForms/DanceForms software (Cunningham, from 1989): 3D avatar-based choreographic planning
      - **Troika Ranch** (Dawn Stoppiello and Mark Coniglio): interactive systems; MidiDancer wearable sensor suit (1994); Isadora software for real-time video manipulation by dancer input
      - **Chunky Move** (Gideon Obarzanek, Australia): *Glow* (2006, interactive reactive projections tracking dancer); *Mortal Engine* (2008)
      - Motion capture as creative tool: Bill T. Jones and Paul Kaiser, *Ghostcatching* (1999 — motion-captured traces without body); Forsythe's choreographic objects
      - AI and machine learning: Wayne McGregor partnering with Google Arts & Culture on AI-generated movement (2018); Kenrick Sandy and AI collaboration experiments; generative choreography algorithms
      - Virtual reality / mixed reality dance: pandemic-era experiments (*Loveseat* by 600 Highwaymen, 2021, remote-audience duet); MetaMovement Lab; *In the Eyes of the Animal* (Marshmallow Laser Feast, 2015 — immersive forest, borderline dance-VR); 360-degree dance film growing field
      - **Real-time interactivity**: Karlheinz Essl, Robert Wechsler, Frieder Weiss — creators of motion-tracking interactive systems using camera-based tracking (EyeCon software) for dance-activated sound/image
  - **Dance Therapy**
    - **Dance/Movement Therapy (DMT)**
      - Definition: psychotherapeutic use of movement as a process to further emotional, social, cognitive, and physical integration (American Dance Therapy Association — ADTA, founded 1966)
      - Founders: **Marian Chace** (1896–1970): "Grande Dame of Dance Therapy"; worked at St. Elizabeths Hospital (Washington, DC) from 1942 with psychiatric patients; used empathic reflection and group rhythmic activity; body action (movement externalizing inner feeling); **Mary Whitehouse** (1911–1979): developed **Authentic Movement** — mover with eyes closed follows inner impulse, witness observes; rooted in Jungian active imagination; **Trudi Schoop** (1903–1999): used humor, body image work, mime; **Blanche Evan** (creative dance therapy approach)
      - Theoretical foundations: body-mind connection; embodied cognition; attachment theory (Kestenberg Movement Profile — KMP, for infant-parent movement analysis); neuroscience of mirror neurons and empathy; trauma theory (Bessel van der Kolk — *The Body Keeps the Score*, 2014, widely influential)
      - Clinical applications: psychiatric disorders (schizophrenia, depression, anxiety), trauma/PTSD, autism spectrum, eating disorders, dementia/Alzheimer's, chronic pain, developmental disabilities, palliative care
      - Credentialing: Registered Dance/Movement Therapist (R-DMT) and Board-Certified Dance/Movement Therapist (BC-DMT) — via ADTA; equivalents in UK (ADMP UK), Europe (EADMT), global expansion
      - Evidence base: growing RCT literature; Cochrane reviews (Koch et al., 2014, 2019); meta-analyses showing significant effects on depression, quality of life, body image; challenges: standardizing protocols across embodied practices
    - **Community dance**: participatory, non-performative, inclusive; People Dancing (UK); community dance practitioners worldwide; applied settings: prisons, hospitals, refugee camps, elder care; **Dance for PD** (Mark Morris Dance Group and Brooklyn Parkinson Group, 2001+) — Parkinson's disease program; evidence of gait, balance, and quality of life improvements
  - **Dance Studies: Academic Field**
    - Emergence as academic discipline: dance departments in universities from mid-20th century; Margaret H'Doubler at University of Wisconsin-Madison (first dance major in a university, 1926)
    - Key journals: *Dance Research Journal* (Society of Dance History Scholars → Dance Studies Association), *Dance Chronicle*, *Dance Research* (UK), *Performance Research*, *The Drama Review (TDR)*, *Choreographic Practices*, *Journal of Dance Education*, *Journal of Dance & Somatic Practices*
    - Major scholarly organizations: **Dance Studies Association (DSA)** (merger 2017 of Society of Dance History Scholars and Congress on Research in Dance); **International Council of Kinetography Laban (ICKL)**; **World Dance Alliance (WDA)**
    - **Dance historiography**: from anecdotal chronicle to critical cultural history; Ivor Guest (19th c. ballet history); Sally Banes (postmodern dance historian — *Terpsichore in Sneakers*, 1980/1987; *Democracy's Body: Judson Dance Theater 1962–1964*, 1983); Susan Leigh Foster (*Reading Dancing*, 1986 — dance as text; choreography and corporeality); Mark Franko (*Dancing Modernism / Performing Politics*, 1995); Ramsay Burt (*The Male Dancer*, 1995/2007 — gender and sexuality in dance); Thomas DeFrantz (Black dance and performance); Brenda Dixon Gottschild (*Digging the Africanist Presence in American Performance*, 1996 — uncovering Black contributions to "white" art forms)
    - **Choreographic theory**: Ann Daly — *Done into Dance: Isadora Duncan in America* (1995 — feminist analysis); André Lepecki — *Exhausting Dance: Performance and the Politics of Movement* (2006 — dance and the political, stillness as choreographic strategy, Deleuzian frameworks); Randy Martin — *Critical Moves: Dance Studies in Theory and Politics* (1998); Harmony Bench — digital dance studies and online circulation; Kate Elswit — *Watching Weimar Dance* (2014, archival reconstruction)
    - **Ethnochoreology**: study of dance in cultural context; overlap with ethnomusicology; fieldwork-based; International Council for Traditional Music (ICTM) Study Group on Ethnochoreology; Anca Giurchescu, Andrée Grau, Drid Williams (anthropology of dance, *semasiology*); Adrienne Kaeppler (Tongan dance, structural analysis of form-meaning relationships)
  - **Frontier and Contemporary Research Directions (2020–present)**
    - **Post-pandemic transformations**
      - COVID-19 devastated live dance: companies furloughed, freelancers lost income; mass pivot to digital (streamed performances, Zoom classes, film commissions); raised questions about liveness, embodiment, and economic precarity
      - Hybrid models persisting: digital access expanding audiences; smaller in-person capacities initially; new revenue models (subscriptions, on-demand)
      - Renewed urgency around dancer welfare: mental health, employment security, collective organizing (Dancers' Alliance; equity campaigns in ballet companies)
    - **Diversity, equity, inclusion in ballet and contemporary dance**
      - Growing reckoning with ballet's Eurocentric, racialized beauty standards: pointe shoe shade range finally expanded (Freed of London, Bloch, Capezio producing skin-tone-matching pointe shoes for darker skin tones, accelerated 2020+)
      - Misty Copeland (b. 1982): first Black female principal dancer at American Ballet Theatre (2015); cultural icon; representation debates
      - Theaster Gates, Alonzo King (LINES Ballet), Abdel Salaam (Forces of Nature), and other Black, Indigenous, and Global Majority choreographers receiving overdue institutional recognition
      - Disability and dance: companies devoted to dance with differing abilities (AXIS Dance Company, Candoco Dance Company, Full Radius Dance; **Alice Sheppard** and *Kinetic Light* — choreography designed from wheelchair-moving bodies); integrated dance; Universal Design principles in dance education; Disability Arts movement
      - Indigenous dance rights and reclamation: concerns over extraction; NAGPRA and cultural patrimony; Indigenous choreographers on international stages (Deborah Brown, Elisa Monte's earlier appropriation controversies; Jack Gray — Māori contemporary performance; Joel Bray — Wiradjuri contemporary dance, Australia; Jacob Boehme — Indigenous burlesque/drag, Australia)
    - **Climate and environment in dance**
      - Eco-choreography and environmental dance: site-specific outdoor work; **Heine Avdal and Yukiko Shinozaki** (fieldworks); **Siobhan Davies** (material objects and environment); dance-on-location confronting ecological crisis
      - Sustainability in touring: carbon footprint of international dance touring debated; digital alternative commitments
      - Climate grief and solastalgia as choreographic territory (Crystal Pite's work engages indirectly; emerging theme in festival-commissioned works 2022–2025)
    - **Technology and AI in choreography**
      - Large language models and generative AI used experimentally to propose movement sequences; ethical concerns about authorship, ownership, "training on" documented choreographic work
      - Machine learning motion analysis: pose estimation feeding into choreographic research databases; Choreographic Coding Lab (Forsythe); Motion Bank expansion
      - XR (extended reality): AR headsets and VR immersive dance experiences developed during pandemic; *Life in Miniature* (Andy Serkis/Marshmallow Laser Feast); Museum of Other Realities
      - Real-time AI-responsive environments: sound and light reactive to dancer's movement via machine learning classifiers; deepening Troika Ranch/Chunky Move legacy
    - **Archival politics and decolonizing the canon**
      - Whose choreographic works are preserved? Notation and recording bias toward Western concert forms; ongoing digitization projects (Siobhan Davies RePlay digital archive; Jacob's Pillow archives; NYPL Jerome Robbins Dance Division)
      - Decolonizing dance curricula: universities worldwide revising all-Western syllabi; centering Africanist, Asian, Indigenous, and Latin American forms; debates about competency, cultural sensitivity, and who can teach what
      - Restorative justice in dance scholarship: rewriting histories to credit Black and Brown innovators (Lindy Hop, jazz dance, vernacular dance; Gottschild's work influential); challenging "discovery" narratives
    - **Key open questions in the field**
      - How should dance archives handle ephemerality — can a dance ever be fully "preserved" or is loss inherent?
      - What ethical frameworks should govern the use of AI-generated or -assisted choreography?
      - How do dance studies reconcile formalist (movement analysis) and culturalist (identity, power, meaning) approaches?
      - Can dance science and somatics move beyond injury prevention toward genuinely holistic training reform?
      - How should the economic model for concert dance adapt to post-pandemic realities: streaming, shortened seasons, gig economies?
      - What methodologies allow equitable comparative study across global dance forms without imposing Western analytical frameworks?

#### Film & Cinema — Full Depth

- **Foundations of Film**
  - Definition: a medium of moving images captured and projected (mechanical, digital) with sound, editing, and narrative or non-narrative structure
  - Film as art vs. entertainment vs. propaganda vs. documentation — ongoing debate since the medium's birth
  - Core elements: cinematography (camera, lens, exposure, movement), mise-en-scène (staging, set design, costume, lighting), sound (dialogue, music, effects, silence), editing (montage, continuity), performance/acting, narrative/screenplay
  - Frame rate: 24 fps standard (cinema), 25 fps (PAL), 30 fps (NTSC); HFR experiments (48 fps — *The Hobbit*, Peter Jackson 2012; 120 fps — *Gemini Man*, Ang Lee 2019)
  - Aspect ratios: Academy ratio 1.37:1 (pre-1953), widescreen 1.85:1, CinemaScope/anamorphic 2.35:1 / 2.39:1, IMAX 1.43:1; artistic and historical significance of ratio choices
  - Celluloid vs. digital: 35mm, 16mm, Super 8, 65mm/70mm (IMAX); digital revolution (RED One 2007; Arri Alexa 2010); ongoing celluloid preservation — Kodak, Christopher Nolan, Quentin Tarantino as advocates
  - The persistence of vision myth vs. phi phenomenon / beta movement (Wertheimer 1912) — actual perceptual mechanism behind motion pictures

- **Pre-Cinema & Origins (pre-1895)**
  - **Optical toys and proto-cinema**
    - Camera obscura: ancient concept (Mozi, Alhazen); Renaissance use by painters (Vermeer hypothesis, David Hockney 2001)
    - Thaumatrope (John Ayrton Paris, 1825); Phenakistoscope (Joseph Plateau, 1832); Zoetrope (William Horner, 1834; popularized 1860s); Praxinoscope (Émile Reynaud, 1877) — *Théâtre Optique* (1892): projected animated shows
    - Magic lantern shows (17th century onward): Athanasius Kircher; phantasmagoria (Étienne-Gaspard Robertson, 1790s) — projected horror shows, precursor to horror cinema
  - **Photography as precursor**
    - Nicéphore Niépce: *View from the Window at Le Gras* (~1826–27), first photograph
    - Daguerreotype (Louis Daguerre, 1839); calotype (Henry Fox Talbot)
    - Eadweard Muybridge: *The Horse in Motion* (1878) — sequential photography proving all hooves leave ground; zoopraxiscope
    - Étienne-Jules Marey: chronophotography (1882) — studying animal locomotion; single-camera sequential exposure; direct influence on cinema
    - George Eastman: roll film (1888) — enabling continuous-strip photography
  - **Edison and the Kinetoscope**
    - Thomas Edison and W. K. L. Dickson: Kinetoscope (1891, patented 1893) — peephole individual viewing device
    - Kinetograph camera; Black Maria studio (West Orange, NJ, 1893) — first purpose-built film studio
    - Edison's commercial model: individual viewer at nickelodeon parlors
    - *Fred Ott's Sneeze* (1894) — earliest surviving copyrighted film
  - **Lumière Brothers and the birth of cinema**
    - Auguste and Louis Lumière: Cinématographe (1895) — combined camera, printer, projector; portable, hand-cranked
    - First public screening: 28 December 1895, Grand Café, Paris — ten short films including *Workers Leaving the Lumière Factory*, *Arrival of a Train at La Ciotat*
    - Myth of audience panic at *Arrival of a Train* — likely apocryphal but culturally persistent
    - Lumières as documentarians: "actuality" films; camera operators sent worldwide (Alexandre Promio); global distribution of the medium within months
  - **Georges Méliès and early narrative**
    - Stage magician turned filmmaker; Star Film Company
    - Special effects pioneer: stop-trick substitution, multiple exposures, dissolves, hand-painted color
    - *A Trip to the Moon* (*Le Voyage dans la Lune*, 1902) — iconic; adapted Jules Verne and H. G. Wells; 14 minutes; proto-science fiction
    - Over 500 films produced; financial ruin by 1913; rediscovered by film historians in 1930s; Martin Scorsese's *Hugo* (2011) as tribute
    - Méliès as father of narrative cinema and special effects

- **Silent Era (1895–1927)**
  - **Early development and industrialization**
    - Nickelodeons (1905–1915): storefront theatres, 5-cent admission; working-class audiences; thousands across US by 1910
    - Edison's Motion Picture Patents Company (MPPC, 1908–1915, the "Trust"): monopolistic control of cameras, film stock, distribution; led independents to flee to California/Hollywood
    - Shift from single-shot "actualities" to multi-shot narratives: Georges Méliès, Edwin S. Porter (*The Great Train Robbery*, 1903 — cross-cutting, on-location shooting, genre: Western)
    - Feature-length films emerge: *The Story of the Kelly Gang* (Australia, 1906); Italian epics (*Cabiria*, Giovanni Pastrone, 1914)
  - **D. W. Griffith and the grammar of cinema**
    - David Wark Griffith (1875–1948): single most important figure in developing film language
    - Biograph shorts (1908–1913): systematic experimentation with close-ups, cross-cutting ("Griffith last-minute rescue"), fade-outs, iris shots, long shots
    - *The Birth of a Nation* (1915): 3-hour epic; revolutionary technique (battle sequences, tracking shots, orchestral score); deeply racist content glorifying the Ku Klux Klan; mass protests by NAACP; enduring debate about art vs. morality
    - *Intolerance* (1916): four parallel stories across millennia; massive Babylonian set; financial failure; formal ambition unprecedented
    - Legacy: codified continuity editing grammar; trained generation of actors and crew; personal decline and marginalization by 1930s
  - **Chaplin, Keaton, Lloyd — Comedy Giants**
    - Charlie Chaplin (1889–1977): the Tramp persona; *The Kid* (1921), *The Gold Rush* (1925), *The Circus* (1928); pathos + physical comedy; co-founded United Artists (1919, with Griffith, Pickford, Fairbanks); later: *City Lights* (1931), *Modern Times* (1936), *The Great Dictator* (1940); McCarthy-era exile; Knighthood 1975; Oscar 1972
    - Buster Keaton (1895–1966): "The Great Stone Face"; extraordinary physical stunts performed personally; *Sherlock Jr.* (1924 — metafilm), *The General* (1926 — Civil War epic, now considered one of the greatest films); decline in MGM era; rediscovery 1950s–60s
    - Harold Lloyd (1893–1971): "glasses character"; *Safety Last!* (1923, building-climbing sequence); most commercially successful comedian of the silent era; less critical reputation than Chaplin/Keaton but enormous influence
    - Other comedians: Roscoe "Fatty" Arbuckle (scandal 1921 — one of cinema's first tabloid scandals); Mack Sennett and Keystone Kops; Harry Langdon; Laurel and Hardy (spanning silent to sound)
  - **German Expressionism (1919–1927)**
    - Context: Weimar Republic instability; psychological trauma of WWI; Expressionist movement in painting, theatre, literature
    - *The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari* (Robert Wiene, 1920): distorted sets, painted shadows, unreliable narrator; foundational horror/art film; "frame story" debate (Kracauer: twist added by producers, weakening subversive intent)
    - F. W. Murnau: *Nosferatu* (1922, unauthorized Dracula adaptation; Max Schreck); *The Last Laugh* (1924 — "unchained camera"/entfesselte Kamera, entirely without intertitles); *Sunrise: A Song of Two Humans* (1927 — first Academy Award for "Unique and Artistic Picture"; virtuoso tracking shots)
    - Fritz Lang: *Dr. Mabuse the Gambler* (1922), *Die Nibelungen* (1924), *Metropolis* (1927 — dystopian sci-fi, robot Maria, massive production; commercial failure; heavily cut; 2010 restored version with recovered Argentine footage); *M* (1931 — sound era, Peter Lorre as child killer; psychological depth)
    - Paul Wegener: *The Golem* (1920); Ernst Lubitsch: historical spectacles before Hollywood; G. W. Pabst: *Pandora's Box* (1929, Louise Brooks)
    - Siegfried Kracauer, *From Caligari to Hitler* (1947): argued Expressionist films prefigured Nazi authoritarianism — influential but debated thesis
    - Influence: film noir visual style; Tim Burton; Guillermo del Toro; horror genre aesthetics
  - **Soviet Montage (1920s)**
    - Context: Russian Revolution 1917; Lenin: "Of all the arts, for us cinema is the most important"; state support for propaganda film; Narkompros and Goskino
    - Lev Kuleshov: Kuleshov effect (1918–1922 experiments) — same shot of actor's face intercut with different objects creates different perceived emotions; foundational demonstration that editing creates meaning
    - Sergei Eisenstein (1898–1948): theorist-practitioner; developed "montage of attractions" — collision of shots creates new meaning (dialectical montage)
      - *Strike* (1925): workers' struggle; mass protagonists; Eisenstein's debut
      - *Battleship Potemkin* (1925): Odessa Steps sequence — iconic; time-expansion through editing; baby carriage; studied in every film school
      - *October* (1928): recreation of Revolution; intellectual montage (God and Country sequence)
      - *Alexander Nevsky* (1938): sound-era collaboration with Prokofiev; "audio-visual counterpoint"
      - *Ivan the Terrible* Part I (1944) and Part II (1946, released 1958): baroque compositions; Stalin allegory; color sequence in Part II
      - Writings: "The Cinematographic Principle and the Ideogram" (1929); *Film Form* (1949); *The Film Sense* (1942)
    - Dziga Vertov (1896–1954): *Kino-Eye* doctrine — camera as more perfect than human eye; rejected scripted narrative
      - *Man with a Movie Camera* (1929): documentary/city symphony; meta-cinematographic (shows its own making); split screens, slow motion, freeze frames, superimpositions; regularly voted among greatest films ever made
      - Kinopravda newsreels; influence on cinéma vérité, direct cinema, essay film
    - Vsevolod Pudovkin: *Mother* (1926), *The End of St. Petersburg* (1927); "linkage" theory of editing (constructive, versus Eisenstein's collision)
    - Alexander Dovzhenko: *Earth* (1930) — Ukrainian rural life; poetic realism; pantheistic imagery
    - Decline: Stalinist Socialist Realism imposed from early 1930s; Eisenstein and Vertov marginalized; formalist experimentation suppressed
  - **French Impressionism and Avant-Garde (1918–1930)**
    - Abel Gance: *J'accuse* (1919), *La Roue* (1923 — rapid editing), *Napoléon* (1927 — triptych Polyvision widescreen, hand-held camera, avant-garde techniques; restored by Kevin Brownlow over decades)
    - Germaine Dulac: *The Seashell and the Clergyman* (1928) — often cited as first Surrealist film (predates Buñuel)
    - Jean Epstein: *The Fall of the House of Usher* (1928); theorist of "photogénie" — cinema's ability to reveal hidden qualities of objects
    - Marcel L'Herbier: *L'Inhumaine* (1924) — Art Deco design; modernist aesthetic
    - Luis Buñuel and Salvador Dalí: *Un Chien Andalou* (1929) — Surrealist short; eye-slicing scene; dream logic; *L'Âge d'Or* (1930) — scandal and ban
    - René Clair: *Entr'acte* (1924) — Dadaist short; *Paris qui dort* (1925)
    - Theoretical context: Louis Delluc: coined "cinéaste"; introduced serious film criticism/cinephilia in France
  - **Scandinavian Cinema**
    - Swedish Golden Age: Victor Sjöström (*The Phantom Carriage*, 1921 — influence on Bergman); Mauritz Stiller (*The Saga of Gösta Berling*, 1924 — launched Greta Garbo's career)
    - Danish cinema: Carl Th. Dreyer: *The Passion of Joan of Arc* (1928) — extreme close-ups, Renée Falconetti's performance widely considered the greatest in silent film; minimal sets; spiritual intensity
    - Benjamin Christensen: *Häxan* (1922) — docufiction about witchcraft
  - **Hollywood studio consolidation (1920s)**
    - Star system: Mary Pickford ("America's Sweetheart"), Douglas Fairbanks (*The Thief of Bagdad* 1924), Rudolph Valentino, Gloria Swanson, Lon Chaney Sr. ("Man of a Thousand Faces")
    - Studios consolidate: Paramount (Adolph Zukor), MGM (Mayer + Thalberg), Fox, Warner Bros., Universal, Columbia, United Artists
    - Hays Code precursor: Hays Office established 1922 after Arbuckle scandal and other controversies; voluntary self-censorship

- **The Transition to Sound (1927–1935)**
  - **Technology**
    - Early experiments: Phonofilm (Lee de Forest, 1919–1923); sound-on-disc vs. sound-on-film
    - Vitaphone (sound-on-disc): Warner Bros.; *Don Juan* (1926, orchestral score synced); *The Jazz Singer* (6 October 1927, Al Jolson — first feature with synchronized dialogue sequences; "Wait a minute, wait a minute, you ain't heard nothin' yet!")
    - Sound-on-film systems: Fox Movietone (Tri-Ergon patents); RCA Photophone — ultimately industry standard
    - Western Electric and ERPI: sound equipment installation in theatres; conversion cost bankrupted many independents
  - **Industry upheaval**
    - Many silent stars unable to transition (accent issues, voice quality, performance style unsuited to microphones): John Gilbert, Norma Talmadge, Vilma Bánky
    - Directors forced to rethink: early sound films static (cameras enclosed in soundproof booths; actors tethered to microphones); "canned theatre" criticism
    - Musical genre explosion: *The Broadway Melody* (1929, first Best Picture sound film); Busby Berkeley choreography spectacles (*42nd Street*, 1933; *Gold Diggers of 1933*)
    - International impact: language barriers created national cinema revivals; dubbing vs. subtitling debate begins; multi-language version production (MLVs) briefly attempted (Paramount's Joinville studio)
    - End of universal language of silent film: loss of international comprehensibility
  - **Early sound masters**
    - Ernst Lubitsch: "The Lubitsch Touch" — sophisticated romantic comedies (*Trouble in Paradise*, 1932; *Design for Living*, 1933); seamless integration of sound and visual wit; *To Be or Not to Be* (1942)
    - Josef von Sternberg and Marlene Dietrich: *The Blue Angel* (1930, Germany), *Morocco* (1930), *Shanghai Express* (1932) — chiaroscuro lighting, exotic settings, erotic tension
    - James Whale: *Frankenstein* (1931), *Bride of Frankenstein* (1935) — Universal Horror cycle: also *Dracula* (Tod Browning, Bela Lugosi 1931), *The Mummy* (Karl Freund, Boris Karloff 1932), *The Wolf Man* (George Waggner, Lon Chaney Jr. 1941)
    - Jean Vigo: *Zéro de conduite* (1933 — boarding school rebellion; banned), *L'Atalante* (1934 — barge life; poetic realism masterpiece); died at 29

- **Classical Hollywood / Studio System (1930–1960)**
  - **The Studio System structure**
    - "Big Five" (vertically integrated: production, distribution, exhibition): Paramount, MGM, 20th Century Fox, Warner Bros., RKO
    - "Little Three": Universal, Columbia, United Artists (distribution only)
    - Vertical integration: studios owned theatre chains; block booking (forcing exhibitors to buy packages of films including B-movies); run-zone-clearance system
    - *United States v. Paramount Pictures, Inc.* (1948, "Paramount Decree"): Supreme Court antitrust case; forced studios to divest theatre ownership; began end of studio system
    - Contract system: actors, directors, writers, technicians under long-term exclusive contracts; studio control over careers; discipline/punishment through loan-outs and suspension
  - **Production Code / Hays Code (1934–1968)**
    - Motion Picture Production Code (written by Martin Quigley and Fr. Daniel Lord SJ, 1930; enforced from 1934 by Joseph Breen's Production Code Administration)
    - Strict rules: no explicit sexuality, miscegenation, profanity, disrespect for clergy, unpunished crime, excessive violence
    - Artistic consequence: filmmakers developed subtext, innuendo, coded imagery; "staircase" shots substituting for sex scenes; villains always punished
    - Pre-Code era (1930–1934): relatively frank sexuality and violence — Mae West (*She Done Him Wrong*, 1933); *Baby Face* (1933); *Scarface* (1932, Howard Hawks); *Freaks* (Tod Browning, 1932)
    - Catholic Legion of Decency (1933): boycott threats enforcing code compliance; intersection of religion and media censorship
  - **Key genres of Classical Hollywood**
    - **Western**: John Ford (*Stagecoach* 1939, *My Darling Clementine* 1946, *The Searchers* 1956 — complex racism, Ethan Edwards/John Wayne; *The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance* 1962); Howard Hawks (*Red River* 1948, *Rio Bravo* 1959); Anthony Mann psychological Westerns; revisionist Westerns later (Sam Peckinpah *The Wild Bunch* 1969)
    - **Musical**: Fred Astaire and Ginger Rogers (RKO, 1930s — *Top Hat* 1935); Gene Kelly (*Singin' in the Rain* 1952 — beloved classic; *An American in Paris* 1951); Vincente Minnelli (*Meet Me in St. Louis* 1944); Judy Garland; MGM's Freed Unit as musical cinema's peak
    - **Film Noir** (term coined by French critic Nino Frank, 1946; elaborated by Raymond Borde and Étienne Chaumeton, *Panorama du film noir américain*, 1955)
      - Visual style: chiaroscuro lighting, Dutch angles, rain-slicked streets, shadows; drawn from German Expressionism and hard-boiled American fiction (Hammett, Chandler, Cain)
      - Thematic concerns: moral ambiguity, fatalism, doomed protagonists, femme fatale, corruption, postwar disillusionment
      - Key films: *Double Indemnity* (Billy Wilder, 1944), *The Maltese Falcon* (John Huston, 1941), *Laura* (Otto Preminger, 1944), *The Big Sleep* (Howard Hawks, 1946), *Out of the Past* (Jacques Tourneur, 1947), *Sunset Boulevard* (Wilder, 1950), *Touch of Evil* (Orson Welles, 1958), *The Third Man* (Carol Reed, 1949, Vienna, zither score, Orson Welles)
      - Neo-noir: revival from 1970s onward — *Chinatown* (Roman Polanski 1974), *Body Heat* (Lawrence Kasdan 1981), *L.A. Confidential* (Curtis Hanson 1997), *Mulholland Drive* (David Lynch 2001), *Sin City* (Rodriguez/Miller 2005)
      - Debate: is noir a genre, a style, a cycle, or a mood? (scholars disagree; James Naremore *More Than Night* 1998)
    - **Screwball comedy**: rapid-fire dialogue, battle of the sexes, class comedy; *It Happened One Night* (Frank Capra, 1934 — swept all five major Oscars), *Bringing Up Baby* (Hawks, 1938), *His Girl Friday* (Hawks, 1940), *The Philadelphia Story* (George Cukor, 1940), *The Lady Eve* (Preston Sturges, 1941)
    - **Melodrama/Women's film**: Douglas Sirk (*All That Heaven Allows* 1955, *Imitation of Life* 1959 — Fassbinder later called Sirk "the most important director"), *Now, Voyager* (Irving Rapper, 1942, Bette Davis), *Mildred Pierce* (Michael Curtiz, 1945, Joan Crawford); "excessive" style later reclaimed by feminist critics
    - **Horror**: Universal cycle (1930s–40s); Val Lewton's RKO horror unit (producer) — *Cat People* (Jacques Tourneur, 1942) — suggestive horror, shadows; low budget, high artistry
    - **War film**: *Mrs. Miniver* (William Wyler, 1942); *Casablanca* (Michael Curtiz, 1942 — Humphrey Bogart, Ingrid Bergman; wartime propaganda and romantic classic); later: *The Bridge on the River Kwai* (David Lean, 1957)
    - **Animation**: Walt Disney: *Steamboat Willie* (1928), *Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs* (1937 — first US feature-length cel animation), *Fantasia* (1940 — classical music anthology; experimental), *Bambi* (1942), multiplane camera; Disney studio as animation factory and brand; Warner Bros. Looney Tunes (Tex Avery, Chuck Jones — *What's Opera, Doc?* 1957); Fleischer Studios (*Betty Boop*, *Popeye*, *Superman* 1941)
  - **Orson Welles and *Citizen Kane***
    - Orson Welles (1915–1985): theatre prodigy (Mercury Theatre; War of the Worlds 1938 radio broadcast panic)
    - *Citizen Kane* (1941): deep focus cinematography (Gregg Toland), low-angle ceilings, non-linear narrative, newsreel pastiche, composite characterization (William Randolph Hearst model); Hearst attempted to suppress it; commercial disappointment; gradually recognized as the greatest film ever made (Sight & Sound polls: #1 in 1962, 1972, 1982, 1992, 2002; displaced by *Vertigo* in 2012; *Jeanne Dielman* in 2022)
    - *The Magnificent Ambersons* (1942): studio re-edited against Welles's wishes; lost footage; Welles's lifelong battle with studios
    - Later Welles: *The Lady from Shanghai* (1948), *Touch of Evil* (1958, restored 1998 per Welles's memo), *Chimes at Midnight* (1965), *F for Fake* (1973 — essay film); *The Other Side of the Wind* (posthumously completed via Netflix, 2018)
    - Welles as archetypal auteur-martyr: genius vs. system narrative
  - **Alfred Hitchcock**
    - Alfred Hitchcock (1899–1980): "Master of Suspense"; British career (1925–1939): *The Lodger* (1927), *Blackmail* (1929 — first British sound film thriller), *The 39 Steps* (1935), *The Lady Vanishes* (1938)
    - Hollywood career: *Rebecca* (1940, Best Picture), *Shadow of a Doubt* (1943), *Notorious* (1946 — Ingrid Bergman, Cary Grant, Claude Rains; champagne bottle scene), *Rope* (1948 — long-take experiment), *Strangers on a Train* (1951), *Rear Window* (1954 — voyeurism/cinema metaphor; James Stewart, Grace Kelly), *Vertigo* (1958 — obsession, identity, the male gaze; Sight & Sound #1 2012), *North by Northwest* (1959 — crop-duster, Mount Rushmore), *Psycho* (1960 — shower scene; killed star 47 minutes in; black-and-white choice; Norman Bates/Anthony Perkins), *The Birds* (1963), *Marnie* (1964), *Frenzy* (1972)
    - Techniques: the MacGuffin (plot device motivating characters but irrelevant to audience); "bomb under the table" suspense theory; cameo appearances; storyboard-precise visual planning; "pure cinema"
    - François Truffaut's book-length interview *Hitchcock/Truffaut* (1966): elevated Hitchcock from entertainer to artist in critical estimation; one of the most influential books on cinema
    - Feminist readings: Laura Mulvey's "Visual Pleasure and Narrative Cinema" (1975) uses Hitchcock (especially *Vertigo* and *Rear Window*) as primary examples of the "male gaze"; Tania Modleski *The Women Who Knew Too Much* (1988) recuperative counter-reading
  - **Other Classical Hollywood masters**
    - John Huston: *The Maltese Falcon* (1941), *The Treasure of the Sierra Madre* (1948), *The African Queen* (1951), *The Asphalt Jungle* (1950)
    - Billy Wilder: *Double Indemnity* (1944), *The Lost Weekend* (1945), *Sunset Boulevard* (1950), *Some Like It Hot* (1959 — AFI's funniest American film), *The Apartment* (1960)
    - Howard Hawks: genre versatility — comedies, Westerns, noirs, adventure; "Hawksian woman" archetype (Lauren Bacall); *His Girl Friday*, *The Big Sleep*, *Rio Bravo*
    - Frank Capra: *It Happened One Night* (1934), *Mr. Smith Goes to Washington* (1939), *It's a Wonderful Life* (1946 — commercial failure, later beloved; copyright lapse enabled TV saturation); populist idealism
    - William Wyler: *The Best Years of Our Lives* (1946 — returning WWII veterans; deep focus, long takes; three Oscars), *Roman Holiday* (1953), *Ben-Hur* (1959 — 11 Oscars, chariot race)
    - George Cukor: "women's director"; *The Women* (1939), *Gaslight* (1944), *A Star Is Born* (1954, Judy Garland), *My Fair Lady* (1964)
    - Michael Powell and Emeric Pressburger (The Archers, UK): *The Red Shoes* (1948 — ballet and color; influence on Scorsese, Coppola), *A Matter of Life and Death* (1946), *Black Narcissus* (1947), *Peeping Tom* (Powell solo, 1960 — voyeurism; destroyed Powell's career; rehabilitated as masterpiece)

- **Postwar European Art Cinema (1945–1975)**
  - **Italian Neorealism (1943–1952)**
    - Context: WWII devastation; fall of Fascism; desire for authentic depiction of Italian life
    - Principles: location shooting (not studio sets), non-professional actors, working-class/poor subjects, episodic narratives, documentary aesthetic, social engagement
    - Roberto Rossellini: *Rome, Open City* (1945 — Nazi occupation; shot with limited film stock; immediacy), *Paisà* (1946), *Germany, Year Zero* (1948); later: *Stromboli* (1950, Ingrid Bergman affair/scandal), spiritual trilogy (*The Flowers of St. Francis* 1950, *Voyage to Italy* 1954 — proto-modernist; enormous influence on Nouvelle Vague)
    - Vittorio De Sica and screenwriter Cesare Zavattini: *Shoeshine* (1946), *Bicycle Thieves* (1948 — man and boy search for stolen bicycle; devastating ending; one of the most acclaimed films ever), *Umberto D.* (1952 — old man and his dog; pure "following" camera)
    - Luchino Visconti: *Ossessione* (1943 — often considered the first Neorealist film; adaptation of James M. Cain), *La Terra Trema* (1948 — Sicilian fishermen; entire cast non-professional; Marxist analysis)
    - Legacy: influenced virtually every subsequent realist movement worldwide — French New Wave, British Kitchen Sink, Iranian New Wave, Dogme 95
  - **Akira Kurosawa and Japanese Cinema**
    - Akira Kurosawa (1910–1998): "Emperor" of Japanese cinema; bridge between East and West
    - *Rashomon* (1950): multiple contradictory perspectives on a crime; introduced Japanese cinema to Western audiences (Venice Golden Lion 1951); "Rashomon effect" entered language
    - *Ikiru* (1952): bureaucrat facing death finds meaning; humanist masterpiece
    - *Seven Samurai* (1954): 3.5-hour action epic; samurai defend village; template for ensemble action films; remade as *The Magnificent Seven* (1960)
    - *Throne of Blood* (1957, Macbeth), *The Hidden Fortress* (1958 — direct influence on *Star Wars*), *Yojimbo* (1961 — remade as *A Fistful of Dollars*), *High and Low* (1963), *Ran* (1985 — King Lear; color epic)
    - Other Japanese masters: Kenji Mizoguchi (*Ugetsu* 1953, *Sansho the Bailiff* 1954 — long takes, tracking shots, feminine suffering); Yasujirō Ozu (*Tokyo Story* 1953 — domestic drama; tatami-level camera; pillow shots; "transcendental style" — Paul Schrader's book, 1972); Masaki Kobayashi (*Harakiri* 1962, *Kwaidan* 1964); Kon Ichikawa (*The Burmese Harp* 1956)
    - Japanese studio system: Toho, Shochiku, Daiei, Nikkatsu; genre cinema: *kaiju* (Godzilla/Gojira, Honda 1954 — nuclear anxiety allegory), *chanbara* (sword films), *yakuza* films
  - **Ingmar Bergman**
    - Ingmar Bergman (1918–2007): Swedish; theatre and film; existential/psychological/spiritual themes
    - *The Seventh Seal* (1957 — knight plays chess with Death; Black Plague; iconic imagery), *Wild Strawberries* (1957 — aging professor's road trip/memory), *Persona* (1966 — two women's identities merge; radical formal experimentation; faces superimposed; fourth wall broken), *Cries and Whispers* (1972), *Scenes from a Marriage* (1973), *Fanny and Alexander* (1982)
    - "Trilogy of Faith": *Through a Glass Darkly* (1961), *Winter Light* (1963), *The Silence* (1963) — God's silence
    - Collaborators: cinematographer Sven Nykvist (extraordinary natural lighting); actresses Liv Ullmann, Bibi Andersson, Harriet Andersson, Ingrid Thulin
    - Influence: Woody Allen (self-described Bergman disciple), Lars von Trier, Michael Haneke, Andrei Tarkovsky debate/dialogue
  - **French New Wave (Nouvelle Vague, 1958–1967)**
    - Origins: *Cahiers du cinéma* critics (André Bazin founded magazine 1951; young critics: Truffaut, Godard, Chabrol, Rohmer, Rivette) attacked "Tradition of Quality" / "cinema of papa" — literary adaptations, polished studio productions
    - François Truffaut: "A Certain Tendency of the French Cinema" (1954 essay) — manifesto against screenwriter-dominated cinema; championed *politique des auteurs* (auteur policy)
    - **Auteur theory**: the director as primary creative author of a film; signatures across a body of work (visual style, thematic obsessions); adapted for Anglo-American criticism by Andrew Sarris (*The American Cinema*, 1968); debated ever since (Pauline Kael's "Circles and Squares" 1963 rebuttal)
    - **Key films and directors**:
      - François Truffaut: *The 400 Blows* (1959 — semi-autobiographical; Antoine Doinel; freeze-frame ending), *Jules and Jim* (1962 — love triangle; Jeanne Moreau; narrative freedom), *Day for Night* (1973 — filmmaking itself)
      - Jean-Luc Godard (1930–2022): *Breathless* (*À bout de souffle*, 1960 — jump cuts, improvisation, Jean-Paul Belmondo; Hollywood genre + European art film collision), *Vivre sa vie* (1962), *Contempt* (*Le Mépris*, 1963 — filmmaking allegory; Bardot, Fritz Lang cameo), *Band of Outsiders* (*Bande à part*, 1964), *Pierrot le fou* (1965), *Alphaville* (1965), *Masculin Féminin* (1966), *Weekend* (1967 — "End of Cinema" title card); post-1968: Dziga Vertov Group, Maoist political filmmaking; late period: *Histoire(s) du cinéma* (1988–1998 — video essay masterwork); *Film Socialisme* (2010); *The Image Book* (2018)
      - Agnès Varda (1928–2019): "Grandmother of the New Wave" (predated male colleagues); *Cléo from 5 to 7* (1962 — real-time anxiety; female subjectivity), *Vagabond* (1985), *The Gleaners and I* (2000 — digital essay film); married to Jacques Demy (*The Umbrellas of Cherbourg* 1964)
      - Claude Chabrol: *Le Beau Serge* (1958 — often considered first New Wave feature), *Les Cousins* (1959); later: Hitchcockian bourgeois thrillers
      - Éric Rohmer: *Six Moral Tales* (1963–1972), *Comedies and Proverbs* (1981–1987), *Tales of the Four Seasons* (1990–1998); philosophical conversation films
      - Jacques Rivette: *Céline and Julie Go Boating* (1974 — 3+ hours; ludic, labyrinthine); *Out 1* (1971 — 13 hours)
      - Chris Marker: *La Jetée* (1962 — science fiction told almost entirely in still photographs; time travel; inspired *12 Monkeys*); *Sans Soleil* (1983 — essayistic travelogue)
    - **Formal innovations**: jump cuts, direct address to camera, hand-held shooting, location sound, natural lighting, long takes, improvised dialogue, non-linear narratives, reflexivity (films about filmmaking), intertextual cinephilia (quoting earlier cinema)
    - Left Bank group: Alain Resnais (*Hiroshima mon amour* 1959 — Marguerite Duras script; memory/time; *Last Year at Marienbad* 1961 — Robbe-Grillet script; ambiguity), Chris Marker, Agnès Varda — more literary, politically engaged, formally experimental than Cahiers group
  - **Other European movements**
    - **British New Wave / Kitchen Sink Realism** (1958–1963): working-class realism; Tony Richardson (*Look Back in Anger* 1959, *The Loneliness of the Long Distance Runner* 1962), Karel Reisz (*Saturday Night and Sunday Morning* 1960), Lindsay Anderson (*This Sporting Life* 1963, *If....* 1968); Free Cinema documentary movement (Anderson, Reisz, 1956–1959 manifestos)
    - **New German Cinema (1960s–1980s)**: Oberhausen Manifesto 1962 ("Papa's cinema is dead"); Rainer Werner Fassbinder (prolific: *Ali: Fear Eats the Soul* 1974, *The Marriage of Maria Braun* 1979, *Berlin Alexanderplatz* 1980 — 15.5-hour TV film), Werner Herzog (*Aguirre, the Wrath of God* 1972, *Fitzcarraldo* 1982 — actual steamship hauled over mountain; *Grizzly Man* 2005), Wim Wenders (*Paris, Texas* 1984, *Wings of Desire* 1987), Volker Schlöndorff (*The Tin Drum* 1979), Margarethe von Trotta (political feminist films; *The Lost Honor of Katharina Blum* 1975 co-directed with Schlöndorff; *Marianne and Juliane* 1981)
    - **Eastern European cinema**: Polish School (Andrzej Wajda: war trilogy — *A Generation* 1955, *Kanał* 1957, *Ashes and Diamonds* 1958); Czechoslovak New Wave (Miloš Forman: *Loves of a Blonde* 1965; Věra Chytilová: *Daisies* 1966; Jiří Menzel: *Closely Watched Trains* 1966); Hungarian (Miklós Jancsó: *The Round-Up* 1966 — long-take choreography; István Szabó: *Mephisto* 1981); Yugoslav Black Wave (Dušan Makavejev: *WR: Mysteries of the Organism* 1971)
  - **Andrei Tarkovsky (1932–1986)**
    - Russian/Soviet; spiritual-philosophical filmmaker; radically slow pacing, long takes, water/fire/earth imagery
    - *Ivan's Childhood* (1962), *Andrei Rublev* (1966 — medieval icon painter; shelved until 1969; bell-casting finale), *Solaris* (1972 — "Soviet response to 2001"), *Mirror* (1975 — autobiographical/dreamlike/non-linear; poetry of Arseny Tarkovsky), *Stalker* (1979 — damaged color film; "the Zone"; metaphysical quest), *Nostalghia* (1983, Italy), *The Sacrifice* (1986, Sweden — made while dying of cancer)
    - *Sculpting in Time* (1967 book): cinema as "imprinting time" — film's unique ability is to record time passing
    - Influence: Béla Tarr, Terrence Malick, Nuri Bilge Ceylan, Apichatpong Weerasethakul; cinephile icon; voted greatest director of all time in multiple directors' polls

- **New Hollywood / American Renaissance (1967–1980)**
  - **Context and causes**
    - Studio system collapse (Paramount Decree 1948; declining audiences post-TV); old moguls dying/retiring; massive flops of bloated spectacles (*Cleopatra* 1963)
    - Youth culture: baby boomers, counterculture, Vietnam War, civil rights, sexual revolution, drug culture
    - MPAA rating system replaces Hays Code (1968): G, M, R, X — allows mature content; Jack Valenti
    - Influence of European art cinema on film-school generation (UCLA, USC, NYU)
  - **Key directors and films**
    - Arthur Penn: *Bonnie and Clyde* (1967 — graphic violence; anti-establishment; Warren Beatty, Faye Dunaway; pivotal film)
    - Mike Nichols: *The Graduate* (1967 — generational alienation; Dustin Hoffman; Simon & Garfunkel soundtrack)
    - Dennis Hopper: *Easy Rider* (1969 — counterculture road movie; Peter Fonda; Jack Nicholson breakout; low budget, massive return; proved youth market)
    - Francis Ford Coppola: *The Godfather* (1972) and *The Godfather Part II* (1974 — dual timeline; first sequel to win Best Picture), *The Conversation* (1974 — surveillance paranoia; Watergate era), *Apocalypse Now* (1979 — Vietnam War; Heart of Darkness adaptation; *Hearts of Darkness* documentary 1991); American Zoetrope studio; financial implosion (*One from the Heart* 1982)
    - Martin Scorsese: *Mean Streets* (1973 — Little Italy; De Niro/Keitel; kinetic energy), *Taxi Driver* (1976 — Travis Bickle; Bernard Herrmann's last score; Paul Schrader script), *Raging Bull* (1980 — black-and-white; De Niro's physical transformation; boxing as self-destruction; often voted best film of the 1980s); later career spans decades — *Goodfellas* (1990), *Casino* (1995), *Gangs of New York* (2002), *The Departed* (2006 — finally won Oscar), *The Wolf of Wall Street* (2013), *The Irishman* (2019 — Netflix; digital de-aging), *Killers of the Flower Moon* (2023)
    - Steven Spielberg: *Duel* (1971 TV), *Jaws* (1975 — created the summer blockbuster; mechanical shark; $470M worldwide; studio/audience dynamic changed), *Close Encounters of the Third Kind* (1977), *Raiders of the Lost Ark* (1981), *E.T.* (1982), *Schindler's List* (1993 — Holocaust; black-and-white; girl in red coat), *Saving Private Ryan* (1998 — D-Day sequence), *Munich* (2005), *The Fabelmans* (2022 — autobiographical); most commercially successful director in history; debates about populism vs. artistry
    - George Lucas: *American Graffiti* (1973), *Star Wars* (1977 — science fiction/fantasy; Joseph Campbell's monomyth; revolutionary VFX — Industrial Light & Magic founded; changed cinema: blockbuster model, merchandising, sequels-as-franchise; cultural phenomenon); THX-1138 (1971 — debut; dystopian); shifted to producing/technology (Lucasfilm, ILM, Pixar founding, digital cinema advocacy)
    - Robert Altman: *M*A*S*H* (1970), *McCabe & Mrs. Miller* (1971 — revisionist Western; foggy, overlapping dialogue), *Nashville* (1975 — multi-strand narrative; 24 characters; country music America), *The Long Goodbye* (1973), *Short Cuts* (1993), *Gosford Park* (2001); overlapping dialogue trademark; ensemble casts; anti-stitorial style
    - William Friedkin: *The French Connection* (1971 — car chase; gritty realism), *The Exorcist* (1973 — horror as mainstream prestige; shocking for era)
    - Terrence Malick: *Badlands* (1973), *Days of Heaven* (1978 — magic hour photography by Néstor Almendros); vanished for 20 years; returned: *The Thin Red Line* (1998), *The Tree of Life* (2011 — Palme d'Or; cosmic scope; Malick's most ambitious), *A Hidden Life* (2019)
    - Brian De Palma: Hitchcock-influenced thrillers (*Sisters* 1973, *Obsession* 1976, *Dressed to Kill* 1980, *Blow Out* 1981, *Scarface* 1983, *The Untouchables* 1987); split-screen, long takes
    - Hal Ashby: *Harold and Maude* (1971), *The Last Detail* (1973), *Shampoo* (1975), *Being There* (1979)
    - Sidney Lumet: *Serpico* (1973), *Dog Day Afternoon* (1975), *Network* (1976 — "I'm as mad as hell, and I'm not going to take this anymore!"; media satire more relevant than ever)
    - Woody Allen: comedy-to-art transition: *Annie Hall* (1977 — romantic comedy as art film; fourth-wall breaks; split screens), *Manhattan* (1979 — gorgeous B&W Gershwin-scored love letter to NYC), *Stardust Memories* (1980), *Zelig* (1983), *Hannah and Her Sisters* (1986), *Crimes and Misdemeanors* (1989); later: *Match Point* (2005), *Midnight in Paris* (2011); career overshadowed by abuse allegations (Dylan Farrow) — contested; critical reassessment ongoing
  - **Blaxploitation (1971–1979)**
    - Black-oriented genre films: *Sweet Sweetback's Baadasssss Song* (Melvin Van Peebles, 1971 — independent; Black Power; X-rated), *Shaft* (Gordon Parks, 1971 — Isaac Hayes score), *Super Fly* (Gordon Parks Jr., 1972, Curtis Mayfield score), *Coffy* and *Foxy Brown* (Pam Grier)
    - NAACP and civil rights groups criticized stereotypes; debated: exploitation or empowerment?
    - Influence: Tarantino, hip-hop culture, *Black Panther* lineage
  - **End of New Hollywood**
    - Blockbuster era: *Jaws* (1975) and *Star Wars* (1977) demonstrated massive returns from wide-release saturation marketing; studios shifted toward tentpole/franchise model
    - Director hubris/excess: *Heaven's Gate* (Michael Cimino 1980 — massive budget overrun; United Artists bankruptcy; cautionary tale; rehabilitated somewhat in 2012 director's cut); *Sorcerer* (Friedkin 1977); *1941* (Spielberg 1979); *New York, New York* (Scorsese 1977)
    - Power shift from directors back to studios and producers; rise of agents (Michael Ovitz, CAA)

- **Global Cinema Movements (1950s–present)**
  - **Indian Cinema**
    - Bollywood (Hindi-language industry, Mumbai): world's largest by output; song-and-dance "masala" films; star system (Raj Kapoor, Dilip Kumar, Dev Anand 1950s; Amitabh Bachchan 1970s–80s; Shah Rukh Khan, Aamir Khan 1990s–2020s); *Sholay* (1975 — iconic); *Dilwale Dulhania Le Jayenge* (1995 — longest-running Bollywood film); musical numbers integral to narrative
    - Parallel cinema/art cinema: Satyajit Ray (*Pather Panchali* 1955 — Apu Trilogy 1955–1959; humanist realism; international acclaim; influenced by Italian Neorealism, Jean Renoir), Ritwik Ghatak (*Meghe Dhaka Tara* 1960), Mrinal Sen (*Interview* 1971); later: Adoor Gopalakrishnan, Shyam Benegal, Mani Kaul, Kumar Shahani
    - South Indian cinemas: Tamil (Kollywood), Telugu (Tollywood), Kannada (Sandalwood), Malayalam; *Baahubali* (S. S. Rajamouli, 2015–2017 — pan-Indian blockbuster); *RRR* (2022 — global viral success; "Naatu Naatu" Oscar)
    - Documentary: Anand Patwardhan (political documentaries)
  - **Latin American Cinema**
    - Brazilian Cinema Novo (1960s): Glauber Rocha (*Black God, White Devil* 1964, *Antonio das Mortes* 1969; "an aesthetics of hunger" 1965 manifesto); Nelson Pereira dos Santos (*Vidas Secas* 1963); Walter Salles (*Central Station* 1998, *The Motorcycle Diaries* 2004)
    - Cuban revolutionary cinema: Tomás Gutiérrez Alea (*Memories of Underdevelopment* 1968); ICAIC (Instituto Cubano del Arte e Industria Cinematográficos); imperfect cinema theory (Julio García Espinosa)
    - Argentine cinema: Fernando Solanas and Octavio Getino: "Toward a Third Cinema" (1969 manifesto) — cinema as revolutionary weapon; *La hora de los hornos* (1968); later: Lucrecia Martel (*La Ciénaga* 2001, *Zama* 2017), Pablo Trapero
    - Mexican cinema: Luis Buñuel's Mexican period (*Los olvidados* 1950, *El ángel exterminador* 1962, *The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie* 1972); Arturo Ripstein; "Three Amigos" (Alfonso Cuarón, Alejandro González Iñárritu, Guillermo del Toro — all won Best Director Oscars in 2010s); *Amores Perros* (Iñárritu 2000); *Y Tu Mamá También* (Cuarón 2001); *Roma* (Cuarón 2018 — Netflix; black-and-white; autobiographical; Oscar)
  - **African Cinema**
    - Ousmane Sembène (1923–2007): "Father of African Cinema" (Senegal); *Black Girl* (*La Noire de...* 1966 — first feature by a sub-Saharan African director), *Xala* (1975), *Moolaadé* (2004 — female genital cutting)
    - Djibril Diop Mambéty: *Touki Bouki* (1973, Senegal — avant-garde classic); Souleymane Cissé: *Yeelen* (1987, Mali)
    - FESPACO (Festival panafricain du cinéma et de la télévision de Ouagadougou, Burkina Faso — biennial since 1969): premier African film festival
    - Nollywood (Nigeria): emerged 1990s; direct-to-video industry; second or third largest film industry by volume; digital production; *Living in Bondage* (1992); global diaspora audiences
    - North African: Youssef Chahine (Egypt; *Cairo Station* 1958); Moufida Tlatli (Tunisia; *The Silences of the Palace* 1994); Abderrahmane Sissako (Mauritania; *Bamako* 2006, *Timbuktu* 2014)
  - **East Asian Cinema**
    - Hong Kong: King Hu (*A Touch of Zen* 1971 — wuxia; Cannes); John Woo (*A Better Tomorrow* 1986, *The Killer* 1989, *Hard Boiled* 1992 — heroic bloodshed; "bullet ballet"); Wong Kar-wai (*Chungking Express* 1994, *In the Mood for Love* 2000 — unrequited desire; Christopher Doyle cinematography; step-printing; saturated color); Stephen Chow (*Shaolin Soccer* 2001, *Kung Fu Hustle* 2004)
    - Mainland China: Fifth Generation: Chen Kaige (*Yellow Earth* 1984, *Farewell My Concubine* 1993), Zhang Yimou (*Red Sorghum* 1988, *Raise the Red Lantern* 1991, *Hero* 2002, *House of Flying Daggers* 2004); Sixth Generation: Jia Zhangke (*Platform* 2000, *A Touch of Sin* 2013, *Still Life* 2006); Wang Bing (*West of the Tracks* 2003 — 9 hours; documentary)
    - Taiwan: Hou Hsiao-hsien (*A City of Sadness* 1989 — Venice Golden Lion; first Taiwanese film to address 228 Incident; *The Assassin* 2015), Edward Yang (*Yi Yi* 2000 — Cannes Best Director; *A Brighter Summer Day* 1991 — 4 hours; Taiwanese identity); Tsai Ming-liang (*Viva l'Amour* 1994, *What Time Is It There?* 2001 — minimalist; slow cinema pioneer; fixed camera; durational emptiness)
    - South Korea: Park Chan-wook (*Oldboy* 2003, *Sympathy for Mr. Vengeance* 2002 — "Vengeance Trilogy"; *The Handmaiden* 2016), Bong Joon-ho (*Memories of Murder* 2003, *The Host* 2006, *Parasite* 2019 — first non-English Best Picture Oscar; class warfare; genre-bending; global phenomenon), Lee Chang-dong (*Burning* 2018, *Poetry* 2010), Kim Ki-duk (*Spring, Summer, Fall, Winter... and Spring* 2003), Hong Sangsoo (prolific minimalist; *Right Now, Wrong Then* 2015)
  - **Iranian Cinema**
    - Pre-revolution: *Dashti and the Cow* (Dariush Mehrjui 1969 — Iranian New Wave catalyst; Gavazn); Forough Farrokhzad (*The House Is Black* 1963 — poetic documentary)
    - Post-revolution flowering: despite strict censorship, produced globally celebrated art cinema
    - Abbas Kiarostami (1940–2016): *Where Is the Friend's House?* (1987), *Close-Up* (1990 — meta-documentary; real impostor plays himself), *Taste of Cherry* (1997 — Palme d'Or; suicide theme; Beckett-level minimalism), *Certified Copy* (2010); theorist of cinema's relationship with reality
    - Mohsen Makhmalbaf: *Gabbeh* (1996), *Kandahar* (2001); daughter Samira Makhmalbaf (*The Apple* 1998, *Blackboards* 2000)
    - Jafar Panahi: *The White Balloon* (1995), *The Circle* (2000), *Offside* (2006); arrested 2010, banned from filmmaking for 20 years; continued making films clandestinely (*This Is Not a Film* 2011 — smuggled out on USB in a cake; *Taxi* 2015 — Berlinale Golden Bear; *No Bears* 2022); symbol of artistic resistance
    - Asghar Farhadi: *A Separation* (2011 — Oscar for Best Foreign Language Film; moral complexity; Iran's middle class), *The Salesman* (2016 — second Oscar); plagiarism controversy 2022
    - Majid Majidi: *Children of Heaven* (1997 — first Iranian Oscar nomination); Ali Abbasi (*Holy Spider* 2022)
  - **Australian & New Zealand Cinema**
    - Australian New Wave (1970s–80s): Peter Weir (*Picnic at Hanging Rock* 1975, *Gallipoli* 1981, *The Year of Living Dangerously* 1982; Hollywood: *Witness*, *Dead Poets Society*, *The Truman Show* 1998), George Miller (*Mad Max* 1979 — Mel Gibson; franchise: *The Road Warrior* 1981, *Fury Road* 2015 — extraordinary stunt work, practical effects, feminist reading; *Furiosa* 2024), Bruce Beresford (*Breaker Morant* 1980), Gillian Armstrong (*My Brilliant Career* 1979)
    - New Zealand: Peter Jackson (*Heavenly Creatures* 1994, *The Lord of the Rings* trilogy 2001–2003 — massive achievement; Oscar sweep *Return of the King*; Weta Workshop VFX), Taika Waititi (*Boy* 2010, *Hunt for the Wilderpeople* 2016, *Jojo Rabbit* 2019 — adapted screenplay Oscar), Jane Campion (*The Piano* 1993 — Palme d'Or; second woman to win; *The Power of the Dog* 2021 — Best Director Oscar)

- **Documentary Film**
  - **Foundations and pioneers**
    - Robert Flaherty: *Nanook of the North* (1922) — Inuit life; reconstructed/staged; "father of documentary"; authenticity vs. construction debate begins immediately
    - John Grierson: coined "documentary" (1926 review of Flaherty's *Moana*); British documentary movement (GPO/Crown Film Unit: *Night Mail* 1936, W. H. Auden narration, Benjamin Britten score); "creative treatment of actuality"
    - Dziga Vertov: *Man with a Movie Camera* (1929); Kinopravda — cameraman as agitator
    - Leni Riefenstahl: *Triumph of the Will* (1935, Nuremberg rally) and *Olympia* (1938, Berlin Olympics) — technically brilliant propaganda; ethical debate about separating art from ideology; most controversial documentarian
  - **Cinéma Vérité and Direct Cinema (1960s)**
    - Technology: portable 16mm cameras (Éclair NPR), sync sound (Nagra recorder); enabled observational filmmaking
    - Direct Cinema (American): Robert Drew, Richard Leacock, D. A. Pennebaker (*Don't Look Back* 1967 — Bob Dylan tour), Albert and David Maysles (*Salesman* 1969, *Grey Gardens* 1975 — cult classic; *Gimme Shelter* 1970 — Rolling Stones/Altamont killing filmed), Frederick Wiseman (*Titicut Follies* 1967, *High School* 1968 — institutional observation; over 40 films; no narration, no interviews; "fly on the wall")
    - Cinéma vérité (French): Jean Rouch and Edgar Morin: *Chronicle of a Summer* (1961) — provocation; filmmaker as participant; "Are you happy?" opening question; acknowledged the camera's presence
    - Distinction: Direct Cinema = observe without interfering; cinéma vérité = provoke and participate; often conflated but philosophically distinct
  - **Essay film**
    - Chris Marker: *Sans Soleil* (1983); Harun Farocki (*Workers Leaving the Factory*, 1995); Agnès Varda (*The Gleaners and I*, 2000); Patricio Guzmán (*Nostalgia for the Light* 2010, *The Pearl Button* 2015)
    - Intellectual, reflexive, poetic; voiceover-driven; mixes documentary and fiction elements; personal and political
  - **Modern documentary movements**
    - Michael Moore: *Roger & Me* (1989), *Bowling for Columbine* (2002 — Oscar), *Fahrenheit 9/11* (2004 — Palme d'Or; highest-grossing documentary at the time); polemical/activist style; criticized for manipulation
    - Errol Morris: *The Thin Blue Line* (1988 — wrongful conviction overturned; re-enactments; Philip Glass score), *The Fog of War* (2003 — Robert McNamara; Interrotron), *Standard Operating Procedure* (2008)
    - Werner Herzog documentaries: *Grizzly Man* (2005), *Cave of Forgotten Dreams* (2010, Chauvet cave in 3D), *Into the Inferno* (2016); blurs fiction and non-fiction; "ecstatic truth"
    - Joshua Oppenheimer: *The Act of Killing* (2012) — Indonesian mass killers re-enact crimes; Brechtian; disturbing; *The Look of Silence* (2014)
    - True crime documentary: *Making a Murderer* (2015 Netflix), *The Jinx* (2015 HBO), *Tiger King* (2020); ethical concerns about exploitation, trial-by-media
    - Nature/Earth documentary: BBC Natural History Unit (*Planet Earth* 2006, *Blue Planet II* 2017, David Attenborough); IMAX nature films; environmental advocacy documentaries (*An Inconvenient Truth* 2006, Al Gore)
    - Animated documentary: *Waltz with Bashir* (Ari Folman, 2008 — Israeli/Lebanese War memories; animation enables trauma representation), *Flee* (Jonas Poher Rasmussen, 2021 — Afghan refugee; triple Oscar nomination: documentary, animated, international)

- **Film Theory & Criticism**
  - **Classical film theory**
    - **Formalism**: Hugo Münsterberg (*The Photoplay: A Psychological Study*, 1916 — film as mind); Rudolf Arnheim (*Film as Art*, 1932 — film's artistic qualities come from its differences from reality; limitations enable art); Sergei Eisenstein (montage as conflicting shots creating new meanings — dialectical); Béla Balázs (*Theory of the Film*, 1945 — close-up reveals inner life; "visible man")
    - **Realism**: André Bazin (1918–1958): "ontology of the photographic image" — film's essence is its indexical relationship to reality; deep focus (Welles, Wyler, Renoir) preferred over montage; long take preserves spatiotemporal integrity; reality and ambiguity over manipulation; *What Is Cinema?* (collected essays, 4 vols); enormously influential; died young
    - Siegfried Kracauer (*Theory of Film: The Redemption of Physical Reality*, 1960): film should reveal the material world; "camera-reality"
  - **Structuralism and semiotics**
    - Christian Metz: *Film Language: A Semiotics of the Cinema* (1968); Grande Syntagmatique — classified shot sequences; applied Saussurean linguistics to film; "cinema is not a language system (*langue*) but has language-like properties (*langage*)"
    - Peter Wollen: *Signs and Meaning in the Cinema* (1969, revised 1972); applied Peirce's icon/index/symbol to film
    - Roland Barthes: "The Death of the Author" (1967) — applicable to film auteurism; photographic theory (*Camera Lucida* 1980: studium/punctum)
  - **Psychoanalytic film theory**
    - Jean-Louis Baudry: "apparatus theory" — cinema as ideological apparatus; Plato's cave analogy; basic cinematographic apparatus creates subject-effect
    - Laura Mulvey: "Visual Pleasure and Narrative Cinema" (1975, *Screen* journal) — most cited film theory article; defined "the male gaze"; scopophilia (Freud), voyeurism, fetishism; women as erotic spectacle ("to-be-looked-at-ness"); three gazes: camera, characters, spectator; called for destruction of visual pleasure to create feminist counter-cinema
    - Critique and extension: Gaylyn Studlar (masochism, not sadism; von Sternberg); Mary Ann Doane (female spectatorship; masquerade); bell hooks (oppositional gaze; Black women viewers); Joan Copjec; Slavoj Žižek (*The Pervert's Guide to Cinema* 2006, with Sophie Fiennes — Lacanian film analysis as performance)
    - Kaja Silverman: *The Acoustic Mirror* (1988) — female voice in cinema; gendered sound
  - **Auteur theory elaborations**
    - *Cahiers du cinéma* politique des auteurs (Truffaut, Godard, Rohmer 1950s)
    - Andrew Sarris: *The American Cinema: Directors and Directions 1929–1968* (1968) — hierarchical ranking; "pantheon" directors; adapted auteurism for American context
    - Pauline Kael: "Circles and Squares" (1963) — attacked auteurism; favored emotional response; *I Lost It at the Movies* (1965); New Yorker critic; championed *Bonnie and Clyde*, *Last Tango in Paris*; personal, passionate criticism; rivalry with Sarris defined American film criticism for decades
    - Post-structuralist auteurism: the auteur as textual construct, not biographical being; "death of the author" applied
  - **Feminist film theory** (beyond Mulvey)
    - Claire Johnston: "Women's Cinema as Counter-Cinema" (1973) — women's genres as ideological; myths of woman in classical cinema
    - Teresa de Lauretis: *Alice Doesn't: Feminism, Semiotics, Cinema* (1984); desire, narrative, gender
    - Carol Clover: *Men, Women, and Chain Saws* (1992) — "Final Girl" in horror (the surviving female protagonist); gender fluidity in horror spectatorship
    - Barbara Creed: *The Monstrous-Feminine* (1993) — Kristeva's abjection applied to horror (woman as monster, not just victim)
    - B. Ruby Rich: coined "New Queer Cinema" (1992, *Sight & Sound*) — Derek Jarman, Todd Haynes, Gregg Araki, Tom Kalin; queer filmmakers in early 1990s
  - **Third Cinema and postcolonial film theory**
    - Fernando Solanas and Octavio Getino: "Toward a Third Cinema" (1969) — first cinema (Hollywood commercial), second cinema (European art house), third cinema (revolutionary/liberatory cinema of the Third World)
    - Teshome Gabriel: *Third Cinema in the Third World* (1982)
    - Frantz Fanon's influence on visual representation theories; Edward Said and Orientalism in cinema; Homi Bhabha's colonial mimicry
    - Ella Shohat and Robert Stam: *Unthinking Eurocentrism* (1994) — critiques of Hollywood representation of non-Western peoples
  - **Deleuze's cinema philosophy**
    - Gilles Deleuze: *Cinema 1: The Movement-Image* (1983) and *Cinema 2: The Time-Image* (1985)
    - Movement-image: classical cinema; action-reaction; sensory-motor link (perception-image, action-image, affection-image)
    - Time-image: postwar art cinema (Ozu, Italian Neorealism, the Nouvelle Vague); break in sensory-motor link; time presented directly; crystal-image; opsigns and sonsigns
    - Enormously influential in film-philosophy; difficult and contested; D. N. Rodowick, Steven Shaviro, Patricia Pisters as elaborators
  - **Cognitivist film theory**
    - David Bordwell: *Narration in the Fiction Film* (1985); constructivist approach; how viewers actively construct narratives; rejected psychoanalytic/ideological apparatus theory as "Grand Theory"
    - Noël Carroll: *Mystifying Movies* (1988), *The Philosophy of Motion Pictures* (2008); analytic philosophy applied to film; intentionalism
    - Murray Smith: *Engaging Characters* (1995) — "structure of sympathy" (recognition, alignment, allegiance) replacing simplistic "identification"
    - Torben Grodal: *Moving Pictures* (1997) — PECMA flow model (perception, emotion, cognition, motor action); bioculturalism
    - Ed Tan: *Emotion and the Structure of Narrative Film* (1996) — film as "emotion machine"
  - **Affect theory and phenomenological approaches**
    - Vivian Sobchack: *The Address of the Eye* (1992) — Merleau-Ponty's phenomenology applied to film; film as embodied perception; "film's body"
    - Steven Shaviro: *The Cinematic Body* (1993) — visceral, affective spectatorship against psychoanalytic distance
    - Jennifer Barker: *The Tactile Eye* (2009) — haptic visuality; skin of film; Laura U. Marks (*The Skin of the Film* 2000 — intercultural cinema and sensory experience)

- **Contemporary and 21st-Century Cinema**
  - **The Blockbuster and Franchise Era (1980s–present)**
    - High-concept cinema: easily summarized, marketable premises; Don Simpson/Jerry Bruckheimer (*Top Gun* 1986, *Beverly Hills Cop* 1984); Joel Silver (*Die Hard* 1988, *Lethal Weapon* 1987, *The Matrix* 1999)
    - Franchise model: *Star Wars* sequels/prequels/sequels (1977–2019); Marvel Cinematic Universe (2008–present: *Iron Man* first; 30+ films; Kevin Feige as producer-auteur; interconnected universe; *Avengers: Endgame* 2019 — $2.8B worldwide; superhero fatigue debate 2023–2025); DC Extended Universe (more troubled: Snyder vs. studio; *The Dark Knight* trilogy Christopher Nolan 2005–2012 as exception); *Harry Potter* (8 films 2001–2011; *Fantastic Beasts* spinoffs); *James Bond* (25 official films, 1962–2021; longest-running franchise); *Fast & Furious*; *Jurassic Park/World*
    - Cinematic universes as dominant industrial logic; IP (intellectual property) as currency; risk aversion; original screenplays decline as share of studio output
  - **Independent cinema (1980s–present)**
    - American independent cinema: Jim Jarmusch (*Stranger Than Paradise* 1984, *Dead Man* 1995, *Paterson* 2016), Spike Lee (*Do the Right Thing* 1989, *Malcolm X* 1992, *BlacKkKlansman* 2018), Steven Soderbergh (*sex, lies, and videotape* 1989 — Palme d'Or; launched Sundance era), Joel and Ethan Coen (*Blood Simple* 1984, *Fargo* 1996, *No Country for Old Men* 2007), Quentin Tarantino (*Reservoir Dogs* 1992, *Pulp Fiction* 1994 — Palme d'Or; non-linear narrative; pop culture dialogue; violence; *Kill Bill* 2003–2004, *Inglourious Basterds* 2009, *Once Upon a Time in Hollywood* 2019), David Lynch (*Eraserhead* 1977, *Blue Velvet* 1986, *Mulholland Drive* 2001 — voted best film of 21st century in multiple polls; *Twin Peaks* TV 1990–91 and *The Return* 2017), Wes Anderson (*Rushmore* 1998, *The Royal Tenenbaums* 2001, *The Grand Budapest Hotel* 2014; meticulously symmetrical compositions; pastel palette; dollhouse aesthetic), Paul Thomas Anderson (*Boogie Nights* 1997, *Magnolia* 1999, *There Will Be Blood* 2007, *Phantom Thread* 2017, *Licorice Pizza* 2021), Richard Linklater (*Slacker* 1990, *Dazed and Confused* 1993, Before trilogy 1995–2013, *Boyhood* 2014 — filmed over 12 years), Kelly Reichardt (*Old Joy* 2006, *Wendy and Lucy* 2008, *First Cow* 2019 — minimalist Americana)
    - Sundance Film Festival (founded 1978, Robert Redford; Park City, Utah): launch pad for indie cinema; *sex, lies, and videotape*, *Reservoir Dogs*, *Little Miss Sunshine*, *Get Out*, *CODA*
    - Miramax (Harvey Weinstein) and the indie boom/bust: aggressive Oscar campaigning; *Shakespeare in Love* (1998); Weinstein's 2017 sexual abuse exposure catalyzed #MeToo movement — seismic industry impact
    - A24 (founded 2012): boutique studio/distributor; *Moonlight* (2016 — Best Picture; Barry Jenkins; Black queer coming-of-age), *Lady Bird* (Greta Gerwig 2017), *The Florida Project* (Sean Baker 2017), *Hereditary* (Ari Aster 2018), *Everything Everywhere All at Once* (Daniel Kwan & Daniel Scheinert "Daniels" 2022 — Best Picture; multiverse; genre-blending; Michelle Yeoh first Asian Best Actress), *Past Lives* (Celine Song 2023); became prestige brand
  - **Digital revolution**
    - Digital cinematography: *Attack of the Clones* (2002, first major feature shot digitally on Sony HDC-F950); Sony CineAlta, RED (RED One 2007, *Che* Soderbergh 2008), Arri Alexa (from 2010, dominant camera); by 2013 digital surpassed celluloid in Hollywood
    - Digital intermediate (DI) and color grading: *O Brother, Where Art Thou?* (2000, first major DI; desaturated sepia); colorists as key creative collaborators
    - Digital projection: replaced 35mm projection in most cinemas by mid-2010s; archival concerns
    - CGI evolution: *Jurassic Park* (ILM, 1993 — photorealistic dinosaurs; game-changer), *Toy Story* (Pixar/Disney 1995 — first fully CGI feature), *The Matrix* (bullet time 1999), *Avatar* (James Cameron 2009 — performance capture; stereoscopic 3D; $2.9B worldwide; *Avatar: The Way of Water* 2022), *The Lord of the Rings* (Weta Digital; Gollum — Andy Serkis performance capture)
    - VFX studios: Industrial Light & Magic (ILM, Lucasfilm), Weta Digital (now Weta FX, NZ), Digital Domain, Framestore, DNEG; VFX labor issues (overwork, underpay; Rhythm & Hues bankruptcy 2013 after winning Oscar)
    - Deepfake and digital resurrection: de-aging (Scorsese's *The Irishman*; Marvel); posthumous performances (Peter Cushing in *Rogue One* 2016); ethical concerns
  - **Streaming era (2010s–present)**
    - Netflix: DVD-by-mail to streaming (2007); original content from 2013 (*House of Cards*); first Best Picture nominee: *Roma* (2018); massive content investment ($17B+ annually); global reach (190+ countries); *Squid Game* (2021 — Korean; most-watched Netflix series; global cultural phenomenon)
    - Disney+ (2019), HBO Max / Max (2020), Amazon Prime Video/MGM, Apple TV+, Paramount+, Peacock: streaming wars; content glut
    - Theatrical window collapse: pandemic accelerated day-and-date or shortened exclusivity; *Tenet* (2020) attempted theatrical-only during COVID; industry tension between theatrical and streaming
    - Algorithm-driven content: Netflix data analytics influence greenlighting; A/B testing of thumbnails; concern about homogenization
    - Martin Scorsese's 2019 essay ("I said Marvel movies aren't cinema") — debate about cinema vs. content; is the theatrical experience dying?
    - Peak TV and its contraction: 2022–2025 retrenchment; layoffs; content spending reduced; "streaming bubble" deflating
  - **Diversity and representation movements**
    - #OscarsSoWhite (2015–2016, April Reign): all 20 acting nominees white two years running; AMPAS expanded membership, diversified; reforms ongoing
    - #MeToo (2017): Weinstein, then industry-wide reckonings; Time's Up organization (2018); inclusion riders; intimacy coordinators now standard on sets
    - Female directors breakthrough: Kathryn Bigelow (*The Hurt Locker* 2009 — first woman to win Best Director Oscar), Greta Gerwig (*Lady Bird*, *Barbie* 2023 — $1.4B worldwide; cultural event), Chloé Zhao (*Nomadland* 2020 — Best Director and Picture), Jane Campion (*The Power of the Dog* 2021 — Best Director)
    - Asian representation: *Crazy Rich Asians* (2018 — first major Hollywood film with majority Asian cast since *The Joy Luck Club* 1993), *Minari* (Lee Isaac Chung, 2020), *Everything Everywhere All at Once* (2022)
    - Indigenous cinema: Warwick Thornton (*Samson and Delilah* 2009, Australia), Taika Waititi (Māori), Sterlin Harjo (*Reservation Dogs* TV 2021–2023)
    - Disability representation: growing but still limited; *CODA* (Siân Heder, 2021 — Best Picture; deaf actors; Marlee Matlin, Troy Kotsur — first deaf male acting Oscar winner)
  - **Contemporary auteurs (2000s–2020s)**
    - Denis Villeneuve (Canada): *Incendies* (2010), *Prisoners* (2013), *Arrival* (2016 — linguistics/time; Sapir-Whorf), *Blade Runner 2049* (2017), *Dune* (2021) and *Dune: Part Two* (2024 — epic sci-fi; massive visual scale)
    - Yorgos Lanthimos (Greece): *Dogtooth* (2009), *The Lobster* (2015), *The Killing of a Sacred Deer* (2017), *The Favourite* (2018), *Poor Things* (2023 — Best Actress Emma Stone; Frankenstein feminist rework)
    - Jordan Peele: *Get Out* (2017 — horror/social satire; racism; Oscar for Original Screenplay), *Us* (2019), *Nope* (2022); redefined horror's relationship to race
    - Barry Jenkins: *Moonlight* (2016), *If Beale Street Could Talk* (2018); poetic Black storytelling
    - Sean Baker: *Tangerine* (2015 — shot on iPhone 5s; trans sex workers), *The Florida Project* (2017), *Red Rocket* (2021), *Anora* (2024 — Palme d'Or; sex worker class comedy-drama)
    - Céline Sciamma (France): *Portrait of a Lady on Fire* (2019 — female gaze counter to Mulvey; queer love story; critical sensation)
    - Ryusuke Hamaguchi (Japan): *Drive My Car* (2021 — 3 hours; Chekhov and Murakami; Best International Feature Oscar; Sight & Sound 2022 poll #4 of 21st century; *Wheel of Fortune and Fantasy* 2021)
    - Justine Triet (France): *Anatomy of a Fall* (2023 — Palme d'Or; courtroom drama; language and truth)
    - RaMell Ross: *Nickel Boys* (2024 — first-person POV filmmaking; Colson Whitehead adaptation)
    - Brady Corbet: *The Brutalist* (2024 — 3.5 hours; post-WWII architect; AI-assisted dialogue controversy; Venice Silver Lion)
  - **Animation — modern era**
    - Pixar: *Toy Story* (1995), *Finding Nemo* (2003), *The Incredibles* (2004), *WALL-E* (2008), *Up* (2009), *Inside Out* (2015, sequel 2024 — $1.6B), *Coco* (2017), *Soul* (2020); "Pixar-brain trust" model of story development
    - Studio Ghibli (Japan): Hayao Miyazaki (*My Neighbor Totoro* 1988, *Princess Mononoke* 1997, *Spirited Away* 2001 — Best Animated Feature Oscar; Berlinale Golden Bear; *The Boy and the Heron* 2023 — Oscar at age 82); Isao Takahata (*Grave of the Fireflies* 1988, *The Tale of The Princess Kaguya* 2013); hand-drawn animation; ecological and humanist themes
    - Disney Animation Studios Renaissance: *The Little Mermaid* (1989), *Beauty and the Beast* (1991 — first animated Best Picture nominee), *The Lion King* (1994), *Frozen* (2013); 3D CGI era: *Tangled* (2010), *Moana* (2016)
    - DreamWorks Animation: *Shrek* (2001), *How to Train Your Dragon* (2010), *Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse* (2018 — Sony; revolutionary mixed-media animation; Oscar; *Across the Spider-Verse* 2023)
    - Adult animation: *Persepolis* (Marjane Satrapi, 2007), *Waltz with Bashir* (2008), *Anomalisa* (Charlie Kaufman, Duke Johnson, 2015), *Flee* (2021); stop-motion: Laika Studios (*Coraline* 2009, *Kubo and the Two Strings* 2016); Wes Anderson stop-motion (*Fantastic Mr. Fox* 2009, *Isle of Dogs* 2018)
    - Rotoscope: Richard Linklater (*Waking Life* 2001, *A Scanner Darkly* 2006)

- **Film Production & Craft**
  - **Cinematography**
    - Director of Photography (DP/Cinematographer): works with director on visual language; controls camera, lighting, lens choice, exposure, framing, movement
    - Lighting: three-point lighting (key, fill, back); high-key vs. low-key; chiaroscuro (noir); natural/available light (Nykvist for Bergman; Lubezki for Malick); color temperature (Kelvin); practical lights
    - Camera movement: pan, tilt, dolly (tracking shot), crane/jib, Steadicam (Garrett Brown, 1975; *Rocky* 1976, *The Shining* 1980 — first major use), handheld, drone (increasingly common 2010s–present); gimbal stabilizers
    - Lenses: wide-angle (distortion, depth), telephoto (compression), anamorphic (lens flares, horizontal stretch/squeeze; cinematic look), tilt-shift; depth of field (shallow vs. deep); focal length choice as storytelling tool
    - Legendary cinematographers: Gregg Toland (*Citizen Kane* — deep focus), James Wong Howe (60-year career; noir master), Vittorio Storaro (*Apocalypse Now*, *The Last Emperor* — color theory), Gordon Willis ("Prince of Darkness" — *The Godfather* — low-light; *Manhattan*), Roger Deakins (*No Country for Old Men*, *Skyfall* 2012, *Blade Runner 2049*, *1917* 2019 — apparent single-take; 15 Oscar nominations, 2 wins), Emmanuel Lubezki (*Gravity*, *Birdman* — long takes, *The Revenant* — natural light; three consecutive Oscars), Hoyte van Hoytema (*Interstellar*, *Dunkirk*, *Oppenheimer* — IMAX black-and-white), Janusz Kamiński (all Spielberg films since *Schindler's List*), Bradford Young (*Arrival*, *Selma* — first Black ASC member nominated for Oscar)
  - **Editing**
    - Editor: shapes the film in post-production; controls rhythm, pacing, narrative structure, emotional impact; Walter Murch: "editor as the last writer"
    - Continuity editing (Classical Hollywood): match on action, eyeline match, 180-degree rule, shot/reverse shot, establishing shot, 30-degree rule; goal: invisible technique serving narrative
    - Soviet montage: collision and contrast (Eisenstein); intellectual, metric, rhythmic, tonal, overtonal montage categories
    - Jump cut (Godard, *Breathless* 1960): discontinuity as style; broke continuity rules
    - Long take vs. montage: Bazin's preference for the long take (preserves reality, viewer's freedom to look); vs. Eisenstein's montage (directs meaning); André Bazin vs. Sergei Eisenstein as cinema's great theoretical axis
    - Non-linear editing systems: Moviola (mechanical, 1924–1990s); flatbed (Steenbeck); Avid (digital, from late 1980s); Final Cut Pro; DaVinci Resolve; Adobe Premiere Pro
    - Notable editors: Thelma Schoonmaker (all Scorsese films from *Raging Bull* onward; aggressive jump cuts in *Goodfellas*), Walter Murch (*Apocalypse Now*, *The English Patient*; wrote *In the Blink of an Eye* 2001 — theory of editing based on emotion, story, rhythm), Dede Allen (*Bonnie and Clyde* — shocking violence through editing)
  - **Sound design**
    - Sound categories: dialogue, sound effects (SFX/Foley), ambience/atmos, music (score and source)
    - Foley: recreated sounds in post-production; named after Jack Foley (Universal Studios); footsteps, clothing rustle, props
    - Walter Murch: coined "sound designer" as title on *Apocalypse Now* (1979); revolutionary multi-track sound; "worldizing" (playing recorded sound through speakers in real spaces and re-recording)
    - Ben Burtt: Star Wars sound design (lightsaber, blaster, R2-D2, Chewbacca, TIE fighter — idling projector + car on wet road); Indiana Jones whip crack; Wall-E vocalizations; "found sound" artist
    - Dolby systems: Dolby Stereo (1975), Dolby SR, Dolby Digital (1992), Dolby Atmos (2012 — object-based immersive sound; ceiling speakers); THX (Lucasfilm; theatre certification standard)
    - Silence as tool: Kubrick's *2001*; Spielberg's *Saving Private Ryan* (muffled hearing after explosion); *A Quiet Place* (John Krasinski 2018, sequel 2020); Denis Villeneuve's *Arrival* and *Dune* (selective silence)
  - **Film music and scoring**
    - Classical Hollywood scoring: Max Steiner (*King Kong* 1933, *Gone with the Wind* 1939 — leitmotif-rich), Erich Wolfgang Korngold (*The Adventures of Robin Hood* 1938 — operatic Romantic style), Bernard Herrmann (long Hitchcock collaboration: *Vertigo* shrieking strings, *Psycho* shower scene — all-strings score; also *Citizen Kane*, *Taxi Driver* — his last score; killed the "non-diegetic music must be pleasant" rule)
