      - al-Kafi (al-Kulayni, d. 941): most comprehensive; usul (theology), furu' (law), rawda (miscellany)
      - Man La Yahduruhu al-Faqih (Ibn Babawaih/al-Saduq, d. 991)
      - Tahdhib al-Ahkam (al-Tusi, d. 1067)
      - al-Istibsar (al-Tusi)
    - Include hadith from the Twelve Imams alongside the Prophet
    - Different isnad standards; acceptance of companion narrators differs from Sunni tradition
  - Modern hadith scholarship
    - Western academic study: Ignaz Goldziher (*Muslim Studies*, 1889–90) — skepticism about hadith authenticity; Joseph Schacht (*The Origins of Muhammadan Jurisprudence*, 1950) — hadith largely fabricated backward from legal positions (back-growth of isnad)
    - G. H. A. Juynboll: common link theory — identifying fabrication points via isnad analysis
    - Harald Motzki: counter-revisionist; isnad-cum-matn analysis; argues substantial hadith material traceable to early period
    - Jonathan Brown (*Hadith: Muhammad's Legacy in the Medieval and Modern World*, 2009): sympathetic critical treatment; *Misquoting Muhammad* (2014)
    - Current debates: digital hadith databases; computational isnad analysis; hadith authentication AI experiments

- **Early Islamic History and the Caliphates**
  - Rashidun Caliphate (632–661 CE)
    - Succession crisis at Saqifat Bani Sa'ida: Abu Bakr chosen; Ali and supporters (later Shia) contested
    - Abu Bakr (r. 632–634): Ridda Wars (Wars of Apostasy); reunification of Arabia; began conquest of Iraq and Syria
    - Umar ibn al-Khattab (r. 634–644): greatest expansion period; conquered Syria, Egypt, Iraq, Persia; established diwan (administrative registers), garrison cities (amsar: Basra, Kufa, Fustat); Pact of Umar (dhimmi protections/restrictions); assassinated
    - Uthman ibn Affan (r. 644–656): Quran standardization; continued expansion; accusations of nepotism (Umayyad favoritism); assassinated — First Fitna begins
    - Ali ibn Abi Talib (r. 656–661): fourth caliph for Sunnis, first rightful Imam for Shia
      - Battle of the Camel (656): Ali vs. Aisha, Talha, Zubayr
      - Battle of Siffin (657): Ali vs. Mu'awiya (Umayyad governor of Syria); arbitration (tahkim)
      - Kharijites: seceded from Ali after accepting arbitration; "judgment belongs to God alone"; egalitarian but extremist; assassinated Ali (661)
  - Umayyad Caliphate (661–750)
    - Mu'awiya I (r. 661–680): established hereditary rule from Damascus; administrative Byzantinization; Arabization of bureaucracy
    - Husayn ibn Ali's martyrdom at Karbala (680): foundational event for Shi'ism; Muharram mourning (Ashura); passion narrative; paradigm of righteous suffering against tyranny
    - Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan (r. 685–705): Dome of the Rock (691); Arabized coinage (dinar/dirham); Arabic as official administrative language
    - Umayyad expansion: North Africa, Iberian Peninsula (711, Tariq ibn Ziyad), Transoxiana, Sind; Battle of Tours/Poitiers (732, Charles Martel — halted advance into France)
    - Umayyad poetry and culture: Ghazal (love poetry: Umar ibn Abi Rabi'a); panegyric (Jarir, al-Farazdaq, al-Akhtal); desert palace frescoes (Qusayr Amra)
    - Internal opposition: non-Arab Muslim (mawali) grievances; Shia revolts; Kharijite rebellions; Abbasid revolutionary da'wa (propaganda network)
    - Fall: Abbasid Revolution (750); Battle of the Zab; Umayyad family massacred; survivor Abd al-Rahman I escaped to Iberia → Umayyad Emirate/Caliphate of Córdoba (756–1031)
  - Abbasid Caliphate (750–1258)
    - Capital moved to Baghdad (762, founded by al-Mansur): "City of Peace" (Madinat al-Salam); Round City; crossroads of world trade
    - Golden Age of Islam (~8th–13th centuries): patronage of translation, science, philosophy, literature
    - Key caliphs: al-Mansur (founder, patron of learning), Harun al-Rashid (r. 786–809, *Thousand and One Nights* association), al-Ma'mun (r. 813–833, founded Bayt al-Hikma/"House of Wisdom", promoted Mu'tazilism, mihna inquisition)
    - Mihna (inquisition, 833–848): al-Ma'mun enforced Mu'tazili doctrine that Quran is created; Ahmad ibn Hanbal's resistance → became symbol of Sunni orthodoxy; mihna ended under al-Mutawakkil
    - Political fragmentation: Buyid Shia dynasty controls Baghdad (945–1055); Fatimids in Egypt; Samanids, Ghaznavids in east; Seljuk Turks (1055): sultans as military power, caliph as figurehead
    - Mongol destruction: Hulagu's siege and sack of Baghdad (1258); last Abbasid caliph al-Musta'sim killed; end of classical caliphate; libraries and irrigation systems destroyed
    - Shadow Abbasid caliphate in Mamluk Cairo (1261–1517): symbolic, not political
  - Later Islamic empires (overview; cross-reference with Medieval/Modern History sections)
    - Ottoman Empire (1299–1922): caliphal claim from 1517; Suleiman the Magnificent; millet system; Tanzimat reforms; dissolution after WWI; Atatürk abolishes caliphate (1924)
    - Safavid Empire (1501–1736): Twelver Shi'ism as state religion of Persia; Shah Isma'il; Isfahan as capital; forced conversion of previously Sunni populations
    - Mughal Empire (1526–1857): Babur, Akbar (religious tolerance, sulh-i kull), Aurangzeb (more orthodox); Indo-Islamic civilization; Taj Mahal
    - West African Islamic polities: Mali (Mansa Musa), Songhay (Askia Muhammad), Sokoto Caliphate (Usman dan Fodio, 1804); Timbuktu as scholarly center

- **Islamic Theology (Kalam)**
  - Definition: kalam = "speech" = dialectical/rational theology; defense of Islamic doctrines through reasoned argument
  - Mu'tazila (Rationalist school, ~8th–10th c.)
    - Five principles (al-usul al-khamsa): (1) tawhid (divine unity — no attributes separate from essence); (2) adl (divine justice — God cannot do evil); (3) al-wa'd wa'l-wa'id (promise and threat — God must reward/punish justly); (4) al-manzila bayn al-manzilatayn (intermediate position — grave sinner neither believer nor unbeliever); (5) al-amr bi'l-ma'ruf (commanding good, forbidding evil)
    - Quran as created (not eternal): most controversial doctrine; mihna enforcement; later rejected by Sunni mainstream
    - Free will (qadar): humans have genuine capacity to choose; God's justice requires human freedom
    - Key figures: Wasil ibn Ata (d. 748, founder); Amr ibn Ubayd; Abu'l-Hudhayl al-Allaf; al-Nazzam; al-Jahiz (also great littérateur); Qadi Abd al-Jabbar (d. 1025, systematic summa of Mu'tazili theology)
    - Decline in Sunni world after mihna; survived in Zaydi Shi'ism (Yemen) and influenced Twelver Shia theology
  - Ash'arism (dominant Sunni orthodox theology)
    - Abu'l-Hasan al-Ash'ari (d. 935/936): former Mu'tazili; broke with them; used rational methods to defend traditional positions
    - Divine attributes: real and eternal, neither identical with nor separate from essence (bila kayf — "without asking how")
    - Quran: uncreated and eternal (contra Mu'tazila)
    - Occasionalism: God recreates the universe at every instant; no natural causation; "habit" (ada) of God creates appearance of regularity; atoms and accidents doctrine
    - Acquisition (kasb/iktisab): human actions created by God but "acquired" by humans — middle ground between Mu'tazili free will and Jabri determinism; criticized as incoherent by opponents
    - Key Ash'ari theologians: al-Baqillani (d. 1013, refined atomistic metaphysics and Quranic inimitability), al-Juwayni (d. 1085, Imam al-Haramayn, teacher of al-Ghazali), al-Ghazali (d. 1111), Fakhr al-Din al-Razi (d. 1210), al-Iji (d. 1355), al-Taftazani (d. 1390), al-Jurjani (d. 1413)
    - Al-Ghazali (1058–1111): most influential Islamic intellectual
      - *Tahafut al-Falasifa* ("Incoherence of the Philosophers"): attacked Neoplatonic-Aristotelian philosophers (Ibn Sina/Avicenna, al-Farabi) on 20 points; three charges of kufr (eternity of world, God's knowledge of particulars, denial of bodily resurrection)
      - *Ihya Ulum al-Din* ("Revival of the Religious Sciences"): monumental synthesis of law, theology, ethics, and Sufism; rehabilitation of inner spiritual life within orthodox framework
      - *al-Munqidh min al-Dalal* ("Deliverance from Error"): spiritual autobiography; epistemological crisis and resolution through Sufi experience
      - Influence: sometimes called "proof of Islam" (Hujjat al-Islam); shaped Sunni synthesis of law + theology + Sufism
  - Maturidism (second major Sunni orthodox school)
    - Abu Mansur al-Maturidi (d. 944): Samarkand; contemporary of al-Ash'ari; developed parallel orthodox theology independently
    - Key differences from Ash'arism: greater role for reason in knowing God (humans can know God's existence through reason alone before revelation); human agency more robust; faith is assent of the heart (not just verbal confession)
    - Dominant among Hanafi communities: Central Asia, Turkey, South Asia, Balkans
    - Key Maturidi theologians: al-Nasafi (d. 1142, *al-Aqaid al-Nasafiyya*); al-Taftazani's commentary on Nasafi became standard textbook
  - Athari / Traditionalist theology
    - Rejects kalam methodology: theology should rely on Quran and hadith without rational interpretation (ta'wil) of divine attributes
    - Ahmad ibn Hanbal (d. 855): icon of resistance to mihna; attributes affirmed literally "without how" (bi-la kayf) and without anthropomorphism (tashbih)
    - Ibn Taymiyya (1263–1328): critique of both Ash'ari and Sufi innovations; literal affirmation of divine attributes; political activism; imprisoned multiple times; massive influence on later Salafism
    - Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya (d. 1350): Ibn Taymiyya's chief student and systematizer
    - Wahhabism: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab (1703–1792); alliance with Saudi house; strict monotheism; rejection of saint veneration, tomb visitation, Sufi practices; foundation of modern Saudi Arabia's religious identity
    - Modern Salafism: spectrum from quietist ("scientific Salafism" — Albani, Bin Baz, Uthaymeen) to activist (Muslim Brotherhood influenced) to jihadi (extreme, e.g. ISIS ideology)
  - Shia theology (kalam)
    - Twelver (Ithna Ashari) theology: divine justice (adl) and imamate (imama) as pillars; rational theology influenced by Mu'tazila
    - Usul al-din (roots of religion, Twelver): tawhid, adl, nubuwwa (prophethood), imama (imamate), ma'ad (resurrection)
    - Nasir al-Din al-Tusi (d. 1274): Shia philosophical theologian; *Tajrid al-I'tiqad* — foundational Shia kalam text
    - Allama al-Hilli (d. 1325): systematizer of Twelver theology and jurisprudence
    - Mulla Sadra (d. 1640): Transcendent Theosophy (al-hikma al-muta'aliya); synthesis of peripatetic philosophy, illuminationism, mysticism, and Shia theology; highly influential in Iranian thought
    - Ismaili theology: Neoplatonic cosmology (emanation); cyclical prophetology; esoteric (batin) hermeneutics; Fatimid-era thinkers: Qadi al-Nu'man, Hamid al-Din al-Kirmani, Nasir Khusraw

- **Islamic Philosophy (Falsafa)**
  - Translation movement (8th–10th c.)
    - Bayt al-Hikma (House of Wisdom, Baghdad): under al-Ma'mun; translation of Greek works (Plato, Aristotle, Galen, Ptolemy, Euclid) into Arabic via Syriac intermediaries
    - Key translators: Hunayn ibn Ishaq (d. 873, Nestorian Christian physician); Thabit ibn Qurra (d. 901, Sabian mathematician); Ishaq ibn Hunayn
    - Aristotle became "the First Teacher" (al-mu'allim al-awwal) in Islamic intellectual tradition
    - Neoplatonic overlay: *Theology of Aristotle* (actually Plotinus's Enneads) and *Liber de Causis* (Proclus) misattributed to Aristotle → shaped Islamic reception
  - Eastern peripatetic tradition (Mashsha'iyyun)
    - Al-Kindi (d. ~873): "Philosopher of the Arabs"; first systematic Islamic philosopher; harmonized philosophy and revelation
    - Al-Farabi (d. 950): "Second Teacher"; *al-Madina al-Fadila* ("The Virtuous City" — Islamic Plato's Republic); theory of prophecy as philosophical intellect + imaginative faculty; emanation cosmology (ten intellects); music theory
    - Ibn Sina / Avicenna (980–1037): greatest Islamic philosopher
      - *al-Shifa* ("The Healing"): encyclopedic — logic, physics, mathematics, metaphysics; *al-Najat* ("The Salvation"): epitome
      - *al-Qanun fi'l-Tibb* ("Canon of Medicine"): standard medical text in Europe and Islamic world for centuries
      - Essence-existence distinction: existence is an accident of essence in contingent beings; Necessary Being (wajib al-wujud) = God, whose essence is existence
      - Emanation: from the One through ten intellects to the material world; Active Intellect illuminates human reason
      - Flying Man thought experiment: disembodied self-awareness proves soul's independence from body
      - Prophet as philosopher-king with perfect imaginative faculty → receives revelation as images/symbols of philosophical truths
    - Ibn Tufayl (d. 1185): *Hayy ibn Yaqzan* (philosophical novel, "Alive Son of Awake"): autodidact reaches philosophical truth on desert island → discovers agreement with revealed religion
    - Ibn Rushd / Averroes (1126–1198): Córdoba; greatest Aristotelian commentator in Islamic tradition
      - *Tahafut al-Tahafut* ("Incoherence of the Incoherence"): defense of philosophy against al-Ghazali's attack
      - *Fasl al-Maqal* ("Decisive Treatise"): philosophy and religion (shari'a) are twin sisters; both seek truth; allegorical interpretation (ta'wil) resolves apparent conflicts
      - Double truth theory (attributed by Latin Averroists, Averroes himself denied it): philosophy and theology can reach different but compatible truths
      - Enormous impact on Latin West: "the Commentator" on Aristotle; Averroism at Paris (Siger of Brabant, Boethius of Dacia); influenced Aquinas
      - Marginal in later Islamic world: post-Ghazali suspicion of falsafa; but revival in modern period
  - Illuminationist philosophy (Ishraq)
    - Suhrawardi (1154–1191): *Hikmat al-Ishraq* ("Philosophy of Illumination"); light metaphysics; critique of Aristotelian logic; fusion of Platonic, Persian (Zoroastrian), and Islamic elements; executed in Aleppo
    - Hierarchy of lights: Light of Lights (God) → descending lights → darkness (matter); knowledge as direct illumination (presential knowledge, ilm huduri)
    - Influence on Mulla Sadra and Iranian philosophical tradition
  - Transcendent Theosophy (al-Hikma al-Muta'aliya)
    - Mulla Sadra (Sadr al-Din al-Shirazi, 1571/2–1640): greatest philosopher of Safavid Iran
    - *al-Asfar al-Arba'a* ("The Four Journeys"): from creation to God, from God with God, from God back to creation with God, in creation with God
    - Primacy of existence (asalat al-wujud) over essence: reversed Suhrawardi's position; existence is real, essence is mental construct
    - Substantial motion (al-haraka al-jawhariyya): substances themselves change, not just accidents; revolutionary in Islamic philosophy
    - Trans-substantial motion of the soul: soul begins material, becomes immaterial through intellectual development
    - Bodily resurrection: philosophical proof via substantial motion; reconciled philosophy and revealed doctrine
    - Still the dominant philosophical tradition in Iranian seminaries (Qom)

- **Islamic Jurisprudence (Fiqh) and Legal Theory (Usul al-Fiqh)**
  - Sources of Islamic law (Sunni)
    - Quran: primary source; ~500 legal verses (ayat al-ahkam)
    - Sunna: prophetic practice as recorded in hadith; second source
    - Ijma' (consensus): agreement of qualified scholars; binding; varies by school on whose consensus counts
    - Qiyas (analogical reasoning): extending known rulings to new cases by shared effective cause (illa); al-Shafi'i systematized
  - Al-Shafi'i (d. 820): "Father of Islamic Jurisprudence"; *al-Risala* — first systematic treatise on usul al-fiqh; established hierarchy of sources
  - Four Sunni legal schools (madhahib)
    - **Hanafi** (Abu Hanifa, d. 767): Iraq; greatest scope for ra'y (independent reasoning), istihsan (juristic preference); dominant in Turkey, Central Asia, South Asia, Balkans, Egypt
    - **Maliki** (Malik ibn Anas, d. 795): Medina; emphasis on practice of Medina (amal ahl al-Madina) as living sunna; istislah (public interest, maslaha); dominant in North/West Africa, parts of Gulf
    - **Shafi'i** (al-Shafi'i, d. 820): systematized usul al-fiqh; strict reliance on hadith over ra'y; dominant in East Africa, Southeast Asia, Egypt, Yemen
    - **Hanbali** (Ahmad ibn Hanbal, d. 855): most textualist; minimal use of analogy; dominant in Saudi Arabia; Hanbali usul: Ibn Taymiyya and Ibn Qayyim expanded methodology
  - Shia jurisprudence
    - Ja'fari school (Twelver): Imam Ja'far al-Sadiq (d. 765) as eponym; hadith from Imams alongside Prophet; aql (reason) replaces qiyas
    - Usuli vs. Akhbari debate: Usulis (majority, allow ijtihad by mujtahids) vs. Akhbaris (strict reliance on hadith of Prophet and Imams, reject ijtihad)
    - Marja' al-taqlid ("source of emulation"): living senior scholar whose rulings laypeople follow; taqlid (following) of living authority required
    - Wilayat al-faqih (Guardianship of the Jurist): Ayatollah Khomeini's political theory; supreme religious authority governs state; basis of Islamic Republic of Iran (1979–)
    - Zaydi jurisprudence: close to Hanafi/Mu'tazili; Yemen tradition
    - Ismaili law: Qadi al-Nu'man's *Da'a'im al-Islam* (Fatimid legal code); Aga Khan as living Imam for Nizari Ismailis
  - Ibadi jurisprudence: neither Sunni nor Shia; Oman majority; Kharijite-derived but moderate; earliest surviving legal works
  - Key concepts in fiqh
    - Ijtihad: independent legal reasoning; "gate of ijtihad" — claimed closed by some after 10th century (controversial); modern reopening movement
    - Taqlid: following established legal opinion; layperson's obligation; debated between reformists and traditionalists
    - Maqasid al-shari'a (objectives of Islamic law): preservation of religion, life, intellect, lineage/honor, property; al-Ghazali and al-Shatibi (d. 1388, *al-Muwafaqat*) developed framework; modern maqasid school: Ibn Ashur, Jasser Auda
    - Five legal categories (al-ahkam al-khamsa): wajib (obligatory), mandub (recommended), mubah (permissible), makruh (discouraged), haram (forbidden)
    - Fatwa: non-binding legal opinion by mufti; ifta' tradition; modern collective fatwa bodies (Islamic Fiqh Academy, European Council for Fatwa and Research)
    - Hudud penalties: Quranic fixed punishments for theft, zina (adultery/fornication), qadhf (false accusation), highway robbery, apostasy (debated), alcohol consumption; rarely implemented historically due to high evidentiary standards; modern debate on applicability
    - Siyasa shar'iyya: governance policy within Islamic legal framework; ruler's discretion for public interest

- **Sufism (Tasawwuf)**
  - Origins and early asceticism
    - Etymology debated: suf (wool, ascetic garb) most common derivation; safwa (purity); suffa (bench, Companions of the Bench)
    - Early ascetics (zuhhad): Hasan al-Basri (d. 728), Rabi'a al-Adawiyya (d. 801, woman mystic of Basra: unconditional divine love — "I want neither to fear hell nor hope for paradise; I want only to love God")
    - Ibrahim ibn Adham (d. ~777): prince who renounced wealth; hagiographic parallels to Buddha
    - Dhu'l-Nun al-Misri (d. 859): Egyptian; ma'rifa (gnosis) as direct experiential knowledge of God
  - Classical Sufi theory
    - Maqamat (stations) and ahwal (states): spiritual journey (suluk) through stages — repentance (tawba), patience (sabr), gratitude (shukr), trust (tawakkul), satisfaction (rida), love (mahabba)
    - Fana' (annihilation) and baqa' (subsistence): self-extinction in God and return to conscious existence with God; Abu Yazid al-Bistami (d. 874, ecstatic utterances/shathiyyat: "Glory be to me!")
    - Al-Junayd of Baghdad (d. 910): "sober Sufism"; fana' reinterpreted as loss of egoistic self, not ontological union; normative for later orthodoxy
    - Al-Hallaj (d. 922): "Ana al-Haqq" ("I am the Truth/God"); executed in Baghdad for blasphemy; martyr-figure for Sufis; Louis Massignon's magisterial study (1922)
    - Al-Qushayri (d. 1072): *al-Risala al-Qushayriyya* — synthesis of Sufi terminology and practice within Ash'ari orthodoxy
    - Al-Hujwiri (d. ~1077): *Kashf al-Mahjub* ("Revelation of the Veiled") — earliest Persian Sufi treatise
    - Abu Hamid al-Ghazali (d. 1111): legitimized Sufism within Sunni orthodoxy; *Ihya Ulum al-Din*; personal spiritual crisis → Sufi renewal (see Kalam section above)
  - Major Sufi theorists and poets
    - Ibn Arabi (1165–1240): "Greatest Master" (al-Shaykh al-Akbar); born Murcia, died Damascus
      - *al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya* ("Meccan Revelations"): encyclopedic mystical summa (~17,000 pages)
      - *Fusus al-Hikam* ("Bezels of Wisdom"): 27 chapters on prophets; each prophet = facet of divine wisdom
      - Wahdat al-wujud ("Unity of Being/Existence"): all existence is God's self-manifestation; multiplicity is theophany (tajalli); debated forever — pantheism accusation vs. defenders (wahdat al-wujud ≠ pantheism but panentheistic monism)
      - Perfect Human (al-insan al-kamil): microcosm reflecting divine names; Muhammad as prototype; developed by Abd al-Karim al-Jili
      - Enormous influence: shaped virtually all later Sufism, Islamic philosophy, and much poetry; controversial among textualists
    - Jalal al-Din Rumi (1207–1273): greatest Sufi poet
      - *Masnavi-yi Ma'navi* ("Spiritual Couplets"): ~25,000 verses; called "the Quran in Persian"; teaching stories, parables, mystical philosophy
      - *Divan-i Shams-i Tabrizi*: lyric ghazals inspired by love for Shams of Tabriz
      - Mevlevi Order (Whirling Dervishes): sama' (spiritual audition/dance) as meditation
      - Global cultural influence; best-selling poet in America (translations by Coleman Barks — criticized by scholars for de-Islamicizing content)
    - Farid al-Din Attar (d. ~1221): *Mantiq al-Tayr* ("Conference of the Birds"): allegory of 30 birds seeking the Simorgh (= themselves reflected)
    - Hafiz of Shiraz (d. ~1390): Divan; ambiguity of wine/love poetry as mystical allegory; "tongue of the unseen" (lisan al-ghayb)
    - Yunus Emre (d. ~1321): Turkish folk Sufi poet; simple language, universal love
    - Shah Waliullah al-Dihlawi (1703–1762): Indian scholar-Sufi; attempted synthesis of all four Sunni schools and Sufi orders; hadith scholarship; influenced later South Asian reform movements
  - Sufi orders (tariqas)
    - Qadiriyya: Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani (d. 1166); Baghdad; one of oldest and most widespread; moderate, mainstream
    - Suhrawardiyya: Abu'l-Najib and Shihab al-Din al-Suhrawardi (different from Illuminationist philosopher); *Awarif al-Ma'arif*; institutional Sufism
    - Chishtiyya: Mu'in al-Din Chishti (d. 1236); dominant in South Asia; Nizamuddin Auliya (Delhi); devotional music (qawwali, popularized by Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan)
    - Shadhiliyya: Abu'l-Hasan al-Shadhili (d. 1258); North Africa; intellectual-devotional; sub-orders: Darqawiyya, Alawiyya
    - Mevleviyya: Rumi's order; sama' ceremony; Ottoman court connections; Konya center
    - Naqshbandiyya: Baha al-Din Naqshband (d. 1389); Central Asia; silent dhikr (remembrance); political involvement; spread: India (Ahmad Sirhindi's "Mujaddidi" reform), Turkey, Southeast Asia, Caucasus
    - Tijaniyya: Ahmad al-Tijani (d. 1815); West Africa; exclusive allegiance required; widespread in Senegal, Nigeria, Morocco
    - Sanusiyya: Muhammad al-Sanusi (d. 1859); Libya/Sahara; combined scholarship and political resistance; anti-colonial
    - Mouridiyya: Amadou Bamba (d. 1927); Senegal; largest Sufi movement in West Africa; Touba pilgrimage
    - Gülen Movement (Hizmet): Fethullah Gülen (d. 2024); Turkey origin; educational network; controversial — designated terrorist organization by Turkish government after 2016 coup attempt
  - Sufi practices
    - Dhikr (remembrance of God): repetitive invocation of divine names (vocal/silent); individual and collective; prayer beads (tasbih/misbaha)
    - Sama' (spiritual audition): music and poetry in devotion; debated legitimacy; Mevlevi whirling; qawwali
    - Muraqaba (meditation/contemplation): varieties of concentration and visualization
    - Khalwa (spiritual retreat/seclusion): prescribed periods of isolation under shaykh's guidance
    - Silsila (chain of initiation): spiritual lineage traced back to Muhammad via Ali or Abu Bakr; authority and blessing (baraka) transmitted
    - Shaykh-murid (master-disciple) relationship: central to tariqa structure; obedience; spiritual direction

- **Sunni-Shia Division and Shia Islam**
  - Origins of the split
    - Succession dispute: Shia (Shi'at Ali, "Party of Ali") hold Ali ibn Abi Talib was designated successor by Muhammad (event of Ghadir Khumm: "Whoever's mawla I am, Ali is his mawla"); Sunni tradition sees Abu Bakr as legitimately chosen by community
    - Political became theological: imamate as divine appointment (Shia) vs. caliphate as community selection (Sunni)
  - Twelver (Ithna Ashari) Shi'ism — largest Shia branch (~85% of Shia)
    - Twelve Imams: Ali, Hasan, Husayn, Ali Zayn al-Abidin, Muhammad al-Baqir, Ja'far al-Sadiq, Musa al-Kazim, Ali al-Rida, Muhammad al-Jawad, Ali al-Hadi, Hasan al-Askari, Muhammad al-Mahdi (12th Imam, in occultation since 874)
    - Occultation (ghayba): Minor Occultation (874–941, four deputies communicated with Hidden Imam) → Major Occultation (941–present, no direct communication; awaited return as Mahdi before Day of Judgment)
    - Imamate theology: Imam is divinely appointed, infallible (ma'sum), possesses special knowledge (ilm); Imam as spiritual and political authority
    - Karbala: martyrdom of Husayn (680) as central narrative; Ashura mourning: ta'ziya (passion plays), matam (chest-beating), processions; theological meaning: suffering, injustice, sacrifice for truth
    - Shrine cities: Najaf (Ali's tomb), Karbala (Husayn's tomb), Samarra (askari shrine), Mashhad (Ali al-Rida), Qom (Fatima al-Ma'suma); pilgrimage (ziyara) to shrines; attacked by ISIS and Wahhabi ideology
    - Marjaʿiyyah system: hierarchy of clerical learning (hujjat al-islam, ayatollah, grand ayatollah/marja'); Qom and Najaf as major seminary centers; pluralism of marja' system
    - Khoei (d. 1992), Sistani (b. 1930): quietist marjaʿiyyah (separation of religion and politics); Khomeini (d. 1989): activist wilayat al-faqih (see Jurisprudence section)
  - Ismaili Shi'ism
    - Split after 6th Imam Ja'far al-Sadiq (765): followed Isma'il ibn Ja'far → different Imam lineage
    - Fatimid Caliphate (909–1171): rival to Abbasids; Cairo founded (969); al-Azhar University (970, oldest surviving university); patronage of arts and sciences
    - Nizari-Musta'li split (1094): Nizaris followed Nizar; Musta'lis followed al-Musta'li
    - Nizari Ismailis (Aga Khani): Assassins legend (Alamut fortress, Hasan-i Sabbah); today: Aga Khan IV as 49th Imam; cosmopolitan; Aga Khan Development Network; emphasis on education and pluralism
    - Musta'li/Dawoodi Bohras: priestly hierarchy (Da'i al-Mutlaq); concentrated in Gujarat, Mumbai; distinctive traditions
    - Druze: originated from Ismaili roots (Fatimid caliph al-Hakim); became distinct religion; closed community; primarily Lebanon, Syria, Israel
  - Zaydism
    - Follows Zayd ibn Ali (d. 740), grandson of Husayn; closest to Sunni Islam doctrinally
    - Imam must be politically active (no occultation); can be any qualified Alid descendant
    - Historical Zaydi states: Tabaristan (Iran), Yemen (Mutawakkilite Kingdom, until 1962 revolution)
    - Houthis (Ansar Allah): Zaydi revival movement in Yemen; ongoing civil war (2014–); complex regional proxy dimensions
  - Alawites (Nusayris)
    - Esoteric Shia-derived sect; Syria primarily; Ali divinized in some interpretations; secretive doctrine
    - Assad family (Hafez, Bashar): Alawite minority rule in Syria; political significance
  - Alevism (Turkey)
    - Syncretic: Shia, Sufi, Turkic shamanic elements; no mosque worship; cem ceremonies; dede leadership; 15–25 million adherents in Turkey
    - Distinct from Alawites despite name similarity

- **Islamic Sciences and Civilization**
  - Mathematics: al-Khwarizmi (d. ~850, *al-Jabr* → "algebra"; algorithm named after him); Omar Khayyam (cubic equations, calendar reform); al-Karaji; al-Samawal; decimal fractions; Indian numeral transmission to Europe ("Arabic numerals")
  - Astronomy: zij tables (astronomical handbooks); al-Battani (trigonometric improvements); al-Tusi (Tusi couple, enabling heliocentric models without epicycles; Maragha observatory); Ibn al-Shatir (Damascus, 14th c.; models geometrically identical to Copernicus's — possible transmission)
  - Optics: Ibn al-Haytham / Alhazen (965–1040): *Kitab al-Manazir* ("Book of Optics"); experimental method; camera obscura; intromission theory of vision (light enters eye, not emitted); influence on Roger Bacon, Kepler
  - Medicine: al-Razi / Rhazes (d. ~925): clinical observation, *al-Hawi* (medical encyclopedia); distinguished measles from smallpox; Ibn Sina's *Canon* (see above); al-Zahrawi (surgery, *al-Tasrif*); Ibn al-Nafis (d. 1288): pulmonary circulation discovery (predates Harvey by 300 years)
  - Chemistry: Jabir ibn Hayyan (Geber, d. ~815): experimental alchemy; distillation, crystallization; debated historical figure; al-Razi (practical chemistry)
  - Geography and travel: al-Idrisi (d. 1165, *Tabula Rogeriana* world map for Roger II of Sicily); Ibn Battuta (d. ~1369, *Rihla*: 75,000+ miles traveled across Islamic world and beyond); al-Biruni (d. ~1048: comparative religion, measurement of earth's circumference, India study)
  - Historiography: al-Tabari (d. 923, *Ta'rikh al-Rusul wa'l-Muluk*, universal history); Ibn Khaldun (1332–1406): *Muqaddima* ("Prolegomena"): first systematic philosophy of history; asabiyyah (social cohesion) as driver of civilizational rise and fall; proto-sociology
  - Architecture: mosque development (hypostyle → four-iwan → Ottoman domed); minarets; muqarnas (stalactite vaulting); arabesque and geometric pattern; calligraphy as supreme art
    - Masterworks: Dome of the Rock (691), Great Mosque of Córdoba (785–987), Alhambra (13th–14th c.), Mosque of Ibn Tulun (879), Sultan Ahmed/Blue Mosque (1616), Taj Mahal (1632–53), Sheikh Zayed Mosque (2007)
  - Calligraphy: sacred art par excellence; Kufic, Naskh, Thuluth, Diwani, Nastaliq scripts; Ibn Muqla (d. 940, proportioned script system); Ibn al-Bawwab; modern calligraphers

- **Modern and Contemporary Islam**
  - 18th–19th century reform movements
    - Wahhabism (see Theology section): Arabian Peninsula; puritanical reform; alliance with House of Saud
    - Shah Waliullah (India): hadith scholarship; synthesis of theological schools; influenced Deoband
    - Deobandi movement (1866, India): Darul Uloom Deoband; traditional hadith-based scholarship; anti-colonial; anti-Sufi innovations; Taliban ideological roots
    - Barelvi movement (Ahmad Raza Khan, 1856–1921): South Asian Sunni Sufism defense; devotion to Prophet (mawlid, na'at); rivalry with Deobandis
    - Salafiyya-modernist: Jamal al-Din al-Afghani (d. 1897, pan-Islamism, anti-imperialism); Muhammad Abduh (d. 1905, Grand Mufti of Egypt, rational reform of Islamic thought, reconcile Islam and modernity); Rashid Rida (d. 1935, *al-Manar* journal, shifted toward Salafism)
  - 20th century Islamic movements
    - Muslim Brotherhood (al-Ikhwan al-Muslimun): Hasan al-Banna (Egypt, 1928); Islam as comprehensive system (nizam shamil); social services; political Islam (Islamism)
      - Sayyid Qutb (1906–1966): *Milestones* (*Ma'alim fi'l-Tariq*): jahiliyyah (pre-Islamic ignorance) applied to modern societies; vanguard; revolutionary ideology; executed by Nasser; foundational text for jihadi movements
    - Jamaat-e-Islami: Abul A'la Mawdudi (Pakistan, 1941); systematic Islamic state ideology; theo-democracy; hakimiyyah (God's sovereignty)
    - Islamic modernism: Fazlur Rahman (d. 1988, double movement hermeneutic: historical context → contemporary principle → modern application); Nasr Hamid Abu Zayd (d. 2010, literary approach to Quran; declared apostate by Egyptian courts)
    - Turkish Islam: Kemalist secularism (1924 caliphate abolition, 1928 Latin script); Said Nursi (*Risale-i Nur*); Erbakan and Milli Görüş; AKP and Erdoğan's political Islam; Diyanet (state religious authority)
    - Iranian Revolution (1979): Ayatollah Khomeini; wilayat al-faqih implemented; Islamic Republic; export of revolution; Iran-Iraq War (1980–88); enduring Shia political model
    - Algerian, Afghan, Chechen, Bosnian conflicts: Islamist dimensions; foreign fighter phenomenon; emergence of transnational jihad
  - Jihad: concepts and debates
    - Quranic basis: jihad fi sabil Allah ("striving in the path of God"); greater jihad (spiritual struggle) vs. lesser jihad (armed combat) — hadith tradition debated
    - Classical jurisprudence: dar al-Islam (abode of Islam) vs. dar al-harb (abode of war); rules of engagement (non-combatant protection, proportionality, environmental)
    - Modern: defensive jihad widely accepted; offensive jihad debated; jihadi-Salafism: Abdullah Azzam (*Join the Caravan*), Osama bin Laden, al-Qaeda; ISIS/ISIL (*Dabiq* magazine, "caliphate" claim 2014)
    - Counter-narrative: vast majority of Muslim scholars condemn terrorism; Marrakesh Declaration (2016); Amman Message (2004); Letter to al-Baghdadi (2014, signed by 120+ scholars)
  - Women in Islam: contemporary debates
    - Quranic gender verses: 4:34 (qiwama, "authority/guardianship"); 2:228 (degrees); 24:31 (hijab/modesty); diverse interpretations
    - Classical positions: patriarchal social norms reflected in fiqh; modern reform: Amina Wadud (*Qur'an and Woman*, 1992; led mixed-gender prayer 2005); Asma Barlas; Kecia Ali; Ziba Mir-Hosseini
    - Hijab/niqab debates: personal choice vs. state requirement (Iran, Saudi) vs. state prohibition (France); body politics
    - Women's mosque movement (Saba Mahmood, *Politics of Piety*, 2005); Islamic feminism as field
  - Islam in the West
    - Historical presence: Iberian Islam (711–1492); Sicilian Islam; Eastern European Muslim communities (Balkans, Tatars)
    - Migration and diaspora: postcolonial migration to Europe; African American Islam (Nation of Islam — Elijah Muhammad, Malcolm X, Warith Deen Mohammed's Sunni turn, Louis Farrakhan; distinct from mainstream Islam); American Muslim communities
    - Islamophobia: post-9/11 intensification; surveillance; media representation; Christchurch 2019; scholarly study: Runnymede Trust report (1997, coined "Islamophobia"); Todd Green; Khaled Beydoun
    - European Islam debates: French laïcité, German Leitkultur, British multiculturalism; Tariq Ramadan (*Western Muslims and the Future of Islam*, 2004); Timothy Winter/Abdal Hakim Murad (Cambridge Muslim College)

- **Frontier / Contemporary Research Directions (2020–present)**
  - Quranic studies
    - Corpus Coranicum project: ongoing digital critical edition with Late Antique context; intertextual commentary
    - Early Quranic manuscripts: carbon dating, palaeography; Birmingham/Sanaa/Tübingen fragments; stabilizing 1st-century-Hijra dating
    - Computational Quran studies: digital stylometry; statistical analysis of Meccan vs. Medinan surahs; text-mining of tafsir traditions
    - Intra-Quranic hermeneutics: reading Quran "as a text" (Angelika Neuwirth); structural analysis; ring composition (Michel Cuypers)
    - "Quran seminar" model (Gabriel Said Reynolds et al.): collaborative academic commentary
  - Legal reform and ijtihad
    - Maqasid-based reform (Jasser Auda, *Maqasid al-Shariah as Philosophy of Islamic Law*, 2008): objectives-based reasoning for modern problems
    - Islamic finance: rapid expansion; sukuk (Islamic bonds); takaful (insurance); Shariah-compliant fintech; theoretical debates on form vs. substance
    - Minorities fiqh (fiqh al-aqalliyyat): Taha Jabir al-Alwani, Yusuf al-Qaradawi — legal framework for Muslims in non-Muslim-majority countries
    - Bioethics: Islamic Fiqh Academy rulings on organ transplation, IVF, genetic engineering, brain death criteria, COVID-19 vaccine permissibility
    - Gender justice: ongoing scholarly work on egalitarian readings of Quran and fiqh; women scholars in traditional seminaries; female muftis in some countries (Turkey, UAE)
  - Political developments
    - Post-Arab Spring: democratic Islam, authoritarian resilience, civil war (Syria, Yemen, Libya); Tunisia's democratic experiment and reversal
    - ISIS collapse and aftermath: deradicalization programs; returnee fighters; ideology's persistence online
    - Uyghur persecution in Xinjiang: Chinese government repression of Muslim minority; documentation and international response
    - Afghanistan Taliban return (2021): implications for Islamic governance debates; women's education restrictions
    - Abraham Accords (2020): Gulf-Israel normalization; Muslim-majority state responses; Palestinian dimensions
    - Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 (MBS reforms): social liberalization alongside political repression; religious establishment reform
  - Academic Islamic Studies
    - Decolonizing Islamic Studies: epistemic critique — who studies Islam and how; indigenous vs. Orientalist frameworks
    - Digital humanities: digitization of manuscript collections (Bodleian, Chester Beatty, Istanbul); AI-assisted manuscript reading; open-access hadith databases
    - Lived religion approaches: moving beyond textual Islam to ethnographic study of Muslim practices, popular religion, material culture
    - Cognitive science of Islam: ritual studies; memory and Quran memorization; embodied cognition in prayer
    - Environmental Islam: Islamic environmental ethics; Quran's stewardship (khilafa) concept; Islamic Declaration on Global Climate Change (2015)
    - Interfaith and comparative theology: Scriptural Reasoning movement; Muslim-Christian-Jewish trilateral engagement; A Common Word (2007, 138 Muslim scholars' open letter to Christian leaders)

#### Judaism — Full Theological & Historical Depth

- **Biblical Period and Origins**
  - Torah / Pentateuch (Five Books of Moses)
    - Genesis (Bereshit): creation narratives, patriarchs (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob/Israel, Joseph); covenant theology begins
    - Exodus (Shemot): slavery in Egypt, Moses, Ten Plagues, Exodus event, Sinai revelation, Ten Commandments (Decalogue), Tabernacle instructions
    - Leviticus (Vayikra): priestly code (Torat Kohanim), sacrificial system, purity laws (tumah/taharah), Holiness Code (Lev 17–26), Yom Kippur ritual
    - Numbers (Bamidbar): wilderness wanderings, census, rebellion narratives (Korah), Balaam oracles
    - Deuteronomy (Devarim): Moses' farewell speeches, legal recapitulation, Shema ("Hear O Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is One" — Deut 6:4), centralization of worship, blessings and curses
    - Torah as foundational text: 613 commandments (mitzvot) traditionally enumerated; Mosaic authorship tradition vs. modern critical scholarship
  - Documentary Hypothesis and modern Pentateuchal criticism
    - Julius Wellhausen (1844–1918): classic JEDP model — Yahwist (J), Elohist (E), Deuteronomist (D), Priestly (P) sources
    - Current erosion of classic JEDP: supplementary model (John Van Seters); fragmentary model; neo-Documentarians (Joel Baden, *The Composition of the Pentateuch*, 2012)
    - European school (Konrad Schmid, David Carr): Persian-period literary formation; D and P as primary literary strata
    - Conservative/traditional responses: Umberto Cassuto, Yehezkel Kaufmann (*The Religion of Israel*) — early dating, pre-exilic monotheism
    - Archaeological dimension: William Dever, Israel Finkelstein (*The Bible Unearthed*, 2001) — minimal vs. maximal historicity debate; no evidence for Exodus as described; conquest model vs. gradual settlement vs. internal revolt (Mendenhall, Gottwald)
  - Nevi'im (Prophets)
    - Former Prophets: Joshua, Judges, Samuel, Kings — Deuteronomistic History (Martin Noth): theological retelling of Israel's history through lens of covenant obedience/disobedience
    - Latter Prophets — Major: Isaiah (First Isaiah 1–39, Second Isaiah/Deutero-Isaiah 40–55: Babylonian exile context, Suffering Servant, "comfort ye my people"; Third Isaiah 56–66); Jeremiah (prophet of doom and new covenant, Jer 31:31–34); Ezekiel (exile in Babylon, chariot vision / merkavah, dry bones, temple vision)
    - Latter Prophets — Minor (Twelve): Hosea (marital metaphor for God-Israel), Joel (Day of the Lord), Amos (social justice, "let justice roll down like waters"), Obadiah, Jonah, Micah ("do justice, love mercy, walk humbly"), Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi
    - Prophetic theology: ethical monotheism; social justice; covenant fidelity; eschatological hope; critique of empty ritual
    - Abraham Joshua Heschel (*The Prophets*, 1962): divine pathos — God as emotionally engaged with human suffering
  - Ketuvim (Writings)
    - Psalms (Tehillim): 150 psalms; Davidic attribution tradition; form-critical types (Hermann Gunkel): hymns, laments (individual/communal), thanksgiving, royal, wisdom, enthronement psalms
    - Proverbs (Mishlei): wisdom tradition; personified Wisdom (Hokhmah) in Proverbs 8; practical ethics
    - Job (Iyyov): theodicy; retribution theology challenged; divine speeches from the whirlwind; no simple answer to innocent suffering
    - Song of Songs (Shir ha-Shirim): love poetry; allegorical reading (God-Israel love) in rabbinic tradition; literal/erotic reading in modern scholarship
    - Ruth: Moabite ancestress of David; conversion narrative; chesed (lovingkindness)
    - Lamentations (Eichah): destruction of Jerusalem 586 BCE; communal grief; Tisha B'Av liturgy
    - Ecclesiastes/Qohelet: skeptical wisdom; "vanity of vanities" (hevel havalim); time and chance; epistemological limits
    - Esther: Purim narrative; Persian diaspora; absence of God's name (unique in Hebrew Bible); Jewish survival in exile
    - Daniel: apocalyptic literature; visions of four kingdoms; Son of Man (Dan 7:13–14); second-century BCE composition (Maccabean context)
    - Ezra-Nehemiah: post-exilic restoration; Second Temple construction; Ezra's Torah reading; intermarriage controversy
    - Chronicles: rewritten history from priestly/Levitical perspective
  - Canon and text
    - Hebrew Bible (Tanakh = Torah + Nevi'im + Ketuvim): 24 books in Jewish enumeration (= 39 in Christian OT counting)
    - Masoretic Text (MT): authoritative Hebrew text; Masoretes (6th–10th c. CE) standardized vowels (nikkud), cantillation (te'amim); Aleppo Codex (~930 CE), Leningrad Codex (~1008 CE, basis of Biblia Hebraica Stuttgartensia/Quinta)
    - Dead Sea Scrolls (1947–): pre-Masoretic manuscripts; proto-MT, proto-Septuagint, and unique textual traditions; every book except Esther; Great Isaiah Scroll (1QIsaᵃ)
    - Septuagint (LXX): Greek translation (3rd–2nd c. BCE); includes deuterocanonical books; basis for early Christian OT; divergences from MT (esp. Jeremiah, Samuel)
    - Targumim: Aramaic translations/paraphrases (Targum Onkelos on Torah, Targum Jonathan on Prophets); exegetical expansions

- **Second Temple Judaism (~516 BCE – 70 CE)**
  - Historical framework
    - Persian period (539–332 BCE): Cyrus's edict (Ezra 1); Second Temple built (~516 BCE); Ezra-Nehemiah's reforms; Yehud as Persian province; composition/editing of much of Hebrew Bible in this period
    - Hellenistic period (332–167 BCE): Alexander the Great; Ptolemaic then Seleucid rule; hellenization controversy; Maccabean Revolt (167–160 BCE, Antiochus IV Epiphanes desecrating Temple)
    - Hasmonean dynasty (140–63 BCE): independent Jewish state; combination priest-king problematic; expansion
    - Roman period (63 BCE–70 CE): Pompey's conquest; Herodian dynasty (Herod the Great, 37–4 BCE, Temple expansion); Roman procurators/prefects; great revolt (66–73 CE); destruction of Second Temple (70 CE, Titus) — catastrophic watershed; Masada (73 CE)
    - Bar Kokhba Revolt (132–135 CE): Simon bar Kokhba; Rabbi Akiva's messianic endorsement; Roman devastation; Judea renamed Syria Palaestina; Jews barred from Jerusalem (Aelia Capitolina)
  - Second Temple sects and movements
    - Pharisees: oral Torah tradition alongside written Torah; belief in resurrection, angels, providence; proto-rabbinic; flexible halakhic interpretation; Hillel (lenient) vs. Shammai (strict) schools
    - Sadducees: priestly aristocracy; Temple-centered; rejected oral Torah, resurrection, angels; ceased to exist after Temple destruction
    - Essenes: ascetic communal movement; identified by most scholars with Qumran community (Dead Sea Scrolls); Teacher of Righteousness; dualistic theology; eschatological expectation; purity rules
    - Zealots: armed resistance to Rome; Fourth Philosophy (Josephus); Sicarii (dagger-men)
    - Samaritans: separate Israelite community; Samaritan Pentateuch; Mount Gerizim temple; survive today (~800 individuals)
  - Apocalyptic and pseudepigraphic literature
    - 1 Enoch (Book of Enoch): Watchers myth, Son of Man, eschatological judgment; hugely influential on early Christianity
    - Jubilees: rewritten Torah with 364-day solar calendar; halakhic; found at Qumran
    - Testaments of the Twelve Patriarchs; Psalms of Solomon; 4 Ezra (2 Esdras); 2 Baruch
    - Apocalypticism: divine determinism, dualism (good vs. evil ages), periodization of history, angelic/demonic cosmology, imminent eschatological reversal
  - Josephus (37–~100 CE): *Jewish War*, *Jewish Antiquities*, *Against Apion*, *Life*; invaluable historical source; wrote in Greek for Roman audience; controversial collaborator figure
  - Philo of Alexandria (~20 BCE – 50 CE): allegorical interpretation of Torah through Middle Platonic philosophy; Logos theology; influenced early Christian exegesis (esp. Gospel of John, Clement of Alexandria, Origen)

- **Rabbinic Judaism: Formation of the Oral Torah**
  - Transition: from Temple to Torah
    - After 70 CE: sacrificial system ends; synagogue (beit knesset) and study house (beit midrash) replace Temple as institutions; rabbis replace priests as religious authorities
    - Yavneh (Jamnia) tradition: Johanan ben Zakkai establishes academy (~70 CE); legendary account of escape from besieged Jerusalem; council debates on canon and practice (Jamnia hypothesis partly disputed)
    - Oral Torah (Torah she-be'al peh): belief that God gave Moses both written and oral Torah at Sinai; oral tradition transmitted through chain of authorities (avot — Mishnah Avot 1:1)
  - Mishnah (~200 CE)
    - Compiled/edited by Rabbi Judah ha-Nasi (Yehudah ha-Nasi, "the Prince") in Galilee
    - Six Orders (Sedarim, mnemonic ZeRa'iM MoED NaShIM NeZiKIN KoDaShIM TaHaROT):
      - Zera'im (Seeds): agricultural laws; Berakhot (blessings/prayer) opening tractate
      - Mo'ed (Festival): Shabbat, Pesahim (Passover), Yoma (Yom Kippur), Sukkah, Rosh Hashanah, Megillah (Purim)
      - Nashim (Women): Yevamot (levirate marriage), Ketubot (marriage contracts), Nedarim (vows), Sotah, Gittin (divorce), Kiddushin (betrothal)
      - Nezikin (Damages): Bava Kamma, Bava Metzia, Bava Batra (torts, property, commerce); Sanhedrin (courts, criminal law); Avot (Ethics of the Fathers — wisdom sayings); Makkot; Horayot
      - Kodashim (Holy Things): sacrificial law (Zevahim, Menahot); Hullin (slaughter for non-sacred consumption); Bekhorot (firstborn)
      - Taharot (Purities): ritual purity laws; Niddah (menstrual purity — still practically observed); Mikva'ot; Kelim (vessels)
    - Legal style: typically presents anonymous law (stam) followed by named disputes; terse Hebrew; legal categories (halakhot) organized topically
    - Tannaim: rabbis of the Mishnaic period (~1st–early 3rd c.): Hillel and Shammai (pre-70 CE), Rabban Gamaliel, Rabbi Akiva (~50–135, systematized halakha, supported Bar Kokhba), Rabbi Ishmael (hermeneutical rival of Akiva), Rabbi Meir, Rabbi Shimon bar Yohai (traditional author of Zohar)
  - Tosefta: supplementary collection to Mishnah; same structure/period; attributed to Rabbi Hiyya and Rabbi Oshaya; often provides parallel and additional traditions
  - Halakhic Midrashim: running legal commentaries on Exodus–Deuteronomy keyed to biblical text
    - Mekhilta de-Rabbi Ishmael (Exodus); Sifra (Leviticus); Sifre (Numbers, Deuteronomy)
    - Hermeneutical rules: 7 middot of Hillel; 13 middot of Rabbi Ishmael (recited daily in traditional liturgy); 32 middot of Rabbi Eliezer ben Yose ha-Gelili (aggadic)
  - Talmud
    - **Jerusalem Talmud (Talmud Yerushalmi, ~400 CE)**
      - Compiled in Galilee (Tiberias, Caesarea, Sepphoris); shorter, less edited; Palestinian Aramaic
      - Coverage: orders Zera'im, Mo'ed, Nashim, Nezikin (partial); no Kodashim or Taharot gemara
    - **Babylonian Talmud (Talmud Bavli, ~500–600 CE)**
      - Compiled in Babylonian academies (Sura, Pumbedita, Nehardea); much more extensive; Babylonian Aramaic
      - Authoritative Talmud in later Judaism; ~2.5 million words; 37 tractates with gemara (of 63 Mishnah tractates)
      - Structure: Mishnah passage → Gemara (Aramaic discussion expanding, debating, cross-referencing, digressing)
      - Amoraim: rabbis of the Gemara period (~3rd–6th c.): Rav and Shmuel (1st generation Babylonian); Rav Huna; Rabbah; Abaye and Rava (4th generation, pivotal dialecticians); Rav Ashi and Ravina (final redactors traditionally)
      - Stammaim ("anonymous" redactors, ~6th–7th c.): David Weiss Halivni and Shamma Friedman argue the anonymous dialectical layer is the latest editorial stratum; debated
    - Talmudic methodology
      - Sugya (passage): basic unit of Talmudic argument; structured as question-answer-objection-resolution
      - Dialectical argumentation: hypothetical questioning (ma'i ka mashma lan?), reductio ad absurdum (teyuvta), difficulty and resolution (kashya/tiruts), analogical reasoning
      - Halakha (law) and Aggadah (narrative/theology/folklore/ethics): interwoven; aggadah includes parables, legends, moral tales, cosmology, theodicy, mystical hints
    - Modern Talmud scholarship
      - Traditional study: yeshiva method; daf yomi (daily page cycle, 7.5 years, instituted by Meir Shapiro, 1923; current cycle completed 2020, next 2028)
      - Wissenschaft des Judentums (19th c.): scientific study of Judaism; Leopold Zunz, Abraham Geiger, Heinrich Graetz (historian)
      - Critical Talmud studies: Saul Lieberman (Palestinian Talmud); Jacob Neusner (prolific translator and analyst); David Weiss Halivni (*Mekorot u-Mesorot*, source criticism); Shamma Friedman (sugyot analysis)
      - Feminist Talmud studies: Judith Hauptman, Charlotte Fonrobert, Tal Ilan; women in Talmudic law
      - Digital Talmud projects: Friedberg Jewish Manuscript Society; Hachi Garsinan (critical editions project); Sefaria (open-source library of Jewish texts)
  - Midrash (Aggadic)
    - Midrash Rabbah: homiletical commentaries on Torah and Five Megillot (Genesis Rabbah, Exodus Rabbah, Leviticus Rabbah, etc.); composed over centuries (~3rd–12th c.)
    - Midrash Tanhuma: sermonic midrash on Torah portions; multiple recensions
    - Pesikta de-Rav Kahana, Pesikta Rabbati: festival/special Shabbat homilies
    - Pirkei de-Rabbi Eliezer: narrative midrash with apocalyptic elements
    - Aggadic method: creative biblical interpretation; fills gaps in narrative (lacunae); intertext; wordplay; gematria (numerical values of letters); parable (mashal) — "The king who..." framework
    - Exegetical principles: peshat (plain sense), remez (hint/allegory), derash (homiletical), sod (mystical) — PaRDeS framework (medieval systematization attributed to Moses de León or earlier)

- **Halakha: Jewish Law**
  - Codification history
    - Talmud as primary halakhic authority (but not organized as a code); requires expert analysis to extract rulings
    - Geonic period (~600–1040 CE): Geonim as heads of Babylonian academies (Sura, Pumbedita); responsa (she'elot u-teshuvot); Rav Amram Gaon (first siddur/prayer book, ~860); Rav Saadia Gaon (882–942, philosophical works, Arabic translations)
    - Rif (Isaac Alfasi, 1013–1103): *Sefer ha-Halakhot* — condensed Talmud, extracting practical rulings; precursor to full codification
    - **Maimonides / Rambam** (Moses ben Maimon, 1138–1204): *Mishneh Torah* (Yad ha-Hazakah, "Strong Hand"): comprehensive 14-volume code of all Jewish law; organized topically; no sources cited (controversial); written in clear Mishnaic Hebrew; still authoritative especially for Yemenite and Sephardic Jews
    - Rosh (Asher ben Yehiel, ~1250–1327): German-born, moved to Spain; Talmudic commentary integrating Ashkenazic and Sephardic traditions; foundation for his son Jacob's code
    - Tur (Jacob ben Asher, ~1270–1340): *Arba'ah Turim* ("Four Rows"): organized by four divisions — Orah Hayyim (daily life, prayer, Shabbat, holidays), Yoreh De'ah (dietary law, purity, vows), Even ha-Ezer (marriage/divorce), Hoshen Mishpat (civil/criminal law); format adopted by Shulhan Arukh
    - **Shulhan Arukh** (Joseph Karo, 1488–1575): "Set Table"; published 1565; follows Tur's four-division structure; Sephardic rulings predominant
      - Mapah ("Tablecloth"): Moses Isserles / Rema (1530–1572, Kraków); Ashkenazic glosses on Shulhan Arukh; together they became universally authoritative
      - Later supercommentaries: Turei Zahav (Taz), Siftei Kohen (Shakh) — 17th-century Polish commentators; Mishnah Berurah (Israel Meir Kagan / Chofetz Chaim, 1904–1907) on Orah Hayyim — standard Ashkenazi authority
    - Arukh ha-Shulhan (Yechiel Michel Epstein, 1829–1908): alternative comprehensive code; historically grounded; somewhat more lenient
  - Responsa literature (She'elot u-Teshuvot)
    - From Geonim through present day: thousands of volumes; case-law application of halakha to new situations
    - Major respondents: Rambam, Rashba (Solomon ben Adret), Rivash, Rabbi Moshe Feinstein (1895–1986, *Igrot Moshe* — leading American Orthodox posek), Rabbi Ovadia Yosef (1920–2013, Sephardic chief rabbi of Israel)
    - Modern responsa topics: electricity on Shabbat, organ transplantation, IVF, internet use, COVID-19 era observance; Tzomet Institute (technology and halakha)
  - Key halakhic concepts
    - Mitzvot: 613 commandments (248 positive, 365 negative; Maimonides' *Sefer ha-Mitzvot*); rabbinic commandments (de-rabbanan) in addition
    - Shabbat: 39 melakhot (categories of creative labor forbidden); eruv (boundary permitting carrying); muktze (objects set aside); central institution of Jewish life; Abraham Joshua Heschel, *The Sabbath* (1951): "palace in time"
    - Kashrut (dietary law): permitted/forbidden animals (Lev 11, Deut 14); separation of meat and dairy (basar be-halav); shechita (ritual slaughter); supervision (hashgacha, hekhsher certification); modern food industry challenges
    - Family purity (taharat ha-mishpacha): niddah laws; mikveh (ritual bath) immersion; intimate life halakha
    - Prayer: three daily services (Shacharit, Mincha, Ma'ariv); Shema and Amidah (Shemoneh Esreh, standing prayer) as core; minyan (quorum of ten); siddur development
    - Life cycle: brit milah (circumcision, 8th day); bar/bat mitzvah; marriage (kiddushin, ketubah, chuppah, seven blessings); divorce (get); mourning (shiva, shloshim, kaddish)
    - Holiday cycle: Rosh Hashanah (New Year, shofar), Yom Kippur (Day of Atonement, fasting), Sukkot (booths), Shemini Atzeret/Simchat Torah, Hanukkah, Purim, Pesach (Passover, seder, matzah, Haggadah), Shavuot (Weeks/Pentecost), Tisha B'Av (9th of Av, Temple destruction mourning)

- **Jewish Philosophy**
  - Medieval Jewish philosophy
    - **Saadia Gaon** (882–942): *Emunot ve-De'ot* ("Beliefs and Opinions"): first systematic Jewish philosophical theology; influenced by Mu'tazili kalam; creation ex nihilo; rational basis for commandments; refuted Karaism
    - **Solomon ibn Gabirol** (Avicebron, ~1021–1058): *Fons Vitae* ("Fountain of Life"): neoplatonic metaphysics; universal hylomorphism; known in Latin West as "Avicebron" (Christian scholars didn't know he was Jewish)
    - **Bahya ibn Paquda** (~11th c.): *Hovot ha-Levavot* ("Duties of the Heart"): inner piety and spiritual ethics; Sufi-influenced; ten gates of spiritual life
    - **Judah Halevi** (~1075–1141): *Kuzari*: dialogue defending Judaism against philosophy, Christianity, and Islam; revelation over reason; Hebrew language's uniqueness; Land of Israel's spiritual centrality; Jewish people's special prophetic capacity; philosophical critique of Aristotelianism
    - **Abraham ibn Ezra** (1089–~1167): biblical commentator; rationalist; hinted at post-Mosaic elements in Torah (precursor to Pentateuchal criticism); astronomy, mathematics
    - **Maimonides / Rambam** (Moses ben Maimon, 1138–1204): greatest Jewish philosopher
      - *Guide of the Perplexed* (Moreh Nevukhim, ~1190): reconciling Aristotelian philosophy with Torah; written in Judeo-Arabic
      - Negative theology: God can only be described by what God is not; divine attributes as negations; critique of anthropomorphism
      - Prophecy: naturalistic (philosophical perfection + imagination) with divine special factor; Moses unique (direct divine communication)
      - Creation: inclines toward creation ex nihilo but acknowledges Aristotelian eternity arguments; neither demonstrable
      - Divine providence proportionate to intellectual perfection; problem of evil
      - Reasons for commandments (ta'amei ha-mitzvot): rational explanations for ritual laws; allegorical interpretation of Temple worship as weaning from paganism
      - 13 Principles of Faith (in Commentary on Mishnah, Sanhedrin 10): God's existence, unity, incorporeality, eternity, exclusive worship, prophecy, Moses' supremacy, divine origin of Torah, Torah's immutability, divine omniscience, reward/punishment, messiah, resurrection
      - Maimonidean controversy: fierce opposition from anti-rationalists; book burnings (1232, Paris); Nachmanides mediated; split continued for generations
    - **Nachmanides / Ramban** (Moses ben Nahman, 1194–1270): kabbalistic-mystical commentator; Barcelona Disputation (1263) with Pablo Christiani; Land of Israel as commandment; critique of Maimonides' rationalism
    - **Gersonides / Ralbag** (Levi ben Gershon, 1288–1344): *Milhamot Hashem* ("Wars of the Lord"): radical Aristotelian; divine knowledge limited to universals (not particulars); creation from pre-existing matter; controversial
    - **Hasdai Crescas** (1340–1410): *Or Hashem* ("Light of the Lord"): critique of Aristotle and Maimonides; infinite space; divine love over intellectual perfection; voluntaristic theology; influenced Spinoza
    - **Joseph Albo** (~1380–1444): *Sefer ha-Ikkarim* ("Book of Principles"): reduced dogmas to three roots — God's existence, revelation, reward and punishment
  - Early modern Jewish philosophy
    - **Baruch Spinoza** (1632–1677): Amsterdam; excommunicated (herem, 1656) from Portuguese-Jewish community
      - *Ethics* (1677, posthumous): geometric method; God = Nature (Deus sive Natura); substance monism; determinism; no personal God, no free will; conatus (striving to persevere)
      - *Theologico-Political Treatise* (1670): first systematic biblical criticism; Torah not by Moses; miracles impossible; separation of philosophy and theology; freedom of thought; democracy
      - Impact: "God-intoxicated man" (Novalis) or atheist; foundational for Enlightenment, secularism, biblical criticism; recent Jewish reclamation (Yirmiyahu Yovel, Steven Nadler, Rebecca Goldstein)
    - **Moses Mendelssohn** (1729–1786): "German Socrates"; father of Haskalah (Jewish Enlightenment)
      - *Jerusalem, or On Religious Power and Judaism* (1783): Judaism as revealed legislation, not dogma; religious tolerance; separation of church and state; universal rational ethics + particular Jewish practice
      - Bible translation into German (in Hebrew characters): brought Bildung (education) to Jewish masses; opposed by traditionalists
      - Emancipation debate: how to be modern European and observant Jew; profound question for all subsequent Judaism
  - Modern Jewish philosophy
    - **Hermann Cohen** (1842–1918): Neo-Kantian; *Religion of Reason out of the Sources of Judaism* (1919, posthumous): God as ideal of ethical perfection; correlation between God and humanity; messianism as infinite ethical task
    - **Franz Rosenzweig** (1886–1929): *The Star of Redemption* (1921): anti-Hegelian; three elements: God, world, human; creation, revelation, redemption as relations; Judaism and Christianity as complementary paths; existential return to Jewish practice; Lehrhaus (House of Learning) with Martin Buber
    - **Martin Buber** (1878–1965): *I and Thou* (Ich und Du, 1923): dialogical philosophy; I-Thou (genuine encounter) vs. I-It (instrumentalizing); God as the Eternal Thou; Hasidic tales; Zionism (cultural, not political statism); dialogue with Christianity and Islam
    - **Abraham Joshua Heschel** (1907–1972): *God in Search of Man* (1955): divine pathos; God needs human response; wonder and radical amazement; depth theology; prophetic consciousness; *The Sabbath* (1951); civil rights activism (marched with MLK at Selma); interfaith pioneer (Vatican II)
    - **Mordecai Kaplan** (1881–1983): *Judaism as a Civilization* (1934): Reconstructionism; Judaism not just religion but evolving civilization; naturalistic theology — God as "Power that makes for salvation"; rejected chosenness; democratic communities; bat mitzvah innovation (his daughter Judith, 1922)
    - **Joseph Soloveitchik** (1903–1993): "the Rav"; leader of Modern Orthodoxy in America
      - *Halakhic Man* (1944): ideal personality type; halakha as creative, world-constructing system; a priori legal categories applied to reality (Kantian-Maimonidean)
      - *The Lonely Man of Faith* (1965): dialectic of Adam I (majestic, creative) and Adam II (covenantal, surrendering); existential tension irresoluble
    - **Emmanuel Levinas** (1906–1995): Lithuanian-born, French philosopher; Judaism inflects all his philosophy
      - *Totality and Infinity* (1961): ethics as first philosophy; the Face of the Other commands "Thou shalt not kill"; infinite responsibility pre-cognitive
      - *Otherwise than Being* (1974): substitution; hostage to the Other; subjectivity as ethical vulnerability
      - Talmudic readings: phenomenological-philosophical interpretation of Talmudic passages (collected in *Difficult Freedom*, *Nine Talmudic Readings*)
    - **Emil Fackenheim** (1916–2003): 614th commandment: "Thou shalt not hand Hitler posthumous victories" — Jews must survive, remember, not despair; *God's Presence in History* (1970); *To Mend the World* (1982)
    - **Eugene Borowitz** (1924–2016): covenant theology for Reform Judaism; *Renewing the Covenant* (1991); postmodern autonomy balanced with covenantal responsibility
    - **Judith Plaskow** (b. 1947): *Standing Again at Sinai* (1990): feminist theology of Judaism; women at Sinai; critique of patriarchal God-language; reimagining halakha, Torah, community
    - **Tamar Ross** (b. 1938): Orthodox feminist; *Expanding the Palace of Torah* (2004): cumulative revelation; divine revelation unfolds through history
    - **David Hartman** (1931–2013): *A Living Covenant* (1985): covenantal philosophy; human dignity and divine-human partnership; pluralism within Orthodoxy

- **Jewish Mysticism (Kabbalah)**
  - Early mysticism
    - Merkavah (Chariot) mysticism: based on Ezekiel 1, Isaiah 6; heavenly ascent through palaces (heikhalot); dangerous; angelic liturgy; *Hekhalot Rabbati*, *Hekhalot Zutarti*, *Ma'aseh Merkavah*; 2nd–7th centuries
    - Sefer Yetzirah ("Book of Creation," 3rd–6th c.): cosmogony through 10 sefirot (primordial numbers) and 22 letters of Hebrew alphabet; foundation for later Kabbalah; brief, cryptic
    - Sefer ha-Bahir ("Book of Brightness," ~12th c., Provence): earliest kabbalistic text; symbolic divine attributes; reincarnation (gilgul); origin debated (Gershom Scholem: redacted from older Oriental materials)
  - Classical Kabbalah
    - Provençal-Catalan school (12th–13th c.): Isaac the Blind (Narbonne); Girona circle: Azriel, Ezra ben Solomon, Nachmanides (drew on Kabbalah subtly in biblical commentary)
    - **Sefer ha-Zohar** ("Book of Splendor," late 13th c.)
      - Traditionally attributed to Rabbi Shimon bar Yohai (2nd c. CE tannaitic sage); modern scholarship (Gershom Scholem) attributes primary authorship to **Moses de León** (1240–1305, Castile)
      - Aramaic mystical commentary on Torah; narrative framework of R. Shimon and companions wandering and discussing
      - Sefirotic system: 10 sefirot (divine emanations) as structure of divine personality: Keter (Crown), Hokhmah (Wisdom), Binah (Understanding), Hesed (Lovingkindness), Gevurah (Judgment/Strength), Tiferet (Beauty/Harmony), Netzach (Victory/Eternity), Hod (Splendor), Yesod (Foundation), Malkhut/Shekhinah (Kingdom/Divine Presence)
      - Shekhinah: feminine divine presence; in exile with Israel; mystical union (yihud) of masculine and feminine within God as cosmic restoration
      - Ein Sof ("Without End"): the infinite God beyond all sefirot; utterly transcendent; sefirot mediate between Ein Sof and created world
      - Torah mysticism: Torah as body of God; esoteric meanings in every letter; white fire on black fire
      - Evil (Sitra Ahra, "Other Side"): demonic realm fed by human sin; Kellipot (shells/husks) trapping divine sparks
      - Became canonical in Jewish mysticism; influenced all subsequent Kabbalah, liturgy, and popular piety
    - Ecstatic/Prophetic Kabbalah: Abraham Abulafia (1240–~1291): letter-combination meditation (hokhmah ha-tzeruf); breathing techniques; prophetic experience; separate stream from theosophical Kabbalah; influenced some Christian Kabbalists
    - Joseph Gikatilla (1248–~1305): *Sha'arei Orah* ("Gates of Light"): systematic guide to sefirot and divine names
  - Lurianic Kabbalah
    - **Isaac Luria** (Ha-Ari, 1534–1572): Safed, Galilee; taught orally; doctrines recorded by Hayyim Vital (*Etz Hayyim*, "Tree of Life") and others
    - **Tzimtzum** (contraction/withdrawal): God contracted infinite light to create "empty space" (halal panui) for creation; divine self-limitation enables finite world; paradox of divine absence/presence
    - **Shevirat ha-Kelim** (Breaking of the Vessels): primordial catastrophe; vessels unable to contain divine light shattered; sparks of holiness fell into kelipot (husks) of material world; origin of evil and disorder
    - **Tikkun** (repair/rectification): cosmic restoration by gathering and elevating fallen sparks through mitzvot, prayer, ethical action; human role essential in repairing God's broken creation
    - Partzufim (divine configurations/faces): reinterpretation of sefirot as interacting personalities (Atik Yomin, Arikh Anpin, Abba, Imma, Ze'ir Anpin, Nukva)
    - Gilgul (reincarnation/transmigration of souls): developed extensively in Lurianic system; *Sha'ar ha-Gilgulim* (Gate of Reincarnations)
    - Impact: enormously influential; reshaped Jewish liturgy, custom, theology; Sabbatean and Hasidic movements rooted in Lurianic ideas
  - Sabbateanism and its aftermath
    - **Shabbetai Tzvi** (1626–1676): declared himself Messiah (1665); massive movement across Jewish diaspora; Nathan of Gaza as prophet; converted to Islam under Ottoman pressure (1666); devastating disillusionment
    - Crypto-Sabbatean groups: Dönmeh (Ottoman converts externally Muslim, secretly Sabbatean); Jacob Frank (1726–1791, Frankism): antinomian; eventually converted followers to Catholicism; nihilistic theology
    - Impact on Kabbalah: discredited public messianism; drove Kabbalah underground in some communities; contributed indirectly to Hasidism and Haskalah as alternative responses
  - Later developments
    - Moshe Hayyim Luzzatto (Ramhal, 1707–1746): *Mesillat Yesharim* ("Path of the Just"): ethical-mystical classic; *Derekh Hashem* ("The Way of God"): theological system; suspected Sabbateanism; produced kabbalistic revelations
    - Vilna Gaon (Elijah ben Solomon Zalman, 1720–1797): greatest Lithuanian scholar; wrote kabbalistic commentaries alongside Talmudic and halakhic works; anti-Hasidic but pro-Kabbalah
    - Modern academic study: **Gershom Scholem** (1897–1982): founder of academic Kabbalah studies; *Major Trends in Jewish Mysticism* (1941); *Sabbatai Sevi* (1957/1973); transformed understanding of Jewish mysticism
    - Moshe Idel (b. 1947): Scholem's successor and critic; *Kabbalah: New Perspectives* (1988); emphasized experiential/ecstatic dimension vs. Scholem's theosophical focus; Hasidism as continuation of ecstatic Kabbalah
    - Elliot Wolfson: phenomenology of visionary experience; gender in Kabbalah; *Through a Speculum That Shines* (1994)
    - Contemporary Kabbalah: Rav Kook's mystical Zionism; Kabbalah Centre (popular/New Age, controversial); neo-Hasidic revival (Arthur Green, *Ehyeh: A Kabbalah for Tomorrow*, 2003)

- **Hasidism**
  - Origins
    - **Israel ben Eliezer (Ba'al Shem Tov / Besht, ~1700–1760)**: founder; Podolia (Ukraine); healer, storyteller, charismatic leader
    - Teaching: divine presence (Shekhinah) permeates everything; joy (simcha) in worship; devekut (cleaving/communion with God) accessible to all, not just scholars; prayer with kavvanah (intention) over dry Talmudic study; elevation of the ordinary
    - Social context: poverty, post-Chmielnicki massacres (1648–49), Sabbatean disillusionment; Hasidism offered spiritual renewal to common Jews
  - Spread and major dynasties
    - Dov Ber of Mezeritch (Maggid, d. 1772): Besht's successor; systematized teachings; trained the major leaders who spread Hasidism across Eastern Europe
    - **Chabad-Lubavitch**: Schneur Zalman of Liadi (1745–1812); *Tanya* (Likkutei Amarim, 1797): Hasidic psychology — divine soul vs. animal soul; intellectual approach to mysticism (Chabad = Chokhmah, Binah, Da'at); Rebbe Menachem Mendel Schneerson (1902–1994): global outreach, shluchim (emissaries); messianic claims controversy (some followers considered him Mashiach; died without being identified as such; movement continues, debate persists)
    - **Breslov**: Nachman of Breslov (1772–1810): great-grandson of Besht; radical faith; *Likkutei Moharan*; famous stories (allegorical tales); hitbodedut (personal, spontaneous prayer in solitude); "dead Hasidim" — never appointed successor; modern revival (Na Nach movement; Uman pilgrimage on Rosh Hashanah)
    - **Satmar**: Joel Teitelbaum (1887–1979): anti-Zionist; *Vayoel Moshe* (1961): theological opposition to secular Jewish state; largest Hasidic group in North America (Williamsburg, Kiryas Joel)
    - **Ger (Gur)**: Yitzchak Meir Alter (1799–1866); *Chiddushei ha-Rim*; largest Hasidic group in Israel; emphasis on Talmud study within Hasidic framework
    - **Belz, Bobov, Vizhnitz, Skvir, Karlin-Stolin, Toldos Aharon, Munkács**: major dynasties; each with distinctive customs (minhagim), dress, music (niggunim), and theological emphases
  - Mitnagdim (Opponents)
    - Vilna Gaon (Elijah ben Solomon Zalman): led opposition; 1772 herem (excommunication) against Hasidim
    - Critique: debased Torah study; pantheistic tendencies; uncontrolled ecstasy; personality cult around rebbes; deviation from normative halakha
    - Gradual reconciliation: by 19th century, common enemy of Haskalah/secularism brought rapprochement; both streams now under "ultra-Orthodox" (Haredi) umbrella
  - Hasidic thought and practice
    - Tzaddik (righteous one / Rebbe): channel between God and community; spiritual intermediary; court (hoyf) with followers (Hasidim); dynastic succession
    - Tish (table gathering): communal meal with Rebbe; singing, Torah teachings, shrayim (food from Rebbe's plate)
    - Niggun (melody): wordless or worded mystical melodies; central to Hasidic worship; each dynasty has distinctive musical traditions
    - Devekut: constant communion with God; even mundane activities as divine service (avodah be-gashmiyut, worship through corporeality)
    - Histalkut and hitlabshut: withdrawal and investiture — contemplative states
    - Modern Hasidism: estimated 750,000+ globally; concentrated in New York (Williamsburg, Borough Park, Crown Heights), Jerusalem, Bnei Brak, Antwerp, London, Montreal; high birth rates; growing political influence; tensions with modernity (internet, education, gender roles)

- **Denominations of Modern Judaism**
  - Orthodox Judaism
    - Modern Orthodoxy: Torah u-Madda (Torah and secular knowledge); Samson Raphael Hirsch (1808–1888, *Torah im Derekh Eretz* — Torah with worldly engagement; Neo-Orthodoxy); Joseph Soloveitchik (see Philosophy section); Yeshiva University; Religious Zionism (Mizrachi movement); engagement with modernity while maintaining halakhic commitment
    - Ultra-Orthodox / Haredi: Hatam Sofer (1762–1839): "hadash asur min ha-Torah" ("the new is forbidden by Torah" — resistance to reform); Agudat Yisrael (1912); yeshiva world (Litvish/Lithuanian): Volozhin, Mir, Ponevezh, Lakewood (Beth Medrash Govoha); Hasidic world; separation from secular culture; strictest halakhic observance; Haredi growth: Israel (12–13% of population, growing rapidly), North America, Europe
    - Open Orthodoxy (Avi Weiss, Yeshivat Chovevei Torah, 2000; Yeshivat Maharat for women clergy): contested status; rejected by mainstream Orthodox institutions
    - Sephardic Orthodoxy: Ovadia Yosef; Shas political party; restoration of Sephardic halakhic practice (Ben Ish Hai, Kaf ha-Hayyim)
  - Conservative Judaism (Masorti)
    - Historical origins: Zacharias Frankel (1801–1875, "positive-historical Judaism": tradition evolves organically; Breslau Seminary); Solomon Schechter (1847–1915, "catholic Israel": consensus of observant community defines halakha; JTS president; Genizah scholar)
    - Jewish Theological Seminary (JTS, New York): center of Conservative movement; Louis Ginzberg, Saul Lieberman, Abraham Joshua Heschel taught there
    - Halakhic approach: binding but developing; Committee on Jewish Law and Standards (CJLS): majority/minority opinions; drove by responsa; significant rulings: driving on Shabbat (1950, Dorff-Nevins), women's equality (counted in minyan 1973; ordained as rabbis 1985), LGBTQ+ inclusion (2006 Dorff-Nevins-Reisner teshuvah)
    - Decline in North America: membership falling since 1990s; ideological middle ground squeezed between Orthodoxy and Reform
    - Masorti movement in Israel and worldwide
  - Reform Judaism
    - Origins: early 19th-century Germany; Israel Jacobson (Seesen temple, 1810); Hamburg Temple (1818); Abraham Geiger (1810–1874): historical criticism of tradition; prophetic ethics as Judaism's essence; ceremonial law not binding
    - Pittsburgh Platform (1885): classical Reform; rejected ritual law, dietary laws, priestly garments; universal ethical monotheism; anti-Zionism
    - Columbus Platform (1937): partial retreat; acknowledged value of ritual, Jewish peoplehood
    - Centenary Perspective (1976) and Pittsburgh Principles (1999): return to ritual, Hebrew, Israel connection; personal autonomy "informed choice"
    - Hebrew Union College-Jewish Institute of Religion (HUC-JIR); Central Conference of American Rabbis (CCAR); Union for Reform Judaism (URJ)
    - Milestones: first woman rabbi (Regina Jonas, 1935, Germany; Sally Priesand, 1972, HUC — first in America); patrilineal descent resolution (1983); same-sex marriage (2000)
    - Global Liberal/Progressive Judaism: Leo Baeck College (UK); World Union for Progressive Judaism; Israel Movement for Reform and Progressive Judaism (growing but minority)
  - Reconstructionist Judaism
    - Mordecai Kaplan (see Philosophy): Judaism as evolving religious civilization
    - Reconstructionist Rabbinical College (1968, Philadelphia); now "Reconstructing Judaism"
    - Smallest denomination; radical democratic governance; liturgical creativity; emphasis on community over theology
    - First denomination to ordain openly gay/lesbian rabbis (1984); non-binary inclusion early
  - Renewal Judaism
    - Zalman Schachter-Shalomi (1924–2014, "Reb Zalman"): founded Jewish Renewal; neo-Hasidic; ecumenical; meditation; eco-Judaism; ALEPH: Alliance for Jewish Renewal
    - Arthur Green, Marcia Falk, Arthur Waskow: key figures; progressive politics + deep Jewish spirituality
  - Secular/Humanistic Judaism
    - Sherwin Wine (1928–2007): Society for Humanistic Judaism (1963); non-theistic; cultural Jewish identity; ethical humanism
    - Secular Jewish culture: Yiddish literature (Sholem Aleichem, I. L. Peretz, Isaac Bashevis Singer); Jewish community centers; secular Zionism
  - Karaism
    - Anan ben David (8th c.): traditional founder; rejected Oral Torah/Talmud; Scripture alone (similar in principle to sola scriptura)
    - Medieval Karaite community: significant in Egypt, Crimea, Lithuania; intellectual engagement with Rabbanite scholars (Saadia Gaon's refutation)
    - Modern Karaites: ~50,000; Israel, United States; recognized as Jews by Israeli law

- **The Holocaust (Shoah) and Jewish Theology**
  - Historical dimensions (cross-reference with Modern History sections)
    - Six million Jews murdered (1941–1945); Eastern European Jewry nearly annihilated; one-third of world's Jews killed
    - Destruction of yeshivot, Hasidic courts, Jewish intellectual culture of Eastern Europe; entire communities wiped out
  - Theological responses
    - **Silence and crisis**: classical theodicy frameworks shattered; how can a covenantal God allow this?
    - **Richard Rubenstein** (*After Auschwitz*, 1966): God is dead; return to paganism/nature religion; no divine meaning in history
    - **Emil Fackenheim**: 614th commandment — refuse Hitler posthumous victories; God commanding at Auschwitz; tikkun (mending) as imperative
    - **Eliezer Berkovits** (*Faith After the Holocaust*, 1973): hester panim (hiding of God's face); free will defense; suffering servant theology applied to Israel; faith despite evidence
    - **Irving Greenberg**: voluntary covenant — after Auschwitz, covenant renewed by Israel's free choice, not compulsion; "moment faith" surviving darkness
    - **Hans Jonas** (*The Concept of God after Auschwitz*, 1987): God limited; divine self-limitation; God suffers; post-Auschwitz theology requires "un-omnipotent" God
    - **Melissa Raphael** (*The Female Face of God in Auschwitz*, 2003): feminist Holocaust theology; Shekhinah present in women's care for each other in camps
    - Holocaust memory: Yad Vashem, USHMM, Shoah Foundation; *Yizkor* books; survivor testimony; intergenerational trauma; memory as religious obligation
    - Post-Holocaust liturgy: Yom HaShoah (Holocaust Remembrance Day); new prayers and poems (Dan Pagis, Paul Celan, Nelly Sachs)

- **Zionism and Judaism**
  - Proto-Zionism and religious antecedents
    - Prayer and yearning for Zion: daily liturgy ("next year in Jerusalem"); messianic hope for return
    - Traditional view: return to Zion through divine Messiah, not political action; quietism; "three oaths" (Ketubot 111a): Israel not to ascend "by a wall," not to rebel against nations; nations not to oppress Israel excessively
    - Judah Alkalai (1798–1878) and Zvi Hirsch Kalischer (1795–1874): rabbinic proto-Zionists; human action can hasten redemption
  - Political Zionism
    - Theodor Herzl (1860–1904): *Der Judenstaat* (1896); First Zionist Congress (Basel, 1897); diplomatic Zionism
    - Labor Zionism: A. D. Gordon (religion of labor); kibbutz movement; secular socialist; David Ben-Gurion
    - Cultural Zionism: Ahad Ha'am (Asher Ginsberg, 1856–1927): spiritual center; Hebrew cultural revival
    - Revisionist Zionism: Vladimir Jabotinsky (1880–1940): maximalist territorial demands; military strength; influenced Likud
  - Religious Zionism
