    - **Abraham Isaac Kook** (Rav Kook, 1865–1935): first Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of British Mandate Palestine; mystical-dialectical theology; secular Zionists unconsciously serving divine plan; holy sparks in secularism; messianic process through national return
    - **Zvi Yehuda Kook** (1891–1982): son of Rav Kook; 1967 Six-Day War as divine miracle; settler ideology; Gush Emunim (Bloc of the Faithful, 1974): religious-nationalist settlement movement in occupied territories; theologically driven territorial maximalism
    - Religious Zionist education: Bnei Akiva youth movement; hesder yeshivot (Torah study + military service); Mercaz HaRav yeshiva
    - Religious Zionism today: spectrum from liberal (Meimad) to far-right (Smotrich, Ben Gvir); political power growing; State of Israel as theological category — "beginning of flowering of redemption" (reishit tzemihat ge'ulateinu, in official prayer)
  - Anti-Zionism (religious)
    - Satmar (Joel Teitelbaum): *Vayoel Moshe* — theological critique; state of Israel as rebellion against God's plan; three oaths violated; false messianism
    - Neturei Karta: most extreme anti-Zionist ultra-Orthodox faction; small but vocal
    - Haredi pragmatic engagement: many Haredim participate in Israeli politics (Shas, UTJ) while maintaining ambivalence about theological status of state
  - Diaspora–Israel relationship: "negation of the diaspora" (shelilat ha-galut) vs. creative diaspora; dual center model; American Jewish identity debates; declining attachment among younger diaspora Jews

- **Contemporary Judaism and Frontier Research (2020–present)**
  - Digital Judaica and text projects
    - **Sefaria**: open-source library of Jewish texts with translations, interconnected; revolutionizing Jewish learning accessibility
    - Friedberg Genizah Project: digital reunification of Cairo Genizah fragments (~400,000 items, scattered across 75 libraries); AI-assisted fragment matching
    - Historical Dictionary of the Hebrew Language (Academy of the Hebrew Language)
    - Dead Sea Scrolls digital editions: Leon Levy Dead Sea Scrolls Digital Library (Israel Antiquities Authority); multispectral imaging
    - Footprints project: tracing Jewish book history and ownership
  - Academic Jewish studies
    - Post-denominational trends: growing number of Jews reject labels; independent minyanim; partnership minyanim (egalitarian Orthodox-style; Shira Hadasha model)
    - Queer Jewish theology: emerging field; Joy Ladin, Gwynn Kessler, Noach Dzmura
    - Jews of color: increasing visibility; diversity within Jewish community; challenge to Ashkenazi-normative assumptions
    - Jewish disability theology: Julia Watts Belser; disability and halakha
    - Environmental Judaism: eco-kashrut (Zalman Schachter-Shalomi); Hazon organization; Jewish environmental ethics; Tu BiShvat seder as environmental ritual
    - Jewish-Muslim dialogue initiatives: expanding post-Abraham Accords; shared Abrahamic heritage; academic programs
    - Genetic studies and Jewish populations: Ashkenazi founder effects; Sephardic and Mizrahi genetics; Cohen Modal Haplotype; aDNA studies of medieval Jewish communities
    - Material culture and archaeology: ongoing excavations at Second Temple period sites; digital reconstruction of destroyed synagogues; Arch of Titus Arch coloring project (Yeshiva University) revealing menorah's original gold paint
  - Theological developments
    - Post-liberal Jewish theology: Peter Ochs (*Another Reformation*, 2011); Scriptural Reasoning movement; pragmatic and text-centered
    - Jewish thought and AI: ethical questions about Shabbat automation, AI as decision-maker, digital presence in minyan (COVID-era debates)
    - Contemplative Judaism revival: Jewish meditation movement (Institute for Jewish Spirituality); mindfulness + traditional practice; Mussar (ethical self-improvement) renaissance (Alan Morinis, *Everyday Holiness*, 2007)
    - Conversion and boundaries: "Who is a Jew?" debates in Israel (Orthodox rabbinate authority vs. Reform/Conservative recognition); Supreme Court rulings
    - Ultra-Orthodox growth and sociological study: Samuel Heilman, Nurit Stadler; haredi society in modernity; internet bans and negotiations; economic challenges; military service debates (Israel)

#### Buddhism — Full Depth

- **The Historical Buddha and Origins**
  - Historical context: 6th–5th century BCE northern India/Nepal; Late Vedic period; śramaṇa (wandering ascetic) milieu alongside Brahmanical orthodoxy
    - Śramaṇa movements: rejection of Vedic ritual authority; emphasis on renunciation, meditation, personal liberation
    - Contemporaries: Mahavira (Jainism, ~599–527 BCE); Ājīvikas (Makkhali Gosāla, fatalistic determinism); Lokāyata/Cārvāka (materialism); various ascetic groups
    - Political context: Magadha, Kosala, Vajjian confederacy; rise of urbanization, trade, monetary economy; social fluidity challenging rigid varṇa system
  - Life of Siddhārtha Gautama (~5th c. BCE; traditional dates vary: 563–483 or 480–400 BCE; current scholarship favors ~480–400 BCE)
    - Birth: Lumbini (modern Nepal); Śākya clan; father Śuddhodana (chieftain, not "king" in the imperial sense); mother Māyādevī (died shortly after birth)
    - Four Sights: old age, disease, death, wandering ascetic — prompted renunciation (the Great Departure, mahābhiniṣkramaṇa)
    - Six years of ascetic practice: studied with teachers Āḷāra Kālāma (sphere of nothingness) and Uddaka Rāmaputta (sphere of neither-perception-nor-non-perception); extreme austerities; rejected both luxury and extreme asceticism
    - Enlightenment (bodhi) at Bodh Gaya under the Bodhi tree: traditionally at age 35; the three knowledges (tevijjā): past lives, karma of beings, destruction of the āsavas (mental defilements); became the Buddha ("Awakened One")
    - First Sermon: Deer Park at Isipatana (Sarnath); "Setting in Motion the Wheel of Dharma" (Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta); taught the Middle Way, Four Noble Truths, Eightfold Path to the five ascetics (pañcavaggiya)
    - 45 years of teaching: wandered Gangetic plain (Magadha, Kosala, Vajji); established the Sangha (monastic community); major patrons: King Bimbisāra of Magadha, Anāthapiṇḍika (Jetavana monastery)
    - Key events: conversion of Śāriputra and Moggallāna (chief disciples); ordination of women (Mahāpajāpatī Gotamī, the Buddha's aunt — first bhikkhunī; establishment of bhikkhunī-sangha, with additional rules debated); Devadatta's schism attempt; miracles and debates with rival teachers
    - Death (parinibbāna/parinirvāṇa) at Kuśinagara (~400 BCE); final teachings: "All conditioned things are impermanent; work out your salvation with diligence" (Mahāparinibbāna Sutta); cremation; relic distribution (8 portions)
  - Sources and historicity
    - No contemporaneous written records; earliest texts oral for centuries (written down ~1st c. BCE in Sri Lanka, Tripiṭaka)
    - Pāli Canon (Theravāda) as primary textual source; Chinese Āgamas as parallel; Gāndhārī manuscripts (earliest surviving Buddhist manuscripts, ~1st c. BCE – 2nd c. CE, Birch bark)
    - Archaeological evidence: Aśokan inscriptions (3rd c. BCE) confirm Buddhist establishment; Lumbini pillar; Bodh Gaya site
    - Modern biography studies: Hermann Oldenberg (*Buddha*, 1881); Edward Conze; Richard Gombrich (*What the Buddha Thought*, 2009); Johannes Bronkhorst (*Buddhist Teaching in India*, 2009)

- **Core Doctrines (Common to All Buddhist Schools)**
  - The Three Marks of Existence (tilakkhaṇa / trilakṣaṇa)
    - Anicca / Anitya (impermanence): all conditioned phenomena are transient; moment-to-moment arising and passing away
    - Dukkha / Duḥkha (suffering / unsatisfactoriness): explicit suffering, suffering of change, suffering of conditioned existence (saṅkhāra-dukkha); pervasive quality of saṃsāric life
    - Anattā / Anātman (non-self): no permanent, unchanging self (ātman) in any phenomenon; critique of Brahmanical ātman doctrine; five aggregates (khandha/skandha) as exhaustive analysis of experience with no "self" found in any of them
  - The Four Noble Truths (Cattāri Ariyasaccāni)
    - Dukkha: life is permeated with suffering/unsatisfactoriness
    - Samudaya (origin): craving (taṇhā/tṛṣṇā) as root cause — craving for sense pleasures (kāma-taṇhā), craving for becoming (bhava-taṇhā), craving for non-existence (vibhava-taṇhā)
    - Nirodha (cessation): nibbāna/nirvāṇa as cessation of craving and suffering; unconditioned
    - Magga (path): the Noble Eightfold Path as the way to cessation
  - The Noble Eightfold Path (Ariyo Aṭṭhaṅgiko Maggo)
    - Wisdom (paññā): Right View (sammā-diṭṭhi), Right Intention (sammā-saṅkappa)
    - Ethics (sīla): Right Speech (sammā-vācā), Right Action (sammā-kammanta), Right Livelihood (sammā-ājīva)
    - Meditation (samādhi): Right Effort (sammā-vāyāma), Right Mindfulness (sammā-sati), Right Concentration (sammā-samādhi)
  - Dependent Origination (Paṭiccasamuppāda / Pratītyasamutpāda)
    - Twelve-link chain: ignorance (avijjā) → formations (saṅkhārā) → consciousness (viññāṇa) → name-and-form (nāmarūpa) → six sense bases (saḷāyatana) → contact (phassa) → feeling (vedanā) → craving (taṇhā) → clinging (upādāna) → becoming (bhava) → birth (jāti) → aging-and-death (jarāmaraṇa)
    - "When this exists, that comes to be; with the arising of this, that arises. When this does not exist, that does not come to be; with the cessation of this, that ceases."
    - Central philosophical principle: no first cause; mutual conditionality; no creator god; process metaphysics
    - Later philosophical elaboration: Nāgārjuna's śūnyatā (emptiness) as deepest reading of pratītyasamutpāda
  - Five Aggregates (Pañcakkhandha / Pañcaskandha)
    - Rūpa (form/matter), vedanā (feeling/sensation), saññā/saṃjñā (perception), saṅkhārā/saṃskārā (mental formations/volitions), viññāṇa/vijñāna (consciousness)
    - Analysis of personal identity: what is conventionally called "a person" is just these five aggregates in flux; no self (attā/ātman) behind them
  - Karma (Kamma) and Rebirth (Saṃsāra)
    - Karma as intentional action (cetanā): wholesome, unwholesome, or neutral; determines quality of rebirth
    - Six realms of rebirth: gods (deva), demigods (asura), humans, animals, hungry ghosts (peta/preta), hell beings (naraka); all impermanent, all within saṃsāra
    - Buddhist vs. Brahmanical karma: Buddha redefined karma as intention (not ritual action); no caste justification; liberation through insight, not sacrifice
  - Nibbāna / Nirvāṇa
    - "Blowing out" (of the fires of greed, hatred, delusion); the unconditioned; beyond birth and death
    - Nirvāṇa with remainder (sopadisesa): attained while alive (arahant still has body); nirvāṇa without remainder (anupadisesa): after death of arahant
    - Not annihilation and not eternal existence; the Buddha refused to categorize it (avyākata — undeclared questions)
    - Debate across traditions: Theravāda — individual liberation (arahantship); Mahāyāna — non-abiding nirvāṇa (apratiṣṭhita-nirvāṇa), bodhisattva remains in saṃsāra to help all beings
  - The Three Jewels (Tiratana / Triratna): Buddha, Dharma (teaching), Sangha (community) — refuge formula as entry into Buddhist life
  - Ethics: Five Precepts (pañcasīla): abstain from killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, false speech, intoxicants; voluntarily undertaken, not commandments
  - The Middle Way: avoiding extremes of self-indulgence and self-mortification; applied to doctrine (avoiding eternalism and annihilationism) and practice

- **Pāli Canon and Early Buddhist Texts**
  - Tipiṭaka (Three Baskets) — Theravāda Pāli Canon
    - Vinaya Piṭaka (Discipline Basket): monastic rules; Pāṭimokkha (227 rules for monks, 311 for nuns); Khandhaka (institutional regulations); Parivāra (analysis)
      - Bhikkhu and bhikkhunī vinaya; rules of communal life; confession ceremonies (uposatha); ordination procedures
    - Sutta Piṭaka (Discourse Basket): discourses of the Buddha organized in five collections (nikāyas):
      - Dīgha Nikāya (Long Discourses, 34 suttas): Mahāparinibbāna, Brahmajāla, Sigālovāda, Sāmaññaphala
      - Majjhima Nikāya (Middle-Length Discourses, 152 suttas): Satipaṭṭhāna (foundations of mindfulness), Anapānasati (breath mindfulness), Mūlapariyāya (root of all things)
      - Saṃyutta Nikāya (Connected Discourses, ~2,904 suttas): organized by topic; key doctrinal collections (khandha, āyatana, nidāna/dependent origination, magga/path)
      - Aṅguttara Nikāya (Numerical Discourses, ~9,557 suttas): organized by number (ones, twos, threes… up to elevens)
      - Khuddaka Nikāya (Minor Collection): Dhammapada (most translated Buddhist text, 423 verses); Sutta Nipāta (archaic poetry, earliest strata); Jātaka (547 birth stories); Theragāthā / Therīgāthā (Verses of Elder Monks/Nuns — Therīgāthā among earliest known women's literature); Udāna; Itivuttaka
    - Abhidhamma Piṭaka (Higher Teaching Basket): systematic philosophical analysis
      - Seven books: Dhammasaṅgaṇī, Vibhaṅga, Dhātukathā, Puggalapaññatti, Kathāvatthu (interschool controversies, attributed to Moggaliputta Tissa, 3rd c. BCE), Yamaka, Paṭṭhāna (conditional relations — 24 modes of conditionality, massively detailed)
      - Abhidhamma as "Buddhist systematic philosophy": atomistic analysis of experience into ultimate realities (paramattha dhamma): citta (consciousness, 89/121 types), cetasika (mental factors, 52), rūpa (matter, 28 types), nibbāna
      - Commentarial tradition: Buddhaghosa (5th c. CE), *Visuddhimagga* ("Path of Purification") — encyclopedic manual of Theravāda doctrine and meditation; Dhammapāla, Anuruddha (*Abhidhammatthasaṅgaha* — standard Abhidhamma textbook)
  - Chinese Āgamas: parallel collections in Chinese translation; sometimes preserve older readings; Dīrghāgama, Madhyamāgama, Saṃyuktāgama, Ekottarāgama; comparative study with Pāli (Anālayo, *A Comparative Study of the Majjhima-nikāya*, 2011)
  - Gāndhārī texts: Birch-bark and palm-leaf manuscripts from Greater Gandhāra; 1st c. BCE – 3rd c. CE; oldest surviving Buddhist manuscripts; Kharoṣṭhī script; Senior Collection, Bajaur Collection; Mark Allon, Richard Salomon, Andrew Glass
  - Early Buddhist Texts (EBT) project: isolating earliest stratum common to all traditions; Bhikkhu Sujato and Bhikkhu Brahmali, *The Authenticity of the Early Buddhist Texts* (2015); SuttaCentral.net as primary digital resource

- **Early Buddhist Schools and Councils**
  - First Buddhist Council (~400 BCE, Rājagṛha)
    - Recitation of discourses (Ānanda) and vinaya (Upāli); oral standardization; historicity debated but accepted by all traditions
  - Second Buddhist Council (~350 BCE, Vaiśālī)
    - Dispute over monastic discipline (ten points of laxity); Sthavira (Elders) vs. Mahāsāṃghika (Great Community) split — seed of later Mahāyāna/Theravāda divergence
  - Third Buddhist Council (~250 BCE, Pāṭaliputra)
    - Under Emperor Aśoka; Theravāda tradition; Moggaliputta Tissa compiled *Kathāvatthu* to refute heterodox views; missions sent across Asia (Sri Lanka: Mahinda, Myanmar, Central Asia, Hellenistic kingdoms)
  - Emperor Aśoka (r. ~268–232 BCE)
    - Mauryan emperor; converted to Buddhism after Kalinga War; rock and pillar edicts: dharma (righteousness), non-violence, religious tolerance, animal welfare
    - Patronage: stūpas, monasteries, missions; spread Buddhism from Afghanistan to Sri Lanka
    - Modern importance: Aśoka as model Buddhist ruler; influence on modern Buddhist political thought; B. R. Ambedkar's neo-Buddhist movement invoked Aśoka
  - Eighteen (or more) early schools (nikāya Buddhism): Sthavira branch → Sarvāstivāda, Dharmaguptaka, Theravāda, Pudgalavāda, etc.; Mahāsāṃghika branch → Lokottaravāda, Caitika, etc.
    - **Sarvāstivāda** ("everything exists"): dharmas (elements of experience) exist in all three times (past, present, future); dominant in Kashmir, Gandhāra, Central Asia; *Mahāvibhāṣā* (great commentary, ~150 CE, Gandhāra); Abhidharma systematization; 75 dharmas
    - **Vaibhāṣika**: school defined by adherence to *Mahāvibhāṣā*; realist; foundational for later Yogācāra and Madhyamaka critiques
    - **Sautrāntika** (Dārṣṭāntika): representationalist epistemology; only present dharmas are real; critical of Vaibhāṣika realism; bridge to Yogācāra idealism
    - **Pudgalavāda** (Vātsīputrīya): controversially posited a "person" (pudgala) neither identical with nor different from the skandhas; criticized by all other schools; yet historically large
    - **Dharmaguptaka**: dominant vinaya in East Asian Buddhism; monastic lineage in China, Korea, Vietnam, Japan
    - Fourth Buddhist Council: two rival traditions — Theravāda claims Ālu-vihāra, Sri Lanka (~1st c. BCE, Pāli Canon written down); Sarvāstivāda tradition places it in Kashmir under Kaniṣka (~1st–2nd c. CE, *Mahāvibhāṣā* compiled)

- **Theravāda Buddhism**
  - Historical spread
    - Sri Lanka: brought by Mahinda (Aśoka's son/envoy, ~3rd c. BCE); Mahāvihāra (Great Monastery, orthodox); Abhayagiri (eclectic, Mahāyāna-influenced); Jetavana; Mahāvaṃsa (Great Chronicle, historical text)
    - Southeast Asia: Myanmar (Bagan period, 11th c., King Anawrahta), Thailand (Sukhothai kingdom, 13th c.), Cambodia (Angkor → Theravāda transition, 13th–14th c.), Laos; Theravāda became dominant, displacing earlier Mahāyāna/Hindu influences
    - Pāli as liturgical and scholarly language; commentarial tradition in Pāli
  - Key doctrinal features
    - Emphasis on arahant ideal: perfect saint who has destroyed all defilements and will not be reborn; contrasted with bodhisattva ideal in Mahāyāna
    - Abhidhamma as "ultimate teaching" (paramattha-desanā): rigorous analysis of mental and physical phenomena; momentariness (khaṇavāda)
    - Buddhaghosa's *Visuddhimagga*: seven stages of purification (satta-visuddhi); detailed meditation instructions; three phases: sīla, samādhi, paññā
    - Meditation: samatha (calm/concentration) → access concentration (upacāra-samādhi) → absorption (jhāna, four rūpa-jhānas and four arūpa-jhānas); vipassanā (insight) → knowledges of arising-and-passing, dissolution, disenchantment → path (magga) and fruition (phala) → nibbāna
    - Stages of enlightenment: sotāpanna (stream-enterer), sakadāgāmī (once-returner), anāgāmī (non-returner), arahant
    - Commentarial and sub-commentarial tradition: Buddhaghosa (5th c. CE, Indian monk at Mahāvihāra); Dhammapāla; Anuruddha; extensive Pāli scholastic literature
  - Modern Theravāda
    - Forest Tradition: Ajahn Mun Bhuridatto (1870–1949, Thailand); Ajahn Chah (1918–1992); Thai Forest Tradition (Kammaṭṭhāna); emphasis on strict vinaya, ascetic practices (dhutaṅga), forest dwelling, meditation; Western students → Ajahn Sumedho, Ajahn Brahm
    - Burmese vipassanā tradition: Ledi Sayadaw (1846–1923, made meditation accessible to laity); Mahasi Sayadaw (1904–1982, noting technique: "rising, falling, sitting, touching"); S. N. Goenka (1924–2013, body-scanning technique from U Ba Khin lineage; secular-framed 10-day retreats, global Vipassana centers); Pa Auk Sayadaw (concentration-based approach with jhāna emphasis)
    - Sri Lankan Buddhism: Dharmapala (1864–1933, Mahā Bodhi Society, Buddhist revival); monastic universities; ethnic conflict dimensions (Sinhala-Buddhist nationalism; Tamil–Sinhala war)
    - Engaged Buddhism in Theravāda: Buddhadāsa Bhikkhu (1906–1993, Thailand — dhammic socialism, interfaith); Sulak Sivaraksa (Thai social critic); Maha Ghosananda (Cambodia, peace marches); A. T. Ariyaratne (Sarvodaya Shramadana movement, Sri Lanka)
    - Bhikkhunī ordination revival: decline of full female ordination in Theravāda lands; revival attempts from 1996 (Sarnath, with Chinese lineage); 1998 Bodh Gaya ordination; Dhammananda Bhikkhuni (Thailand); highly controversial; supported in Sri Lanka, resisted in Thailand, Myanmar

- **Mahāyāna Buddhism**
  - Origins and emergence (~1st c. BCE – 2nd c. CE)
    - Gradual emergence within existing nikāya Buddhism; not a sudden schism; earliest Mahāyāna texts from Gandhāra/Central India
    - Term: Mahāyāna = "Great Vehicle"; polemically contrasted with Hīnayāna ("Lesser Vehicle") for earlier schools (term now considered pejorative; "Śrāvakayāna" or "Nikāya Buddhism" preferred)
    - Theories of origin: forest-dwelling meditators (Paul Harrison); lay cult movement (Gregory Schopen challenged this — manuscript evidence shows monastic authorship); stūpa veneration cults; visionary/revelatory new sūtras
    - Jan Nattier: Mahāyāna initially a marginal movement within monastic institutions; "minority voice" for centuries
  - Key Mahāyāna sūtras
    - **Prajñāpāramitā** (Perfection of Wisdom) literature: earliest Mahāyāna texts (~1st c. BCE onward)
      - *Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā* (8,000-line): earliest; emptiness (śūnyatā) of all dharmas; perfection of wisdom as non-grasping
      - *Heart Sūtra* (Prajñāpāramitā-hṛdaya): "Form is emptiness, emptiness is form"; shortest and most recited; Jan Nattier's theory: composed in Chinese, back-translated into Sanskrit (debated)
      - *Diamond Sūtra* (Vajracchedikā): "A bodhisattva who creates the perception of a being cannot be called a bodhisattva"; earliest dated printed book (868 CE, Dunhuang)
      - *Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā* (25,000-line) and *Śatasāhasrikā* (100,000-line): expanded versions
    - **Saddharmapuṇḍarīka Sūtra** (Lotus Sūtra): one Buddha vehicle (ekayāna) — all beings will attain Buddhahood; skillful means (upāya); cosmic Buddha eternal and omnipresent; parables (Burning House, Prodigal Son); foundation for Tiantai and Nichiren schools; most influential sūtra in East Asian Buddhism
    - **Vimalakīrtinirdeśa Sūtra**: layman Vimalakīrti outdebates monastics; "thundering silence" on non-duality; paradoxical teachings; lay ideal
    - **Sukhāvatīvyūha Sūtras** (Longer and Shorter Pure Land Sūtras): Amitābha Buddha's 48 vows; Pure Land (Sukhāvatī) as rebirth destination; foundation for Pure Land Buddhism
    - **Avataṃsaka Sūtra** (Flower Garland/Huayan Sūtra): cosmic vision of interpenetrating realities; Indra's net metaphor (each jewel reflects all others); Samantabhadra's vows; Sudhana's pilgrimage (Gaṇḍavyūha); foundation for Huayan school
    - **Laṅkāvatāra Sūtra**: ālayavijñāna (storehouse consciousness); yogācāra ideas; tathāgatagarbha (Buddha-nature); important for Chan/Zen
    - **Tathāgatagarbha Sūtras** (Buddha-nature literature): *Tathāgatagarbha Sūtra*, *Śrīmālā Sūtra*, *Ratnagotravibhāga/Uttaratantra*: all beings possess Buddha-nature (tathāgatagarbha); potential for Buddhahood inherent; controversial interpretation — is it ātman reinvented? (debated)
    - **Nirvāṇa Sūtra** (Mahāyāna Mahāparinirvāṇa Sūtra): Buddha-nature universal; icchantika (beings with no wholesome roots) can still attain Buddhahood; eternality, bliss, self, purity of nirvāṇa
  - Bodhisattva ideal
    - Bodhisattva: being who aspires to full Buddhahood for the benefit of all sentient beings; contrasted with arahant ideal
    - Bodhicitta (awakening-mind): aspiration for enlightenment motivated by compassion; conventional bodhicitta (aspiration + engagement) and ultimate bodhicitta (direct realization of emptiness)
    - Bodhisattva vow: "Sentient beings are numberless; I vow to save them all"
    - Six Pāramitās (Perfections): dāna (generosity), śīla (morality), kṣānti (patience), vīrya (vigor), dhyāna (meditation), prajñā (wisdom); some lists add: upāya (skillful means), praṇidhāna (vow), bala (power), jñāna (knowledge) = ten pāramitās
    - Bodhisattva stages (bhūmi): ten stages from *Daśabhūmika Sūtra*; Progressive realization from joyous (pramuditā) through dharma-cloud (dharmameghā)
    - Celestial/archetypal bodhisattvas: Avalokiteśvara (compassion; Guanyin in China, Kannon in Japan, Chenrezig in Tibet); Mañjuśrī (wisdom); Samantabhadra (practice); Kṣitigarbha (Jizō, protector of children and hell beings); Maitreya (future Buddha)
  - Mahāyāna philosophy
    - **Mādhyamaka** (Middle Way school)
      - **Nāgārjuna** (~150–250 CE): greatest Buddhist philosopher
        - *Mūlamadhyamakakārikā* (Fundamental Verses of the Middle Way, 27 chapters, 449 verses): systematic demonstration that all phenomena are empty (śūnya) of inherent existence (svabhāva)
        - Śūnyatā (emptiness): not nihilism but absence of independent, intrinsic existence; all things arise dependently (pratītyasamutpāda = śūnyatā); two truths: conventional (saṃvṛti) and ultimate (paramārtha)
        - *Vigrahavyāvartanī* (Refutation of Objections): Nāgārjuna has no thesis of his own (no svabhāva-vāda); uses prasaṅga (reductio ad absurdum)
        - "Emptiness wrongly grasped is like a snake wrongly picked up" — emptiness is not a metaphysical position but the absence of all positions
      - Āryadeva (3rd c.): Nāgārjuna's chief disciple; *Catuḥśataka* (400 Verses); refutation of rival schools
      - Buddhapālita (~470–540): *Buddhapālitavṛtti* — prasaṅga method only (no independent syllogisms); founder of Prāsaṅgika approach
      - **Bhāviveka/Bhavya** (~500–570): *Prajñāpradīpa*, *Madhyamakahṛdaya*; used independent syllogisms (svātantra) to establish emptiness; founder of **Svātantrika** Mādhyamaka
      - **Candrakīrti** (~600–650): *Prasannapadā* (Clear Words, commentary on Nāgārjuna); *Madhyamakāvatāra* (Introduction to the Middle Way): defended **Prāsaṅgika** — only prasaṅga reduction, no autonomous reasoning; rejected Yogācāra; enormously influential in Tibetan Buddhism (Gelug school adopted Prāsaṅgika as definitive)
      - **Śāntideva** (~685–763): *Bodhicaryāvatāra* (Guide to the Bodhisattva's Way of Life): masterpiece of Mādhyamaka ethics and philosophy; equalization and exchange of self and other; emptiness chapter (ch. 9); one of most beloved Buddhist texts
      - Svātantrika-Mādhyamaka: further divided into Sautrāntika-Svātantrika (Bhāviveka) and Yogācāra-Svātantrika (Śāntarakṣita's *Madhyamakālaṃkāra*; Kamalaśīla, *Bhāvanākrama*)
    - **Yogācāra** (Mind-Only / Vijñānavāda school)
      - **Asaṅga** (~4th c. CE): attributed to Maitreya's inspiration; *Yogācārabhūmi-śāstra*; *Mahāyānasaṃgraha* (Compendium of Mahāyāna)
      - **Vasubandhu** (~4th–5th c. CE): Asaṅga's brother; wrote Abhidharma masterwork *Abhidharmakośa* (Sarvāstivāda analysis) then converted to Mahāyāna; *Vijñaptimātratāsiddhi* (Twenty Verses, Thirty Verses): only consciousness (vijñapti-mātra) exists; external objects are constructions of consciousness
      - Eight consciousnesses: five sense consciousnesses, mental consciousness (manovijñāna), afflicted mind (kliṣṭa-manas, self-grasping), ālayavijñāna (storehouse consciousness — contains seeds/bījas of all experience; karmic continuity without self)
      - Three natures (trisvabhāva): imagined (parikalpita), dependent (paratantra), perfected (pariniṣpanna); reality is the dependent nature purified of imagined projections
      - Debate with Mādhyamaka: Yogācāra → consciousness is real, external world illusory; Mādhyamaka → both consciousness and objects equally empty; Tibetan doxographers ranked Mādhyamaka higher (debated)
      - Later Yogācāra: Dignāga (~480–540) and **Dharmakīrti** (~600–660): Buddhist logic (pramāṇa) and epistemology; *Pramāṇavārttika* — theory of valid cognition; apoha (exclusion) theory of meaning; arguably most important Indian Buddhist philosophers for logic; dominated Indian Buddhist universities for centuries
      - Yogācāra in East Asia: Xuanzang (7th c.) brought texts to China; Faxiang/Hossō school
    - **Tathāgatagarbha** (Buddha-nature thought)
      - All beings possess the seed/embryo/womb of the tathāgata; a luminous, inherently pure mind (prakṛti-prabhāsvara-citta) temporarily obscured by adventitious defilements
      - Key texts: *Tathāgatagarbha Sūtra*, *Śrīmālā Sūtra*, *Ratnagotravibhāga* (Uttaratantra, attributed to Maitreya/Asaṅga)
      - Controversy: is tathāgatagarbha a skillful means to encourage practice? Or does it smuggle in ātman (permanent self)? Mādhyamaka and critical Yogācāra scholars differ
      - East Asian importance: basis for Huayan, Tiantai, Chan/Zen metaphysics; "original enlightenment" (hongaku) thought in Japanese Buddhism
      - Critical Buddhism (hihan bukkyō): Matsumoto Shirō and Hakamaya Noriaki (1980s–90s): tathāgatagarbha thought is non-Buddhist "dhātu-vāda" (substantialism); provoked major debate in Japanese Buddhist studies

- **Vajrayāna / Tantric Buddhism**
  - Origins and nature
    - Emerged ~6th–7th c. CE in India; "Diamond Vehicle" or "Thunderbolt Vehicle"; also called Mantrayāna, Tantrayāna
    - Relationship to Mahāyāna: Vajrayāna self-identifies as Mahāyāna with additional tantric methods (upāya) for rapid enlightenment
    - Tantric texts (tantras): classified in Tibetan system as four classes: Action (Kriyā), Performance (Caryā/Ubhaya), Yoga, Highest Yoga (Anuttarayoga) tantras
    - Major tantras: *Guhyasamāja Tantra* (earliest major yoga tantra, ~7th–8th c.); *Hevajra Tantra*; *Cakrasaṃvara Tantra*; *Kālacakra Tantra* (astronomy, eschatology, cosmology)
  - Key features
    - Guru-disciple relationship (vajra master/ācārya): empowerment (abhiṣeka/wang) required for practice; secrecy; samaya (vows)
    - Deity yoga (devayoga): visualization of oneself as enlightened deity (yidam); mandala as sacred palace; complex liturgies (sādhana)
    - Mantras, mudrās (hand gestures), maṇḍalas (sacred diagrams): body-speech-mind integration; mantra as embodiment of deity's enlightened nature; "Oṃ maṇi padme hūṃ" (Avalokiteśvara)
    - Transformation of poisons into wisdom: desire, anger, ignorance directly used as path; "samsara equals nirvāṇa" taken literally in practice
    - Subtle body: channels (nāḍī), winds (prāṇa/vāyu), drops (bindu/tigle); central channel (avadhūtī); cakras; prāṇāyāma; inner heat (caṇḍālī/tummo)
    - Mahāmudrā and Dzogchen: non-dual awareness practices; direct realization of mind's nature
    - Antinomian elements: transgressive practices (sexual yoga, charnel ground rituals, consumption of impure substances); literal vs. symbolic interpretation debated; mahāsiddha tradition
  - Indian Tantric Buddhism
    - Mahāsiddhas (great accomplished ones, ~8th–12th c.): 84 mahāsiddhas legendary; Tilopa, Nāropa, Virūpa, Saraha, Padmasambhava; unconventional, yogic, iconoclastic
    - Nālandā Mahāvihāra: greatest Buddhist monastery-university (~5th–12th c. CE, Bihar); Śāntarakṣita, Kamalaśīla, Atīśa taught there; destroyed ~1200 CE by Bakhtiyar Khilji's forces
    - Vikramaśīla: second great tantric university; Atīśa (982–1054) was head scholar (adhyakṣa) before going to Tibet
    - Decline in India: Muslim invasions destroyed monasteries; loss of institutional patronage; Buddhism largely vanished from Indian subcontinent by ~13th century (survived in Nepal, Bengal borderlands)

- **Tibetan Buddhism**
  - Historical development
    - First Dissemination (snga dar, 7th–9th c.): Songtsen Gampo (d. ~650) — introduced Buddhism, married Chinese and Nepali Buddhist princesses; Thonmi Sambhota created Tibetan script (based on Indian Brāhmī)
    - Trisong Detsen (r. ~755–797): invited Śāntarakṣita and Padmasambhava; founded Samye monastery (~779); Great Debate at Samye (~792–794): Indian gradualism (Kamalaśīla) vs. Chinese sudden enlightenment (Hashang/Moheyan); Indian side declared winner → Tibetan Buddhism followed Indian Mādhyamaka path
    - Padmasambhava (Guru Rinpoche): legendary tantric master from Oḍḍiyāna; tamed local spirits; foundation figure of Nyingma school; "Second Buddha" in Tibetan tradition
    - Lang Darma's persecution (~838–842): anti-Buddhist king; dark period; Buddhism survived in eastern Tibet and among lay tantric practitioners
    - Second Dissemination (phyi dar, ~10th–11th c.): Rin chen bzang po (translator); Atiśa (arrived Tibet 1042; *Bodhipathapradīpa*, "Lamp for the Path to Enlightenment" — graduated path framework); revival of monasticism and scholarship
  - Four major schools
    - **Nyingma** (rnying ma, "Ancient Ones"): oldest school; traces to Padmasambhava; Dzogchen (Great Perfection) as highest teaching; terma (hidden treasure) tradition — texts concealed by Padmasambhava, rediscovered by tertöns; nine yānas classification; Longchenpa (1308–1364, systematizer); Jigme Lingpa (1730–1798, Longchen Nyingthig cycle); Mipham Rinpoche (1846–1912, philosophical defender)
    - **Kagyu** (bka' brgyud, "Oral Transmission"): lineage from Tilopa → Nāropa → Marpa the Translator (1012–1097) → Milarepa (1040–1123, Tibet's great poet-saint, practiced in caves, "hundred thousand songs") → Gampopa (1079–1153, synthesis of Kadampa and mahāmudrā)
      - Sub-schools: Karma Kagyu (Karmapa incarnation lineage, Black Hat — 1st Karmapa Dusum Khyenpa established tulku reincarnation system ~1193, predating Dalai Lama system); Drikung Kagyu; Drukpa Kagyu (state religion of Bhutan)
      - Mahāmudrā (Great Seal): direct recognition of mind's luminous-empty nature; stages: one-pointedness, simplicity, one-taste, non-meditation; Wangchuk Dorje (9th Karmapa), *Mahāmudrā: Ocean of Definitive Meaning*
    - **Sakya** (sa skya, "Pale Earth"): founded by Khön Könchok Gyalpo (1034–1102); Sakya Paṇḍita (1182–1251: greatest Tibetan logician-scholar; defeated Hindu pandits in debate); Phakpa (1235–1280: priest-patron relation with Kublai Khan, invented 'Phags-pa script; Sakya school ruled Tibet under Mongol Yuan dynasty)
      - Lamdré (lam 'bras, "Path and Fruit"): Hevajra-based system; inseparability of saṃsāra and nirvāṇa
    - **Gelug** (dge lugs, "Virtuous Ones"): founded by **Tsongkhapa** (1357–1419)
      - *Lamrim Chenmo* (Great Treatise on the Stages of the Path): synthesis of Atiśa's graduated path with rigorous Mādhyamaka-Prāsaṅgika philosophy; comprehensive study program
      - *Lekshé Nyingpo* and tantric works: integration of sūtra and tantra; emphasis on monastic discipline, debate (via Dharmakīrti's logic), scholastic education before tantric practice
      - Drepung, Sera, Ganden: three great monasteries near Lhasa; thousands of monks; geshe degree (doctoral equivalent via debate)
      - **Dalai Lama** institution: 1st Dalai Lama Gendun Drup (1391–1474, posthumously recognized); 5th Dalai Lama (1617–1682, "Great Fifth": unified Tibet, built Potala Palace, political-religious authority); 14th Dalai Lama Tenzin Gyatso (b. 1935): Nobel Peace Prize 1989; global Buddhist ambassador; Tibetan government-in-exile (Dharamsala, India); relinquished political authority 2011
      - **Panchen Lama**: second-highest incarnation lineage; 10th Panchen Lama (died 1989); succession controversy (Chinese government installed rival candidate 1995; Gendun Choekyi Nyima, recognized by Dalai Lama, disappeared at age 6 — world's youngest political prisoner)
  - Key practices and institutions
    - Tulku (sprul sku, incarnate lama) system: recognized reincarnations of great teachers; unique to Tibetan Buddhism; divination, oracles, tests to identify child reincarnations
    - Debate tradition: clap-and-stomp dialectical debate on monastery grounds; training in logic (Dharmakīrti), Mādhyamaka, Abhidharma, Vinaya, Pramāṇa
    - Tantric rituals: elaborate deity practices; sand maṇḍala creation and dissolution; chöd (cutting through ego, Machig Labdrön, ~1055–1149, female founder); phowa (consciousness transference); bardo teachings (*Bardo Thödol*, "Tibetan Book of the Dead" — liberation through hearing in the intermediate state)
    - Rimé (nonsectarian) movement (19th c.): Jamyang Khyentse Wangpo, Jamgön Kongtrül, Chogyur Lingpa; cross-pollination among schools; treasure compilations; revitalized Nyingma and Kagyu traditions
  - Tibetan Buddhism in modernity
    - Chinese invasion (1950); Tibetan uprising (1959); Cultural Revolution destruction (1966–76): thousands of monasteries destroyed; massive cultural loss
    - Exile community: Dharamsala; re-establishment of monasteries in India (Karnataka), Nepal; preservation efforts
    - Global spread: Chögyam Trungpa (1939–1987, Shambhala, "crazy wisdom," controversial legacy); Sogyal Rinpoche (*The Tibetan Book of Living and Dying*, 1992; sexual abuse scandals); Thubten Yeshe and Zopa Rinpoche (FPMT); Tarthang Tulku (Nyingma Institute); Dzongsar Jamyang Khyentse Rinpoche; Mingyur Rinpoche
    - Academic Tibetan Buddhist studies: David Snellgrove, Matthew Kapstein, Janet Gyatso, José Cabezón, Holly Gayley

- **East Asian Buddhism**
  - Chinese Buddhism
    - Transmission: along Silk Road and maritime routes; earliest evidence ~1st c. CE; translation projects — Kumārajīva (~344–413, translated *Heart Sūtra*, *Lotus Sūtra*, *Vimalakīrti*, Mādhyamaka texts; transformed Chinese Buddhist literature); Xuanzang (602–664, *Da Tang Xiyu Ji* — *Great Tang Records on the Western Regions*; pilgrimage to India; translated massive Yogācāra corpus; inspired *Journey to the West*)
    - Major Chinese Buddhist schools:
      - **Tiantai** (天台): Zhiyi (538–597); *Mohe Zhiguan* (Great Calming and Contemplation); classification of Buddha's teachings into five periods; Lotus Sūtra as supreme; threefold truth (emptiness, conventional, middle); "three thousand realms in a single moment of thought" (yiniansanqian)
      - **Huayan** (華嚴, Flower Garland): Fazang (643–712, third patriarch); *Avataṃsaka Sūtra* as basis; interpenetration of all phenomena (shishi wuai — "phenomenon-phenomenon non-obstruction"); Indra's net; totality metaphysics; influenced Chan/Zen and Korean Buddhism
      - **Chan** (禪, Meditation → Zen in Japan, Sŏn in Korea, Thiền in Vietnam)
        - Bodhidharma (legendary, ~5th–6th c.): Indian monk; "wall-gazing" (biguan); "pointing directly at the mind, seeing one's nature, becoming a Buddha"
        - Sixth Patriarch Huineng (638–713): *Platform Sūtra*; sudden enlightenment; "originally there is not a single thing — where could dust alight?"; Northern (gradual, Shenxiu) vs. Southern (sudden, Huineng) split
        - Linji (Rinzai) school: Linji Yixuan (d. 866); encounter dialogue; shout (katsu!); iconoclasm; kōan practice; "If you meet the Buddha, kill the Buddha"
        - Caodong (Sōtō) school: Dongshan Liangjie (807–869); "five ranks" (wuwei); silent illumination (mozhao); shikantaza ("just sitting") in Japanese Sōtō
        - Song dynasty Chan (10th–13th c.): kōan collections — *Blue Cliff Record* (Biyan lu, 1125), *Gateless Gate* (Wumen guan, 1228); literary Chan; Dahui Zonggao (kōan introspection); Hongzhi Zhengjue (silent illumination)
      - **Pure Land** (淨土, Jìngtǔ): Tanluan (476–542), Daochuo (562–645), Shandao (613–681); reliance on Amitābha Buddha's salvific power; nianfo/nembutsu (reciting "Namo Amituofo" / "Namu Amida Butsu"); faith-based path accessible to all; most widely practiced form in East Asia
      - **Yogācāra/Faxiang** (法相): Xuanzang and Kuiji; ālayavijñāna theory; less popular than other schools but philosophically important
      - **Esoteric/Zhenyan** (真言, Mantrayāna): Śubhakarasiṃha, Vajrabodhi, Amoghavajra (8th c.); Tang-era transmission; Mahāvairocana Sūtra; merged with other traditions in China; survives independently as Shingon in Japan
    - Chinese Buddhist history: periods of persecution (Three Destructions and One Suppression — 446, 574, 845 CE being most severe — Huichang Suppression under Emperor Wuzong destroyed 4,600 monasteries); Buddhism deeply sinicized: ancestor veneration, filial piety justified
    - Modern Chinese Buddhism: Taixu (1890–1947, reform, "Buddhism for Human Life" / rensheng fojiao); Humanistic Buddhism (Hsing Yun, Fo Guang Shan; Sheng Yen, Dharma Drum Mountain; Cheng Yen, Tzu Chi); PRC state control (Buddhist Association of China); Tibetan areas under political pressures
  - Korean Buddhism
    - Transmission from China (4th c. CE); Unified Silla and Koryŏ periods: flourishing; Wŏnhyo (617–686, ecumenical synthesis; *Awakening of Faith* commentary); Chinul (1158–1210, Korean Sŏn reformer; sudden awakening-gradual cultivation; *Secrets on Cultivating the Mind*); Koryŏ Tripiṭaka (13th c., 80,000+ woodblocks, UNESCO heritage)
    - Joseon suppression (1392–1897): Neo-Confucian state marginalized Buddhism; monks barred from cities; survival in mountains
    - Modern Korean Buddhism: Jogye Order (largest); Manhae (Han Yongun, 1879–1944: poet, independence activist, reformer); temple-stay programs; growing global presence
  - Japanese Buddhism
    - Nara period (710–794): Six Schools from Chinese Buddhism; state-sponsored; Kegon (Huayan), Hossō (Yogācāra), Ritsu (Vinaya)
    - Heian period (794–1185): **Tendai** (= Tiantai; Saichō, 767–822; Mt. Hiei; comprehensive training); **Shingon** (Kūkai/Kōbō Daishi, 774–835; esoteric Buddhism; mantra, maṇḍala, mudra; Mt. Kōya); these two dominated Japanese Buddhism for centuries
    - Kamakura period (1185–1333): explosive reform movements — key new schools:
      - **Jōdo-shū (Pure Land)**: Hōnen (1133–1212); exclusive nembutsu (Namu Amida Butsu) — sole practice; Other Power (tariki) reliance on Amida's vow; radical simplification; accessible to all
      - **Jōdo Shinshū (True Pure Land)**: Shinran (1173–1263): most radical Pure Land thinker; faith (shinjin) as gift from Amida, not practitioner's effort; rejected monasticism; married clergy; "even a good person can be born in the Pure Land, how much more so an evil person" (*Tannishō*); largest Japanese Buddhist denomination today
      - **Rinzai Zen**: Eisai (1141–1215); kōan practice; influenced warrior (samurai) culture; tea ceremony; arts
      - **Sōtō Zen**: **Dōgen** (1200–1253): *Shōbōgenzō* (Treasury of the True Dharma Eye): philosophical masterpiece; shikantaza (just sitting); "practice IS enlightenment" (shushō-ittō); being-time (uji); Buddha-nature as impermanence itself; Eiheiji monastery; one of world's great philosophical minds
      - **Nichiren** (1222–1282): polemic; exclusive devotion to Lotus Sūtra; chanting "Namu Myōhō Renge Kyō" (Nam-myoho-renge-kyo); criticized all other schools; political activism; persecution; Nichiren schools, Soka Gakkai International (lay movement, 12M+ members; controversial; Daisaku Ikeda, 1928–2023)
    - Zen aesthetics: wabi-sabi (imperfect beauty); karesansui (dry landscape garden, Ryōan-ji); chadō (tea ceremony, Sen no Rikyū); ikebana; haiku (Bashō, Ryōkan); sumie (ink painting); profound influence on Japanese culture
    - Modern Japanese Buddhism: Meiji persecution (1868–, shinbutsu bunri — forced separation of Buddhism from Shinto); Buddhist modernism; Nishida Kitarō (1870–1945, Kyoto School founder, "pure experience," "logic of place/basho"); Suzuki Daisetsu (D. T. Suzuki, 1870–1966: popularized Zen in the West; *Zen and Japanese Culture*; criticized for essentialism and Orientalism: Robert Sharf); Nishitani Keiji (*Religion and Nothingness*, 1961); Buddhist engagement with philosophy (Heidegger dialogue)
    - War Buddhism: Japanese Buddhist institutions' complicity with militarism and imperialism (WWII); Brian Victoria, *Zen at War* (1997, 2006): documentation of Zen masters supporting war; ongoing reckoning; institutional apologies
  - Vietnamese Buddhism
    - Thiền (Chan/Zen): Trúc Lâm school (Trần Nhân Tông, 13th c., king-turned-monk, unified Vietnamese Zen); Pure Land-Zen synthesis common
    - Thích Nhất Hạnh (1926–2022): towering figure; Engaged Buddhism; Order of Interbeing; Plum Village; mindfulness popularizer ("The miracle is to walk on Earth"); *The Heart of the Buddha's Teaching* (1998); exile from Vietnam (1966–2018); enormous Western influence

- **Southeast Asian and South Asian Buddhism**
  - Sri Lanka: see Theravāda section; Mahāvaṃsa chronicle; colonial impact; Buddhist modernism (Olcott, Dharmapala); ethnic conflict
  - Myanmar/Burma: Bagan temples (11th–13th c.); Theravāda orthodoxy; vipassanā movement (Ledi Sayadaw → Mahasi Sayadaw → Goenka); modern political Buddhism (monks in 2007 Saffron Revolution; anti-Rohingya Buddhist nationalism, 969 movement, Ma Ba Tha — complicating "peaceful Buddhism" narrative)
  - Thailand: Thai Forest Tradition; King Mongkut (Rama IV) and Dhammayut reform; temple culture; Buddhism and monarchy deeply intertwined; Buddhadāsa; commercial Buddhism tensions
  - Cambodia: Angkor transition from Hinduism/Mahayana to Theravāda; Khmer Rouge devastation (1975–79, majority of monks killed/disrobed); slow recovery; Maha Ghosananda's peace walks
  - Laos, Bangladesh (Chittagong Hills), Nepal (Newar Buddhism — unique Vajrayāna survival)
  - Ambedkar Buddhism (Navayana, India): B. R. Ambedkar (1891–1956): Dalit leader; architect of Indian Constitution; converted to Buddhism with 500,000 followers (1956); rejection of caste; social liberation Buddhism; Navayana as "new vehicle" distinct from traditional schools; millions of followers in Maharashtra; ongoing socio-political dimension

- **Buddhist Ethics and Social Thought**
  - Core ethical framework: five precepts (pañcasīla); non-harm (ahiṃsā) as foundational; karma-driven ethics; compassion (karuṇā) and lovingkindness (mettā)
  - Buddhist economics: E. F. Schumacher (*Small Is Beautiful*, 1973, "Buddhist Economics" chapter); Payutto (*Buddhist Economics*, 1994); right livelihood; sufficiency; critique of endless growth
  - Engaged Buddhism: term coined by Thích Nhất Hạnh; Sulak Sivaraksa (Thailand); A. T. Ariyaratne (Sarvodaya, Sri Lanka); Robert Aitken; Bernie Glassman (Zen Peacemakers); Joanna Macy (eco-Buddhism, "The Work That Reconnects"); David Loy (*A New Buddhist Path*, 2015)
  - Buddhist environmentalism: interdependence (pratītyasamutpāda) as ecological principle; deep ecology connections; Dalai Lama's environmental advocacy; Buddhist Climate Action Network
  - Buddhist feminism: Rita Gross (*Buddhism After Patriarchy*, 1993); bhikkhunī ordination debates; women teachers (Pema Chödrön, Jetsunma Tenzin Palmo, Ayya Khema); gender in Buddhist texts — problematic passages and progressive readings
  - Buddhist bioethics: views on abortion (mixed — karma of intention; cultural variation); euthanasia (ahiṃsā principles); organ donation; genetic engineering; Damien Keown (*Buddhist Ethics: A Very Short Introduction*; *Buddhism and Bioethics*)
  - Buddhism and violence: tension between ahiṃsā ideal and historical reality; Buddhist nationalism (Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand); Michael Jerryson and Mark Juergensmeyer, *Buddhist Warfare* (2010); self-immolation as protest (Thích Quảng Đức, 1963; Tibetan self-immolations 2009–present)

- **Buddhism in the West**
  - Historical reception: Schopenhauer (earliest Western philosopher influenced by Buddhism, *The World as Will and Representation*); Theosophical Society (Olcott, Blavatsky); Rhys Davids (Pali Text Society, 1881); early academic Orientalism
  - 20th-century transmission: D. T. Suzuki (Zen to America); Beat Zen (Alan Watts, Jack Kerouac, Gary Snyder); Shunryu Suzuki (*Zen Mind, Beginner's Mind*, 1970); Chögyam Trungpa (Tibetan Buddhism in America/UK); Thích Nhất Hạnh (mindfulness movement); S. N. Goenka (vipassanā)
  - Western Buddhist teachers: Joseph Goldstein, Jack Kornfield, Sharon Salzberg (Insight Meditation Society, 1975; Theravāda); Robert Aitken and Philip Kapleau (*The Three Pillars of Zen*, 1965); Bernie Glassman; Pema Chödrön; Tara Brach; Jon Kabat-Zinn (MBSR — Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction, secularized Buddhism)
  - Mindfulness movement: clinical applications (MBSR, MBCT); neuroscience of meditation (Richard Davidson, Wisconsin; Clifford Saron, Shamatha Project); mindfulness in education, workplace, military; "McMindfulness" critique (Ronald Purser, *McMindfulness*, 2019): stripping Buddhism of ethical-liberative dimensions
  - Secular Buddhism: Stephen Batchelor (*Buddhism Without Beliefs*, 1997; *After Buddhism*, 2015): agnostic approach; stripping metaphysics (rebirth, karma as cosmic law); focus on four tasks not four truths; controversial among traditionalists
  - Convert vs. heritage Buddhism: distinction between Western converts (often meditation-focused, elite, white) and Asian diaspora communities (temple-centered, cultural, devotional); racial dynamics; call for inclusion (Aaron Lee, Funie Hsu, Chenxing Han *Be the Refuge*, 2021)

- **Frontier / Contemporary Research Directions (2020–present)**
  - Textual and manuscript studies
    - Gāndhārī manuscript discoveries: ongoing conservation, decipherment, publication (University of Washington Early Buddhist Manuscripts Project; Mark Allon, Stefan Baums); revolutionizing understanding of early Buddhist textual history
    - Digital Pāli Canon and parallel study: SuttaCentral.net (Bhikkhu Sujato): multilingual parallel reading of early texts; AI-assisted translation
    - Sanskrit Buddhist manuscript recovery: Nepal-German Manuscript Cataloguing Project; Göttingen Academy; Sanskrit texts previously known only through Tibetan/Chinese translation
    - Dunhuang materials: ongoing study of cave library manuscripts (British Library, Bibliothèque nationale de France); Chinese, Tibetan, Sanskrit, Khotanese Buddhist texts
  - Historical and archaeological research
    - Lumbini UNESCO site: ongoing excavations (Robin Coningham, 2013): Māyādevī Temple pre-Aśokan timber structure supports ~6th c. BCE dating
    - Mes Aynak (Afghanistan): massive Buddhist monastery-city threatened by copper mining; salvage archaeology; Kushan-era (1st–3rd c. CE) Buddhist art and texts
    - Bamiyan (ongoing): post-destruction (2001 Taliban) analysis; hidden chambers with manuscripts and paintings; conservation debates about reconstruction
    - Maritime Silk Routes archaeology: Buddhist spread through Indian Ocean trade; Indonesian and Southeast Asian Buddhist sites
  - Science and Buddhism dialogue
    - Mind and Life Institute (founded 1987): Dalai Lama-scientist dialogues; contemplative neuroscience; Evan Thompson, Francisco Varela (*The Embodied Mind*, 1991)
    - Neuroscience of meditation: long-term meditator studies (Davidson lab); neuroplasticity; attention networks; default mode network changes; compassion training
    - Physics-Buddhism parallels: Fritjof Capra (*The Tao of Physics*, 1975, popular but oversimplified); quantum mechanics and Mādhyamaka (Michel Bitbol, *Quantum Mechanics and the Philosophy of Schrödinger*); Carlo Rovelli's engagement with Nāgārjuna (*Helgoland*, 2021)
    - Psychology: Buddhist-informed psychotherapy; Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT); Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT, Marsha Linehan — incorporates Zen); 4th wave cognitive therapy
  - Philosophical developments
    - Comparative philosophy: Mark Siderits (*Buddhism as Philosophy*, 2007); Jay Garfield (Nāgārjuna translation and interpretation, *The Fundamental Wisdom of the Middle Way*, 1995); Evan Thompson (*Waking, Dreaming, Being*, 2015; later *Why I Am Not a Buddhist*, 2020 — critique of Buddhist exceptionalism and scientism)
    - Buddhist ethics as philosophical system: Damien Keown (virtue-ethics reading); Charles Goodman (consequentialist reading); Barbra Clayton (Śāntideva scholarship); Mark Siderits; ongoing "Buddhism and analytic philosophy" intersection
    - Cross-traditional dialogue: Buddhist-Christian (Masao Abe, John Cobb); Buddhist-process philosophy; Buddhist-phenomenology (Dan Zahavi, Nāgārjuna and Husserl); Buddhist-pragmatism
  - Contemporary institutional developments
    - Bhikkhunī ordination: accelerating globally; Alliance for Bhikkhunis; ordination ceremonies in Australia, US, Sri Lanka, Thailand (ongoing resistance from conservative Sangha authorities; 2021–2025 gradual expansion)
    - Buddhist responses to climate crisis: One Earth Sangha; Buddhist Declaration on Climate Change (2015); eco-dharma retreats; Buddhist-environmental activism
    - Abuse and institutional reform: Shambhala, Rigpa, and other organizations faced abuse revelations (2018–); An Olive Branch mediation; safeguarding policies; clergy ethics reckoning across traditions
    - Digital Buddhism: meditation apps (Insight Timer, Plum Village, Waking Up); virtual retreats (accelerated by COVID); online sanghas; AI-generated dharma talks; digital ethics from Buddhist perspective
    - Buddhist demographics: ~500 million adherents globally (estimates vary); growth in China (despite restrictions); decline in Japan; stability in Southeast Asia; growth in West; Ambedkarite expansion in India

#### Hinduism — Full Depth

- **Foundations and Scope**
  - Hinduism: world's oldest living religious tradition; ~1.2 billion adherents (2025); predominantly India and Nepal; diaspora worldwide
  - Not a single unified religion but a family of traditions sharing overlapping concepts, texts, practices, and sacred geography
  - No single founder, no single creed, no single institutional authority; "Hinduism" as a term: originally geographical (Persian "Sindhu" → "Hindu" for people beyond the Indus); consolidated as religious label under colonial-era categorization (19th c.)
  - Self-designations: Sanātana Dharma ("Eternal Order/Law"); Vaidika Dharma ("Vedic Religion"); various sampradāya (lineage-tradition) identities
  - Hindu diversity spectrum: village folk religion ↔ Brahmanical ritualism ↔ philosophical speculation ↔ devotional movements ↔ tantric traditions ↔ modern reform movements ↔ political Hinduism
  - Core shared concepts: dharma (cosmic/moral order, duty), karma (action and consequence), saṃsāra (cycle of rebirth), mokṣa/mukti (liberation), ātman (self/soul), Brahman (ultimate reality)
  - Four puruṣārthas (aims of human life): dharma (righteousness), artha (wealth/prosperity), kāma (pleasure/desire), mokṣa (liberation) — comprehensive life framework; not world-denying

- **The Vedas and Vedic Religion (~1500–500 BCE)**
  - The four Vedas — śruti ("that which is heard," revealed/authorless)
    - **Ṛgveda** (Veda of Verses): oldest (~1500–1200 BCE); 1,028 hymns (sūktas) in 10 maṇḍalas; addressed to devas (gods): Indra (warrior king, storm), Agni (fire, ritual mediator), Soma (sacred plant/drink), Varuṇa (cosmic order/ṛta), Uṣas (dawn), Sūrya (sun), Mitra, Aśvins
      - Nāsadīya Sūkta (10.129, "Creation Hymn"): radical questioning — "Who really knows? Who will here proclaim it? Whence was it produced? Whence is this creation?"; philosophical skepticism within Vedic corpus itself
      - Puruṣa Sūkta (10.90): cosmic man (puruṣa) sacrificed to create the world; origin of four varṇas (social classes) from body parts — Brāhmaṇa (mouth), Kṣatriya (arms), Vaiśya (thighs), Śūdra (feet); foundational (and contested) text for caste ideology
    - **Yajurveda** (Veda of Ritual Formulas): mantras and procedures for yajña (fire sacrifice); two recensions: Śukla (White, Vājasaneyi Saṃhitā) and Kṛṣṇa (Black, Taittirīya and Kaṭha Saṃhitās)
    - **Sāmaveda** (Veda of Melodies): chants (sāman) set to Ṛgvedic verses; musical notation; liturgical singing; foundation of Indian classical music theory
    - **Atharvaveda** (Veda of Atharvan the Fire Priest): hymns, spells, incantations for daily life — healing, protection, love, cursing enemies; reflects popular/folk religion alongside priestly ritual; philosophical speculation in later portions
  - Each Veda has four layers (chronological strata):
    - **Saṃhitā** (hymn collections): oldest layer; mantras and poetry
    - **Brāhmaṇas** (~900–700 BCE): prose ritual commentary; theology of sacrifice; Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa (most important; cosmogony, flood myth, Prajāpati theology); Aitareya Brāhmaṇa
    - **Āraṇyakas** ("Forest Books"): transitional texts between ritual and philosophy; internalization of sacrifice; meditative reinterpretation
    - **Upaniṣads** (~800–200 BCE): philosophical climax; "Vedānta" = "end of the Vedas"; ~108 traditionally; 13 principal Upaniṣads considered most authoritative:
      - **Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad**: longest; dialogue of Yājñavalkya and Maitreyī; "neti neti" (not this, not this) — Brahman cannot be defined positively; ātman-Brahman identity
      - **Chāndogya Upaniṣad**: Uddālaka Āruṇi teaches son Śvetaketu; "tat tvam asi" (thou art that) — mahāvākya (great saying); identity of individual self and ultimate reality
      - **Taittirīya Upaniṣad**: five sheaths (pañcakośa) of the self — food (anna), breath (prāṇa), mind (manas), knowledge (vijñāna), bliss (ānanda)
      - **Kaṭha Upaniṣad**: Naciketas dialogues with Death (Yama); ātman cannot be killed; chariot metaphor (self as charioteer, body as chariot, intellect as driver, mind as reins, senses as horses)
      - **Īśā Upaniṣad**: "All this is pervaded by the Lord"; renunciation within action
      - **Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad**: two kinds of knowledge — lower (ritual, grammar) and higher (knowledge of Brahman); "Satyameva jayate" (Truth alone triumphs — modern Indian national motto)
      - **Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad**: shortest (12 verses); analysis of OṂ (A-U-M) and four states of consciousness: waking (viśva), dreaming (taijasa), deep sleep (prājña), turīya (the fourth — pure consciousness); Gauḍapāda's Kārikā commentary → foundation of Advaita Vedānta
      - Also: Aitareya, Praśna, Kena, Śvetāśvatara (early theism: proto-Śaiva; possible earliest mention of Rudra-Śiva as supreme), Maitrī
  - Vedic ritual (yajña/sacrifice): fire rituals performed by Brahmin priests; Agnihotra (daily fire offering); Soma ritual; Aśvamedha (horse sacrifice — royal sovereignty ritual); hierarchical priesthood (hotṛ, adhvaryu, udgātṛ, brahmā); Śrauta (major public) and Gṛhya (domestic) rituals
  - Vedic cosmology: ṛta (cosmic order maintained by ritual and truth); tripartite cosmos (heaven, atmosphere, earth); cyclical time; gods as personifications of natural/cosmic forces
  - Vedic society: varṇa system (four classes, increasingly rigid); āśrama system (four life-stages: brahmacārya/student, gṛhastha/householder, vānaprastha/forest-dweller, sannyāsa/renunciant); patriarchal but some notable women sages (Gārgī, Maitreyī)

- **Smṛti Literature and the Epics**
  - Smṛti ("that which is remembered") — secondary but immensely influential; includes epics, Purāṇas, Dharmaśāstras, Āgamas
  - **The Mahābhārata** (~400 BCE – 400 CE): world's longest epic (~100,000 ślokas/verses = ~1.8 million words); attributed to Vyāsa; Bhārata dynasty's civil war (Pāṇḍavas vs. Kauravas); vast encyclopaedic scope: dharma debates, cosmology, philosophy, law, politics
    - **Bhagavad Gītā** (Gītā, ~200 BCE – 200 CE): 700 verses, 18 chapters; dialogue between Prince Arjuna and Lord Kṛṣṇa (his charioteer, avatar of Viṣṇu) on the battlefield of Kurukṣetra
      - Arjuna's crisis: refusal to fight kinsmen; ethical paralysis
      - Kṛṣṇa's teaching — three yogas (paths): Karma Yoga (selfless action, niṣkāma karma — action without attachment to fruits); Jñāna Yoga (knowledge — discerning ātman from body/mind); Bhakti Yoga (loving devotion to God)
      - Sthitaprajña (person of steady wisdom): equanimity in pleasure/pain, success/failure
      - Kṛṣṇa's theophany (Chapter 11, Viśvarūpa — cosmic form): overwhelming vision of universal form; divine terror and majesty
      - Synthesis: action, knowledge, and devotion are complementary; each person according to temperament (svadharma)
      - Influence: central Hindu scripture; commented on by virtually every major philosopher (Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, Madhva, Abhinavagupta, Gandhi, Aurobindo, Tilak); translated into every major world language; Gandhi called it his "spiritual reference book"
    - Other Mahābhārata sections: Anu Gītā; Śānti Parva (Book of Peace — Bhīṣma's deathbed teachings on dharma, politics, philosophy); Mokṣa Dharma (liberation teachings); Nala and Damayantī (love story); Sāvitrī and Satyavān
  - **The Rāmāyaṇa** (~500 BCE – 300 CE): ~24,000 ślokas; attributed to Vālmīki; story of Prince Rāma (avatar of Viṣṇu): exile, abduction of Sītā by Rāvaṇa (demon king of Laṅkā), rescue with Hanumān's help, triumphant return, reign of Rāma-rājya (ideal kingdom)
    - Rāma: ideal king, husband, son — maryādā puruṣottama (supreme person of propriety); model of dharmic conduct
    - Sītā: model of wifely devotion; subjected to agni parīkṣā (fire ordeal) to prove purity; later abandoned despite innocence — profoundly problematic for feminist readings; modern retellings reclaim Sītā's agency
    - Hanumān: monkey god; devotee exemplar; celibacy (brahmacārya); strength and selfless service; immensely popular folk deity
    - Regional Rāmāyaṇas: Kamban's Tamil Irāmāvatāram (12th c.); Tulsīdās's Rāmcaritmānas (Avadhi Hindi, 16th c. — most popular North Indian devotional text); Southeast Asian versions: Ramakien (Thai), Reamker (Khmer), Kakawin Rāmāyaṇa (Javanese); A. K. Ramanujan's essay "Three Hundred Ramayanas" (1991) — plurality of tellings; controversial: removed from Delhi University syllabus 2011, sparking academic freedom debate
  - **Purāṇas** (~3rd–16th c. CE): 18 Mahāpurāṇas + 18 Upapurāṇas; cosmology, mythology, genealogy, pilgrimage, ritual
    - Viṣṇu Purāṇa, Bhāgavata Purāṇa (most beloved: Kṛṣṇa's childhood/youth, rāsa līlā — cosmic dance with gopīs; theology of divine love; Prahlāda story; influence on Bhakti poetry and arts), Śiva Purāṇa, Devī Māhātmya/Mārkaṇḍeya Purāṇa (Goddess mythology: Durgā slaying Mahiṣāsura; foundation text of Śāktism), Brahmāṇḍa Purāṇa (Lalitā Sahasranāma), Garuḍa Purāṇa (afterlife, funeral rites), Skanda Purāṇa (largest; pilgrimage sites)
    - Purāṇic cosmology: cyclical time — four yugas (Satya/Kṛta, Tretā, Dvāpara, Kali); mahāyuga (~4.32 million years); kalpa (day of Brahmā = 1,000 mahāyugas = ~4.32 billion years); dissolution (pralaya) and recreation; currently in Kali Yuga (age of strife)
    - Daśāvatāra (Ten Avatars of Viṣṇu): Matsya (fish), Kūrma (tortoise), Varāha (boar), Narasiṃha (man-lion), Vāmana (dwarf), Paraśurāma (warrior sage), Rāma, Kṛṣṇa, Buddha (incorporated — controversial syncretic move), Kalki (future avatar, eschatological)
  - **Dharmaśāstras** (legal/ethical treatises): Manusmṛti (Laws of Manu, ~200 BCE – 200 CE: duties by varṇa and āśrama; gender rules; highly influential and highly controversial — patriarchal, caste-hierarchical; criticized by Ambedkar, burned publicly as symbol of caste oppression); Yājñavalkya Smṛti; Nārada Smṛti; Arthaśāstra of Kauṭilya (political science treatise, pragmatic statecraft, separate from dharmic idealism)

- **Six Orthodox Schools of Hindu Philosophy (Ṣaḍ Darśana)**
  - All accept Vedic authority (āstika); each a complete philosophical system with foundational sūtra text + commentarial tradition
  - **Sāṃkhya** (Enumeration)
    - Attributed to Kapila (legendary sage); earliest systematic Indian philosophy
    - *Sāṃkhyakārikā* (Īśvarakṛṣṇa, ~350 CE): foundational text (73 verses)
    - Metaphysical dualism: Puruṣa (consciousness, passive witness, many individual souls) and Prakṛti (primal matter/nature, active, unconscious, one)
    - Prakṛti evolves through three guṇas (qualities): sattva (illumination/purity), rajas (activity/passion), tamas (inertia/darkness); guṇa theory pervades all Hindu thought
    - Evolution (pariṇāma) of Prakṛti: mahat (cosmic intellect) → ahaṃkāra (ego-principle) → manas (mind) + five sense organs + five action organs + five subtle elements → five gross elements; 25 tattvas (principles) total
    - Liberation (kaivalya): Puruṣa's discriminative knowledge (viveka) that it is eternally separate from Prakṛti; cessation of suffering
    - No God (nirīśvara): classical Sāṃkhya is atheistic; Puruṣa-Prakṛti dualism without divine creator
    - Influence: foundational for Yoga, Āyurveda, Hindu cosmology; guṇa theory used everywhere
  - **Yoga** (Discipline/Union)
    - **Patañjali**: *Yogasūtras* (~200 BCE – 400 CE; dating debated; 196 sūtras in 4 pādas)
    - Definition: "yogaś citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ" — yoga is the cessation of the fluctuations of the mind (1.2)
    - Aṣṭāṅga Yoga (Eight Limbs): yama (restraints: ahiṃsā, satya, asteya, brahmacarya, aparigraha), niyama (observances: śauca, santoṣa, tapas, svādhyāya, īśvarapraṇidhāna), āsana (posture), prāṇāyāma (breath control), pratyāhāra (sense withdrawal), dhāraṇā (concentration), dhyāna (meditation), samādhi (absorption)
    - Samādhi types: savikalpa/samprajñāta (with cognitive content — vitarka, vicāra, ānanda, asmitā) and nirvikalpa/asamprajñāta (beyond cognitive content → kaivalya/liberation)
    - Kleśas (afflictions): avidyā (ignorance), asmitā (ego), rāga (attachment), dveṣa (aversion), abhiniveśa (fear of death)
    - Sāṃkhya metaphysics adopted (Puruṣa-Prakṛti) but with addition of Īśvara (God as special Puruṣa, untouched by karma — saguṇa theism grafted onto Sāṃkhya dualism)
    - Key commentaries: Vyāsa's Bhāṣya (foundational); Vācaspati Miśra's Tattvavaiśāradī; Vijñānabhikṣu
    - Later yoga traditions: Haṭha Yoga texts — *Haṭhayogapradīpikā* (Svātmārāma, 15th c.); *Gheraṇḍa Saṃhitā*; *Śiva Saṃhitā*; āsana-based physical practices; kuṇḍalinī yoga (awakening serpent energy at spine base, rising through cakras to sahasrāra); nāḍīs (energy channels: iḍā, piṅgalā, suṣumnā); modern postural yoga: T. Krishnamacharya (1888–1989, "father of modern yoga"), B.K.S. Iyengar (precision alignment), Pattabhi Jois (Ashtanga Vinyasa), T.K.V. Desikachar (Viniyoga); Swami Sivananda (Divine Life Society); global yoga industry (~$80B+)
  - **Nyāya** (Logic/Reasoning)
    - **Akṣapāda Gautama**: *Nyāyasūtras* (~2nd c. BCE – 2nd c. CE)
    - Pramāṇas (means of valid knowledge): pratyakṣa (perception), anumāna (inference), upamāna (comparison/analogy), śabda (testimony/verbal authority) — four accepted; rigorous epistemology
    - Sixteen categories (padārthas) for philosophical debate; syllogistic reasoning: five-membered syllogism (pratijñā/thesis, hetu/reason, udāharaṇa/example, upanaya/application, nigamana/conclusion)
    - Later Nyāya: **Navya-Nyāya** (New Logic, ~13th c.): **Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya** (*Tattvacintāmaṇi*, ~1325 CE): extraordinarily sophisticated formal logic; technical language rivaling modern formal logic; Raghunātha Śiromaṇi (16th c.); Navadwip (Bengal) as center
    - Theistic: Nyāya developed proofs for God's existence (Udayana, *Nyāyakusumāñjali*, 11th c. — five arguments for Īśvara)
  - **Vaiśeṣika** (Particularism/Atomism)
    - **Kaṇāda**: *Vaiśeṣikasūtras* (~2nd c. BCE)
    - Ontological categories (padārthas): dravya (substance), guṇa (quality), karma (action), sāmānya (universality), viśeṣa (particularity — gives school its name), samavāya (inherence); later added abhāva (absence)
    - Atomic theory: paramāṇu (indivisible atoms) of four elements (earth, water, fire, air); atoms combine in pairs (dyads) then triads to form perceptible objects; remarkably similar in structure (though not detail) to Greek atomism (Democritus); developed independently
    - Merged with Nyāya from ~10th c. (Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika as combined realist-logical school); Praśastapāda (*Padārthadharmasaṅgraha*, 6th c.)
  - **Mīmāṃsā** (Pūrva Mīmāṃsā — Ritual Exegesis)
    - **Jaimini**: *Mīmāṃsāsūtras* (~200 BCE; ~2,500 sūtras — largest Indian philosophical sūtra text)
    - Purpose: rational defense of Vedic ritual action (karma-kāṇḍa); Vedas as authorless (apauruṣeya), eternal, self-validating
    - Philosophy of language: words have intrinsic (autpattika) relation to objects, not conventional; elaborate theory of sentence meaning; madhyama (sentence as primary meaning-unit); influential for all Indian language philosophy
    - Dharma = Vedic injunction (vidhi); emphasis on duty, ritual performance as means to heaven (svarga); originally no God needed (ritual itself is effective)
    - Major commentators: Śabara (*Śabarabhāṣya*); Kumārila Bhaṭṭa (~7th c., *Ślokavārttika* — massive defense of Vedic authority, critique of Buddhist epistemology; Bhaṭṭa sub-school); Prabhākara (Prābhākara sub-school — different theory of error, sentence meaning)
    - Influence: hermeneutical methods adopted in Indian jurisprudence; foundation for Vedānta philosophy (Vedānta = Uttara Mīmāṃsā)
  - **Vedānta** (Uttara Mīmāṃsā — End of the Vedas / Culmination)
    - Foundation text: *Brahmasūtras* / *Vedāntasūtras* (Bādarāyaṇa, ~200 BCE – 200 CE; 555 sūtras; cryptically terse; meaning determined by commentaries)
    - Prasthānatraya (three foundational texts): Upaniṣads + Bhagavad Gītā + Brahmasūtras; every major Vedāntin wrote commentaries on all three
    - **Advaita Vedānta** (Non-Dualism)
      - **Gauḍapāda** (~6th–7th c.): *Māṇḍūkyakārikā*; argued for ajātivāda (non-origination — nothing is ever born, world is like a dream); influenced by/parallel to Mādhyamaka Buddhism (debated)
      - **Śaṅkara** (Ādi Śaṅkarācārya, ~788–820 CE): greatest Indian philosopher for many; *Brahmasūtrabhāṣya* (foundational commentary); *Upadeśasāhasrī*; commentaries on principal Upaniṣads and Gītā
        - Brahman: sole reality; nirguṇa (without qualities, beyond all predication); saguṇa Brahman (with qualities, Īśvara) = Brahman as conceived through māyā
        - Ātman = Brahman: identity of individual self and ultimate reality; "Ahaṃ brahmāsmi" (I am Brahman)
        - Māyā/Avidyā: cosmic ignorance/illusion; the phenomenal world is neither fully real nor fully unreal (mithyā) — it has empirical reality (vyāvahārika) but not ultimate reality (pāramārthika); three levels of reality: absolute (pāramārthika — Brahman alone), empirical (vyāvahārika — everyday world), illusory (prātibhāsika — dreams, mirages)
        - Mokṣa: direct knowledge (jñāna) that ātman IS Brahman; not something to be "achieved" but a recognition of what always was; removal of avidyā
        - Establishment of four maṭhas (monasteries): Śṛṅgerī (south), Dvārakā (west), Purī (east), Jyotirmaṭh/Badrīnāth (north); Śaṅkarācārya lineage continues today
        - Influence: dominant Hindu philosophical school for centuries; criticized by virtually all others (Rāmānuja, Madhva, Buddhists); renewed in modern era by Swami Vivekananda
      - Post-Śaṅkara Advaita: Sureśvara (*Naiṣkarmyasiddhi*); Padmapāda; Vācaspati Miśra (*Bhāmatī* sub-school — avidyā located in individual jīva); Prakāśātman; Vimuktātman; Citsukha; Madhusūdana Sarasvatī (16th c., synthesized Advaita with bhakti); Vidyāraṇya (14th c., *Pañcadaśī*)
    - **Viśiṣṭādvaita Vedānta** (Qualified Non-Dualism)
      - **Rāmānuja** (1017–1137 CE): *Śrībhāṣya* (commentary on Brahmasūtras); *Vedārthasaṅgraha*; *Gītābhāṣya*
        - Brahman is real, the world is real, souls (jīvas) are real — all three; Brahman = Viṣṇu/Nārāyaṇa with infinite auspicious qualities (saguṇa)
        - Brahman's body = the entire world (including jīvas and matter); organic relation (śarīra-śarīrī-bhāva — body-soul relation between world and God)
        - Against Śaṅkara: māyā as cosmic illusion is incoherent; if Brahman is the only reality, who is ignorant? (famous objection); world is Brahman's real transformation (pariṇāma), not illusion
        - Mokṣa: eternal loving communion with God in Vaikuṇṭha (Viṣṇu's heaven); not merger/identity but proximity and service; bhakti (devotion) + prapatti (total surrender) as primary means
        - Śrī Vaiṣṇavism: tradition continued; split into Vaḍakalai (northern, Vedānta Deśika, 1268–1369: Sanskrit-focused, effort + grace) and Teṅkalai (southern, Piḷḷai Lokācārya, 1213–1311: Tamil-focused, pure grace — "cat school" vs. "monkey school" analogy for divine grace)
    - **Dvaita Vedānta** (Dualism)
      - **Madhva** (Madhvācārya, 1238–1317 CE): *Brahmasūtrabhāṣya*; radical pluralistic realism
        - Five eternal differences (pañca-bheda): God differs from souls; God differs from matter; souls differ from matter; souls differ from each other; material entities differ from each other
        - Viṣṇu as supreme; independently real God, souls, matter — never merge; hierarchical dependence (souls eternally dependent on God)
        - Mokṣa: eternal bliss in proximity to God; graded liberation (different souls attain different levels of closeness); some souls eternally damned (tamo-yogya) — unique among Hindu schools
        - Jayatīrtha (13th–14th c., "Ṭīkācārya": commentarial genius); Vyāsatīrtha (15th–16th c.): debated Advaita extensively
        - Dvaita → Haridāsa movement → Purandaradāsa ("father of Carnatic music")
    - Other Vedānta schools:
      - **Dvaitādvaita** (Dual Non-Dual): Nimbārka (~13th c.); Kṛṣṇa-devotion; difference-and-non-difference simultaneously real
      - **Śuddhādvaita** (Pure Non-Dualism): Vallabha (1479–1531); world is real manifestation of Brahman (not māyā); puṣṭi-mārga (path of divine grace/nourishment); Kṛṣṇa devotion; influential among Gujarati Vaiṣṇavas
      - **Acintya Bhedābheda** (Inconceivable Difference-and-Non-Difference): Caitanya tradition (Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavism); jīvas and world are simultaneously one with and different from Kṛṣṇa; formulated by Jīva Gosvāmī (16th c.); basis for ISKCON theology

- **Śaivism (Traditions Centered on Śiva)**
  - Scope: Śiva as supreme deity; one of Hinduism's two great theistic streams (with Vaiṣṇavism); diverse traditions from ascetic to tantric
  - **Pāśupata** (earliest Śaiva sect): Lakulīśa (~2nd c. CE); ascetic practices; deliberately provocative behavior to burn karma; Kauṇḍinya's *Pañcārthabhāṣya*; largely extinct but historically significant as earliest organized Śaiva movement
  - **Śaiva Siddhānta** (Śaiva Orthodoxy)
    - South Indian tradition (Tamil Nadu); dualistic theology: pati (Lord/Śiva), paśu (individual soul), pāśa (bonds: āṇava-mala/innate impurity, karma, māyā)
    - Liberation: Śiva's grace (śaktipāta) removes bonds; soul achieves eternal proximity to Śiva (not merger)
    - 63 Nāyaṉmārs (Tamil Śaiva saint-poets, 6th–8th c.): Appar, Sundarar, Sambandar, Māṇikkavācakar (*Tiruvācakam*); devotional poetry in Tamil; Tēvāram hymns; parallel to Vaiṣṇava Āḻvārs
    - Meykaṇṭar (13th c.): *Civañāṉapōtam* (12 Tamil sūtras → Śaiva Siddhānta philosophical canon); Umāpati Civācāriyar (14 Siddhānta treatises)
    - Sanskrit Śaiva Siddhānta: Sadyojyoti, Bṛhaspati, Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha, Rāmakaṇṭha (10th–11th c. Kashmiri commentators: dualistic, scriptural Āgama tradition)
  - **Kashmir Śaivism** (Pratyabhijñā / Trika / Non-Dual Śaivism)
    - Non-dual tantric tradition: Śiva as universal consciousness (cit/saṃvit); the world is Śiva's real self-expression (ābhāsa), not illusion
    - **Vasugupta** (~9th c.): *Śiva Sūtras* (revealed text; "Consciousness is the self"); *Spanda Kārikās* (doctrine of vibration/pulsation — reality as dynamic consciousness)
    - **Somānanda** (*Śivadṛṣṭi*): recognition (pratyabhijñā) — we need only recognize that we are already Śiva
    - **Utpaladeva** (~925–975): *Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā* (Verses on the Recognition of the Lord): philosophical masterwork; Śiva as supreme agent, knower, actor
    - **Abhinavagupta** (~950–1016 CE): greatest Kashmiri Śaiva philosopher; polymath
      - *Tantrāloka* (Light on the Tantras): encyclopedic summa of tantric theory and practice; 37 chapters; synthesis of all Śaiva tantric traditions
      - *Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī* and *-vivṛtivimarśinī*: commentaries on Utpaladeva; recognition philosophy fully elaborated
      - *Parātrīśikāvivaraṇa*: linguistics and mantra theory
      - Aesthetic theory: *Abhinavabhāratī* (commentary on Bharata's *Nāṭyaśāstra*): rasa theory — aesthetic experience (rasāsvāda) as analogous to spiritual bliss (brahmāsvāda); camatkāra (aesthetic wonder); enormously influential for Indian art theory
      - 36 tattvas (categories of reality): extension of Sāṃkhya's 25 with 11 additional Śaiva tattvas above puruṣa-prakṛti; Śiva-Śakti at summit; hierarchy from pure consciousness to gross matter
    - Kṣemarāja (*Pratyabhijñāhṛdayam* — "Heart of Recognition": accessible 20-sūtra summary of the entire system; popular introductory text)
  - **Vīraśaivism / Liṅgāyatism**: Basava (Basavaṇṇa, 12th c., Karnataka): radical social reform; rejected caste, Vedic authority, image worship; worshipped Śiva through personal iṣṭaliṅga (small Śiva-liṅga worn on body); Vīraśaiva vacanas (free-verse devotional poetry); Akka Mahādevī (12th c., woman saint); ~10 million followers today; egalitarian ethos; ongoing community classified as distinct from broader Hinduism debate
  - **Nātha Yogīs**: Gorakṣanātha/Gorakhnāth (~10th–12th c.); synthesis of Śaiva tantra, yoga, alchemy (rasāyana); haṭha yoga origins; influential across North India; Kanphata (split-ear) yogis; Nātha lineage → early Sikh connections; Matsyendranāth (legendary founder)
  - **Śiva Theology**: Naṭarāja (cosmic dancer — dance of creation and destruction, Cidambaram temple); Ardhanārīśvara (half-male, half-female form — Śiva-Śakti union); Dakṣiṇāmūrti (Śiva as silent teacher); liṅga worship (aniconic representation; fertility and transcendence symbolism); Śiva as Mahādeva, Bhairava, Paśupati; third eye; Mt. Kailāsa; Gaṅgā in matted hair

- **Vaiṣṇavism (Traditions Centered on Viṣṇu/Kṛṣṇa/Rāma)**
  - Scope: Viṣṇu (and his avatars, especially Kṛṣṇa and Rāma) as supreme deity; numerically the largest Hindu tradition
  - **Pāñcarātra / Early Vaiṣṇavism**: Vāsudeva-Kṛṣṇa worship (pre-2nd c. BCE evidence: Heliodorus Pillar, Besnagar, ~110 BCE — Greek ambassador identifies as Vāsudeva devotee); emanation (vyūha) theology: Vāsudeva → Saṅkarṣaṇa → Pradyumna → Aniruddha; Pāñcarātra Āgamas/Saṃhitās (ritual and temple manuals)
  - **Āḻvārs** (Tamil Vaiṣṇava saint-poets, 6th–9th c.): 12 Āḻvārs; *Nālāyira Divya Prabandham* (4,000 Tamil hymns, "Tamil Veda"); Nammāḻvār (*Tiruvāymoḻi* — theological masterpiece; ecstatic devotion); Āṇḍāḷ (female Āḻvār, *Tiruppāvai* — 30 verses; devotional role-play as gopīs longing for Kṛṣṇa; still recited daily in temples); influence on Rāmānuja and Śrī Vaiṣṇavism
  - **Śrī Vaiṣṇavism**: Rāmānuja's tradition (see Viśiṣṭādvaita above); temple-centered; Tirupati (Tirumala Vēṅkaṭēśvara temple — richest temple in the world; ~100,000+ pilgrims daily); Śrīraṅgam (Raṅganātha temple, largest Hindu temple complex); 108 Divya Desams (sacred Vaiṣṇava sites)
  - **Bhāgavata / Kṛṣṇa Bhakti movements**: Kṛṣṇa as supreme God (svayaṃ bhagavān); *Bhāgavata Purāṇa* as central text
    - **Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavism**: **Caitanya Mahāprabhu** (1486–1533, Bengal): ecstatic devotion; saṅkīrtana (congregational chanting); seen as combined avatar of Rāḍhā and Kṛṣṇa; theology of five rasas (moods of devotion): śānta, dāsya, sakhya, vātsalya, mādhurya (conjugal — highest, Rādhā-Kṛṣṇa divine love)
      - Six Gosvāmīs of Vṛndāvana (Rūpa Gosvāmī, Sanātana Gosvāmī, Jīva Gosvāmī, etc.): systematized theology; *Bhaktirasamṛtasindhu* (Rūpa): devotional aesthetics mapped onto rasa theory
      - ISKCON (International Society for Krishna Consciousness): A. C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupāda (1896–1977): founded 1966 in New York; "Hare Krishnas"; global temple network; *Bhagavad-gītā As It Is* translated into 70+ languages; controversial (cult accusations, institutional abuse scandals); now ~10,000 temples/centers worldwide
    - Puṣṭimārga (Vallabhācārya tradition): see Vallabha above; Śrīnāthjī temple (Nathdwara, Rajasthan); Haveli saṅgīt (devotional music tradition)
  - **Rāma Bhakti**: Tulsīdās (*Rāmcaritmānas*, 16th c.: Hindi devotional epic; most read Hindu text in North India); Rāmānanda (14th–15th c.): popularized Rāma-devotion among lower castes; Kabīr tradition overlaps
  - **Viṣṇu Theology**: Nārāyaṇa reclining on cosmic serpent Śeṣa/Ananta; Lakṣmī (consort — prosperity, grace, beauty); Garuḍa (eagle mount); Śaṅkha (conch), Cakra (discus), Gadā (mace), Padma (lotus); avatāra doctrine (divine descent into world to restore dharma); Viṣṇu's accessibility and grace (kṛpā); Vaikuṇṭha (heavenly abode)

- **Śāktism (Traditions Centered on the Goddess / Devī / Śakti)**
  - Scope: the supreme divine principle is feminine — Śakti (Power/Energy); Goddess as ultimate reality; intersects Śaivism (Śiva-Śakti) and tantra
  - **Devī Māhātmya** (*Durgā Saptaśatī*, ~5th–6th c. CE, in Mārkaṇḍeya Purāṇa): foundational text; Goddess slays buffalo-demon Mahiṣāsura; three episodes: Madhu-Kaiṭabha, Mahiṣāsura, Śumbha-Niśumbha; Goddess as supreme cosmic power; recited during Navarātrī festival
