  - **Devī Bhāgavata Purāṇa**: Goddess-centered Purāṇa; parallel to Bhāgavata Purāṇa with Devī as supreme
  - Forms of the Goddess: Durgā (warrior, demon-slayer); Kālī (fierce, time-death-transformation; black, skull-garlanded; Dakṣiṇā Kālī — cremation ground mistress; liberation through destruction of ego); Pārvatī (Śiva's consort, gentle, domesticity); Lakṣmī (Viṣṇu's consort, abundance); Sarasvatī (Brahmā's consort, knowledge, arts, music); Tripurasundarī/Lalitā (tantric supreme goddess, Śrīvidyā tradition); Ṣoḍaśī; Tārā; Chinnamastā; ten Mahāvidyās (wisdom goddesses — esoteric Śākta tantra)
  - **Śrīvidyā** tradition: worship of Tripurasundarī through Śrīcakra/Śrīyantra (sacred geometric diagram); mantra: ṣoḍaśī or pañcadaśī (15/16-syllable mantra); sophisticated ritual system; Kaśmīr and South Indian lineages; Bhāskararāya (18th c., greatest Śrīvidyā commentator; *Varivasyārahasya*)
  - **Bengali Śāktism**: Kālī worship; **Rāmakṛṣṇa Paramahaṃsa** (1836–1886): Dakṣiṇeśwar temple priest; ecstatic mystic; practiced and affirmed all paths (Hindu, Muslim, Christian); experienced Kālī as divine mother; saw all religions as paths to same goal; foundation for Hindu universalism
  - Goddess theology: Śakti as dynamic feminine principle without which Śiva is inert ("Śiva without Śakti is śava — a corpse"); prakṛti-puruṣa reinterpreted; female power as creative, sustaining, dissolving; feminist potential debated (empowering archetype vs. patriarchal framework using goddess imagery while controlling women)
  - Village goddess traditions: Māriyammaṇ (South India, smallpox/disease goddess), Śītalā (North India), Manasā (snake goddess, Bengal); folk Śāktism; animal sacrifice traditions; distinct from Brahmanical systematization

- **Tantra in Hinduism**
  - Definition: tantra as system of ritual practice, theology, and yoga found across Śaiva, Śākta, and (to lesser extent) Vaiṣṇava traditions; texts called Tantras, Āgamas, Saṃhitās
  - Not a separate religion but a mode within Hinduism (and Buddhism); "tantric" vs. "Vedic" as two streams (debated — often intertwined)
  - Key features: mantra (sacred sound formulas), yantra/maṇḍala (sacred diagrams), nyāsa (ritual placement of deities on body), dīkṣā (initiation), guru-śiṣya relationship, kuṇḍalinī/subtle body practices, deity visualization
  - Left-hand tantra (vāmācāra): transgressive practices — pañcamakāra (five M's: madya/wine, māṃsa/meat, matsya/fish, mudrā/grain, maithuna/sexual union); ritual inversion of social norms; practiced literally by some, symbolically/internally by most
  - Right-hand tantra (dakṣiṇācāra): orthodox, substitutive; visualization and mantra without transgressive substances
  - Kashmir Śaiva tantra: see Abhinavagupta above; sophisticated non-dual philosophy
  - Śākta tantra: see Śrīvidyā; ten Mahāvidyās; Kaulism (Kaula tradition — body as vehicle of liberation)
  - Key tantric texts: *Vijñānabhairava Tantra* (112 meditation techniques; widely taught today); *Kulārṇava Tantra*; *Mahānirvāṇa Tantra*; *Netra Tantra*; *Svacchanda Tantra*; *Mālinīvijayottara Tantra*
  - Modern academic study: Sir John Woodroffe/Arthur Avalon (*The Serpent Power*, 1919; *Shakti and Shākta*: first serious Western treatment); David Gordon White (*Kiss of the Yogini*, 2003; *The Alchemical Body*, 1996); Alexis Sanderson (Oxford: definitive scholarship on Śaiva-Śākta tantra lineages); André Padoux (*Vāc: The Concept of the Word in Selected Hindu Tantras*, 1990); Gavin Flood

- **Bhakti Movement (~6th–17th c. CE)**
  - Pan-Indian devotional revolution: direct emotional relationship with personal God (Viṣṇu/Kṛṣṇa/Rāma/Śiva/Devī); challenged ritualism, caste hierarchy, Sanskrit exclusivity; vernacular languages
  - South Indian origins:
    - Āḻvārs (Vaiṣṇava; see above) and Nāyaṉmārs (Śaiva; see above) — 6th–9th c.; Tamil
    - Basava (12th c., Kannada, Vīraśaiva; see above)
  - Mahārāṣṭra:
    - **Jñāneśvar/Jñānadev** (1275–1296): *Jñāneśvarī* (Marathi commentary on Gītā); mystical poetry; linked philosophy and devotion
    - **Nāmdev** (1270–1350): Marathi-Punjabi bhakta; inclusivism; also in Sikh Guru Granth Sahib
    - **Eknāth** (1533–1599): Marathi poet; social reform; challenged untouchability
    - **Tukārām** (1608–1649): Marathi; abhaṅgas (devotional poems); Viṭṭhala-devotion (Paṇḍharpur); Vārkarī pilgrimage tradition (annual walking pilgrimage to Pandharpur, ~1 million participants)
  - North India:
    - **Kabīr** (~1440–1518): weaver-poet; neither Hindu nor Muslim exclusively; nirguna bhakti (formless God); satirized both Hindu and Muslim orthodoxy; *Bījak*; oral tradition; couplets (dohe) in vernacular Hindi; influenced Sikhism, Kabīrpanthīs
    - **Guru Nānak** (1469–1539): founder of Sikhism; strongly influenced by Kabīr and bhakti; one God beyond form (Ik Oaṃkār); (treated more fully under Sikhism)
    - **Rāmānanda** (~14th–15th c.): opened Rāma-devotion to all castes; teacher of Kabīr (traditional claim)
    - **Sūrdās** (~1478–1583): Hindi/Braj; *Sūrsāgar*: Kṛṣṇa's childhood in Vṛndāvana; blind poet (tradition); maternal love (vātsalya) rasa
    - **Tulsīdās** (~1532–1623): *Rāmcaritmānas*: most influential Hindi literary work; Rāma-bhakti; Avadhi language
    - **Mīrābāī** (~1498–1546): Rajput princess who renounced royal life for Kṛṣṇa-devotion; ecstatic poetry; transgressed gender norms; icon of female spiritual independence; "Mero to Girdhar Gopāl, dūsaro na koī" — songs still sung daily across India
    - **Caitanya Mahāprabhu** (see Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavism above)
  - South India (later period):
    - **Purandaradāsa** (1484–1564): "father of Carnatic music"; Kannada kīrtanas; Dvaita-Bhakti synthesis
    - **Tyāgarāja** (1767–1847): Telugu; greatest Carnatic music composer; Rāma-devotion; thousands of kṛtis
    - **Annamayya** (1408–1503): Telugu; 32,000 saṅkīrtanas for Vēṅkaṭēśvara
  - Bengal: Caitanya's ecstatic movement (see above); Bāul tradition (itinerant mystic musicians; body-centered practice; blurred Hindu-Muslim-tantric boundaries; Lalon Fakir/Shah, 1774–1890)
  - Bhakti theology: saguṇa vs. nirguṇa bhakti; nine modes of devotion (navadha bhakti from Bhāgavata Purāṇa): listening (śravaṇa), singing (kīrtana), remembering (smaraṇa), service (pāda-sevana), worship (arcana), prostration (vandana), servitude (dāsya), friendship (sakhya), self-offering (ātma-nivedana)
  - Social impact: challenged caste orthodoxy (many bhakti saints were lower-caste or outcast); empowered women and marginalized groups; vernacular literatures flourished; BUT: did not permanently dismantle caste system; reabsorbed into Brahmanical framework in many cases

- **Hindu Ritual, Temple, and Festival Culture**
  - Pūjā (worship): daily and festival; ṣoḍaśopacāra (16 offerings: invocation, seat, water, bathing, clothing, sacred thread, sandal paste, flowers, incense, lamp, food, prostration, circumambulation, etc.); temple pūjā vs. home pūjā
  - Temple architecture: Nāgara (North Indian: śikhara tower — Khajuraho, Bhubaneshwar, Somnath); Drāviḍa (South Indian: gopuram gateway towers — Madurai Meenakshi, Thanjavur Bṛhadīśvara); Vesara (hybrid, Deccan — Hoysala temples at Belur, Halebidu); temples as cosmic diagrams (vāstu-puruṣa-maṇḍala); garbhagṛha (inner sanctum), maṇḍapa (hall), prākāra (enclosure walls)
  - Major pilgrimages (tīrtha-yātrā): Char Dham (Badrīnāth, Dvārakā, Purī Jagannāth, Rāmeśvaram); Kumbh Melā (Prayāgraj/Allahabad, Haridwar, Ujjain, Nashik — largest religious gathering on Earth, ~150 million at 2019 Kumbh); Varanasi/Kāśī (oldest living city, Gaṅgā ghāṭs, cremation and liberation); Vṛndāvana; Tirupati
  - Major festivals: Dīvālī/Dīpāvalī (festival of lights, Lakṣmī-pūjā, Rāma's return); Navarātrī/Durgā Pūjā/Dasara (nine nights of Goddess worship, especially Bengal and Gujarat); Holī (spring, colors, Kṛṣṇa-Rādhā); Mahā Śivarātrī (Śiva's great night); Gaṇeśa Caturthī (especially Maharashtra); Kṛṣṇa Janmāṣṭamī (Kṛṣṇa's birthday); Pongal/Makar Saṅkrānti (harvest); Ratha Yātrā (Jagannāth chariot procession, Puri)
  - Saṃskāras (life-cycle rituals, ~16 traditionally): nāmakaraṇa (naming), upanayana (sacred thread ceremony, twice-born initiation), vivāha (marriage: agni-parikramā/seven steps), antyeṣṭi (funeral/cremation), śrāddha (ancestor rites)
  - Āyurveda: traditional medicine system; three doṣas (vāta, pitta, kapha); based on Sāṃkhya cosmology; *Caraka Saṃhitā*, *Suśruta Saṃhitā* (surgery — rhinoplasty described); Dhanvantari (divine physician); herbal pharmacopoeia; WHO recognition; ongoing research integration
  - Jyotiṣa (Hindu astrology): Vedāṅga (limb of Veda); nakṣatras (27 lunar mansions); rāśi (zodiac signs); planetary influence; pervasive in Hindu life decisions (marriage, naming, auspicious timing); distinct from Western astrology in sidereal vs. tropical zodiac

- **Caste, Dharma, and Social Structure**
  - Varṇa-jāti system: varṇa (four theoretical classes: Brāhmaṇa, Kṣatriya, Vaiśya, Śūdra) vs. jāti (thousands of birth-groups, endogamous, occupation-linked); Dalits ("untouchables," avarṇa — outside the four varṇas): extreme social exclusion
  - Dharmaśāstra justification: Manusmṛti codified hierarchy; karma theory used to justify birth-status (your caste = result of past-life karma); Brāhmaṇical ideology
  - Critiques within Hinduism: Bhakti saints (Kabīr, Basava, Ravīdās, Cokhamēḷā, Tukārām) challenged caste; egalitarian strands
  - Colonial and modern transformation: British census reified caste categories; social reform: Jyotirao Phule (1827–1890, Satyashodhak Samaj, anti-Brahmin movement, female education); **B. R. Ambedkar** (1891–1956): Dalit leader; architect of Indian Constitution; PhD from Columbia; exposed caste as fundamentally incompatible with democracy and human dignity; burned Manusmṛti (Dec 25, 1927); converted to Buddhism (1956) — rejected Hinduism as irreformable on caste; *Annihilation of Caste* (1936 — undelivered speech, now classic anti-caste text)
  - Reservation/affirmative action in India: Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, Other Backward Classes; constitutional provisions (Articles 15, 16, 17, 46); Mandal Commission (1980, implemented 1990)
  - Caste persists: honor killings for inter-caste marriage; occupational segregation; manual scavenging; Dalit activism; ongoing scholarly debate: is caste intrinsic to Hinduism or a social distortion? (Traditionalists vs. Ambedkarite critique vs. Subaltern Studies perspectives)

- **Modern Hindu Reform and Revival (19th–20th c.)**
  - **Rām Mohan Roy** (1772–1833): "Father of Modern India"; Brahmo Samaj (1828); monotheistic reform; campaigned against satī (widow immolation) → British abolition 1829; opposed idolatry, caste rigidity; defended Hindu rationalism via Upaniṣads
  - **Debendranath Tagore** (1817–1905) and **Keshab Chandra Sen** (1838–1884): developed Brahmo Samaj; ecumenical theism
  - **Dayānanda Sarasvatī** (1824–1883): Ārya Samāj (1875); "Back to the Vedas"; rejected Purāṇas, image worship, caste by birth; Vedas as sole authority; education reform; aggressive polemicism (śuddhi/reconversion movement); influential in Punjab/North India; social modernization within Hindu framework
  - **Rāmakṛṣṇa Paramahaṃsa** (1836–1886): see Śāktism section; universalist mysticism; all paths valid
  - **Swami Vivekananda** (1863–1902): Rāmakṛṣṇa's chief disciple; electrifying speech at Parliament of World Religions, Chicago (1893): "Sisters and Brothers of America"; founded Ramakrishna Mission (social service + Advaita Vedānta); presented Hinduism as rational, universal, philosophical; practical Vedānta; seva (service) as spiritual practice; enormous influence on modern Hindu identity; criticized for elitism and nation-building ideology by some scholars (Meera Nanda)
  - **Rabindranath Tagore** (1861–1941): Nobel Prize in Literature 1913 (*Gītāñjali*); universal humanism; rejected narrow nationalism; Śāntiniketan (Visva-Bharati University); poetic synthesis of Upaniṣadic and Baul spirituality
  - **Sri Aurobindo** (1872–1950): revolutionary-turned-mystic; *The Life Divine* (1939–40): evolutionary spirituality; supramental transformation; integral yoga; Auroville (experimental international community, 1968, near Pondicherry); "The Mother" (Mirra Alfassa, 1878–1973, French collaborator and spiritual partner)
  - **Mahatma Gandhi** (1869–1948): ahiṃsā (non-violence) and satyāgraha (truth-force) as political-spiritual practice; Gītā as life guide; renamed "untouchables" as Harijans ("children of God" — now considered patronizing; Dalits prefer "Dalit"); Hindu-Muslim unity; assassinated by Hindu nationalist (Nathuram Godse, 1948); critique: Ambedkar's disagreement on caste reform approach; Gandhi's early racist writings in South Africa; complex legacy including Indian independence
  - **Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan** (1888–1975): philosopher-president of India; *The Hindu View of Life* (1926); *Indian Philosophy* (2 vols, 1923–27); presented Hinduism to Western academy as rational mysticism; Advaita-influenced; criticized for sanitizing/homogenizing Hindu diversity
  - **Ramana Maharshi** (1879–1950): Advaita sage of Arunachala (Tiruvannamalai); ātma-vicāra (self-inquiry: "Who am I?"); direct path; silence as teaching; enormous influence on Western spiritual seekers; Paul Brunton (*A Search in Secret India*, 1934)

- **Hinduism and Nationalism (Hindutva)**
  - V. D. Savarkar (*Hindutva: Who Is a Hindu?*, 1923): defined Hindu identity as cultural-geographical-racial, not strictly religious; Hindutva = "Hindu-ness"; India as Hindu rashtra (nation); marginalized Muslims and Christians as foreign
  - RSS (Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh, founded 1925, K. B. Hedgewar): Hindu nationalist paramilitary volunteer organization; ideological parent of BJP; M. S. Golwalkar (*We, or Our Nationhood Defined*, 1939: admired Nazi treatment of minorities — later editions revised)
  - BJP (Bharatiya Janata Party): Hindu nationalist political party; Babri Masjid demolition (1992, Ayodhya — Hindu mobs destroyed 16th c. mosque claimed to be on Rāma's birthplace); 2002 Gujarat riots (anti-Muslim violence); Narendra Modi (PM 2014–present): Hindu nationalist governance; Ram Mandir (new temple at Ayodhya, inaugurated Jan 2024); Citizenship Amendment Act 2019 (excludes Muslims from fast-track citizenship for refugees); cow-vigilantism; "love jihad" laws
  - Academic debate: Wendy Doniger (*The Hindus: An Alternative History*, 2009 — Penguin India pulped Indian edition 2014 under Hindutva pressure); Sheldon Pollock (critiqued by Rajiv Malhotra); Hindu right vs. Western Indology; appropriation vs. representation debates
  - Distinction: Hinduism ≠ Hindutva; many Hindus and Hindu scholars reject Hindutva's political-exclusivist ideology; progressive Hindu voices (Romila Thapar, Irfan Habib, Upinder Singh among historians defending pluralistic readings)

- **Hindu Diaspora and Global Hinduism**
  - 19th-century indentured labor: Trinidad, Guyana, Suriname, Fiji, Mauritius, South Africa, Malaysia, Singapore; Hindu communities maintained temple and festival cultures
  - 20th–21st century professional migration: US, UK, Canada, Australia; temple-building movement (BAPS Swaminarayan mandirs; Hindu Temple of Greater Chicago; etc.)
  - Gurus in the West: Maharishi Mahesh Yogi (Transcendental Meditation, 1958; Beatles; TM movement; corporate mindfulness); Swami Muktananda (Siddha Yoga); Bhagwan Shree Rajneesh/Osho (1931–1990, highly controversial; Rajneeshpuram, Oregon; eclectic neo-tantra); Mata Amritanandamayi (Amma, "the hugging saint"); ISKCON (see above); Sadhguru Jaggi Vasudev (Isha Foundation, popular YouTube presence); Sri Sri Ravi Shankar (Art of Living)
  - Yoga as global phenomenon: postural yoga industry; cultural appropriation debates; Hindu American Foundation's "Take Back Yoga" campaign; yoga vs. Hinduism relationship contested
  - Hindu Chaplaincy, Hindu studies in Western universities: growing field

- **Frontier / Contemporary Research Directions (2020–present)**
  - Textual and philosophical studies
    - Critical editions and translations: new translations of Upaniṣads (Patrick Olivelle, Oxford); new Mahābhārata scholarship (Vishwa Adluri and Joydeep Bagchee, *Philology and Criticism*, 2018 — challenged 19th c. German philological assumptions about Mahābhārata textual layers; controversial)
    - Śaiva studies renaissance: Alexis Sanderson's lineage-mapping; Dominic Goodall (Pondicherry, critical editions of Śaiva texts); Shaman Hatley (Yoginī tantra); current generation expanding non-Vedānta Hindu philosophy
    - Navya-Nyāya revival: Jonardon Ganeri (*The Lost Age of Reason*, 2011): arguing Indian analytic philosophy was sophisticated and independent; Matthew Dasti; Stephen Phillips; recovering Nyāya realism for comparative epistemology
    - Mīmāṃsā studies: Elisa Freschi (ritual hermeneutics); Lawrence McCrea; new appreciation for Mīmāṃsā philosophy of language and action theory
  - Historical and archaeological research
    - Indus Valley / Sarasvatī Civilization: Rakhigarhi excavation (2018 aDNA study, Vasant Shinde and Vagheesh Narasimhan: no Iranian farmer or steppe ancestry in IVC individual → challenges simple Aryan migration-into-IVC narrative, though steppe ancestry clearly enters later); genetic evidence for Indo-Aryan migrations (David Reich, *Who We Are and How We Got Here*, 2018: aDNA confirms steppe-ancestry influx ~2000–1500 BCE; politically contentious — Hindutva nationalists reject "Aryan migration")
    - Sarasvatī River research: satellite imagery; paleochannel identification; debate on Ghaggar-Hakra = Vedic Sarasvatī; geological evidence for river drying ~2000 BCE
    - Temple archaeology: ASI (Archaeological Survey of India) surveys; Ayodhya excavation reports (2003, Babri Masjid site); ongoing politicization of archaeology in India
    - Maritime and trade archaeology: Dholavira, Lothal (dock), ancient Indian Ocean trade networks
  - Hindu-science dialogue: consciousness studies (David Chalmers's "hard problem" and Advaita/Yogācāra parallels); quantum Physics and Vedānta (popular but philosophically contested — Deepak Chopra's appropriations vs. serious comparative work by Michel Bitbol, etc.); neuroscience of yoga and meditation (NIH-funded studies on yoga for PTSD, chronic pain); Āyurveda clinical trials (AYUSH ministry, India; integration with modern medicine; randomized controlled trials increasing)
  - Political and social studies
    - Caste and Hinduism: growing Dalit intellectual movement; Suraj Yengde (*Caste Matters*, 2019); Gopal Guru (philosophy of humiliation); caste in diaspora studies
    - Hindutva studies: Christophe Jaffrelot (*Hindu Nationalism: A Reader*); Ornit Shani; Angana Chatterji; impact of Hindu nationalism on historiography, textbook revision, university curricula
    - Gender and Hinduism: Ruth Vanita (same-sex love in Indian traditions); Sondra Hausner; Leela Prasad; Hindu feminist theology; trans/hijra identities and Hindu mythology (Ardhanarishvara, Shikhandi in Mahabharata); Section 377 decriminalization in India (2018)
  - Environmental Hinduism: Hindu Declaration on Climate Change (2015, Parliament of Religions); Gaṅgā restoration (Namami Gange project); sacred grove conservation (devarakadu, kavus); Chipko movement roots in Hindu sacred ecology; Vandana Shiva (ecofeminism, seed sovereignty — controversial scientific claims but significant activist)
  - Digital Hinduism: virtual darśana (temple livestreams accelerated by COVID); AI chatbots trained on Hindu scripture; online pūjā services; digitization of manuscripts (IFP Pondicherry, NGMPP Nepal-German project, IGNCA); Hindu Wikipedia editing activism; social media amplification of both devotional content and Hindutva politics

#### Other World Religions & Comparative Religion — Full Depth

- **Zoroastrianism**
  - Foundations and origins
    - One of the oldest revealed religions; founded by Zarathustra/Zoroaster (~1500–1000 BCE, dating heavily debated; some scholars place as early as 1700 BCE, others ~600 BCE; linguistic analysis of Gāthās suggests earlier date)
    - Geographic origin: Central Asian steppes or eastern Iran (Bactria/Khorasan region); spread across Iranian plateau
    - Zarathustra as prophet: received revelation from Ahura Mazdā ("Wise Lord"); rejected polytheism of Indo-Iranian religion; retained some deities as yazatas (worthy of worship); converted King Vishtāspa (patron)
  - Sacred texts
    - **Avesta**: Zoroastrian scripture; surviving text is fraction of original (much lost after Alexander's conquest and later Islamic conquest)
      - **Gāthās** (17 hymns): oldest portion; attributed directly to Zarathustra; composed in Old Avestan (closely related to Ṛgvedic Sanskrit); philosophical, ethical, devotional; difficult to interpret; Yasna 28–34, 43–51, 53
      - **Yasna** (liturgy of worship): 72 chapters; includes Gāthās; Yasna Haptaŋhāiti (Seven-Chapter Liturgy, archaic)
      - **Yashts** (hymns to individual deities/yazatas): Mithra (Yasht 10), Anāhitā (Yasht 5, water/fertility goddess), Tištrya (Yasht 8, star/rain)
      - **Vendidād/Vīdēvdād** (Law Against Demons): purity codes, cosmological myth, funerary practices; latest Avestan text
      - **Bundahišn** (Middle Persian): "Creation" — cosmogony, eschatology; key for understanding Zoroastrian worldview
    - Middle Persian (Pahlavi) literature: *Dēnkard* (encyclopedic compendium); *Arda Vīrāz Nāmag* (Book of Righteous Vīrāz — vision of heaven and hell, may have influenced Dante); *Mēnōg ī Xrad* (Spirit of Wisdom); *Škand Gumānīg Vizār* (doubt-dispelling treatise)
  - Core theology and worldview
    - Ethical dualism: cosmic struggle between Ahura Mazdā (Wise Lord, supreme good deity) and Angra Mainyu/Ahriman (Destructive Spirit, source of evil, lies, death)
    - Amesha Spentas (Holy Immortals, 7): divine attributes/emanations of Ahura Mazdā — Vohu Manah (Good Mind), Asha Vahishta (Best Truth/Righteousness), Khshathra Vairya (Desirable Dominion), Spenta Ārmaiti (Holy Devotion), Haurvatāt (Wholeness), Ameretāt (Immortality), Spenta Mainyu (Holy Spirit — sometimes identified with Ahura Mazdā himself)
    - Human choice (free will): each person must choose between asha (truth/righteousness) and druj (lie/deceit); this choice determines cosmic outcome; humans are allies of Ahura Mazdā in the cosmic struggle
    - Three pillars: Humata (good thoughts), Hūkhta (good words), Hvarshta (good deeds)
    - Eschatology: individual judgment at death (Chinvat Bridge — soul weighed; righteous cross to paradise, wicked fall to hell); cosmic eschatology: Frashōkereti (renovation/making wonderful) — final defeat of Angra Mainyu; resurrection of the dead; purification by molten metal; renewed perfect world; universal salvation (even the wicked are purified); Saoshyant (savior figure, born of virgin — parallels with later messianic concepts)
    - Fire cult: fire (ātash) as symbol of Asha (truth/righteousness); fire temples (ātash bahrām, ātash ādurān); eternal flames maintained by priests (mōbeds); fire not worshipped but venerated as representation of divine light
    - Purity laws: corpse pollution (nasu); sky burial / Tower of Silence (dakhma) — exposure to vultures (Zoroastrians don't cremate or bury to avoid polluting fire, earth, water); ritual purification (bareshnum)
  - Historical development
    - Achaemenid Empire (~550–330 BCE): Zoroastrianism as state/royal religion; Cyrus the Great, Darius I, Xerxes; Persepolis inscriptions; possible syncretism with Mesopotamian and other traditions; what was "Achaemenid religion" remains debated (was it strict Zoroastrianism? worship of Ahura Mazdā without Zoroastrian theology? scholarly debate ongoing)
    - Hellenistic/Seleucid period: decline of priestly infrastructure; Greek influence
    - Parthian Empire (~247 BCE – 224 CE): tolerance; oral transmission of Avesta continued; Mithra cult spread westward → Roman Mithraism (debated connection — Roger Beck, Manfred Clauss)
    - Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE): Zoroastrianism as official state religion; Kirdēr (powerful high priest, 3rd c.); persecution of Manichaeans, Christians, Buddhists; codification of Avesta in written form; theological systematization; Zurvanism (heretical sect: Zurvan/Infinite Time as father of both Ahura Mazdā and Angra Mainyu — reduces dualism to monism; suppressed but influential)
    - Islamic conquest of Iran (7th c.): gradual decline; conversion to Islam (social/economic pressure, jizya tax, restricted rights); Zoroastrians became marginalized minority; some fled to Gujarat, India → Parsis
  - Modern Zoroastrianism
    - **Parsis** (Indian Zoroastrians): arrived Gujarat ~8th–10th c. CE; thrived under Mughal/British rule; Bombay/Mumbai as center; disproportionately influential in business (Tata family), law, military, arts; ~60,000 in India (declining — intermarriage debates, low birth rate)
    - Iranis (Zoroastrians who remained in Iran): ~25,000; centered on Yazd, Kerman; Safavid-era persecution; 20th–21st c. recognition under Iranian constitution as protected minority
    - Demographics: ~100,000–200,000 worldwide; genuine existential crisis of numbers; debates over conversion (Parsis traditionally reject converts; reform movements advocate acceptance)
    - Scholarly study: Mary Boyce (*Zoroastrians: Their Religious Beliefs and Practices*, 1979; fieldwork in Sharīfābād village, last traditional Zoroastrian community in Iran); Jenny Rose; Michael Stausberg; Almut Hintze (Avestan linguistics); Prods Oktor Skjærvø
  - Influence on other religions
    - Possibly influenced Judaism (during Babylonian Exile/Achaemenid period): angelology/demonology, Satan figure, apocalypticism, resurrection, heaven/hell, messianic expectations, cosmic dualism; causal influence debated but widely accepted as plausible
    - Influence on Christianity: eschatological framework, dualistic theology, Magi of Nativity story (Zoroastrian priests)
    - Influence on Islam: concepts of bridge of judgment (Ṣirāṭ), paradise (firdaus < Avestan pairi.daēza), individual judgment
    - Manichaeism drew heavily from Zoroastrianism (and Buddhism and Christianity)

- **Jainism**
  - Foundations and origins
    - Ancient Indian religion; founded/revived by **Mahāvīra** (Vardhamāna, 599–527 BCE traditional dating; modern scholarship: ~540–468 BCE); 24th and last Tīrthaṅkara ("ford-maker" / spiritual teacher) of the current cosmic cycle
    - **Pārśvanātha** (23rd Tīrthaṅkara, ~872–772 BCE traditional): historical figure; may represent earlier organized Jain community; four-fold restraint (later expanded to five by Mahāvīra)
    - Śramaṇa tradition: same milieu as early Buddhism; contemporary of Siddhārtha Gautama; both rejected Vedic authority; Jainism may be older than Buddhism (Pārśva predates Buddha)
    - Mahāvīra: born Kṣatriya prince in Bihar; renounced at age 30; 12 years of extreme asceticism; attained kevala-jñāna (omniscience); established four-fold community (monks, nuns, laymen, laywomen); taught for 30 years; died at Pāvāpurī (attained mokṣa)
  - Core doctrines
    - **Ahiṃsā** (non-violence): most radical form in any religion; extends to all living beings — one-sensed (plants, earth-bodies, water-bodies, fire-bodies, air-bodies), two-sensed (worms), three-sensed (ants), four-sensed (bees), five-sensed (animals, humans); monks sweep paths, strain water, wear mouth-covers; vegetarianism mandatory; strict Jains avoid root vegetables (killing plant); non-violence in thought, word, and deed
    - **Anekāntavāda** (many-sidedness / non-absolutism): reality is complex and many-faceted; no single viewpoint captures complete truth; **Syādvāda** (doctrine of "perhaps/in some respect"): seven-fold predication — every statement is conditionally true; **Nayavāda** (standpoint epistemology): seven nayas (partial perspectives); sophisticated logical pluralism; relevant to modern pluralism and epistemology
    - **Karma** (materialistic theory): karma is fine matter (karma-pudgala) that physically adheres to the soul; 8 types of karmic matter; influx (āsrava) through activity; bondage (bandha); stoppage of influx (saṃvara) through discipline; shedding (nirjarā) through austerity; complete removal → mokṣa; uniquely Jain: karma is a physical, not just moral, concept
    - **Jīva and Ajīva**: jīva (soul/living substance — infinite in number; each has infinite knowledge, perception, bliss, and energy in its pure state); ajīva (non-living): matter (pudgala), space (ākāśa), motion (dharma), rest (adharma), time (kāla); no creator God; universe is eternal and self-regulating
    - **Mokṣa/Kevala**: liberation = complete removal of all karmic matter from soul; soul rises to **siddha-loka** (apex of universe); infinite knowledge, infinite perception, infinite bliss, infinite energy; permanent; no return to saṃsāra; Jain cosmology: universe shaped like a cosmic person; multilayered heavens and hells
    - **Five Great Vows** (mahāvrata, for monks/nuns): ahiṃsā (non-violence), satya (truth), asteya (non-stealing), brahmacarya (celibacy), aparigraha (non-possession); laity observe same as aṇuvrata (minor vows, less strict)
    - Asceticism: central to Jain path; fasting (up to death: sallekhanā/santhārā — rational, voluntary fasting unto death, not suicide; legal controversy in modern India — Indian Supreme Court 2015 initially banned it, reversed 2016); nudity (Digambara monks); standing meditation (kāyotsarga); bodily mortification as karma-shedding
  - Sects and divisions
    - **Digambara** ("sky-clad"): monks go entirely naked (sky as garment); women cannot attain mokṣa in this life (must be reborn as men first — contested by Digambara women scholars); reject several texts accepted by Śvetāmbaras; stricter; dominant in Karnataka, Maharashtra, Rajasthan
    - **Śvetāmbara** ("white-clad"): monks/nuns wear white robes; women can attain mokṣa; larger textual canon (Āgamas: 45 texts, classified in 6 groups; Ṣaṭkhaṇḍāgama and Kaṣāyaprābhṛta are key Digambara texts); dominant in Gujarat, Rajasthan
      - Śvetāmbara sub-sects: Mūrtipūjaka (image-worshipping), Sthānakavāsī (no image worship, plain meeting halls), Terāpanthī (founded by Ācārya Bhikṣu, 1760; reformed; Ācārya Tulsi's Anuvrata Movement, 20th c.)
    - Historical split: traditionally dated to ~3rd c. BCE; possibly gradual divergence over centuries
  - Key texts
    - Śvetāmbara canon (Āgamas): 12 Aṅgas (limbs; 12th — Dṛṣṭivāda — lost); Upāṅgas; Cheda Sūtras; Mūla Sūtras; Prakaraṇas; *Tattvārtha Sūtra* (Umāsvāti/Umāsvāmī, ~2nd–5th c. CE: most authoritative philosophical text, accepted by both sects; 10 chapters systematizing all Jain metaphysics)
    - Digambara texts: *Ṣaṭkhaṇḍāgama*; *Kaṣāyaprābhṛta*; Kundakunda (~2nd c. CE: *Samayasāra*, *Pravacanasāra*, *Pañcāstikāyasāra* — mystical-philosophical; heavily influenced Digambara tradition; emphasis on niścaya-naya (transcendent perspective) vs. vyavahāra-naya (conventional perspective))
    - Major philosophers: Kundakunda; Siddhasena Divākara (~5th c., Jain logic); **Haribhadra Sūri** (~8th c.: ecumenical; *Ṣaḍdarśanasamuccaya* — compendium of six philosophies; yoga text; comparative religion pioneer); **Hemacandra** (1089–1172: "omniscient of the degenerate age"; polymath — grammar, logic, poetics, history, yoga; *Yogaśāstra*; advisor to Caulukya kings of Gujarat; promoted vegetarianism state-wide)
    - *Kalpasūtra* (Bhadrabāhu, ~3rd c. BCE): biographies of Tīrthaṅkaras; monastic rules; liturgical text read during Paryuṣana festival
  - Jain contributions to Indian culture
    - Mathematics: Jain cosmological texts contain advanced mathematical concepts (set theory, infinity types — countable vs. uncountable infinities, predating Cantor by ~2,000 years; logarithmic ideas; permutations/combinations)
    - Architecture: Dilwara temples (Mount Abu, Rajasthan — exquisite marble carving, 11th–13th c.); Ranakpur Jain temple; Śravaṇabeḷagoḷa (57-foot monolithic Gommaṭeśvara/Bāhubali statue, 981 CE, Karnataka); Jain caves at Udayagiri-Khandagiri, Badami, Ellora
    - Literature: contribution to Kannada, Tamil, Sanskrit, Prakrit, Hindi, Gujarati literatures; among the earliest Kannada literary works are Jain; Pampa, Ranna, Ponna (Kannada); Jain Rāmāyaṇa and Mahābhārata versions (alternative tellings)
    - Non-violence activism: Jain influence on Gandhi's ahiṃsā; Jain community lobbying against animal slaughter; influence on Indian vegetarianism
  - Modern Jainism
    - ~4.5–6 million adherents (primarily India; diaspora in East Africa, UK, US, Canada)
    - Economically influential: Jain business communities (baniyā); diamond industry (Palanpur Jains dominate global diamond trade, Antwerp/Surat); wealth combined with ascetic ideals = philanthropy
    - Ācārya Mahāprajña and Ācārya Tulsi: Terāpanth leaders; Preksā meditation; Jain Vishva Bharati University
    - Contemporary issues: sallekhanā/santhārā legal debates; activist campaigns for animal rights; Jain minority status in India (granted 2014); temple administration disputes; declining monastic numbers
    - Academic study: Paul Dundas (*The Jains*, 1992/2002); John Cort; Peter Flügel; Whitney Kelting; Phyllis Granoff

- **Sikhism**
  - Foundations and origins
    - Founded by **Guru Nānak** (1469–1539) in Punjab, northwestern Indian subcontinent; emerged from bhakti movement milieu; engaged with both Hindu and Islamic mystical traditions but constitutes a distinct religion
    - Guru Nānak's travels (udāsīs): traveled widely (Punjab, Bengal, Sri Lanka?, Mecca? — hagiographic traditions; historical extent debated); dialogues with Hindu sadhus, Muslim pirs, Siddha yogis; rejected caste, ritualism, idolatry; proclaimed one formless God (Ik Oaṃkār — "One Universal Creator")
    - Core message: meditation on the divine Name (Nām simran); honest living (kirat karō); sharing with others (vaṇḍ chhakō); equality of all humans; rejection of caste, asceticism, and ritualism
  - Ten Gurus (living spiritual guides, 1469–1708)
    - **Guru Nānak** (1469–1539): founder; hymns form largest portion of Guru Granth Sahib; established community (sangat) and communal kitchen (langar — free meal for all, regardless of caste/creed; still operational in every gurdwara)
    - **Guru Angad** (1504–1552): standardized Gurmukhī script (used for Punjabi and for writing Guru Granth Sahib)
    - **Guru Amar Das** (1479–1574): institutionalized langar; established manji system (diocesan structure); opposed satī; social reform for women
    - **Guru Ram Das** (1534–1581): founded Amritsar ("Pool of Nectar"); composed Lāvaṇ (wedding hymns, still used in Sikh marriages)
    - **Guru Arjan** (1563–1606): compiled *Ādi Granth* (first edition of scripture, 1604); built Harimandir Sāhib (Golden Temple); first Sikh martyr — executed by Mughal Emperor Jahangir (heated iron plate, hot sand)
    - **Guru Hargobind** (1595–1644): militarized Sikh community; concept of mīrī-pīrī (temporal and spiritual authority); wore two swords; Akal Takht (Throne of the Timeless, supreme seat of Sikh political authority, Amritsar)
    - **Guru Har Rai** (1630–1661) and **Guru Har Krishan** (1656–1664): relatively peaceful periods; Har Krishan died of smallpox at age 8; youngest Guru
    - **Guru Tegh Bahadur** (1621–1675): ninth Guru; protected Kashmiri Brahmins' right to religious freedom; executed by Emperor Aurangzeb in Delhi; epitomized sacrifice for religious liberty; "Hind dī Chādar" (Shield of India)
    - **Guru Gobind Singh** (1666–1708): tenth and last human Guru; established **Khālsā** (1699, Baisākhī, Anandpur Sahib): baptized community of saint-soldiers; pañj kakkār (Five Ks): keś (uncut hair), kaṅghā (comb), kaṛā (steel bracelet), kirpān (ceremonial sword/dagger), kacchera (undergarment); Sikh men take name "Singh" (lion), women "Kaur" (princess/sovereign)
      - Military campaigns against Mughal persecution; all four sons killed in Mughal wars; extraordinary resilience
      - Before death, declared Guru Granth Sahib as eternal Guru (no more human Gurus); and Guru Panth (the Sikh community collectively as decision-making body)
  - **Guru Granth Sahib** (Ādi Granth, installed as eternal Guru 1708)
    - 1,430 pages; 5,894 hymns (shabads); organized by rāga (musical modes — 31 rāgas; scripture meant to be sung)
    - Contents: hymns of 6 Sikh Gurus + 15 bhagats (saints from other traditions including Kabīr, Nāmdev, Ravīdās, Farīd, Trilochan, Pīpā, Sain, Dhannā); deliberate inclusion of Hindu and Muslim voices under one cover
    - Theology: Ik Oaṃkār (One God, formless, eternal, self-existent, beyond birth and death, immanent in creation); Nām (divine Name); Hukam (divine order/will); Haumai (ego — the fundamental obstacle); five thieves (pañj chor): lust, anger, greed, attachment, pride; liberation through Nām simran, sevā (selfless service), living truthfully in the world (householder ideal, not renunciation)
    - Treated with extreme reverence: placed on a throne (palakī/manji sahib); covered with rumālā (ceremonial cloth); continuous reading (akhaṇḍ pāṭh, 48 hours); processional installation (prakāsh) and resting (sukhāsan) daily
  - Other texts: *Dasam Granth* (attributed to Guru Gobind Singh; controversial; contains Jāp Sāhib, Tav Prasād Savaiyye — used in daily liturgy; also contains mythological narratives and Charitropakhyān — debate over authorship); *Sarbloh Granth* (iron-related; less widely accepted); *Bhai Gurdas Vārāṅ* (commentary, "key to Guru Granth Sahib"); *Janamsākhīs* (hagiographic biographies of Guru Nānak)
  - Sikh history after the Gurus
    - **Banda Singh Bahadur** (1670–1716): first Sikh military leader; carved out Sikh territory; executed by Mughals
    - Misl period (~1710s–1790s): Sikh confederacies (12 misls); guerrilla warfare against Mughals and Afghans
    - **Mahārāja Ranjit Singh** (1780–1839): unified Sikh Empire (Punjab, Kashmir, parts of Afghanistan); secular governance (Hindu, Muslim, Sikh officials); Sher-e-Punjab (Lion of Punjab); gilded the Golden Temple; treaty with British (no expansion beyond Sutlej River)
    - Anglo-Sikh Wars (1845–46, 1848–49): British defeated Sikhs; Punjab annexed to British India
    - Singh Sabha movement (~1870s–1920s): reform movement; purification of Sikh identity from Hindu syncretism; Tat Khalsa (pure Khalsa) identity; modern Sikh Reht Maryada (code of conduct) standardized; gurdwara reform (Akali movement, 1920s)
    - Partition of India (1947): Punjab divided between India and Pakistan; massive violence; ~1 million killed; Sikh homeland split; Sikh shrines (including Nankana Sahib, Guru Nānak's birthplace) left in Pakistan
    - Punjabi Suba movement (1960s): Sikhs demanded Punjabi-speaking state; created 1966 (trifurcation: Punjab, Haryana, Himachal Pradesh)
    - **1984 crisis**: Operation Blue Star (Indian Army assault on Golden Temple complex to remove Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale and armed militants; massive damage to Akal Takht; deep trauma); assassination of PM Indira Gandhi by Sikh bodyguards; 1984 anti-Sikh pogroms in Delhi (~3,000+ killed; long fight for justice); Khalistan separatist movement (demand for independent Sikh state; 1980s–90s militancy; diaspora activism continues)
  - Sikh diaspora: ~25–30 million Sikhs worldwide; UK (largest Sikh population outside Punjab: Birmingham, Southall, Slough); Canada (~800,000: British Columbia, Ontario; politically prominent — Jagmeet Singh, NDP); US (~500,000); Australia, Malaysia, East Africa, Italy
  - Sikh theology and ethics: Mīrī-Pīrī (spiritual + temporal); Sevā (selfless service — langar, disaster relief, community service; worldwide; COVID-era langar distribution); equality: no priestly class (any Sikh can lead worship, read scripture, perform ceremonies); gender equality (in principle; practice varies); Dasvandh (tithing 10% of earnings); Chardī Kalā (eternal optimism/resilient spirit)
  - Academic study: W. H. McLeod (1932–2009: critical historical method applied to Sikh studies, controversial among Sikhs; *Guru Nānak and the Sikh Religion*, 1968); Nikky-Guninder Kaur Singh (feminist Sikh theology); J. S. Grewal; Pashaura Singh; Harjot Oberoi (*The Construction of Religious Boundaries*, 1994 — challenged Sikh distinctiveness from Hindu world; protested by Sikh community); Arvind-Pal Mandair; Gurinder Singh Mann

- **Confucianism**
  - Foundations and origins
    - **Kǒngzǐ / Confucius** (551–479 BCE): born in state of Lǔ (modern Shandong); teacher, political advisor, editor of ancient texts; not a prophet or mystic but a moral philosopher and social reformer
    - Context: Spring and Autumn Period (~771–476 BCE); political fragmentation; breakdown of Zhōu dynasty feudal order; Confucius sought to restore social harmony through moral cultivation and proper ritual/governance
    - Not a "religion" in typical Western sense (no creation myth, no theology of salvation, minimal metaphysics of the supernatural); yet functions as religious tradition in East Asia — ancestor veneration, temple worship, ethical-spiritual cultivation
  - Core concepts
    - **Rén** (仁 benevolence/humaneness): supreme virtue; loving others; "Do not impose on others what you do not wish for yourself" (shù 恕, negative Golden Rule, *Analects* 15.24); heart of Confucian ethics
    - **Lǐ** (禮 ritual propriety/ceremony): proper conduct, etiquette, social norms, rites; shapes relationships and creates social harmony; includes ancestor worship, court ritual, daily comportment; inner moral disposition must match outer ritual form
    - **Xiào** (孝 filial piety): reverence and care for parents and ancestors; foundational social virtue; extends to respect for elders, teachers, rulers; *Xiào Jīng* (Classic of Filial Piety) as dedicated text
    - **Yì** (義 righteousness/duty): doing what is morally right; contrasted with lì (利 profit); the junzi acts from yì, the petty person from lì
    - **Zhì** (智 wisdom): moral discernment; knowing right from wrong; practical wisdom in human affairs
    - **Xìn** (信 trustworthiness/fidelity): keeping promises; sincerity in relationships
    - **Jūnzǐ** (君子 exemplary person/"gentleman"): Confucian ideal — morally cultivated person; not defined by birth but by character; contrasted with xiǎorén (小人 petty/small person); junzi leads by moral example, not coercion
    - Five Relationships (Wǔlún 五倫): ruler-subject, parent-child, husband-wife, elder-younger, friend-friend; each has reciprocal duties; hierarchical but mutual obligation; benevolent rule ↔ loyal service; loving parents ↔ filial children
    - **Zhèngmíng** (正名 rectification of names): words must correspond to reality; a ruler who doesn't rule properly is not truly "ruler"; restoring correct naming restores social order
    - **Dé** (德 virtue/moral power): ruler governs by moral example (dé) rather than force (lì) or punishment; government legitimacy depends on ruler's virtue; Mandate of Heaven (Tiānmìng 天命): Heaven grants authority to virtuous rulers and withdraws it from corrupt ones — justification for dynastic change
  - Key texts (Five Classics & Four Books)
    - **Five Classics** (Wǔjīng 五經): pre-Confucian texts compiled/edited by Confucius (tradition claims): *Yìjīng* (Book of Changes — divination + cosmological philosophy; yīn-yáng, 64 hexagrams; most influential Chinese classic), *Shījīng* (Book of Songs/Odes — 305 poems), *Shūjīng* (Book of Documents/History), *Lǐjì* (Book of Rites — ritual, education, music; includes *Dàxué* and *Zhōngyōng*), *Chūnqiū* (Spring and Autumn Annals — history of Lǔ state); lost Sixth Classic: *Yuèjīng* (Book of Music)
    - **Four Books** (Sìshū 四書): selected by **Zhū Xī** (1130–1200) as Confucian curriculum core:
      - *Lúnyǔ* (Analects): sayings of Confucius compiled by disciples; 20 books; most direct source for Confucius's thought
      - *Mèngzǐ* (Mencius): elaboration by Mencius (see below)
      - *Dàxué* (Great Learning): short text on self-cultivation → governing — "eight steps" from investigating things to bringing peace to all under heaven
      - *Zhōngyōng* (Doctrine of the Mean): attributed to Confucius's grandson Zǐsī; sincerity (chéng 誠); centrality; harmony of emotions
  - Development and major thinkers
    - **Mèngzǐ / Mencius** (~372–289 BCE): human nature is inherently good (xìng shàn 性善); four sprouts (sìduān): compassion → rén, shame → yì, deference → lǐ, moral judgment → zhì; needs cultivation like watering sprouts; benevolent government (rénzhèng); right of people to rebel against tyrannical rulers; second most important Confucian after Confucius
    - **Xúnzǐ / Xunzi** (~310–235 BCE): human nature is inherently inclined toward evil/selfishness (xìng è 性惡); therefore ritual (lǐ) and education are essential to transform nature; realist; emphasis on learning, social institutions, music, ritual; students included Han Feizi (Legalism) and Li Si; sometimes considered proto-Legalist in emphasis on institutions
    - **Dǒng Zhòngshū** (~179–104 BCE): synthesized Confucianism with yīn-yáng cosmology and Five Phases (wǔxíng); established Confucianism as Han dynasty state ideology; "Mutual Responsiveness of Heaven and Humankind" (tiānrén gǎnyìng); Confucian canon established in imperial academy
    - **Neo-Confucianism** (Sòng-Míng, 11th–17th c.): response to Buddhist and Daoist metaphysical challenge; created sophisticated Confucian cosmology and metaphysics
      - **Zhōu Dūnyí** (1017–1073): *Tàijítú Shuō* (Explanation of the Diagram of the Supreme Polarity); cosmological foundation of Neo-Confucianism
      - **Zhāng Zài** (1020–1077): qì (vital energy/material force) monism; *Xīmíng* (Western Inscription): all people are siblings, all things share one substance; universal compassion grounded in metaphysics
      - **Chéng Hào** (1032–1085) and **Chéng Yí** (1033–1107): brothers; principle (lǐ 理) as universal rational pattern inherent in all things; self-cultivation through "reverential attention" (jìng 敬); foundation for Zhu Xi's synthesis
      - **Zhū Xī** (1130–1200): greatest Neo-Confucian synthesizer; **Lǐxué** (School of Principle/Rationalism)
        - Dualism of lǐ (principle/pattern, normative, incorporeal) and qì (material force, constitutive, corporeal); all things have lǐ; the Supreme Polarity (tàijí) as the sum of all principles
        - Investigation of things (géwù 格物) → extension of knowledge (zhìzhī): studying external phenomena to understand principles; scholarly, analytical, systematic
        - Compiled Four Books with commentaries → became basis for civil service examinations (1313–1905); dominant form of Confucianism for ~700 years in China, Korea, Japan, Vietnam
      - **Wáng Yángmíng** (1472–1529): **Xīnxué** (School of Mind/Idealism)
        - Lǐ exists within the mind, not external objects; "xīn jí lǐ" (mind/heart is principle)
        - **Liángzhī** (良知 innate moral knowledge): every person possesses innate knowledge of good and evil; available immediately, without study; echoes Mencius's "four sprouts"
        - **Zhī xíng hé yī** (知行合一 unity of knowledge and action): true knowledge necessarily entails action; if you truly know the good, you necessarily do it; knowing and acting are one process
        - Influential in Japan (Yōmeigaku → influenced Meiji reformers; Ōshio Heihachirō); Korea (debate between Lǐxué and Xīnxué)
    - Korean Confucianism: enormous importance; **Yi Hwang/T'oegye** (1501–1570: Korean Zhu Xi, Four-Seven Debate on emotions and moral feelings; face on 1,000-won note); **Yi I/Yulgok** (1536–1584); Joseon dynasty (1392–1897): most Confucian state in history; Sŏnbi (scholar-gentleman) culture; Seonggyungwan academy; ancestor rites (jesa) still observed; Confucianism deeply embedded in Korean social structure (hierarchical relationships, educational emphasis)
    - Japanese Confucianism: Tokugawa period (1600–1868); Hayashi Razan (official Zhu Xi school); Ogyū Sorai (ancient learning school); Confucian education for samurai; shaped bushidō ethics; less metaphysically focused than Korean/Chinese
    - Vietnamese Confucianism: civil service exam system (1075–1919); Confucian literati (nho sĩ); deep influence on family structure, education, governance
  - Confucianism in modernity
    - Decline: Kāng Yǒuwéi (1858–1927: radical reinterpretation; proposed Confucianism as state religion/church); May Fourth Movement (1919: anti-Confucian; "Down with Confucius and sons"; Confucianism blamed for China's backwardness); Mao-era destruction (Cultural Revolution 1966–76: Pī Lín Pī Kǒng campaign — "Criticize Lin Biao, Criticize Confucius")
    - Revival: **New Confucianism** (Xīn Rújiā 新儒家): Mou Zongsan (1909–1995: Kantian-Confucian synthesis; intellectual intuition); Tang Junyi; Xu Fuguan; Tu Weiming (Harvard; "third epoch" of Confucianism; Confucian dialogue with modernity and global ethics; 1988 Nobel laureate statement on Confucian resources for world ethics); Roger Ames (process-Confucianism)
    - PRC: post-Mao rehabilitation; Confucius Institutes (language/culture promotion, controversial — shut down in many Western countries over academic freedom concerns); Xi Jinping invokes Confucian values; state-sponsored Confucius ceremonies (Qufu); Jiang Qing (political Confucianism, hierarchical, illiberal — debated)
    - "Confucian capitalism" thesis: Max Weber's *Religion of China* (1915) saw Confucianism as obstacle to capitalism; challenged by East Asian economic miracle (Japan, Korea, Taiwan, Singapore); Confucian work ethic, education emphasis, meritocracy debated as economic drivers
    - Academic study: Benjamin Elman; Daniel Gardner; Philip J. Ivanhoe; Bryan Van Norden (*Introduction to Classical Chinese Philosophy*); Michael Nylan; Mark Csikszentmihalyi; Karyn Lai

- **Daoism / Taoism**
  - Foundations and origins
    - Indigenous Chinese religious-philosophical tradition; emerged ~4th c. BCE (philosophical Daoism) with roots in ancient Chinese shamanic and cosmological traditions
    - Dào (道 the Way): ultimate reality, source and pattern of the universe; ineffable, formless, nameless; prior to heaven and earth; "The Dào that can be spoken is not the eternal Dào" (*Dàodéjīng* 1)
  - Classical/Philosophical Daoism (Dàojiā 道家)
    - **Lǎozǐ / Laozi** (legendary, 6th–4th c. BCE?): traditionally author of *Dàodéjīng*; historicity debated (may be composite text rather than single author); traditionally a senior contemporary of Confucius
    - **Dàodéjīng** (Tao Te Ching, ~4th–3rd c. BCE; Guodian bamboo strips ~300 BCE = earliest manuscript): ~5,000 characters; 81 chapters; most translated Chinese text after the Bible
      - Dào as origin of all things: "The Dào that can be spoken is not the eternal Dào; the Name that can be named is not the eternal Name" (ch. 1); Dào gave birth to One, One to Two, Two to Three, Three to the ten thousand things (ch. 42)
      - Dé (德 virtue/power): the Dào's manifestation in each thing; natural virtue; effortless excellence
      - Wúwéi (無為 non-action / effortless action): acting in accordance with nature; not forcing; like water — "the highest good is like water" (ch. 8); yielding overcomes rigid; soft overcomes hard
      - Zìrán (自然 naturalness / self-so-ness): the spontaneous, uncontrived nature of things; Dào follows its own nature
      - Political philosophy: sage-ruler governs by wúwéi; "Governing a great state is like cooking a small fish" (ch. 60 — don't over-handle); anti-war; simplicity; small communities; critique of civilization, artifice, excessive knowledge, moralism
      - Paradox and reversal: "The Dào that is bright seems dim; the Dào that advances seems to retreat" (ch. 41); dialectical reversal of conventional values; weakness as strength; emptiness as fullness
    - **Zhuāngzǐ / Zhuangzi** (~369–286 BCE): *Zhuāngzǐ* text (Inner Chapters attributed to Zhuangzi himself; Outer and Miscellaneous Chapters by followers)
      - Perspectivism and relativism: "Am I Zhuangzi who dreamed he was a butterfly, or a butterfly now dreaming he is Zhuangzi?" (butterfly dream); challenges fixed identity and knowledge
      - Equalization of things (qíwù 齊物): all distinctions (right/wrong, beautiful/ugly, large/small) are relative, perspectival, conventional; from the Dào's perspective, all things are equal
      - Spontaneity and skill: stories of the cook butchering an ox (Cook Ding, ch. 3 — effortless mastery through following natural patterns); wheelwright Bian; swimmer in rapids; knack-knowledge beyond words
      - Death as transformation: when his wife died, Zhuangzi drummed on a pot — death is natural transformation, not tragedy; "the universe gives me form, gives me toil in life, ease in old age, and rest in death"
      - Free and easy wandering (xiāoyáoyóu 逍遙遊): spiritual freedom; "uselessness" as freedom; great Peng bird vs. little birds — vastly different perspectives, neither "right"
      - Critique of Confucian moralism: artificiality of benevolence and righteousness; "When the great Dào was abandoned, benevolence and righteousness appeared" (*Dàodéjīng* 18; echoed in *Zhuāngzǐ*)
    - Other early Daoist-influenced texts: *Lièzǐ* (attributed to Lie Yukou; compilation ~3rd c. CE; includes Liezi's fatalism, Yang Zhu's hedonism, paradoxes); *Huáinánzǐ* (2nd c. BCE, Han dynasty synthesis of Daoism, Confucianism, cosmology; encyclopedic)
  - Religious Daoism (Dàojiào 道教)
    - Emergence as organized religion ~2nd c. CE; not a "corruption" of philosophical Daoism but continuous development
    - **Celestial Masters** (Tiānshī Dào 天師道): Zhang Daoling (~142 CE): claimed revelation from Laozi deified; theocratic community in Sichuan; talismans, repentance rituals, communal ceremonies; hereditary lineage; still continues (65th Celestial Master); Zhèngyī (Orthodox Unity) tradition
    - **Shangqing** (Supreme Clarity, ~4th c. CE): Maoshan revelations (Yang Xi, 364–370 CE); visualization of celestial deities; internal meditation; heavenly journeys of the spirit; literary; aristocratic; Táo Hóngjǐng (456–536, pharmacologist, cosmographer, Shangqing systematizer)
    - **Língbǎo** (Numinous Treasure, ~4th–5th c. CE): liturgical tradition; elaborate communal rituals (zhāi); influenced by Buddhism (universal salvation, ritual structure); scripturally based
    - **Quánzhēn** (Complete Perfection/Reality, ~12th c.): Wáng Chóngyang (1113–1170); monastic, celibate, ascetic; synthesis of Daoism, Buddhism, Confucianism (Three Teachings are One); Qiu Chuji (1148–1227, met Chinggis Khan); White Cloud Temple (Báiyúnguān, Beijing — headquarters); dominant monastic Daoist order today in PRC
    - Daoist deities and cosmology: Sānqīng (Three Purities): Yuánshǐ Tiānzūn (Celestial Worthy of Primordial Beginning), Língbǎo Tiānzūn (Celestial Worthy of Numinous Treasure), Dàodé Tiānzūn/Taishang Laojun (deified Laozi); Jade Emperor (Yùhuáng Dàdì — popular supreme deity in Chinese folk religion); Xīwángmǔ (Queen Mother of the West — ancient deity, predating Daoism); Eight Immortals (Bā Xiān — folk heroes); vast celestial bureaucracy mirroring Chinese imperial governance
    - Daoist practices: inner alchemy (nèidān 內丹): transformation of jīng (essence) → qì (vital energy) → shén (spirit) → return to void (xū); meditation; qìgōng; tàijíquán (internal martial art based on Daoist principles); outer alchemy (wàidān 外丹, seeking elixir of immortality through mineral/herbal preparations — declined after poisoning deaths); fēngshuǐ (geomancy); divination (Yìjīng); talisman writing (fú 符); ritual liturgy (jiào 醮); scriptural recitation; dietary practices; sexual yoga (fángzhōng shù — "arts of the bedroom," longevity techniques)
    - **Dàozàng** (Daoist Canon): ~1,500 texts; organized in Three Caverns (Sāndòng) + Four Supplements; massive compendium of scripture, ritual, meditation, philosophy, medicine, cosmology; Ming dynasty edition (1445); modern critical edition project ongoing
  - Daoism in modernity
    - Cultural Revolution devastation: temples destroyed; priests persecuted; texts burned; even worse than Buddhist destruction in some areas
    - PRC revival since 1980s: Chinese Daoist Association; temple restoration; Wudang Mountain tourism; state-approved ordinations; Zhèngyī and Quánzhēn recognized
    - Western reception: *Dàodéjīng* as one of most-translated texts in world; Fritjof Capra (*The Tao of Physics*, 1975); Alan Watts (*The Way of Zen*, includes extensive Daoism); Ursula K. Le Guin's *Dàodéjīng* rendition (1997); New Age appropriations (debated)
    - Academic study: Kristofer Schipper (*The Taoist Body*, 1993); Isabelle Robinet; Livia Kohn; Russell Kirkland (*Taoism: The Enduring Tradition*, 2004); Stephen Bokenkamp; Terry Kleeman; James Miller; Edward Davis

- **Shinto (Shintō 神道)**
  - Foundations and nature
    - Indigenous religion of Japan; "Way of the Kami (gods/spirits)"; no single founder, no single scripture, no systematic theology; animistic, polytheistic, nature-reverencing
    - Kami (神): divine spirits, forces, or essences present in nature, ancestors, remarkable phenomena; not "gods" in Western omnipotent sense; mountains, rivers, trees, rocks, storms, ancestors, emperors can be or house kami; ~8 million kami (yaoyorozu no kami — "eight million" = innumerably many)
    - Relationship with Buddhism: arrived 6th c. CE; shinbutsu-shūgō (kami-Buddha combinatory religion) from ~8th c.; honji suijaku theory (buddhas/bodhisattvas as true essence, kami as local manifestations); deep syncretism for ~1,000 years until forced Meiji separation
  - Key texts and mythology
    - **Kojiki** (712 CE, Record of Ancient Matters): Japan's oldest text; mythological origins of Japan and imperial lineage; compiled by Ō no Yasumaro from oral traditions
    - **Nihon Shoki / Nihongi** (720 CE, Chronicles of Japan): official court history with mythological preface; more Chinese-style historiography
    - Creation myth: primordial pair Izanagi and Izanami stir the ocean with jeweled spear, creating Japanese islands; birth of kami; Izanami dies giving birth to fire-kami; Izanagi's descent to underworld (Yomi) and return; purification → birth of **Amaterasu** (sun goddess, most important kami, ancestress of imperial line), Tsukuyomi (moon), and Susanoo (storm)
    - Amaterasu and the cave (Ama-no-Iwato): Amaterasu withdraws into cave (world plunges into darkness); kami lure her out with mirror, jewels, and dance (Ame-no-Uzume's dance — origin of sacred dance); Amaterasu sends grandson Ninigi to earth with three imperial regalia (sanshu no jingi: mirror, sword, jewel — still symbols of Japanese imperial legitimacy)
    - Norito (ritual prayers/liturgical texts): *Engishiki* (927 CE) collects official Shinto prayers
  - Shinto practice and institutions
    - Shrines (jinja 神社): ~80,000 in Japan; torii gates (iconic vermillion gateway marking sacred space); shimenawa (sacred rope); komainu (guardian lion-dogs); worship (sampai): clap twice, bow, offer prayer; seasonal festivals (matsuri); ritual purity (harae — purification) central
    - Major shrines: Ise Jingū (Grand Shrine of Ise, Mie Prefecture — dedicated to Amaterasu; rebuilt every 20 years since 7th c. — shikinen sengū; most sacred Shinto site); Izumo Taisha (Shimane — Ōkuninushi, kami of nation-building/relationships); Meiji Jingū (Tokyo); Fushimi Inari Taisha (Kyoto — 10,000 vermillion torii; Inari, kami of rice/prosperity/foxes)
    - Matsuri (festivals): Gion Matsuri (Kyoto, July — one of Japan's three great festivals); Awa Odori; Tenjin Matsuri; local shrine festivals throughout year; mikoshi (portable shrine carried through streets); kagura (sacred dance); sumo wrestling origins in Shinto festival ritual
    - Purity and pollution (kegare): death, blood, disease as sources of ritual impurity; purification (misogi — waterfall/ocean purification; harae — ritual cleansing; ōharae — great purification ceremony, June and December); not moral "sin" but ritual/natural pollution; this-worldly orientation
    - Priests (kannushi): hereditary or professional; wear distinctive garments (white robes, black caps); perform rituals; miko (shrine maidens) assist; no priestly celibacy requirement
  - Historical development
    - Ancient period: clan (uji) kami worship; each clan had tutelary kami (ujigami); imperial clan's kami (Amaterasu) became supreme as Yamato state unified Japan
    - Medieval syncretism: Ryōbu Shintō (Two-Aspect Shinto, Shingon Buddhist interpretation); Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō (Tendai Buddhist interpretation); kami and buddhas deeply intertwined
    - Intellectual Shinto: Yoshida Kanetomo (1435–1511: Yoshida Shintō — reversed honji suijaku, claimed kami are primary, buddhas are manifestations); Motoori Norinaga (1730–1801: greatest scholar of kokugaku/National Learning; purified Shinto from Buddhist and Confucian accretions; *Kojikiden* — 44-volume commentary on Kojiki; mono no aware — "the pathos of things"; Japanese aesthetic-spiritual sensibility); Hirata Atsutane (1776–1843: theological Shinto; afterlife doctrine; anti-Buddhist; influenced Meiji revolution)
    - **State Shinto** (1868–1945): Meiji government forcibly separated Shinto from Buddhism (shinbutsu bunri); elevated Shinto as state ideology; emperor as living kami; shrine system nationalized; kokka shintō (state Shinto) supported Japanese imperialism and militarism; Yasukuni Shrine (war dead enshrined as kami; includes convicted war criminals; prime ministerial visits provoke diplomatic crises with China and Korea)
    - Post-WWII: US-imposed separation of religion and state (Shinto Directive, 1945); Shinto became private religion; Association of Shinto Shrines (Jinja Honchō) coordinates; emperor renounced divinity (1946); Shinto reverted to decentralized, local shrine-based practice
  - Shinto new religions: Tenrikyō (Nakayama Miki, 1838; healing focus; ~2 million); Ōmoto (Deguchi Nao and Onisaburō, 1892; prophetic; influenced other new religions); Konkōkyō; many Japanese "new religions" (shinshūkyō) have Shinto roots or Shinto-Buddhist mixtures
  - Modern Shinto: ~80–110 million (Japanese census; many observe Shinto practices — hatsumōde/New Year shrine visit, shichi-go-san/children's festival — without identifying as "Shinto"; religion and culture blur); environmental Shinto (sacred forests, chinju no mori, conservation); academic study: Helen Hardacre (*Shintō: A History*, 2017); John Breen and Mark Teeuwen (*A New History of Shinto*, 2010); Bernhard Scheid

- **Indigenous and Traditional Religions Globally**
  - Scope and definitional challenges
    - Indigenous/traditional/primal/tribal religions: local, place-based, oral, communal, often animistic; not "primitive" — complex cosmological and ritual systems; 300+ million practitioners globally; enormous diversity
    - Terminology debates: "animism" revised (see Edward Tylor's 19th c. definition vs. Graham Harvey's "new animism" — relational, not about believing objects have souls but about being in relationship with other-than-human persons); "shamanism" (see below)
  - African Traditional Religions (ATR)
    - Enormous diversity: thousands of ethnic groups, each with distinct traditions; generalization risks distortion
    - Common features (with caution): supreme deity (often remote: Olodumare/Yoruba, Nyame/Akan, Amma/Dogon, Nzambi/Kongo, Mulungu/Bantu-speaking peoples); lesser spirits/divinities (orishas/Yoruba, abosom/Akan); ancestor veneration (vital communion with deceased who influence living); ritual specialists (diviners, healers, priests/priestesses)
    - **Yoruba religion** (Nigeria, Benin): Olodumare (supreme God); orishas (400+): Ògún (iron, war, technology), Ṣàngó (thunder, lightning, justice), Ọṣun (river, love, fertility), Yemọja (ocean, motherhood), Èṣù (trickster, messenger, crossroads), Ọbàtálá (creation, purity), Ọya (wind, death, rebirth); Ifá divination system (Babalawos/diviner-priests; 256 odù/figures; oral literary corpus — UNESCO Masterpiece of Intangible Heritage, 2005); elaborate priesthood; annual festivals; cosmology of àṣẹ (vital force/power)
    - Yoruba diaspora religions: Atlantic Slave Trade carried Yoruba religion to Americas → **Candomblé** (Brazil: Salvador da Bahia; orixás; synchronized with Catholic saints; terreiro as temple; initiated devotees; Pierre Verger's scholarship); **Santería/Lucumí** (Cuba, now US/Caribbean; orisha worship; Catholic syncretism under slavery; divination; animal sacrifice — US Supreme Court: Church of the Lukumi Babalu Aye v. City of Hialeah, 1993, protected); **Vodou/Voodoo** (Haiti: lwa/spirits; Fon/Dahomey + Kongo + Yoruba + Catholic elements; houngan/mambo priests; possession; demonized in Western media, actually sophisticated theological system; Karen McCarthy Brown, *Mama Lola*); Trinidad Orisha; Umbanda (Brazil: blend of Candomblé, Spiritism, Catholicism, indigenous elements)
    - **Akan religion** (Ghana, Côte d'Ivoire): Nyame/Onyame (supreme God); abosom (lesser deities); nsamanfoɔ (ancestors); sunsum (spirit), ɔkra (soul); proverbs and oral philosophy; Adinkra symbols; funeral customs; Kwame Gyekye (*African Cultural Values*)
    - **Dogon** (Mali): Amma (creator God); Nommo (primordial beings); complex cosmological system documented by Marcel Griaule (*Conversations with Ogotemmêli*, 1948 — groundbreaking but methodology now heavily critiqued; Walter van Beek's 1991 restudy challenged Griaule's ethnography); mask societies; Sigui ceremony (every 60 years)
    - Bantu-speaking peoples: ancestor veneration; concept of force vitale (Placide Tempels, *Bantu Philosophy*, 1945 — pioneering but paternalistic; launched "ethnophilosophy" debate); ubuntu philosophy ("I am because we are" — Desmond Tutu; relational ontology; now invoked in post-apartheid South African constitutional jurisprudence)
    - ATR and Christianity/Islam: complex syncretism, dual practice, African Initiated Churches (Kimbanguism, Zionism, Aladura); ATR revivalism; academic study: E. Bolaji Idowu, John Mbiti (*African Religions and Philosophy*, 1969), Kwasi Wiredu, Jacob Olupona
  - **Native American / First Nations / Indigenous Americas religions**
    - Enormous diversity: 500+ nations/tribes in North America alone; distinct languages, cosmologies, ritual systems
    - Common themes (with caution against universalizing): animate universe; spiritual power/medicine (orenda/Haudenosaunee, manitou/Algonquian, wakan/Lakota); vision quest; sweat lodge; pipe ceremony (sacred pipe/chanunpa — Lakota); Sun Dance (Plains peoples — Lakota, Cheyenne, Crow); kiva ceremonies (Pueblo peoples); potlatch (Northwest Coast — ceremonial gift-giving, identity-establishing); trickster figures (Coyote, Raven, Iktomi/Spider); creation stories (emergence myths — Navajo/Diné; earth-diver myths — widespread)
    - **Navajo/Diné**: Blessingway (Hózhóójí) ceremony; sand paintings (destroyed after healing ceremony); Changing Woman (Asdzą́ą́ Nádleehé); four sacred mountains; beauty way (hózhó); complex ceremonial system
    - **Lakota**: Wakan Tanka (Great Mystery); seven sacred rites (including Inipi/sweat lodge, Hanbleceya/vision quest, Wiwanyang Wacipi/Sun Dance); Black Elk (1863–1950): *Black Elk Speaks* (as told to John Neihardt, 1932 — classic but filtered through non-Native lens; controversy over accuracy); sacred Black Hills (He Sapa)
    - **Haudenosaunee (Iroquois)**: Great Law of Peace (Gayanashagowa); Thanksgiving Address; Longhouse religion; Handsome Lake (1735–1815: Gaiwiio/Good Message — revitalization movement)
    - Peyote religion / Native American Church (NAC): pan-tribal syncretic movement (~1880s onward); peyote (Lophophora williamsii — psychoactive cactus) as sacrament; prayer, singing, visions; legal battles for religious freedom (American Indian Religious Freedom Act 1978; Employment Division v. Smith 1990 — US Supreme Court ruled against peyote protection, leading to Religious Freedom Restoration Act 1993)
    - Mesoamerican contemporary: Maya spirituality revitalization (daykeeper traditions, calendar ceremonies, fire ceremonies); Aztec/Mexica revivalism (Mexicayotl)
    - Issues: forced assimilation history (boarding/residential schools — Canada, US, Australia: "Kill the Indian, save the man"; systematic cultural destruction; Truth and Reconciliation Commission Canada 2015; US boarding school report 2022); sacred site protection (Standing Rock, Bears Ears, Oak Flat/Chi'chil Biłdagoteel); repatriation of sacred objects (NAGPRA 1990 — Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act; 2023 strengthened regulations); cultural appropriation debates (non-Native sweat lodges, vision quests, "playing Indian")
  - **Australian Aboriginal religions**
    - Oldest continuous cultures on Earth (~65,000+ years of continuous habitation)
    - **Dreaming / Dreamtime (Jukurrpa, Tjukurpa)**: creation epoch when Ancestral Beings shaped the land, established law, created species; not past-only but ongoing, eternal; the land itself IS the religious text — every feature has narrative significance; songlines/dreaming tracks (paths traveled by Ancestral Beings; navigation; Bruce Chatwin's *The Songlines*, 1987 — popular but critiqued for romanticism)
    - Tjukurpa / Law (Anangu term): moral, religious, and social code inseparable from land; totemic system (person connected to specific dreaming ancestor/animal/plant); initiation ceremonies; men's and women's secret-sacred knowledge (restricted; gender-separated); ceremonies involve song, dance, body painting, sand painting
    - Sacred sites: Uluṛu (Ayers Rock — returned to Anangu ownership 1985; climbing banned 2019 at Anangu request); Kata Tjuṭa; Kakadu painted rock shelters (oldest rock art on Earth); significance of country — Aboriginal identity inseparable from specific land
    - Knowledge system: sophisticated ecological knowledge; fire-stick farming; astronomical knowledge (Bill Yidumduma Harney, Duane Hamacher — Aboriginal astronomy); complex kinship systems (moiety, subsection, skin name systems)
    - Dispossession and survival: terra nullius doctrine (overturned by Mabo v. Queensland 1992 — native title recognized); Stolen Generations (forced removal of children); ongoing land rights struggle; cultural revitalization; Uluru Statement from the Heart (2017)
    - Academic study: W. E. H. Stanner (*The Dreaming*, 1956; "Great Australian Silence" — Australian historians ignoring Aboriginal presence); Deborah Bird Rose (*Nourishing Terrains*, 1996); Howard Morphy; Fred Myers; Françoise Dussart
  - **Pacific Island / Polynesian religions**: mana (spiritual power; Marshall Sahlins, Roger Keesing debated interpretation); tapu/taboo (sacredness/restriction — English word "taboo" derives from Tongan "tabu"); cosmogonic chants (Hawaiian Kumulipo; Māori Ranginui-Papatūānuku creation); elaborate genealogies (whakapapa/Māori); tohunga (priests/specialists); marae/heiau (sacred gathering places); Te Whiti o Rongomai (Māori pacifist resistance); Hawaiian sovereignty movement; Māori renaissance
  - **Siberian and Central Asian shamanism**: shamanism as academic concept originates from Tungus (Evenki) "šaman"; spirit communication, ecstatic trance, soul journeys; drumming; Mircea Eliade (*Shamanism: Archaic Techniques of Ecstasy*, 1964 — foundational but critiqued for universalizing; romanticizing); Mongolian shamanism revival (post-Soviet); Buryat, Tuvan, Sakha (Yakut) traditions; neoshamanism in West (Michael Harner's "core shamanism" — criticized as cultural appropriation/decontextualization)

- **New Religious Movements (NRMs)**
  - Definitional issues: "new" relative to established religions; "cult" as pejorative vs. sociological term (Eileen Barker, INFORM; Rodney Stark, William Sims Bainbridge — sociology of NRMs; "cult" → "NRM" in academic usage)
  - **Bahá'í Faith**: Bahá'u'lláh (1817–1892, Iran): prophet-founder; progressive revelation (all religions are stages of one unfolding truth); unity of God, unity of religion, unity of humanity; no clergy; elected administrative bodies (Local/National Spiritual Assemblies → Universal House of Justice, Haifa); ~5–8 million adherents globally; persecuted in Iran (execution, imprisonment, denial of education — Bahá'í Institute for Higher Education operates underground); Lotus Temple (Delhi); Wilmette Temple; strong presence in Africa, Pacific Islands, India
  - **Latter-day Saints (Mormonism)**: Joseph Smith (1805–1844): Book of Mormon (1830, claimed angelic revelation); Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS); Brigham Young → Utah; ~17 million members; temple ordinances, genealogical research, missionary program; theology: God has physical body; eternal progression; plural marriage (discontinued 1890); Reorganized (Community of Christ) split; academic study: Terryl Givens, Richard Bushman
  - **Jehovah's Witnesses**: Charles Taze Russell (1852–1916); Watchtower Bible and Tract Society; non-Trinitarian; apocalyptic; refuse blood transfusions, military service; ~8.7 million; door-to-door proselytizing; shunning of ex-members (disfellowshipping); legal contributions to religious freedom law (US Supreme Court cases on free speech — Cantwell v. Connecticut 1940, Barnette 1943)
  - **Seventh-day Adventism**: Millerite origins; Ellen G. White (1827–1915, prophetess); Saturday Sabbath; health emphasis (vegetarianism, hospitals, Loma Linda University/Blue Zone); ~22 million
  - **Cao Đài** (Vietnam, 1926): Victor Hugo, Sun Yat-sen, and Joan of Arc as saints; syncretic (Buddhism, Daoism, Confucianism, Christianity, Spiritism); elaborate hierarchy mirroring Catholic structure; Tây Ninh Holy See; ~3–5 million
  - **Tenrikyo** (Japan, 1838): Nakayama Miki; God the Parent (Tenri-Ō-no-Mikoto); joyous life; hinokishin (daily service); Tenri City headquarters; ~1.5–2 million
  - **Sōka Gakkai International**: lay Nichiren Buddhist movement; Daisaku Ikeda (1928–2023); value creation philosophy; Kōmeitō political party (Japan); ~12 million+ claimed; controversy over aggressive proselytizing and financial practices
  - **Scientology**: L. Ron Hubbard (1911–1986, science fiction writer); Dianetics (1950); auditing; thetans; Operating Thetan levels; Church of Scientology (1954); Sea Org; persistent allegations of abuse, financial exploitation, stalking of critics; legal battles; David Miscavige (current leader); tax-exempt in US (1993 IRS agreement), not recognized as religion in many countries; scholarly treatment: Hugh Urban (*The Church of Scientology*, 2011)
  - **Unification Church** (Moonies/Family Federation for World Peace): Sun Myung Moon (1920–2012, Korea); mass weddings; anti-communism; extensive business empire; controversies over authoritarian leadership, financial exploitation; influence in US politics
  - **Wicca / Contemporary Paganism**: Gerald Gardner (1884–1964, Wicca founder, 1954); Doreen Valiente; Starhawk (*The Spiral Dance*, 1979); reconstructionist polytheisms (Ásatrú/Heathenry — Norse; Hellenismos — Greek; Kemeticism — Egyptian); nature religion; seasonal festivals (Wheel of the Year); Goddess spirituality; Ronald Hutton (*The Triumph of the Moon*, 1999 — critical history of Wicca)
  - **Rastafari**: Jamaica, 1930s; Marcus Garvey's Black nationalism + Ethiopian Emperor Haile Selassie I as divine; ital livity; dreadlocks; sacramental ganja; repatriation to Africa; reggae music (Bob Marley); Ennis Edmonds (*Rastafari: A Very Short Introduction*)

- **Gnosticism and Esotericism**
  - Ancient Gnosticism
    - Diverse early Christian and Jewish heterodox movements (~1st–4th c. CE); "gnosis" (γνῶσις) = salvific knowledge of divine reality and the self's origin
    - Common Gnostic themes: material world as flawed or evil creation by a lesser deity (Demiurge, often identified with Hebrew God/Yaldabaoth); true God is utterly transcendent; divine spark trapped in human body; salvation through gnosis (self-knowledge = knowledge of divine origin); secret/esoteric teachings from Jesus or other revealers
    - Major Gnostic systems: **Valentinianism** (Valentinus, ~100–165 CE: sophisticated Pleroma of divine aeons; Sophia's fall; most influential; Marcus, Ptolemy); **Sethianism** (Seth as progenitor of spiritual race; *Apocryphon of John*, *Gospel of Judas*); Basilides; Marcion (sometimes classified separately; radical dualism; rejected Hebrew Bible entirely; New Testament-only canon → prompted Orthodox canon formation); Mandaeism (surviving Gnostic religion — Mandaeans in Iraq/Iran; John the Baptist revered; ~60,000–100,000; embattled; UNESCO endangered heritage; Jorunn Buckley scholarship)
    - **Nag Hammadi Library** (discovered 1945, Upper Egypt): 13 codices, ~52 texts in Coptic; revolutionized Gnosticism studies (previously known mainly through heresiologists' attacks — Irenaeus, Hippolytus, Epiphanius); key texts: *Gospel of Thomas* (114 sayings of Jesus; possibly independent of Synoptics; dated by some scholars to 1st c. CE; Helmut Koester, April DeConick); *Gospel of Philip*; *Gospel of Truth* (possibly by Valentinus); *Apocryphon of John*; *Thunder, Perfect Mind*; *Hypostasis of the Archons*
    - **Manichaeism**: Mani (216–274/277 CE, Mesopotamia); radical dualism (Light vs. Darkness); cosmic mythology; elect and hearers; once a world religion (spread from North Africa to China); Augustine was Manichaean before converting to Christianity; persecuted into extinction; revived awareness through manuscript discoveries (Cologne Mani Codex, Chinese Manichaean texts, frescoes at Bezeklik)
    - Academic study: Elaine Pagels (*The Gnostic Gospels*, 1979 — popular classic); Karen King (*What Is Gnosticism?*, 2003; *The Gospel of Mary of Magdala*); Michael Williams (*Rethinking "Gnosticism"*, 1996 — challenged "Gnosticism" as coherent category); Birger Pearson; David Brakke
  - Western Esotericism
    - Academic field: Wouter Hanegraaff (Amsterdam chair); Kocku von Stuckrad; Antoine Faivre (defined Western esotericism through six characteristics: correspondences, living nature, imagination/mediations, transmutation, concordance, transmission); now established academic discipline
    - **Hermeticism**: attributed to Hermes Trismegistus (legendary figure, fusion of Greek Hermes and Egyptian Thoth); *Corpus Hermeticum* (2nd–3rd c. CE, Alexandria): philosophical-mystical cosmology; "As above, so below"; deification of the mind (nous); heavily influenced Renaissance thought (Marsilio Ficino translated *Corpus Hermeticum* into Latin, 1463 — launched Hermetic revival; Giordano Bruno; Pico della Mirandola; eroded by Isaac Casaubon's 1614 dating proving texts were post-Christian, not ancient Egyptian)
    - **Kabbalah** (Jewish mysticism; covered in Judaism section): Sefirot, Ein Sof, Zohar; Christian Kabbalah (Pico, Reuchlin, Agrippa); Hermetic Qabalah (Golden Dawn reinterpretation)
    - **Alchemy**: "great work" (opus magnum); transmutation of base metals to gold as spiritual allegory; Jabir ibn Hayyan (Islamic alchemy); Paracelsus (1493–1541: iatrochemistry, spagyrics); Flamel legend; Jung's psychological interpretation (*Psychology and Alchemy*, 1944)
    - **Renaissance magic and Neoplatonism**: Marsilio Ficino (1433–1499); Giovanni Pico della Mirandola (1463–1494: *Oration on the Dignity of Man*); Cornelius Agrippa (*De occulta philosophia*, 1533); John Dee (1527–1608/9: Enochian magic, angel communication, advisor to Elizabeth I)
    - **Rosicrucianism**: manifestos (1614–1616: *Fama Fraternitatis*, *Confessio Fraternitatis*, *Chemical Wedding of Christian Rosenkreutz*); secret brotherhood of illuminated adepts (probably literary fiction/utopian ideal); influenced Freemasonry and later esoteric orders
    - **Freemasonry**: medieval stonemason guilds → speculative Masonry (1717, Grand Lodge of England); initiatory degrees; allegorical ritual; deism; Enlightenment connections; enormous 18th–19th c. social/political influence; conspiracy theories (Illuminati); academic study: Jessica Harland-Jacobs, Jan Snoek
