    - Paul Otlet and Henri La Fontaine: International Institute of Bibliography; Universal Decimal Classification (UDC); *Traité de Documentation* (1934); Répertoire Bibliographique Universel; proto-hypertext and networked knowledge visions such as the Mundaneum
    - Suzanne Briet (*Qu'est-ce que la documentation?*, 1951): broadened document theory through the famous antelope example; documents are socially and institutionally constituted
  - **Information science formation after WWII**
    - Vannevar Bush, "As We May Think" (1945): memex, associative trails, precursor vision for hypertext and personal information systems
    - Wartime and postwar information explosion drove machine indexing, abstracting, scientific documentation, and retrieval systems
    - Calvin Mooers coined "information retrieval" (1950s); Mooers' Law: systems that are painful to use will be avoided
    - Hans Peter Luhn: KWIC indexing, selective dissemination of information, early automatic abstracting, business intelligence uses of text
    - Mortimer Taube: uniterm coordinate indexing; machine searching before full-text systems
    - Cranfield experiments (Cyril Cleverdon): precision and recall, test collections, relevance judgments; basis of modern IR evaluation
    - Eugene Garfield: Science Citation Index, citation indexing, impact factor; transformed scholarly communication metrics and literature discovery
    - Gerard Salton: SMART system, vector space model, tf-idf, automatic indexing, ranked retrieval; foundational modern IR figure
  - **Late 20th century to digital era**
    - MARC (Machine-Readable Cataloging, Henriette Avram, 1960s) enabled networked catalog exchange and integrated library systems
    - OCLC (founded 1967) scaled cooperative cataloging and WorldCat as global bibliographic infrastructure
    - OPACs replaced card catalogs; federated search, discovery layers, link resolvers, and digital repositories followed
    - Internet, web search, digitization, institutional repositories, open access, and research data mandates reoriented LIS toward digital stewardship and information infrastructures
    - Contemporary LIS spans not only libraries and archives but platforms, data commons, machine-actionable metadata, algorithmic ranking, digital preservation, and AI-mediated discovery

- **Foundational Thinkers, Schools, and Canonical Works**
  - **Librarianship and bibliography**
    - Melvil Dewey: decimal classification, library standardization, training, efficiency movement
    - Charles Ammi Cutter: catalog objectives, dictionary catalog logic, author-title-subject access
    - S. R. Ranganathan: among the most important LIS theorists; Five Laws of Library Science (1931): books are for use; every reader his or her book; every book its reader; save the time of the reader; the library is a growing organism; Colon Classification; faceted classification; chain indexing; deeply influential on modern knowledge organization and user-centered service design
    - Jesse Hauk Shera: social epistemology, public knowledge systems, librarianship as part of knowledge organization in society
    - Margaret Egan and Jesse Shera: social epistemology as LIS framework connecting knowledge production, distribution, and use
    - Patrick Wilson: bibliographic control, authority, cognitive authority, *Two Kinds of Power* (1968), *Second-hand Knowledge* (1983)
    - Michael Buckland: information-as-thing, library services, document theory, history of information science
  - **Documentation and information science**
    - Paul Otlet: universal bibliography, documentation, networked knowledge architecture
    - Suzanne Briet: document theory
    - Vannevar Bush: associative retrieval imagination
    - Calvin Mooers, Hans Peter Luhn, Mortimer Taube, Gerard Salton, Karen Spärck Jones, Cyril Cleverdon, Marcia Bates, Nicholas Belkin, Tefko Saracevic, Birger Hjørland, Peter Ingwersen, Nicholas J. Belkin, Stephen Robertson: core information science and IR figures
    - Karen Spärck Jones: inverse document frequency, automatic thesaurus construction, natural language processing influence on retrieval
    - Stephen Robertson: probabilistic retrieval, BM25 ranking model
    - Marcia Bates: berrypicking, information search tactics, evolving information needs, design from user behavior rather than idealized queries
    - Nicholas Belkin: anomalous state of knowledge (ASK) model
    - Tefko Saracevic: relevance, information science foundations, human-centered IR
    - Birger Hjørland: domain analysis, socio-cognitive theory, epistemology of knowledge organization
    - Peter Ingwersen: cognitive IR, polyrepresentation
  - **Archival science and records**
    - Hilary Jenkinson: archives as impartial evidence, sanctity of record, archivist as neutral custodian; highly influential but later challenged
    - Theodore Schellenberg: records appraisal for evidential vs. informational value; active records management orientation
    - Ernst Posner, Ernst Bernheim, Luciana Duranti, Terry Cook, Hugh Taylor, Verne Harris, Randall Jimerson, Michelle Caswell, Jeannette Bastian, Eric Ketelaar: central archival theorists
    - Terry Cook: postcustodial and macroappraisal, archivist as active shaper rather than neutral keeper
    - Luciana Duranti: diplomatics, authenticity, electronic records, InterPARES project
    - Michelle Caswell and community archives scholars: memory, power, representation, affect, and justice in archival practice

- **Knowledge Organization: Classification, Cataloging, and Metadata**
  - **Classification systems**
    - Dewey Decimal Classification: hierarchical decimal notation; practical global dominance in public and school libraries; Christian and Eurocentric structuring long criticized and gradually revised
    - Library of Congress Classification: discipline-based arrangement optimized for large research collections; less philosophically systematic than DDC but operationally dominant in academic libraries in the US
    - Universal Decimal Classification: faceted extensibility, analytico-synthetic notation, strong documentation tradition
    - Colon Classification: Ranganathan's faceted system using personality, matter, energy, space, time (PMEST) categories; foundational for facet theory and modern metadata design
    - Bliss Bibliographic Classification (BC2), Colon, and faceted systems influenced information architecture, thesauri, and ontologies
    - Domain-specific vocabularies: Medical Subject Headings (MeSH), Art & Architecture Thesaurus (AAT), ERIC descriptors, INSPEC classification, Chemical Abstracts indexing
  - **Cataloging theory**
    - Cutter's catalog objectives: find, collocate, choose; still foundational to discovery design
    - AACR and AACR2 shaped Anglo-American descriptive cataloging through the late 20th century
    - RDA (Resource Description and Access): entity-relationship-informed successor to AACR2 designed for linked-data environments and flexible resource description
    - FRBR, FRAD, FRSAD, then IFLA LRM: work, expression, manifestation, item and related entities; shifted bibliographic thinking toward conceptual modeling
    - ISBD, authority records, name disambiguation, corporate bodies, uniform titles, controlled vocabularies, subject access, genre/form terms
    - BIBFRAME as linked-data successor to MARC; efforts to transition bibliographic infrastructure to graph-oriented web standards remain incomplete and operationally difficult
  - **Metadata ecosystems**
    - Descriptive metadata: title, creator, subject, abstract, identifiers
    - Administrative metadata: acquisition, rights, provenance, ownership, access conditions
    - Technical metadata: file formats, checksums, capture devices, compression, software dependencies
    - Structural metadata: relationships among files and objects, page order, hierarchy, sequencing
    - Preservation metadata: PREMIS, fixity, event logs, migration history, authenticity evidence
    - Common schemas: Dublin Core, MODS, METS, EAD, MARC21, VRA Core, TEI, DataCite, schema.org, IIIF manifests
    - Metadata politics: every schema encodes assumptions about identity, genre, culture, legitimacy, and discoverability; metadata work is never neutral
  - **Subject analysis and vocabulary control**
    - Controlled vocabularies, taxonomies, ontologies, thesauri, folksonomies, synonym rings, crosswalks, authority files
    - Faceted navigation and post-coordinate indexing reflect attempts to balance precision with user flexibility
    - Critiques of LCSH and classification bias: Sanford Berman's activism; problematic headings around race, sexuality, religion, and colonial categories; long campaign around changing "Illegal aliens" heading
    - Indigenous knowledge organization and culturally responsive metadata challenge universalist Western schemes

- **Information Retrieval and Search**
  - **Foundations of retrieval theory**
    - Boolean retrieval: exact-match querying, logical operators, field restriction; still vital in databases, legal research, archives, and technical search
    - Vector space model: documents and queries as weighted term vectors; cosine similarity; tf-idf weighting
    - Probabilistic retrieval: ranking by likelihood of relevance; Robertson and Sparck Jones; BM25 as durable baseline
    - Language models for IR: query likelihood, smoothing, probabilistic term generation; large role in web search and academic IR
    - Relevance feedback, query expansion, thesaurus-assisted retrieval, pseudo-relevance feedback, interactive retrieval, session search
  - **Evaluation and metrics**
    - Precision, recall, fallout, F-measure, mean average precision, normalized discounted cumulative gain, reciprocal rank, novelty/diversity metrics
    - Test collections and relevance judgments originated in Cranfield paradigm and continue in TREC, CLEF, NTCIR, FIRE, and domain evaluation campaigns
    - Laboratory evaluation vs. real-world search: known-item search, exploratory search, search-as-learning, serendipity, user satisfaction, trust, and explainability
  - **Web search and discovery systems**
    - Citation analysis and hyperlinks shaped web ranking; PageRank's rise connected bibliometrics and web graph analysis
    - Search engine interfaces mediate knowledge exposure through autocomplete, snippets, ranking, personalization, localization, and safe-search filters
    - Discovery layers in libraries aggregate catalogs, article indexes, repositories, and digital collections through centralized indexes and relevance ranking
    - Federated search vs. indexed discovery; link resolvers and knowledge bases support access across licensed resources
    - Current issues: algorithmic opacity, SEO manipulation, platform lock-in, multilingual retrieval, misinformation, and bias in ranking

- **Information Behavior and User Studies**
  - **Major frameworks**
    - Information need, seeking, searching, encountering, use, avoidance, sharing, and literacy as distinct but interrelated processes
    - Thomas D. Wilson: nested models of information behavior and barriers
    - Brenda Dervin's sense-making: people bridge gaps in situations by constructing meaning; deeply influential qualitative framework
    - Carol Kuhlthau's Information Search Process (ISP): uncertainty, optimism, confusion, formulation, confidence; emotional and cognitive stages of research
    - Marcia Bates's berrypicking: search evolves iteratively through cues, browsing, and shifting needs rather than single-query optimization
    - Elfreda Chatman: small worlds, information poverty, everyday life information seeking, social norms and secrecy in marginalized communities
    - Reijo Savolainen: everyday life information seeking (ELIS); way of life and mastery of life as structuring concepts
  - **User communities**
    - Children and youth information behavior, school library learning, media literacy, adolescent search and safety
    - Scholars and scientists: literature review practices, citation chaining, data reuse, preprints, lab communication, open notebook practices
    - Patients and health seekers: health literacy, online misinformation, support communities, stigma and disclosure, patient portals
    - Workers and professionals: workplace information use, boundary objects, records, compliance systems, dashboards, tacit knowledge, enterprise search
    - Marginalized communities: information precarity, surveillance harms, language access, disability access, prison libraries, refugee information needs, community memory work
  - **Information literacy**
    - Traditional library instruction moved from bibliographic orientation toward research strategy, source evaluation, algorithmic literacy, and critical information literacy
    - ACRL Framework for Information Literacy for Higher Education (2015): authority is constructed and contextual, information creation as process, information has value, research as inquiry, scholarship as conversation, searching as strategic exploration
    - Critical information literacy challenges neutral-skills models and addresses power, infrastructure, capitalism, coloniality, and platform influence on knowledge

- **Librarianship and Library Types**
  - **Public libraries**
    - Mission: universal access, literacy, civic inclusion, lifelong learning, community programming, digital access, and local memory
    - Services: lending, reference, makerspaces, job support, language learning, citizenship resources, social service referrals, early childhood programming, community archives, digital media labs
    - Public library history tied to philanthropy, municipal governance, working-class education, segregation, labor politics, and austerity
    - Contemporary debates: book challenges and censorship, homelessness and social work partnerships, digital divide mitigation, security, community-led librarianship, platform lending terms for ebooks
  - **Academic and research libraries**
    - Collection development, subject liaison work, instruction, scholarly communication support, institutional repositories, digitization, licensing, data management plans, bibliometrics support
    - Shift from ownership to access through consortial licensing and big deals; later pushback through transformative agreements and open access strategies
    - Research support includes systematic review methods, digital humanities consulting, GIS, data services, ORCID and identity management, and scholarly publishing support
  - **School libraries**
    - Information literacy, reading culture, curriculum integration, youth media literacy, learning commons models, teacher-librarianship, equity of access
    - Research consistently links credentialed school librarians and well-funded library programs to literacy and academic outcomes, though policy support varies sharply
  - **Special libraries**
    - Medical, legal, corporate, government, news, museum, military, patent, and technical libraries emphasize domain-specific retrieval, current awareness, and high-stakes evidence use
    - Special librarianship intersects strongly with records management, taxonomy design, knowledge management, and enterprise search
  - **National libraries and legal deposit**
    - National bibliography, legal deposit, cultural heritage preservation, ISBN/ISSN assignment roles, authority infrastructure, web archiving, national memory projects

- **Reference, Information Services, and Instruction**
  - Samuel Swett Green (1876) associated with formal reference service tradition; reference as mediation between users and collections
  - Reference interview theory: uncover actual information need through dialogue; approachable neutrality, context gathering, question negotiation
  - Ready reference, research consultation, virtual reference, chat reference, embedded librarianship, expert searching
  - Reader's advisory: matching readers with texts, genres, moods, interests; now extends to media advisory and algorithm-aware recommendation contexts
  - Instructional roles: database searching, source evaluation, citation management, research design, data management, scholarly identity, copyright and fair use guidance
  - Accessibility and inclusion: universal design, assistive technologies, multilingual services, plain-language guidance, trauma-informed service models

- **Archival Science and Records Management**
  - **Archival principles**
    - Provenance: records of a creator should be kept together to preserve context
    - Respect des fonds and original order: arrangement should preserve relationships established by creator activity where meaningful
    - Appraisal: determining what to keep, destroy, transfer, or prioritize; no archive can preserve everything
    - Arrangement and description: from fonds to series to file to item; finding aids, scope notes, administrative histories, access restrictions
    - Authenticity, reliability, integrity, and usability: especially central for digital records
  - **Appraisal theories and debates**
    - Jenkinsonian custodial neutrality vs. Schellenbergian active appraisal
    - Macroappraisal (Terry Cook): appraise functions and societal structures rather than simply selecting from surviving records at low level
    - Documentation strategy: coordinated appraisal across institutions to document complex social phenomena
    - Participatory and community-centered appraisal: communities should shape what is preserved and how it is described
    - Silences in archives: Michel-Rolph Trouillot, Verne Harris, Saidiya Hartman-adjacent archival critique; archives contain absences produced by power
  - **Records continuum and digital records**
    - Australian records continuum model treats creation, capture, organization, and pluralization as simultaneous rather than linear lifecycle stages
    - Electronic records management: retention schedules, email archiving, born-digital records, authenticity evidence, audit trails, digital signatures, records governance
    - Standards: ISO 15489 for records management, OAIS as preservation framework, MoReq in European contexts, InterPARES for digital authenticity
  - **Community archives and memory justice**
    - Community archives preserve marginalized histories outside state and elite institutions; examples include Black archives, queer archives, migrant archives, neighborhood archives, activist media archives
    - Reparative description and anti-oppressive metadata challenge harmful legacy language while preserving discoverability and evidence chains

- **Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Data Curation**
  - **Digital libraries**
    - Digital libraries combine digitized and born-digital content, metadata, interfaces, storage, preservation, and user services
    - Major initiatives: Project Gutenberg, JSTOR, HathiTrust, Europeana, Digital Public Library of America (DPLA), Internet Archive, Gallica, Trove, World Digital Library, Google Books
    - Architecture involves object storage, metadata stores, search indexes, persistent identifiers, OCR pipelines, image servers, rights management, and preservation workflows
    - IIIF transformed interoperability for images and manuscripts; annotation layers and deep zoom support scholarly reuse
  - **Institutional and disciplinary repositories**
    - DSpace, EPrints, Fedora, Samvera, Invenio, Dataverse, Zenodo, arXiv, bioRxiv, SSRN, PubMed Central, institutional repositories for theses, articles, datasets, preprints, and gray literature
    - Persistent identifiers: DOI, ORCID, ROR, Handle, ARK, ISBN, ISSN, ISNI; crucial for discovery, citation, linking, and machine readability
    - Open Archives Initiative Protocol for Metadata Harvesting (OAI-PMH) enabled repository interoperability; later complemented by APIs and linked data practices
  - **Research data management and curation**
    - Data management plans, FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable), CARE principles for Indigenous data governance, repository deposit, metadata documentation, file normalization, anonymization, access controls
    - Data curation lifecycle: create, process, describe, preserve, provide access, enable reuse, document provenance, monitor integrity
    - Sensitive data stewardship: human subjects data, genomic data, geospatial risk, indigenous knowledge, confidential business and government records
    - Research software preservation and reproducibility increasingly treated as adjacent LIS challenge

- **Preservation and Conservation**
  - **Physical preservation**
    - Conservation of paper, parchment, bindings, photographs, audiovisual carriers, magnetic media, microfilm, artifacts, and mixed-media collections
    - Environmental controls: temperature, humidity, light, pests, storage materials, disaster preparedness
    - Mass deacidification, brittle books, conservation ethics, minimal intervention, reversibility, documentation of treatments
  - **Digital preservation**
    - Bit-level preservation: fixity checks, redundancy, distributed storage, checksums, replication, LOCKSS and CLOCKSS
    - Logical preservation: format migration, emulation, normalization, dependency tracking, software preservation, rendering fidelity
    - OAIS reference model: ingest, archival storage, data management, administration, preservation planning, access
    - Trusted digital repositories: certification, audit, documentation, policy frameworks, sustainability planning
    - Web archiving: crawlers, seed lists, scope rules, robots exclusions, replay systems, legal and ethical issues, dynamic content challenges
  - **Preservation challenges**
    - Link rot, reference rot, proprietary formats, cloud dependence, DRM, platform collapse, disappearing local news, at-risk activist media, and storage sustainability
    - Preservation of games, social media, virtual worlds, livestreams, and AI-generated content remains especially difficult because of interactivity, rights constraints, and infrastructural dependence

- **Scholarly Communication, Bibliometrics, and Open Knowledge**
  - **Scholarly communication systems**
    - Journal publishing, peer review, monographs, conferences, preprints, data publication, registered reports, overlay journals, open notebooks, post-publication review
    - Serials crisis: rising journal subscription costs and concentration among major commercial publishers; library consortia negotiations and cancellations
    - Open access routes: gold, green, diamond, bronze; APCs, transformative agreements, Plan S, institutional mandates, rights retention strategies
    - Copyright and licensing in scholarship: author rights, institutional repositories, Creative Commons, text and data mining exceptions, open educational resources
  - **Bibliometrics and scientometrics**
    - Citation indexing: Garfield and SCI; later Web of Science, Scopus, Google Scholar, Dimensions, OpenAlex
    - Metrics: impact factor, h-index, Eigenfactor, CiteScore, immediacy index, field-weighted citation impact, usage metrics, altmetrics
    - Co-citation, bibliographic coupling, science mapping, author networks, topic evolution, collaboration patterns, open citation infrastructure
    - Critiques: Goodhart's law, metric gaming, field inequities, English-language bias, disciplinary mismatch, prestige capture, evaluation distortions
    - DORA, Leiden Manifesto, and HuMetricsHSS advocate more responsible research assessment and values-based evaluation

- **Knowledge Management and Information Systems**
  - Knowledge management overlaps LIS through taxonomy design, enterprise search, records governance, expertise location, intranets, digital asset management, and organizational memory
  - Explicit vs. tacit knowledge debates: Michael Polanyi background; Nonaka and Takeuchi's SECI model in management literature; LIS adapts these ideas through documentation and retrieval infrastructures
  - Enterprise content management, document management systems, records compliance, legal hold, e-discovery, retention automation, and workflow design link LIS with corporate and governmental information governance
  - Taxonomy and ontology work in organizations supports findability, compliance, analytics, and interoperability across siloed systems

- **Information Ethics, Policy, and Justice**
  - **Foundational concerns**
    - Access to information as democratic infrastructure; public libraries and archives as institutions of citizenship, education, and memory
    - Intellectual freedom: American Library Association's Library Bill of Rights, freedom to read, anti-censorship principles, confidentiality of reading records
    - Privacy in libraries and archives: borrowing records, search logs, patron data, vendor analytics, law enforcement access, surveillance technologies
    - Copyright vs. access, licensing restrictions, DRM, controlled digital lending debates, e-book monopolies, accessibility exceptions, orphan works
  - **Critical and justice-oriented LIS**
    - Critical librarianship (#critlib): neutrality critique, labor issues, whiteness in librarianship, anti-racist metadata, pedagogy, community accountability
    - Information justice and data justice: uneven visibility, discoverability, categorization harm, surveillance disparities, algorithmic redlining, infrastructural exclusion
    - Indigenous data sovereignty: OCAP and CARE principles, tribal archives, culturally restricted knowledge, traditional knowledge labels (Local Contexts)
    - Feminist and queer archival practices: affect, intimacy, embodiment, reparative description, refusal of extractive institutional frameworks
    - Carceral information studies: prison libraries, censorship, informational deprivation, datafied punishment, records-based exclusion
  - **Policy domains**
    - Freedom of information laws, public records access, classification and secrecy, state archives, records disposal scandals, whistleblower records
    - Net neutrality, platform regulation, AI governance, digital accessibility, disability law, educational access, broadband policy, procurement and preservation mandates

- **Library and Information Infrastructures**
  - Integrated library systems (ILS) and library services platforms (LSP): acquisitions, circulation, serials, cataloging, patron management, analytics
  - Discovery systems, knowledge bases, authentication, proxy access, federated identity, usage statistics, COUNTER standards, SUSHI harvesting
  - Cooperative infrastructures: OCLC, consortia, union catalogs, interlibrary loan (ILLiad, resource sharing), shared print, HathiTrust, preservation networks
  - Open-source ecosystem: Koha, Evergreen, VuFind, Blacklight, ArchivesSpace, Islandora, Omeka, CollectiveAccess; governance and sustainability matter as much as code
  - Infrastructure politics: vendor consolidation, lock-in, subscription platforms, data extraction, interoperability failures, and labor required to maintain local systems

- **Advanced / Specialist Areas**
  - Legal informatics and e-discovery: document review, relevance ranking, privilege detection, records holds, chain of custody, TAR (technology-assisted review)
  - Health informatics interfaces with LIS through controlled vocabularies, patient information, evidence synthesis, clinical librarianship, and knowledge translation
  - Museum informatics and GLAM integration: galleries, libraries, archives, and museums share metadata, preservation, rights, and discovery challenges but differ in object ontology and use cases
  - Digital humanities and cultural analytics rely heavily on LIS infrastructures, metadata labor, OCR correction, repository design, and preservation expertise
  - Government information and civic records: legal deposit, declassification, public records, transparency, secrecy, and administrative memory
  - School of information expansions: human-computer interaction, UX research, policy informatics, data science, platform studies, and socio-technical systems scholarship

- **Frontier / Contemporary Research in Library & Information Science (2020–present)**
  - **Linked data and bibliographic transition**
    - MARC-to-BIBFRAME transition remains incomplete; major research questions concern interoperability, entity resolution, multilingual knowledge graphs, and migration cost
    - Knowledge graphs, Wikidata integration, authority reconciliation, and machine-assisted metadata creation are reshaping cataloging workflows
  - **AI in libraries, archives, and discovery**
    - Generative AI is being tested for metadata suggestion, summarization, transcription, conversational discovery, reference support, and backlog processing
    - Frontier questions: hallucination control, provenance labeling, bias replication, explainability, copyright exposure, and whether AI-assisted description can meet professional and legal standards
    - Human-in-the-loop archival description, OCR correction, handwriting recognition for manuscripts, and audiovisual transcription are rapidly expanding areas
  - **Platform dependency and digital preservation risk**
    - Scholarship, news, and cultural memory increasingly reside on commercial clouds, proprietary formats, and unstable platforms; LIS research now treats platform collapse as a preservation problem
    - Social media archiving, news archive continuity, creator-content preservation, and restrictions on API access are key practical challenges
  - **Open science and reproducibility infrastructures**
    - Research data services now include notebook preservation, code deposit, software citation, provenance capture, reproducibility review, and support for computational notebooks
    - Open infrastructure movement responds to dependence on commercial scholarly platforms through nonprofit, community-governed alternatives
  - **Reparative description and decolonizing infrastructures**
    - Institutions are revising harmful subject headings, legacy descriptions, and colonial provenance narratives while preserving historical traceability
    - Debates continue over how to balance evidence of historical language with non-harmful access points for current users
  - **Information disorder and epistemic infrastructure**
    - LIS contributes to misinformation research through source evaluation, authority models, community information resilience, search design, and digital literacy interventions
    - Public libraries increasingly function as civic resilience institutions amid conspiracy cultures, election denialism, and health misinformation
  - **Climate, disaster, and sustainability**
    - Climate risk to collections, energy-intensive digital storage, disaster preparedness, salvage networks, and community archives after wildfire, flood, and war are major research fronts
    - Preservation planning now includes environmental sustainability, resilient infrastructure, and relocation strategies for threatened heritage collections
  - **Open problems and live debates**
    - Whether universal classification is achievable or whether plural, community-specific ontologies should replace it in many settings
    - How to preserve born-digital and platform-dependent culture at scale without violating privacy, copyright, or community norms
    - How to design discovery systems that surface marginalized knowledge without flattening context or reproducing ranking bias
    - How libraries and archives should govern AI tools while preserving trust, privacy, and professional accountability
    - How to fund long-term public information infrastructure outside extractive vendor models

---

## SOCIAL SCIENCES

### Anthropology
- **Basics**
  - **Scope of the field**
    - Anthropology is the comparative, holistic study of humanity across time and space, integrating culture, biology, language, material remains, kinship, ecology, power, and history
    - Core four-field model in the US: cultural anthropology, biological anthropology, linguistic anthropology, and archaeology; with many regional traditions organizing the field differently
    - Central questions: what makes humans human, how do human lifeways vary, how are societies organized, how do biology and culture interact, how do material remains reveal past worlds, and how are power and inequality reproduced or contested?
    - Persistent tensions: holism vs. specialization, emic vs. etic interpretation, relativism vs. universalism, science vs. humanistic interpretation, colonial legacy vs. decolonizing practice, comparison vs. historical particularity
    - Major objects of study: kinship, ritual, subsistence, exchange, language, embodiment, environment, material culture, migration, race, ethnicity, gender, colonialism, health, technology, and human evolution
  - **Anthropological orientation**
    - Holism: social life, biology, ecology, language, and material culture are best understood in relation rather than isolation
    - Comparison: anthropology developed through cross-cultural and cross-temporal comparison, though comparative method has been repeatedly criticized for flattening context
    - Fieldwork: long-term immersion, participant observation, language learning, relational knowledge, and reflexive interpretation are central, especially in cultural anthropology
    - Contextualization: meaning depends on local history, institutions, cosmology, infrastructure, and power
    - Reflexivity: anthropologists analyze not only others but also their own categories, institutions, and colonial inheritances

- **Historical Development of Anthropology**
  - **Early precursors and travel knowledge**
    - Herodotus, Ibn Battuta, al-Biruni, Jesuit missionaries, colonial administrators, merchants, and explorers produced proto-ethnographic descriptions, though often filtered through imperial and religious lenses
    - Enlightenment thought developed comparative ideas of stages, civilization, and human nature; Montesquieu, Rousseau, Ferguson, Smith, and Herder influenced later anthropology
    - Early racial typologies and comparative philology shaped ideas of difference but also laid foundations for scientific racism and colonial hierarchy
  - **Nineteenth-century evolutionism and diffusionism**
    - E. B. Tylor: culture as "that complex whole"; survivals; animism; unilineal cultural evolution
    - Lewis Henry Morgan: kinship, social evolution, *Ancient Society*; savagery-barbarism-civilization scheme, later deeply criticized but historically influential
    - James Frazer: comparative mythology, magic-religion-science sequence, armchair anthropology
    - Diffusionist currents: Grafton Elliot Smith, W. J. Perry, Fritz Graebner, Wilhelm Schmidt; traits and cultural areas spreading across regions rather than arising independently
    - These traditions built the discipline's early comparative ambitions but were entangled with colonial extraction, ranking, and speculation detached from field evidence
  - **Historical particularism and the Boasian revolution**
    - Franz Boas rejected unilineal evolution and emphasized historical particularity, rigorous fieldwork, linguistic competence, anti-racist critique, and cultural relativism
    - Boas challenged biological determinism and scientific racism through craniometric and migration studies
    - Major Boasian students: Ruth Benedict, Margaret Mead, Alfred Kroeber, Edward Sapir, Zora Neale Hurston, Melville Herskovits, Ella Deloria; each extended culture, personality, language, folklore, and race scholarship
    - Boasian anthropology institutionalized the four-field framework and made salvage ethnography central, though salvage projects themselves were shaped by settler colonial assumptions about disappearance
  - **British social anthropology**
    - Bronislaw Malinowski: participant observation, functionalism, Trobriand fieldwork, exchange and magic; canonical model of ethnographic immersion
    - A. R. Radcliffe-Brown: structural functionalism, social structure, comparative institutions, kinship and descent systems
    - E. E. Evans-Pritchard, Meyer Fortes, Max Gluckman, Audrey Richards, Victor Turner, Edmund Leach: lineage systems, political systems, ritual, conflict, symbolism, Manchester School processual analysis
    - British traditions emphasized institutions, structure, and political order more than American culture-and-personality traditions
  - **French structuralism and allied traditions**
    - Marcel Mauss: gift exchange, body techniques, personhood, total social fact; among the most foundational anthropological thinkers
    - Claude Levi-Strauss: structuralism, kinship, myth, binary oppositions, transformations, nature/culture distinction
    - Louis Dumont, Maurice Godelier, Pierre Clastres, Françoise Héritier extended structural and comparative analysis in kinship, hierarchy, and politics
  - **Post-1960s transformation**
    - Decolonization, civil rights, feminism, Indigenous activism, and reflexive critique transformed anthropology's objects and methods
    - Interpretive anthropology: Clifford Geertz, symbolic analysis, thick description
    - Marxist and political-economic anthropology: Eric Wolf, Sidney Mintz, Maurice Godelier, June Nash; capitalism, colonialism, peasants, labor, commodity chains
    - Practice theory and reflexive turns: Pierre Bourdieu, Sherry Ortner, Marshall Sahlins, James Clifford, George Marcus, Renato Rosaldo, Lila Abu-Lughod
    - Science and technology studies, multispecies ethnography, ontological anthropology, medical anthropology, and decolonial anthropology expanded late-20th and early-21st-century agendas

- **Research Methods and Fieldwork**
  - **Participant observation and ethnography**
    - Long-term immersion, learning by participation, observing everyday practice, building rapport, recording fieldnotes, and contextual interpretation remain core anthropological methods
    - Malinowski's Trobriand fieldwork became the iconic model, though later critiques highlighted asymmetries, diaries, gender exclusions, and colonial dependency
    - Ethnographic outputs include monographs, collaborative media, life histories, archives, oral histories, maps, and multimodal documentation
  - **Interviewing and life history**
    - Semi-structured interviews, genealogies, elicitation, oral tradition recording, narrative life histories, key informant methods, focus groups, collaborative workshops
    - Life history methods illuminate subjectivity, migration, trauma, gender, labor, and historical change
  - **Comparative and archival methods**
    - Ethnohistory, colonial archives, mission records, legal records, museum collections, census data, and oral tradition used to reconstruct social worlds
    - Comparative method remains important but must account for colonial entanglement, translation asymmetry, and selection bias
  - **Material and spatial methods**
    - Mapping, sensory ethnography, visual anthropology, museum object study, GIS-linked archaeology, settlement analysis, remote sensing, bioarchaeological sampling, network analysis
  - **Ethics and politics of method**
    - Informed consent, reciprocity, harm reduction, community review, Indigenous sovereignty, repatriation, language access, collaborative authorship, data governance, and refusal are central ethical issues
    - Classic AAA ethics debates followed Project Camelot, Cold War entanglements, military anthropology, and misuse of ethnographic knowledge

- **Cultural Anthropology**
  - **Core themes**
    - Culture, social organization, kinship, exchange, ritual, symbolism, personhood, embodiment, gender, political authority, law, economy, cosmology, migration, media, and everyday life
    - Culture as shared meaning was central in Boasian and symbolic traditions, but later approaches treat culture as contested, strategic, embodied, and unequally distributed rather than coherent consensus
  - **Kinship and social organization**
    - Lewis Henry Morgan and early kinship systems; descent theory, alliance theory, household analysis, relatedness studies
    - Structural-functionalism: Fortes, Evans-Pritchard, African lineage systems
    - Alliance theory: Levi-Strauss on exchange of women, restricted and generalized exchange, elementary structures of kinship
    - Feminist and queer critiques: kinship beyond descent and heterosexual marriage; Janet Carsten on relatedness; Kath Weston on chosen families; queer kinship and reproductive technologies
    - Household, care, adoption, fostering, inheritance, marriage strategies, domestic labor, and reproductive politics remain central topics
  - **Economic anthropology**
    - Gift, reciprocity, redistribution, household production, barter, money, markets, labor, debt, informal economies, value, and exchange systems
    - Mauss's *The Gift*; Karl Polanyi's embeddedness and substantivism; Marshall Sahlins on reciprocity; Annette Weiner on inalienable possessions; David Graeber on debt and value
    - Debates: formalism vs. substantivism; market universals vs. socially embedded economies; capitalism, precarity, extraction, and financialization
  - **Political anthropology**
    - Segmentary lineages, chiefdoms, states, stateless societies, sovereignty, law, violence, colonial rule, resistance, and everyday power
    - Evans-Pritchard and Fortes on African political systems; Pierre Clastres on societies against the state; James Scott on state legibility, evasion, and infrapolitics; Akhil Gupta on bureaucracy and state effect
    - Citizenship, border regimes, policing, humanitarianism, NGOs, and governance assemblages are now central topics
  - **Religion and ritual**
    - Tylor, Frazer, Durkheim, Mauss, Evans-Pritchard, Victor Turner, Mary Douglas, Talal Asad, Birgit Meyer, Webb Keane, Saba Mahmood
    - Ritual, sacrifice, purity, possession, pilgrimage, shamanism, ethics, everyday piety, secularism, material religion, and conversion studies
  - **Symbolic and interpretive anthropology**
    - Clifford Geertz: thick description, webs of significance, religion as cultural system, interpretation of symbols
    - Victor Turner: symbols, liminality, communitas, social drama
    - Mary Douglas: purity, classification, pollution, risk and blame
    - Sherry Ortner: key symbols, practice, resistance, serious games
  - **Practice, power, and political economy**
    - Bourdieu: habitus, field, symbolic violence, practice
    - Eric Wolf: people without history; capitalism and connected worlds
    - Sidney Mintz: sugar, slavery, plantations, consumption, historical anthropology
    - Michel-Rolph Trouillot: silencing, power in the production of history, anthropology after colonialism
    - Lila Abu-Lughod: writing against culture, partiality, feminism, critique of reified cultural wholes

- **Biological / Physical Anthropology**
  - **Human evolution and paleoanthropology**
    - Primate evolution, hominin origins, bipedalism, encephalization, tool use, diet, dispersal, sociality, symbolic behavior
    - Major taxa and debates: Sahelanthropus, Ardipithecus, Australopithecus, Paranthropus, Homo habilis, Homo erectus, Homo heidelbergensis, Neanderthals, Denisovans, Homo sapiens, Homo floresiensis, Homo naledi
    - Key researchers: Raymond Dart, Louis and Mary Leakey, Richard Leakey, Donald Johanson, Tim White, Meave and Louise Leakey, Chris Stringer, Svante Pääbo
    - Ancient DNA revolution transformed kinship, migration, admixture, population history, and human uniqueness debates
  - **Primatology**
    - Nonhuman primates as comparative models for behavior, sociality, cognition, tool use, conflict, cooperation, and life history
    - Major figures: Jane Goodall, Dian Fossey, Biruté Galdikas, Sarah Blaffer Hrdy, Frans de Waal, Robert Sapolsky, Christophe Boesch
    - Topics: dominance, kin selection, female choice, coalitions, culture in primates, communication, disease ecology, conservation
  - **Human variation and adaptation**
    - Population genetics, phenotypic plasticity, adaptation to altitude, heat, cold, diet, pathogens, and urban/ecological stress
    - Race as a socially real but biologically non-discrete classification; anti-racist anthropology rejects typological race science while studying embodiment of racism and inequality
    - Human biological diversity, epigenetics, developmental plasticity, microbiomes, life history theory, biocultural approaches
  - **Bioarchaeology and skeletal biology**
    - Diet, health, trauma, paleopathology, activity patterns, migration, isotopes, mortuary practice, childhood stress, inequality from human remains
    - Major methods: stable isotope analysis, dental microwear, osteology, paleogenomics, biomechanics, incremental growth records

- **Linguistic Anthropology**
  - **Core concerns**
    - Language as social action, cultural practice, semiotic process, political instrument, and medium of personhood
    - Topics: language ideology, indexicality, discourse, performance, multilingualism, socialization, language shift, literacy, translation, genre, ritual speech, media circulation
  - **Foundations**
    - Franz Boas, Edward Sapir, Benjamin Lee Whorf, Dell Hymes, Roman Jakobson, John Gumperz, Michael Silverstein, William Hanks, Elinor Ochs, Bambi Schieffelin, Alessandro Duranti
    - Sapir-Whorf tradition reframed as linguistic relativity rather than deterministic worldview lock-in
    - Ethnography of speaking: Hymes's SPEAKING model; communicative competence
  - **Major domains**
    - Language socialization: Ochs and Schieffelin; acquisition as culturally organized participation
    - Indexicality and ideology: Silverstein, Irvine and Gal; how language marks class, race, gender, moral worth, and political belonging
    - Performance and verbal art: Bauman, Briggs; narrative, poetics, ritual, folklore, stance
    - Linguistic inequality: accent discrimination, standard language ideology, colonial language policy, endangered languages, revitalization
    - Semiotic anthropology: Peircean signs, icon/index/symbol, distributed semiosis, material-semiotic chains

- **Archaeology**
  - **Foundations and scope**
    - Archaeology reconstructs past human life through material remains, landscapes, architecture, bioarchaeology, paleoenvironmental evidence, and scientific dating
    - Subfields: prehistoric archaeology, classical archaeology, historical archaeology, maritime archaeology, industrial archaeology, landscape archaeology, archaeometry, bioarchaeology, zooarchaeology, paleoethnobotany, geoarchaeology
  - **History of archaeological thought**
    - Antiquarianism to scientific archaeology: Thomsen's Three Age System, Pitt-Rivers's systematic excavation, Petrie's seriation
    - Culture-historical archaeology: Gordon Childe, migration and diffusion models, typology, culture areas
    - Processual archaeology / New Archaeology: Lewis Binford, systems theory, scientific explanation, adaptation, settlement systems, middle-range theory
    - Post-processual archaeology: Ian Hodder, Michael Shanks, Christopher Tilley; symbolism, agency, interpretation, reflexivity, multivocality
    - Indigenous, community, feminist, Black, and decolonial archaeologies critique extractive excavation and heritage ownership regimes
  - **Methods and evidence**
    - Survey, excavation, stratigraphy, context, seriation, typology, radiocarbon dating, dendrochronology, thermoluminescence, OSL, archaeomagnetism, aDNA, isotope analysis, residue analysis, LiDAR, GPR, GIS, remote sensing
    - Formation processes: Michael Schiffer; taphonomy, site disturbance, curation, depositional histories
    - Settlement archaeology, household archaeology, regional survey, landscape archaeology, network analysis, experimental archaeology
  - **Key domains**
    - Hunter-gatherer archaeology, origins of agriculture, urbanism, state formation, trade, colonial encounters, slavery, industrialization, conflict archaeology, heritage and museum studies
    - Public archaeology and heritage management: NAGPRA, UNESCO, looting, provenance, repatriation, community interpretation, digital heritage

- **Forensic Anthropology**
  - Human identification from skeletal remains: sex estimation, age-at-death, ancestry debates, stature, trauma analysis, taphonomy, commingling, mass disasters, missing persons investigations
  - Major figures: Clyde Snow, William Bass, Douglas Ubelaker, Sue Black
  - Interfaces with law, human rights, and disaster victim identification; Argentine Forensic Anthropology Team as model of politically engaged forensic practice
  - Mass graves, transitional justice, genocide investigations, humanitarian forensic work, Indigenous missing persons cases, ethical treatment of remains

- **Medical Anthropology**
  - Culture, illness, embodiment, diagnosis, suffering, healing systems, pharmaceuticals, psychiatry, care, public health, biosociality, disability, epidemics
  - Foundational figures: Arthur Kleinman, Byron Good, Nancy Scheper-Hughes, Margaret Lock, Veena Das, Paul Farmer, Merrill Singer, Emily Martin, Didier Fassin
  - Explanatory models of illness, structural violence, syndemics, medical pluralism, biopolitics, reproductive justice, chronicity, mental health, disability worlds
  - Biosocial and syndemic approaches integrate biology, inequality, environment, and political economy

- **Major Theoretical Currents**
  - **Functionalism and structural functionalism**
    - Malinowski on institutions meeting needs; Radcliffe-Brown on social structure and functional integration
  - **Structuralism**
    - Levi-Strauss on kinship, myth, binary oppositions, transformations
  - **Marxist and political-economic anthropology**
    - Wolf, Mintz, Godelier, Meillassoux, Nash, Taussig; capitalism, labor, colonialism, commodity chains
  - **Interpretive anthropology**
    - Geertz, Turner, Douglas; symbols, meaning, ritual, thick description
  - **Practice theory**
    - Bourdieu, Ortner, Sahlins; habitus, reproduction, agency, history
  - **Feminist anthropology**
    - Michelle Rosaldo, Louise Lamphere, Sherry Ortner, Gayle Rubin, Rayna Rapp, Marilyn Strathern, Saba Mahmood, Audra Simpson; gender systems, reproduction, kinship, coloniality, refusal
  - **Postcolonial and decolonial anthropology**
    - Talal Asad, Edward Said, Johannes Fabian, Trouillot, Linda Tuhiwai Smith, Leanne Betasamosake Simpson, Glen Coulthard, Zoe Todd; critique of anthropology's imperial formation and epistemic extraction
  - **Ontological and multispecies turns**
    - Eduardo Viveiros de Castro, Philippe Descola, Tim Ingold, Anna Tsing, Donna Haraway, Eben Kirksey; human-nonhuman relations, animism, ontological pluralism, infrastructures of life

- **Major Topic Areas**
  - Kinship, marriage, household, reproduction, adoption, descent, alliance, care economies
  - Exchange, debt, money, labor, markets, precarity, informal economies, financialization
  - Ritual, religion, secularism, ethics, morality, witchcraft, shamanism, conversion, material religion
  - Gender, sexuality, embodiment, queer worlds, masculinity, reproductive technologies, family diversity
  - Race, ethnicity, nationalism, indigeneity, colonialism, diaspora, border regimes, migration
  - State, bureaucracy, law, sovereignty, policing, violence, humanitarianism, citizenship, development
  - Media, infrastructure, digital life, platform labor, surveillance, AI, science and technology
  - Environment, climate, multispecies relations, extraction, conservation, disaster, food systems

- **Anthropology of Science, Technology, and Media**
  - Laboratory ethnography: Latour and Woolgar, Knorr Cetina; scientific fact-making, epistemic cultures
  - Infrastructure and platform studies: data centers, logistics, algorithmic systems, maintenance, repair, digital labor, automation
  - AI and machine learning as anthropological objects: datasets, annotation labor, cultural assumptions embedded in models, human-machine interaction, governance
  - Media anthropology: circulation, publics, visuality, broadcast histories, smartphones, social media, meme cultures, mediation of intimacy and politics

- **Applied, Public, and Collaborative Anthropology**
  - Development anthropology, policy anthropology, business anthropology, design anthropology, legal anthropology, educational anthropology, environmental anthropology, museum anthropology
  - Public anthropology engages wider publics, advocacy, expert testimony, community projects, repatriation, policy critique, and collaborative research design
  - Engaged anthropology includes participatory action research, Indigenous collaboration, activist scholarship, humanitarian forensics, public health work, and heritage stewardship

- **Ethics, Colonialism, and Decolonization**
  - Anthropology was historically entangled with empire, missionization, extraction, museum accumulation, salvage paradigms, and racial hierarchy
  - Decolonizing anthropology addresses land, sovereignty, archives, collections, research governance, authorship, and epistemic justice
  - Repatriation and restitution: NAGPRA, Indigenous remains, looted objects, sacred materials, museum ethics, digital repatriation
  - Refusal, reciprocity, data sovereignty, community review, Indigenous methods, collaborative curation, reparative description

- **Frontier / Contemporary Research in Anthropology (2020–present)**
  - **Ancient DNA and biocultural synthesis**
    - aDNA, isotopes, proteomics, and residue analysis continue to transform archaeology and biological anthropology, especially around migration, kinship, diet, disease, and demography
    - Major debates concern genetic determinism, overinterpretation, consent, descendant community rights, and integration with archaeology and oral tradition
  - **Climate, extraction, and multispecies worlds**
    - Climate change, wildfires, drought, coastal retreat, food insecurity, extinction, and extractive infrastructures are central to contemporary ethnography
    - Multispecies and environmental anthropology study forests, microbes, fungi, animals, pollution, toxicity, energy systems, and more-than-human governance
  - **Digital, platform, and AI anthropology**
    - Researchers analyze algorithmic governance, creator economies, digital intimacy, gaming, crypto communities, content moderation labor, surveillance, and generative AI infrastructures
    - Online/offline distinction is increasingly treated as analytically insufficient; digital ethnography now studies hybrid infrastructures and translocal publics
  - **Decolonial and Indigenous resurgence**
    - Indigenous anthropologies, tribal IRB systems, land-based research, language revitalization, refusal of extractive publication norms, and community-controlled heritage work are expanding rapidly
  - **Migration, borders, and violence**
    - Border infrastructures, asylum regimes, deportation, detention, humanitarian corridors, war displacement, and climate migration remain major ethnographic and archaeological topics
  - **Health, care, and inequality**
    - Pandemic aftermath, long-COVID, disability justice, reproductive politics, pharmaceutical governance, care crises, and mental health infrastructures remain active domains
  - **Open problems and live debates**
    - How to preserve anthropology's holistic ambitions under increasing specialization
    - How to integrate biological, archaeological, linguistic, and ethnographic evidence without collapsing distinct epistemologies
    - How to practice anthropology ethically when communities face surveillance, extraction, and political risk
    - How to decolonize fieldwork, museums, archives, and theory without merely adding marginalized cases to unchanged institutional structures

### Psychology
- **Basics**
  - **Scope of the field**
    - Psychology is the scientific study of mind, brain, behavior, experience, development, and social interaction across humans and nonhuman animals
    - Core levels of analysis: biological, computational, cognitive, affective, developmental, interpersonal, cultural, institutional, and evolutionary
    - Central questions: how do organisms perceive, learn, remember, decide, feel, develop, relate, suffer, adapt, and change?
    - Enduring tensions: mind vs. behavior; nature vs. nurture; individual differences vs. universal processes; explanation vs. interpretation; laboratory control vs. ecological validity; nomothetic vs. idiographic approaches
    - Major branches: experimental psychology, cognitive psychology, developmental psychology, social psychology, personality psychology, clinical psychology, counseling psychology, neuropsychology, health psychology, industrial-organizational psychology, quantitative psychology, comparative psychology, cultural psychology, forensic psychology
  - **What counts as evidence in psychology**
    - Experimental manipulation and causal inference
    - Psychometrics and latent-variable modeling
    - Behavioral observation and coding
    - Self-report and experience sampling
    - Neuroimaging, psychophysiology, lesion evidence, and electrophysiology
    - Longitudinal and developmental designs
    - Cross-cultural comparison and ethnographic contextualization
    - Meta-analysis, systematic review, and reproducibility checks

- **Historical Development of Psychology**
  - **Philosophical and physiological precursors**
    - Ancient sources: Plato on tripartite soul, Aristotle's *De Anima*, Stoic theories of emotion, Buddhist analyses of mind and attention, early Indian theories of cognition in Nyaya and Buddhist epistemology
    - Early modern philosophy: Descartes on dualism and reflexes; Locke, Berkeley, and Hume on associationism and empiricism; Kant on limits of introspective science; Leibniz on unconscious petites perceptions
    - Physiology and psychophysics foundations: Johannes Muller, Hermann von Helmholtz, Ernst Weber, Gustav Fechner, Franciscus Donders; reaction time, sensory thresholds, nerve conduction, lawful relations between stimulus and sensation
  - **Psychology as a distinct science (late 19th century)**
    - Wilhelm Wundt founded the Leipzig laboratory in 1879; experimental introspection, apperception, voluntarism, and reaction time paradigms
    - Edward B. Titchener developed structuralism; analysis of conscious elements into sensations, images, and feelings; later criticized for narrow introspectionism
    - William James (*Principles of Psychology*, 1890): stream of consciousness, habit, attention, emotion, pragmatism; foundational for functionalism and American psychology
    - Franz Brentano's act psychology influenced phenomenology, Gestalt psychology, and intentionality-centered approaches
    - Hermann Ebbinghaus pioneered experimental memory research with nonsense syllables; forgetting curve and spacing effects
    - Francis Galton launched differential psychology, mental testing, psychometrics, and behavior genetics, though entangled with eugenics
  - **Functionalism and applied expansion**
    - John Dewey, James Rowland Angell, Harvey Carr: functionalism emphasized adaptation, function, and practical activity over structural decomposition
    - G. Stanley Hall institutionalized American psychology, child study, and professional organizations but also promoted recapitulation theories now rejected
    - Lightner Witmer founded clinical psychology (1896 psychological clinic at Penn)
    - Hugo Munsterberg linked psychology to law, industry, and applied settings
  - **Psychoanalysis and dynamic psychologies**
    - Sigmund Freud: unconscious conflict, repression, psychosexual stages, dream interpretation, transference, structural model (id, ego, superego)
    - Carl Jung: collective unconscious, archetypes, individuation, introversion/extraversion
    - Alfred Adler: inferiority, compensation, social interest
    - Karen Horney, Erik Erikson, Anna Freud, Melanie Klein, Donald Winnicott, Heinz Kohut, Jacques Lacan: major post-Freudian schools including ego psychology, object relations, self psychology, and structural psychoanalysis
  - **Behaviorism**
    - Ivan Pavlov: classical conditioning, conditioned reflexes
    - John B. Watson: methodological behaviorism; psychology as prediction and control of behavior
    - B. F. Skinner: radical behaviorism, operant conditioning, reinforcement schedules, verbal behavior, behavior modification
    - Edward C. Tolman: purposive behaviorism, cognitive maps
    - Clark Hull and Kenneth Spence: neo-behaviorist formal theories
    - Behavior analysis remains active in learning science, autism interventions, animal training, and decision research
  - **Gestalt and phenomenological alternatives**
    - Max Wertheimer, Wolfgang Kohler, Kurt Koffka: perception organized into wholes; figure-ground, closure, proximity, insight learning
    - Kurt Lewin: field theory, group dynamics, action research; bridge between Gestalt and social psychology
    - Phenomenological and human science traditions later influenced qualitative psychology, existential therapy, and embodied cognition
  - **The cognitive revolution**
    - Noam Chomsky's critique of Skinner's *Verbal Behavior* helped undermine behaviorist dominance in language research
    - George A. Miller, Jerome Bruner, Ulric Neisser, Herbert Simon, Allen Newell: information-processing, memory limitations, problem solving, cognitive science
    - Cognitive psychology integrated computation, representation, attention, memory, language, and reasoning; later extended to embodied, ecological, and predictive approaches
  - **Humanistic and existential psychologies**
    - Carl Rogers: client-centered therapy, unconditional positive regard, person-centered growth
    - Abraham Maslow: hierarchy of needs, self-actualization, peak experiences
    - Rollo May, Viktor Frankl, Irvin Yalom: existential anxiety, meaning, freedom, death, responsibility
  - **Contemporary integration and fragmentation**
    - Late 20th and early 21st century psychology became methodologically plural but institutionally fragmented
    - Cognitive neuroscience, affective science, behavioral genetics, computational psychiatry, cultural psychology, and open science became major contemporary axes

- **Research Methods and Quantitative Foundations**
  - **Research design**
    - True experiments, quasi-experiments, natural experiments, longitudinal designs, twin and adoption studies, ecological momentary assessment, cross-lagged panels, intensive repeated-measures studies
    - Internal validity threats: confounding, demand characteristics, experimenter expectancy, attrition, instrumentation changes, regression to the mean
    - External validity concerns: WEIRD samples, laboratory artificiality, narrow operationalization, publication bias
  - **Measurement and psychometrics**
    - Classical test theory: reliability, true score, measurement error
    - Factor analysis: Charles Spearman, Thurstone, Cattell, modern exploratory and confirmatory approaches
    - Item response theory and Rasch modeling
    - Construct validity tradition: Cronbach and Meehl, nomological networks, convergent and discriminant validity
    - Key assessments: WAIS/WISC, MMPI-2/MMPI-3, PAI, NEO PI-R/NEO-PI-3, BDI, STAI, CBCL, Conners, Raven's Progressive Matrices
  - **Statistics and causal inference**
    - NHST, effect sizes, confidence intervals, Bayesian inference, multilevel models, structural equation models, mediation/moderation, latent growth modeling, network analysis, machine learning in prediction
    - Meta-analysis: Gene Glass origin; heterogeneity, p-curve, multiverse analyses, publication bias diagnostics, registered reports
  - **Replication crisis and open science**
    - Problems: low power, researcher degrees of freedom, questionable research practices, file-drawer effect, conceptual vs. direct replication confusion
    - Flashpoints: Daryl Bem precognition paper (2011), social priming controversies, ego depletion debates, power posing debate, facial feedback debate, reproducibility projects
    - Reform movement: preregistration, registered reports, open data/code/materials, Many Labs, Psychological Science Accelerator, better measurement, adversarial collaboration
    - Key figures in reform and debate: Brian Nosek, Simine Vazire, Dorothy Bishop, Uri Simonsohn, Daniel Lakens, Andrew Gelman, Joseph Simmons, Leif Nelson

- **Biological Bases of Behavior**
  - **Neural foundations**
    - Neurons, synapses, neurotransmitters, action potentials, plasticity, long-term potentiation, neurogenesis limits and debates
    - Brain systems: cortex, basal ganglia, hippocampus, amygdala, cerebellum, hypothalamus, brainstem, default mode network, salience network, executive control network
    - Key methods: lesion studies, single-unit recording, EEG/ERP, MEG, fMRI, DTI, TMS, tDCS, optogenetics in animals
  - **Behavioral genetics**
    - Twin, family, and adoption designs; heritability; gene-environment correlation and interaction
    - Genome-wide association studies (GWAS), polygenic scores, missing heritability, population stratification concerns
    - Major contributors: Thomas Bouchard, Robert Plomin, Eric Turkheimer; debate over interpretation and social misuse
  - **Hormones and physiology**
    - Stress systems: HPA axis, cortisol, allostatic load, McEwen
    - Social hormones and their overstatements: oxytocin, testosterone, vasopressin; context-sensitive effects
    - Autonomic measures: heart-rate variability, skin conductance, pupillometry, startle response

- **Sensation, Perception, and Psychophysics**
  - Absolute and difference thresholds, Weber's law, Fechner's law, signal detection theory
  - Vision: color, depth, motion, object recognition, visual attention, predictive coding accounts, dorsal/ventral streams
  - Audition: pitch, localization, speech perception, music cognition
  - Multisensory integration, body representation, proprioception, pain perception, interoception
  - Gibson's ecological psychology: affordances, direct perception
  - Major figures: Helmholtz, Fechner, Gibson and Eleanor Gibson, Hubel and Wiesel, Anne Treisman, David Marr, Stephen Kosslyn, Elizabeth Spelke

- **Learning and Behavior Analysis**
  - Classical conditioning: acquisition, extinction, spontaneous recovery, generalization, discrimination, Rescorla-Wagner model
  - Operant conditioning: reinforcement, punishment, shaping, chaining, schedule effects, matching law, stimulus control
  - Observational learning and social learning: Albert Bandura, Bobo doll studies, self-efficacy
  - Reinforcement learning and modern computational approaches link psychology with AI and neuroscience
  - Applications: ABA, contingency management, organizational behavior management, exposure therapy, habit formation, addiction treatment

- **Cognitive Psychology**
  - **Attention and executive control**
    - Selective attention: Broadbent early filter, Treisman attenuation, Deutsch and Deutsch late-selection debates
    - Working memory: Baddeley and Hitch model, central executive, phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, episodic buffer; Cowan's embedded processes model
    - Cognitive control: task switching, inhibition, conflict monitoring, Stroop, Flanker, Simon tasks; prefrontal cortex and control networks
  - **Memory**
    - Atkinson-Shiffrin modal model, levels of processing (Craik and Lockhart), Tulving's episodic vs. semantic memory, autobiographical memory
    - False memory research: Elizabeth Loftus; misinformation effect; memory reconsolidation
    - Prospective memory, metamemory, collective memory, flashbulb memory debates
  - **Reasoning and decision making**
    - Heuristics and biases: Kahneman and Tversky, prospect theory, framing effects, availability, representativeness, anchoring
    - Dual-process debates: System 1/System 2, Evans and Stanovich, critiques of simplistic dichotomies
    - Gigerenzer's ecological rationality; bounded rationality (Simon)
  - **Problem solving and creativity**
    - Gestalt insight, analogical reasoning, expertise, deliberate practice, incubation, constraint relaxation
    - Creativity research: Guilford, Torrance tests, Teresa Amabile's componential theory, Dean Simonton, Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi systems view
  - **Language and psycholinguistics**
    - Language acquisition debates: Chomsky, Pinker, usage-based theorists such as Tomasello
    - Sentence processing, lexical access, embodied semantics, bilingual cognition, reading and dyslexia research
  - **Consciousness and metacognition**
    - Global workspace theory, higher-order thought theories, integrated information theory, predictive processing accounts, metacognitive accuracy, error monitoring

- **Developmental Psychology**
  - **Foundations**
    - Jean Piaget: sensorimotor, preoperational, concrete operational, formal operational stages; conservation, egocentrism, constructivism
    - Lev Vygotsky: sociocultural development, zone of proximal development, scaffolding, internalization
    - Information-processing developmental accounts and dynamic systems alternatives
  - **Attachment and socioemotional development**
    - John Bowlby and Mary Ainsworth: attachment theory; secure, anxious, avoidant, disorganized attachment; Strange Situation
    - Temperament: Thomas and Chess; Rothbart models
    - Theory of mind: false belief tasks; developmental trajectories and autism-related debates
  - **Lifespan development**
    - Erik Erikson psychosocial stages
    - Bronfenbrenner ecological systems theory
    - Adolescence research: Laurence Steinberg, identity, risk-taking, peer influence, puberty, digital media effects
    - Adult development: Baltes, socioemotional selectivity (Carstensen), cognitive aging, dementia research, resilience in aging
  - **Developmental psychopathology**
    - Cicchetti and Sroufe traditions: trajectories, risk, protective factors, equifinality and multifinality
    - Adverse childhood experiences, trauma, foster care, neurodevelopment, school transitions, resilience processes

- **Social Psychology**
  - **Classic foundations**
    - Kurt Lewin: behavior as function of person and environment
    - Solomon Asch on conformity, Muzafer Sherif on norms, Stanley Milgram on obedience, Leon Festinger on cognitive dissonance, Fritz Heider on attribution
    - Gordon Allport on prejudice; Henri Tajfel and John Turner on social identity
  - **Core domains**
    - Attitudes and persuasion: Hovland, Petty and Cacioppo ELM, dual attitudes, implicit attitudes debates
    - Attribution: Jones and Davis, Kelley covariation model, actor-observer, self-serving bias
    - Self and identity: self-concept, self-verification, self-discrepancy (Higgins), symbolic self-completion, possible selves
    - Group processes: conformity, polarization, social loafing, deindividuation, leadership, collective action, intergroup conflict
    - Prosociality and morality: empathy, helping, bystander intervention (Darley and Latane), moral foundations debates, moral judgment dual-process models
    - Relationships: attraction, love, commitment, attachment in adulthood, interdependence theory
  - **Critical reappraisals**
    - Replication issues hit social psychology especially hard: priming, depletion, stereotype threat effect-size disputes, power posing, facial feedback
    - Cross-cultural and historical variability challenge claims of universality; Henrich, Heine, and Norenzayan on WEIRD minds

- **Personality Psychology**
  - **Trait traditions**
    - Gordon Allport, Raymond Cattell, Hans Eysenck, Lewis Goldberg, Costa and McCrae
    - Big Five / Five-Factor Model: openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, neuroticism
    - HEXACO adds honesty-humility (Ashton and Lee)
  - **Psychodynamic and motivational views**
    - Freud, object relations, attachment-based personality, defense mechanisms, implicit motives (McClelland)
  - **Social-cognitive personality**
    - Walter Mischel's critique of traits and the person-situation debate
    - CAPS model: if-then signatures, self-regulation, goals, expectancies
    - Self-determination theory (Deci and Ryan): autonomy, competence, relatedness
  - **Narrative identity**
    - Dan McAdams: life stories, redemptive and contamination sequences, identity across lifespan
  - **Assessment and debates**
    - Projective tests: Rorschach, TAT; controversies over reliability and validity
    - Personality disorders, maladaptive traits, DSM-5 alternative model, dimensional psychopathology overlap

- **Emotion and Affective Science**
  - William James and Carl Lange: bodily feedback theories of emotion
  - Cannon-Bard critiques, Schachter-Singer two-factor theory, appraisal theories (Magda Arnold, Richard Lazarus, Klaus Scherer)
  - Basic emotions tradition: Paul Ekman; debates over universality and construction
  - Psychological construction: Lisa Feldman Barrett; core affect, conceptualization, category construction
  - Affective neuroscience: Jaak Panksepp primary-process systems; Joseph LeDoux on fear and defensive circuits
  - Emotion regulation: James Gross process model, reappraisal, suppression, flexibility, psychopathology links
  - Social and moral emotions: shame, guilt, pride, embarrassment, envy, gratitude, awe, compassion

- **Clinical Psychology and Psychopathology**
  - **Nosology and diagnosis**
    - DSM history: DSM-I to DSM-5-TR; shifts from psychodynamic to descriptive psychiatry
    - ICD classification global role; DSM vs. ICD alignment issues
    - Categorical vs. dimensional debate; HiTOP, RDoC, network models of psychopathology
  - **Major disorders and research traditions**
    - Mood disorders: depression, bipolar spectrum; Beck cognitive theory; learned helplessness (Seligman); interpersonal models; inflammatory and reward-processing research
    - Anxiety disorders: phobias, panic, GAD, OCD, PTSD; fear conditioning, intolerance of uncertainty, inhibitory learning models
    - Psychotic disorders: schizophrenia spectrum; dopamine hypotheses, predictive-processing accounts, social defeat and trauma pathways
    - Personality disorders: borderline, narcissistic, antisocial, avoidant; attachment, trauma, temperament, dimensional models
    - Neurodevelopmental conditions: ADHD, autism, intellectual disability, learning disorders; heterogeneity and neurodiversity perspectives
    - Substance use and addiction: incentive salience (Robinson and Berridge), allostasis (Koob), habit models, harm reduction perspectives
    - Eating disorders: anorexia, bulimia, binge eating; cognitive, sociocultural, and neurobiological models
  - **Psychotherapy schools**
    - Psychodynamic therapies: transference, interpretation, mentalization-based therapy, transference-focused psychotherapy
    - Behavioral therapies: exposure, contingency management, behavioral activation
    - Cognitive therapy and CBT: Aaron Beck, Albert Ellis REBT, schema therapy, third-wave developments
    - Third-wave and contextual approaches: ACT (Hayes), DBT (Linehan), mindfulness-based CBT, compassion-focused therapy, functional analytic psychotherapy
    - Humanistic and experiential: Rogers, Gestalt therapy, emotion-focused therapy (Greenberg)
    - Family and systems therapies: Minuchin, Bowen, Satir, strategic therapy, attachment-based family work
  - **Evidence and process research**
    - Common factors vs. specific ingredients debate; therapeutic alliance (Bordin), expectancy, empathy, rupture and repair
    - Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence, clinician expertise, and patient values; debates over manualization and ecological validity
    - Psychotherapy meta-analysis: Smith and Glass; Wampold contextual model; Cuijpers on treatment comparison and methodological inflation

- **Counseling, Health, and Community Psychology**
  - Counseling psychology focuses on adjustment, strengths, vocational development, multicultural competence, and less severe but overlapping clinical issues
  - Health psychology: illness behavior, adherence, stress, coping (Lazarus and Folkman), psychoneuroimmunology, placebo/nocebo, chronic pain, sleep, lifestyle change
  - Community psychology: prevention, empowerment, ecological models, settings-level intervention, participatory research, liberation psychology (Ignacio Martin-Baro)
  - Positive psychology: Seligman, Csikszentmihalyi, character strengths (Peterson and Seligman), flourishing, meaning, hope, gratitude; criticized for individualism and neglect of structure

- **Neuropsychology and Cognitive Neuroscience**
  - Historical roots: Broca, Wernicke, Luria, Brenda Milner, patient H.M., split-brain research (Sperry, Gazzaniga)
  - Domains: executive dysfunction, aphasia, agnosia, amnesia, neglect, apraxia, dementia, TBI, stroke, epilepsy surgery outcomes
  - Assessment batteries: Halstead-Reitan, Wisconsin Card Sorting Test, Trail Making Test, Rey-Osterrieth, Boston Naming Test
  - Contemporary work integrates lesion mapping, connectomics, neuroimaging, computational modeling, and rehabilitation science

- **Industrial-Organizational Psychology**
  - Personnel selection, performance appraisal, training, motivation, leadership, team performance, safety, organizational justice, burnout, job design, work-family conflict
  - Major theories: expectancy theory (Vroom), job characteristics model (Hackman and Oldham), goal-setting theory (Locke and Latham), organizational citizenship behavior, transformational leadership, psychological safety (Amy Edmondson)
  - Psychometrics central to hiring and assessment; fairness, adverse impact, algorithmic hiring, workplace surveillance are current frontiers

- **Forensic and Legal Psychology**
  - Eyewitness memory: Loftus, Wells, lineup procedures, confidence-accuracy debates, false memory implications
  - Competency, insanity, risk assessment, psychopathy, jury decision making, confession research, false confessions (Kassin)
  - Forensic assessment ethics, violence prediction limits, actuarial vs. clinical judgment, adolescent culpability, trauma-informed interviewing

- **Cultural, Cross-Cultural, and Indigenous Psychologies**
  - Cross-cultural psychology compares populations on common constructs; cultural psychology argues psyche and culture are mutually constitutive
  - Major figures: Richard Shweder, Hazel Markus, Shinobu Kitayama, Michael Cole, Jerome Bruner, Jaan Valsiner, Carl Ratner
  - Individualism-collectivism, self-construal, emotion norms, moral reasoning, family structure, developmental pathways, culturally variable psychopathology concepts
  - Indigenous psychologies critique universalized Western constructs and seek locally grounded categories, methods, and healing models
  - Decolonial psychology addresses power, coloniality, language, epistemic violence, and community repair

- **Evolutionary, Comparative, and Ecological Psychology**
  - Comparative psychology studies cognition and behavior across species; Morgan's canon, Tinbergen's four questions, ethology and behavioral ecology intersections
  - Evolutionary psychology: Cosmides and Tooby, Buss, Pinker; adaptationist accounts of mating, jealousy, cooperation, kinship, cheater detection; criticized for speculative storytelling and weak evidentiary constraints
  - Behavioral ecology and cultural evolution alternatives: Boyd and Richerson, Henrich, niche construction, gene-culture coevolution
  - Ecological psychology and embodied cognition challenge internalist representation-heavy models

- **Consciousness, Self, and Embodiment**
  - Self-awareness, agency, minimal self, narrative self, body ownership, rubber hand illusion, depersonalization, meditation research
  - Embodied and enactive cognition: Varela, Thompson, Noe, Barsalou; cognition as action-oriented and situated in body-world systems
  - Predictive processing and active inference increasingly used as unifying frameworks across perception, emotion, and psychopathology, though still debated

- **Applied Domains and Public Relevance**
